\begin{array} { r } { U = M + \omega = ( u + \omega ) - e \sin { [ ( u + \omega ) - \omega ] } } \\ { = U _ { 0 } + n ( t - t _ { 0 } ) = \phi - X \sin { \phi } + Y \cos { \phi } , } \end{array}
$$

and hence

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { r } { a } \cos u = \cos \phi - X + \frac { Y ( X \sin \phi - Y \cos \phi ) } { 1 + ( 1 - X ^ { 2 } - Y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } } , } \\ { { \frac { r } { a } \sin u = \sin \phi - Y - \frac { X ( X \sin \phi - Y \cos \phi ) } { 1 + ( 1 - X ^ { 2 } - Y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Write $\delta \bar { \bf S } / d t = \alpha \bf R$ ; then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { \delta { \bf R } } { d t } } = { \frac { { \bf R } \cdot { \bf F } } { c } } \left[ ( r \sin u ) { \bf S } - ( r \cos u ) { \bf T } \right] } , \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { \delta { \bf S } } { d t } } = - ( r \sin u ) { \frac { { \bf R } \cdot { \bf F } } { c } } { \bf R } } , { \displaystyle ~ { \frac { \delta { \bf T } } { d t } } = ( r \cos u ) { \frac { { \bf R } \cdot { \bf F } } { c } } { \bf R } } , \ ~ } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { \delta \mathbf { r } } { d t } } = \left[ \left( r \cos u \right) \mathbf { T } - \left( r \sin u \right) \mathbf { S } \right] { \frac { \delta u } { d t } } + \left( r \cos u \right) { \frac { \delta \mathbf { S } } { d t } } + \left( r \sin u \right) { \frac { \delta \mathbf { T } } { d t } } = { }
$$

$$
r ^ { 2 } \frac { \delta u } { d t } = \left. ( r \cos u ) ( [ \mathbf { T } \times \mathbf { R } ] \cdot \mathbf { r } ) - ( r \sin u ) ( [ \mathbf { S } \times \mathbf { R } ] \cdot \mathbf { r } ) \right. \frac { \delta u } { d t } = 0 .
$$

Since $e \mathbf { P } = X \mathbf { S } + Y \mathbf { T }$ and $d \mathbf { r } / d t = \mathbf { v } _ { : }$ we have, putting

$$
\begin{array} { r } { c \mathbf { v } = ( X + \cos u ) \mathbf { T } - ( Y + \sin u ) \mathbf { S } , } \end{array}
$$

and operating with $\delta / d t$ ，

$$
{ \frac { \delta c } { d t } } \mathbf { v } + c { \frac { \delta \mathbf { v } } { d t } } = { \frac { \delta X } { d t } } \mathbf { T } - { \frac { \delta Y } { d t } } \mathbf { S } + ( X + \cos u ) { \frac { \delta \mathbf { T } } { d t } } - ( Y + \sin u ) { \frac { \delta \mathbf { S } } { d t } }
$$

Furthermore, operating with ·S and $\mathbf { \nabla \cdot } \mathbf { T }$ ,we find

$$
{ \frac { \delta X } { d t } } = \Big ( \mathbf { R } \cdot { \frac { \delta \mathbf { c } } { d t } } \Big ) ( \mathbf { v } \cdot \mathbf { T } ) + c ( \mathbf { F } \cdot \mathbf { T } ) , { \frac { \delta Y } { d t } } = - \Big ( \mathbf { R } \cdot { \frac { \delta \mathbf { c } } { d t } } \Big ) ( \mathbf { v } \cdot \mathbf { S } ) - c ( \mathbf { F } \cdot \mathbf { S } )
$$

The rate at which $\mathbf { P }$ and $\mathbf { Q }$ rotate within the orbital plane represents the rate of increase of $\pmb { \omega }$ Hence,

$$
\frac { \delta \omega } { d t } = - \mathbf { P } \cdot \frac { \delta \mathbf { Q } } { d t } = - \frac { 1 } { G } \mathbf { P } \cdot \frac { \delta \mathbf { G } } { d t } = - \frac { \mathbf { P } \cdot \mathbf { K } } { G } ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { \delta \mathbf { G } } { d t } = \mathbf { K } - \frac { 2 r ( \mathbf { F } \cdot \mathbf { R } ) } { c ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { R } ,
$$

and

$$
\frac { \delta { \cal U } _ { 0 } } { d t } = - { \bf P } \cdot \frac { \delta { \bf Q } } { d t } = - \frac { 1 } { G } { \bf P } \cdot \frac { \delta { \bf G } } { d t } = - \frac { { \bf P } \cdot { \bf K } } { G } .
$$

Evidently $\pmb { \omega }$ is the argument of the perihelion counted from the departure point S. Hence we adopt $\mathbf { S } _ { 0 } = \mathbf { T } _ { 0 }$ at the epoch $t _ { 0 }$ Thus $\omega _ { 0 } = 0 , X _ { 0 } = e , Y _ { 0 } = ($ .But it does not follow that we have $\mathbf { s } = \mathbf { T }$ at any subsequent time. Actually,

$$
\mathbf { P } = ( X \mathbf { S } + Y \mathbf { T } ) / ( X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

There exists a vector $\mathbf { Q } ( t )$ such that a rigid-body rotation about $\mathbf { Q }$ of the mutually perpendicular unit vectors $\mathbf { R } _ { 0 } , \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } , \mathbf { T } _ { 0 }$ will transform them to the instantaneous $\mathbf { R }$ ,S, $\mathbf { T }$ respectively. We use dyadics

$$
\Phi = { \bf R } { \bf R } _ { 0 } + { \bf S } { \bf S } _ { 0 } + { \bf T } { \bf T } _ { 0 } = \frac { ( 1 - Q ^ { 2 } ) { \bf I } + 2 { \bf Q } { \bf Q } + 2 { \bf I } \times { \bf Q } } { 1 + Q ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Any vector ${ \bf { U } } _ { 0 }$ is transformed to ${ \bf \delta U }$ by means of

$$
\mathbf { U } - \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } = \mathbf { Q } \times ( \mathbf { U } + \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } ) .
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 + Q ^ { 2 } ) ( \mathbf { U } + \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } ) = \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } + \mathbf { { Q } } \times \mathbf { { U } } _ { 0 } + ( \mathbf { { Q } } { \cdot } \mathbf { { U } } ) \mathbf { { Q } } } \\ { = \mathbf { U } - \mathbf { { Q } } \times \mathbf { { U } } + ( \mathbf { { Q } } { \cdot } \mathbf { { U } } ) \mathbf { { Q } } . \qquad } \end{array}
$$

Applying $\delta / \delta t$ to this, we obtain

$$
\frac { \delta \mathbf { U } } { \delta t } = \frac { \delta \mathbf { Q } } { \delta t } \times ( \mathbf { U } + \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } ) + \mathbf { Q } \times \frac { \delta \mathbf { U } } { \delta t } , \qquad \frac { \delta { U } } { \delta t } = \mathbf { K } \cdot [ \mathbf { r } \times \mathbf { U } ] ,
$$

where $\mathbf { U }$ represents either $\mathbf { R } , \mathbf { s } _ { \mathrm { : } }$ or $\mathbf { T }$ To derive $\delta \mathbf { Q } / \delta t$ from this we substitute for $\mathbf { \delta \tilde { \mathbf { U } } } / \delta t$ the expression

$$
\mathbf { r } \times \mathbf { U } = \left[ \frac { \mathbf { r } + \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \mathbf { Q } \times \frac { \mathbf { r } + \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } } { 2 } \right] \times \left[ \frac { \mathbf { U } + \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \mathbf { Q } \times \frac { \mathbf { U } + \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } } { 2 } \right]
$$

and expand all brackets; then the triple vector products cancel and recombine. It is justifiable to equate the coefficients of $\mathbf { \nabla } \times ( \mathbf { U } + \mathbf { U } _ { 0 } )$ ， because $\mathbf { \sigma } _ { \mathbf { U } }$ represents $\mathbf { R }$ ,S,and $\mathbf { T }$ simultaneously. The result is

$$
\frac { \delta \mathbf { Q } } { \delta t } = \frac { ( \mathbf { R } \cdot \mathbf { F } ) } { c } \frac { ( 1 + Q ^ { 2 } ) } { 4 } ( \mathbf { r } + \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } ) , \qquad \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } = ( r \cos u ) \cdot \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } + ( r \sin u ) \cdot \mathbf { T }
$$

Thus our final result is this last equation and

$$
U = U _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) + \int \frac { \delta U _ { 0 } } { \delta t } d t + \int \int \frac { \delta n } { \delta t } d t ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $\delta U _ { 0 } / \delta t$ is computed by the above formula and the equations for $\delta X / \delta t$ and $\delta Y / \delta t$ derived in the above, with

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d c } { d t } = \mathbf { r } \cdot [ \mathbf { F } \times \mathbf { R } ] , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d n } { d t } = \mathbf { \ } - \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { a } } \left( \mathbf { F } { \cdot } \mathbf { G } + \frac { 1 } { r c } \mathbf { R } \cdot \frac { d \mathbf { c } } { d t } \right) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
{ \frac { d \Delta M } { d t } } = { \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { G } } \mathbf { P } \cdot \mathbf { K } - { \frac { 2 \mathbf { F } \cdot \mathbf { r } } { \sqrt { a } } } \cdotp
$$

A further modification has been attempted by Musen (l961,1964, 1965),to include the effects of higher orders.Although Stromgren's method achieves mathematical elegance by using vectorial elements, it has the disadvantage of taking into account only the first-order perturbations； this is because Stromgren obtained the Gibbs rotation vector that indicates only to the first approximation the integrated value of the angular velocity of rotation of the osculating ellipse.Musen obtained the accurate form for the rotation matrix in terms of the Gibbs vector and gave the differential equation for the perturbations of this vector.At the same time, Musen's modification (Musen,l965) of Hill's method (Section lO.2） removes its inconveniences,that is, the interdependent constants of integration peculiar to Hill's method. The solution is given in a form that makes it possible to write the differential equations immediately for the general perturbations proportional to any prescribed product of masses.

Musen and Carpenter (l963) developed a new theory of general planetary perturbations in rectangular coordinates. Their theory has certain characteristics in common with Hill's method.They decomposed the perturbations along $\mathbf { r } , \mathbf { v } _ { \mathrm { : } }$ and $\mathbf { R }$ ,in contrast to Hill's decomposition along $\mathbf { r } _ { : }$ ， $\mathbf { R } \times \mathbf { r } ,$ and $\mathbf { R }$ .This decomposition leads to a direct method of integration, and the final formulas are in a convenient form for computer programming.Furthermore,the six integration constants are determined in a direct manner, in contrast to the seven constants appearing in Hill's double and triple integrals.As is generally accepted, the computation of higher-order perturbations is simpler in the method of rectangular coordinates than in Hansen's theory (Sections l0.5-l0.9).The final expressions are obtained in the form of trigonometric series with the number of arguments equal to three times the number of planets in the problem.

The potential is expanded in terms of multipoles,which facilitates the computation of the perturbations of any order.Let $\mathbf { r } _ { 0 }$ be the undisturbed value of the radius vector $\mathbf { r } ,$ and let $\mathbf { r } _ { 1 } , \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，be the perturbations of the first, second，... orders; then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { r } = \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } + \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } + \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } + \cdots , } \\ { \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } = \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \cdots . } \end{array}
$$

Write

$$
\nabla = \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { r } } , \qquad \nabla ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

and

$$
D = ( \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \nabla + \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } { \cdot } \nabla ^ { \prime } ) + ( \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } { \cdot } \nabla + \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } { \cdot } \nabla ^ { \prime } ) + { \cdot } \cdot \cdot { \cdot } .
$$

The values of $\mathbf { r } _ { i }$ and $\mathbf { r } _ { i } ^ { \prime } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ are considered to be constant during the del operations. Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \exp \cal { D } = 1 + ( { \bf r } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \nabla + { \bf r } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } { \cdot } \nabla ^ { \prime } ) } \\ { \displaystyle + \left[ ( { \bf r } _ { 2 } { \cdot } \nabla + { \bf r } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } { \cdot } \nabla ^ { \prime } ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( { \bf r } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \nabla + { \bf r } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } { \cdot } \nabla ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } \right] + \cdots , } \\ { \displaystyle ^ { \prime } \exp \cal { D } ) \frac { 1 } { r } = \frac { 1 } { r } - \frac { { \bf r } _ { 1 } { \cdot } { \bf r } } { r ^ { 3 } } + \left[ - \frac { { \bf r } { \cdot } { \bf r } _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( { \bf r } { \cdot } { \bf r } _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 5 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { { \bf r } _ { 1 } { \cdot } { \bf r } _ { 1 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \right] + \cdots . } \end{array}
$$

Applying these operations to

$$
\Omega = \mu _ { 1 } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { \rho } - \frac { { \bf r } \cdot { \bf r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \bigg ) , \qquad \boldsymbol { \rho } = { \bf r } ^ { \prime } - { \bf r } , \qquad \mu _ { 1 } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { M + m ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \nabla \Omega = \mu _ { 1 } \bigg ( \frac { \Theta } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime \prime } } \bigg ) , } \\ & { \nabla ^ { \prime } \Omega = \mu _ { 1 } \bigg ( - \frac { \Theta } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r ^ { \prime \prime } } + \frac { 3 \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } \cdot \mathbf { r } \right) } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } \bigg ) , } \\ & { \nabla \nabla \Omega = \mu _ { 1 } \bigg ( - \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 3 \boldsymbol { \mathsf { p } } \cdot \boldsymbol { \mathsf { p } } } { \rho ^ { 5 } } \bigg ) , } \\ & { \nabla ^ { \prime } \nabla \Omega = \mu _ { 1 } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 \boldsymbol { \mathsf { p } } \cdot \boldsymbol { \mathsf { p } } } { \rho ^ { 5 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime } } + \frac { 3 \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } \cdot \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } \bigg ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence the equations for the perturbation of various orders are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf x } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } { \bf x } } { r ^ { 3 } } = 0 , } & { ~ \mu = k ^ { 2 } ( M + m ) , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf x } _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \mu ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { { \bf x } _ { 1 } } { r ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 { \bf x } ( { \bf r } \cdot { \bf x } _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 5 } } \Big ) = \mu ^ { 2 } \nabla \Omega , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf x } _ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \mu ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { { \bf r } _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 { \bf r } ( { \bf r } \cdot { \bf x } _ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 5 } } \Big ) = } & { \mu ^ { 2 } ( { \bf r } _ { 1 } \cdot \nabla \nabla \Omega + { \bf r } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cdot \nabla ^ { \prime } \nabla \Omega ) + \frac { 3 \mu ^ { 2 } ( { \bf r } \cdot { \bf x } _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 5 } } } \\ & { ~ + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } \frac { { \bf x } ( { \bf x } _ { 1 } \cdot { \bf x } _ { 1 } ) } { r ^ { 5 } } - \frac { 1 5 } { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } \frac { { \bf x } ( { \bf r } \cdot { \bf x } _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 7 } } , } \end{array}
$$

These are of the form

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf x } } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + \mu ^ { 2 } \Big ( { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } } - { \frac { 3 { \bf r } \cdot { \bf r } } { r ^ { 5 } } } \Big ) { \bf x } = \mu ^ { 2 } { \bf F } ,
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { F } = m ^ { \prime } \left( { \frac { \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } - \mathbf { r } } { \rho ^ { 3 } } } - { \frac { \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } } \right) .
$$

Write

$$
\mathbf { x } = T \mathbf { r } + S \mathbf { v } + z \mathbf { R } ,
$$

where

$$
{ \frac { d \mathbf { r } } { d t } } = \mathbf { v } , { \frac { d \mathbf { v } } { d t } } = - \mu { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r ^ { 3 } } } ,
$$

and the area integral hR. $[ { \bf r } \times { \bf v } ] = 1$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } T } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 3 \mu ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } T - \frac { 2 \mu } { r ^ { 3 } } \frac { d S } { d t } = \mu ^ { 2 } h \mathbf { F } \cdot [ \mathbf { v } \times \mathbf { R } ] , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } S } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \mu \frac { d T } { d t } = \mu ^ { 2 } h \mathbf { F } \cdot [ \mathbf { R } \times \mathbf { r } ] , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } z } { r ^ { 3 } } = \mu ^ { 2 } \mathbf { F } \cdot \mathbf { R } . } } } \end{array}
$$

We have an integral

$$
\frac { d S } { d t } + 2 \mu T = \mu K _ { 3 } + \int \mu ^ { 2 } h \mathbf { F } { \cdot } \left[ \mathbf { R } \times \mathbf { r } \right] d t .
$$

Substituting this integral, we obtain

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } T } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } T } { r ^ { 3 } } } = \mu ^ { 2 } h \mathbf { F } \cdot [ \mathbf { v } \times \mathbf { R } ] + { \frac { 2 \mu ^ { 2 } K _ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } } + { \frac { 2 } { r ^ { 3 } } } \int \mu ^ { 3 } h \mathbf { F } \cdot [ \mathbf { R } \times \mathbf { r } ] d t .
$$

If we solve this equation and the equation for $s$ ,we find

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { T = K _ { 1 } r \cos f + K _ { 2 } r \sin f + 2 K _ { 3 } + A , } \\ & { S = 2 a ^ { 5 / 2 } K _ { 1 } [ \frac { 3 } { 2 } e n t + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) \sin \epsilon + \frac { 1 } { 4 } e \sin 2 \epsilon ] } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ \ ~ \ } + 2 a ^ { 5 / 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } K _ { 2 } ( \cos \epsilon - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \epsilon \cos 2 \epsilon ) - 3 \mu K _ { 3 } t + K _ { 4 } + B , } \\ & { z = K _ { 5 } r \cos f + K _ { 6 } r \sin f + C , } \end{array}
$$

in terms of the true anomaly $f$ and the eccentric anomaly $\epsilon$ , according to Hansen, where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal A } = \int h ^ { 2 } \mu ( { \bf F } \cdot [ { \bf v } \times { \bf R } ] ) ( { \bf R } \cdot [ { \bf x } \times { \bf \bar { r } } ] ) ~ d t } \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + ~ 2 \int h \mu ( { \bf F } \cdot [ { \bf R } \times { \bf x } { \bf r } ] ) ( 1 + h { \bf R } \cdot [ { \bf v } \times { \bf \bar { r } } ] ) ~ d t } , } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal B } = \int \int \mu ^ { 2 } h { \bf F } \cdot [ { \bf R } \times { \bf x } ] ~ d t ^ { 2 } - 2 \int \mu A d t } , } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ C = \int h \mu ( { \bf F } \cdot { \bf R } ) ( { \bf R } \cdot [ { \bf x } \times { \bf \bar { r } } ] ) ~ d t } . } \end{array}
$$

For computing the perturbations, we expand the components of the disturbing force in trigonometric series by means of numerical double harmonic analysis. Musen tried Hansen's method of expanding $1 / \rho ,$ where $\pmb { \rho }$ denotes the mutual distance, by using the Laplace coefficients. This has been successful in programming electronic computers for the computation of Hansen's perturbations of asteroids. But for highly eccentric orbits the iteration process does not converge rapidly enough. Hence Musen referred to direct double harmonic analysis. The development can be done in the mean anomalies $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l , \tilde { \mathbf { \xi } } }$ ,the eccentric anomalies $\epsilon$ or in the variables $\epsilon$ and $\phi _ { : }$ ,such that (Section 10.10)

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle r = a ( 1 - e \cos \epsilon ) , \qquad r ^ { \prime } = a ^ { \prime } ( 1 - e ^ { \prime } \cos \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \epsilon ^ { \prime } - e ^ { \prime } \sin \epsilon ^ { \prime } = \phi - \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } e \sin \epsilon . } } \end{array}
$$

The double harmonic analysis is carried out with two arguments $\epsilon$ and $\pmb { \eta }$ of Hansen (Section 10.7).A, $\pmb { B }$ ，and $c$ are expressed as integrals in terms of $\epsilon$ and $\pmb { \eta }$ . After each integration, $\pmb { \eta }$ is replaced by $\epsilon .$ .For computing the integration constants the series $A , B , C ,$ and their derivatives, are evaluated at $t = 0$ ，From these values and the initial conditions we obtain $K _ { 1 } , K _ { 2 } , K _ { 3 } , K _ { 4 } , K _ { 5 } , K _ { 6 }$ 、Adding the integration constants to the series, we find $T , S ,$ and $_ z$ in terms of $\epsilon$ .Then the perturbation is written

$$
\delta \mathbf { r } = T \mathbf { r } + S \mathbf { v } + z \mathbf { R } ,
$$

where $\delta \mathbf { r } = \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } , \mathbf { r } _ { 2 } , . . . .$ The operators $T , S , z$ are different for perturbations of different order.

The equations for general perturbation are based on the matrix whose elements are either the Lagrange or Poisson brackets for the Keplerian orbital elements,but the corresponding matrix becomes complicated for algebraic combination of the Keplerian elements； the more so if some redundant elements are introduced for the sake of symmetry,as in Musen's theory.Musen (l964a) referred to Pfaffian equations (Section 1.22) and Cartan's integral invariants (Sections 1.2, 4.17, and 4.22) by extending the work of Bilimovich (1943).

Let $r _ { i }$ and ${ \boldsymbol { v } } _ { \mathbf { i } }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ be respectively the position and velocity vectors,and consider the Pfaffian

$$
\phi = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } m _ { i } v _ { i } d r _ { i } - \left( T - U \right) d t .
$$

By the transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r } { v _ { i } = v _ { i } ( { / p } _ { { 1 } } , { / p } _ { { 2 } } , . . . , { / p } _ { { m } } ; q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) , } \\ { r _ { i } = r _ { i } ( { / p } _ { { 1 } } , { / p } _ { { 2 } } , . . . , { / p } _ { { m } } ; q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

with the conditions

$$
f _ { \rho } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { m } ; q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) = a _ { \rho } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , s ) ,
$$

the Pfaffian is transformed to

$$
\phi = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } P _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { \hat { p } } _ { 2 } , . . . , \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { m } ; q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) d \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { i }
$$

$$
+ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } Q _ { j } ( \boldsymbol { \hat { p } _ { 1 } } , \boldsymbol { \hat { p } _ { 2 } } , . . . , \boldsymbol { \hat { p } _ { m } } ; q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) d q _ { j } + F d t ,
$$

where $\ k m \mathrm { ~ + ~ } n - \ s = 6 N .$

The first Pfaffian (24) can be written

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { i } \phi - d P _ { i } \right) \delta p _ { i } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial q _ { j } } - d Q _ { j } \right) \delta q _ { j } + \left( \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial t } - d F \right) \delta t = 0
$$

with the additional conditions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { i } f _ { \rho } \right) \delta p _ { i } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial f _ { \rho } } { \partial q _ { j } } \delta q _ { j } = 0 } } \\ { { ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ; j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ; \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , s ) , } } \end{array}
$$

which are supposed to be constraints. Introducing the Lagrange multipliers $\lambda _ { \rho ; }$ ,we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { g r a d } _ { i } \phi - d P _ { i } + \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { s } \lambda _ { \rho } d t \cdot \mathrm { g r a d } _ { i } f _ { \rho } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial q _ { j } } - d Q _ { j } + \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { s } \lambda _ { \rho } d t \cdot \frac { \partial f _ { \rho } } { \partial q _ { j } } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { \partial F } { \partial t } d t - d F = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and the $\lambda _ { \rho }$ are determined by

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \operatorname { g r a d } _ { i } f _ { \rho } \right) d \rho _ { i } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial f _ { \rho } } { \partial q _ { j } } } d q _ { j } = 0 \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , s ) .
$$

The existence of a Cartan integral invariant on the hypersurface

$$
f _ { \rho } = a _ { \rho } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , s )
$$

defined in the $( k m + n )$ -dimensional space serves as a basis for deriving the Pfaffian equations.

Take Eckert-Brouwer's expresson (1937) for the variation of the position vector. In the case of disturbed planetary motion the first Pfaffian (24) takes the form

$$
\phi = \mathbf { v } \cdot d \mathbf { r } - \left( { \frac { \mathbf { v } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } - { \frac { \mu } { r } } - { \boldsymbol { \Omega } } \right) d t ,
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { r } = d \oint \mathbf { \nabla } \times \mathbf { r } + { \frac { \mathbf { v } } { n } } d t + { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { a } } d a + \left( { \frac { r + \oint - 2 a } { e \oint \mathbf { \nabla } } } \mathbf { r } + { \frac { r + \oint } { e \oint } } { \frac { ( \mathbf { r } \cdot \mathbf { v } ) } { a ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } } \mathbf { v } \right) d \ell ,
$$

as shown by Eckert and Brouwer (l937).Decomposing the infinitesimal rotation $d \Psi$ of the orbit along the axes $\mathbf { P }$ ${ \bf \Gamma } , { \bf Q } , { \bf R } ,$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \bf r } = { \bf P } a ( \cos u - e ) + { \bf Q } a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin u , \quad } \\ { { \mathrm r } \times { \bf v } = { \bf R } \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , \quad \quad u - e \sin u = l , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
d \Psi = d \mathbf { P } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } \mathbf { R } + d \mathbf { Q } { \cdot } \mathbf { R } \mathbf { P } + d \mathbf { R } { \cdot } \mathbf { P } \mathbf { Q } .
$$

Finally the Pfaffian takes the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \phi = \sqrt { \mu a } { \cdot } d l + \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { \cdot } { \bf Q } { \cdot } d { \bf P } + F d t , } } \\ { { F = \frac { \mu } { 2 a } + \Omega . } } \end{array}
$$

We choose, as a next step, Milankovich's elements $l , a , e , \mathbf { P } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ Q; then the Pfaffian is

$$
\phi = \sqrt { \mu a } \cdot d l + { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot ( { \bf Q } \cdot d { \bf P } - { \bf P } \cdot d { \bf Q } ) + F d t
$$

with the additional conditions

$$
\mathbf { P } { \cdot } \mathbf { P } = 1 , \qquad \mathbf { Q } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } = 1 , \qquad \mathbf { P } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } = 0 .
$$

The Pfaffian equations are now

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname { g r a d } _ { P } \phi - d [ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } ] + ( a \mathbf { P } + w \mathbf { Q } ) d t = 0 , } \\ { \operatorname { g r a d } _ { \Theta } \phi + d [ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { \cdot } \mathbf { P } ] + ( a \mathbf { Q } + w \mathbf { P } ) d t = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\frac { \partial \phi } { \partial a } = 0 , \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \epsilon } = 0 , \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial t } - d ( \sqrt { \mu a } ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\mathbf { g r a d } _ { P }$ ， ${ \tt g r a d } _ { Q }$ denote $\mathbf { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { p } }$ ， $\mathbf { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { q } }$ respectively, and $u , v , w$ are the Lagrange multipliers. After some calculation we obtain, taking the identity $\mathbf { I } = \mathbf { P } \mathbf { P } + \mathbf { Q } \mathbf { Q } + \mathbf { R } \mathbf { R }$ into account,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d \mathbf { P } } { d t } } = - { \frac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - \mathrm { \Lambda } } e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \mathbf { R } \mathbf { R } \right) \cdot \mathbf { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { q } } F + { \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \mathrm { \Lambda } } e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } } \mathbf { Q } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d \mathbf { Q } } { d t } } = { \frac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - \mathrm { \Lambda } } e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \mathbf { R } \mathbf { R } \right) \cdot \mathbf { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { p } } F - { \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \mathrm { \Lambda } } e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } } \mathbf { P } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d a } { d t } } = { \frac { 2 } { n a } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } } , \quad { \displaystyle { \frac { d l } { d t } } } = - { \frac { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } } - { \frac { 2 } { n a } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial a } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d e } { d t } } = { \frac { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } } + { \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \mathrm { \Lambda } } e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } } \left( \mathbf { P } \cdot \mathbf { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { q } } F - \mathbf { Q } \cdot \mathbf { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { p } } F \right) _ { \mathbf { q } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The constraints (27) are now satisfied and the existence of these constraints makes the determination of the additive constants of integration associated with these two equations (28) for $d \mathbf { P } / d t$ and $d \mathbf { Q } / d t$ extremely simple.This justifies in part the choice of $\mathbf { P }$ and $\mathbf { Q }$ as the basic elements.

We change the notation and take $\boldsymbol { \mathbf { \mathit { E } } }$ as the undisturbed element; then the disturbed element is expressed in a series $E + E _ { 1 } + E _ { 2 } + \cdots _ { ; }$ where $E _ { k }$ is the perturbation of the kth order. Substituting in the condition (27) and equating the terms of the same order on both sides of (27) and (28), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { l { \bf P } _ { k } } { d t } = { \bf p } _ { k } ( { \bf P } , { \bf Q } , a , l , e ; { \bf P } _ { 1 } , { \bf Q } _ { 1 } , a _ { 1 } , l _ { 1 } , e _ { 1 } ; \dots ; \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \qquad \quad { \bf P } _ { k - 1 } , { \bf Q } _ { k - 1 } , a _ { k - 1 } , l _ { k - 1 } , e _ { k - 1 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { { \bf Q } _ { k } } { d t } = { \bf q } _ { k } ( { \bf P } , { \bf Q } , a , l , e ; { \bf P } _ { 1 } , { \bf Q } _ { 1 } , a _ { 1 } , l _ { 1 } , e _ { 1 } ; \dots ; \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \quad { \bf P } _ { k - 1 } , { \bf Q } _ { k - 1 } , a _ { k - 1 } , l _ { k - 1 } , e _ { k - 1 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The solution is

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { { \bf P } _ { k } = ( { \bf P } _ { k } ) + j _ { k 1 } { \bf P } + j _ { k 2 } { \bf Q } + j _ { k 3 } { \bf R } , } } & { { ~ ( { \bf P } _ { k } ) = \displaystyle { \int } { { \bf p } _ { k } d t } , } } \\ { { { \bf Q } _ { k } = ( { \bf Q } _ { k } ) + q _ { k 1 } { \bf P } + q _ { k 2 } { \bf Q } + q _ { k 3 } { \bf R } , } } & { { ~ ( { \bf Q } _ { k } ) = \displaystyle { \int } { { \bf q } _ { k } d t } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the integrals are carried out formally. Denote by a bracket <) the constant part in the multiple Fourier expansion; then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \langle ( \mathbf { P } _ { k } ) \rangle { } . = \langle ( \mathbf { Q } _ { k } ) \rangle = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { = 0 , } & & { \phi _ { 2 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \langle \mathbf { P } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \rangle , \quad } & { \phi _ { 3 1 } = - \langle \mathbf { P } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \mathbf { P } _ { 2 } \rangle , \ldots , } \\ & { = 0 , } & & { q _ { 2 2 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \langle \mathbf { Q } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \rangle , \quad } & { q _ { 3 2 } = - \langle \mathbf { Q } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } _ { 2 } \rangle , \ldots , } \\ & { = - \phi _ { 1 2 } , } & & { q _ { 2 1 } = - \phi _ { 2 2 } - \langle \mathbf { P } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } _ { 1 } \rangle , } \\ & { } & & { q _ { 3 1 } = - \phi _ { 3 2 } - \langle \mathbf { P } _ { 1 } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } _ { 2 } \rangle - \langle \mathbf { P } _ { 2 } { \cdot } \mathbf { Q } _ { 1 } \rangle , \ldots } \end{array}
$$

In deriving the perturbation of the kth order in $\mathbf { P }$ and $\mathbf { Q }$ , we consider the three constants ${ p _ { k 2 } } , { p _ { k 3 } } , { q _ { k 3 } }$ as independent. There is no redundant constant, although there are redundant elements $\pmb { x }$ determined by (25) when $a , e , \mathbf { P }$ ， $\mathbf { Q }$ are known.

Since

$$
\sin I \frac { d \Omega } { d t } = { \bf R } \cdot \bigg ( - \frac { d { \bf P } } { d t } \cos \omega + \frac { d { \bf Q } } { d t } \sin \omega \bigg ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { d I } { d t } = { \bf R } \cdot \left( \frac { d { \bf P } } { d t } \sin \omega + \frac { d { \bf Q } } { d t } \cos \omega \right) } , } \\ { \displaystyle { \frac { d \omega } { d t } + \cos I \frac { d \Omega } { d t } = { \bf Q } \cdot \frac { d { \bf P } } { d t } = - { \bf P } \cdot \frac { d { \bf Q } } { d t } } , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain the Pfaffian (26) in the form

$$
\phi = \sqrt { \mu a } \cdot d l + \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } ( d \omega + \cos { I } \cdot d \Omega ) + F d t .
$$

Introducing the Gibbs rotation vector (Musen, 1961)

$$
g _ { 1 } = \frac { \cos \frac { \omega - \Omega } { 2 } } { \cos \frac { \omega + \Omega } { 2 } } \tan \frac { I } { 2 } , \qquad g _ { 2 } = - \frac { \sin \frac { \omega - \Omega } { 2 } } { \cos \frac { \omega + \Omega } { 2 } } \tan \frac { I } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
g _ { 3 } = \tan \frac { \omega + \Omega } { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \omega - \Omega = - 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { g _ { 2 } } { g _ { 1 } } , \qquad \omega + \Omega = 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } g _ { 3 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\tan ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } = { \frac { g _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { 1 + g _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

we transform the Pfaffian to

$$
\phi = \sqrt { \mu a } d l + \frac { 2 \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } { 1 + { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } { \bf s } { \cdot } d { \bf g } + F d t ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { s } = \mathbf { k } + \mathbf { g } \times \mathbf { k } , \mathbf { k } = \left( \mathbf { P } \sin \omega + \mathbf { Q } \cos \omega \right) \sin I + \mathbf { R } \cos I . } \end{array}
$$

The Pfaffian equations associated with $\pmb { \mathrm { \pmb { g } } }$ are

$$
\mathrm { r a d } _ { \bf g } \left\{ \frac { 2 \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } { 1 + { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } { \bf s \cdot } d { \bf g } \right\} - d \left\{ \frac { 2 \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } { 1 + { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } { \bf s } \right\} + \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \bf g } F d t =
$$

or

$$
\frac { d \mathbf { g } } { d t } = \mathbf { h } \mathbf { s } \cdot \frac { d \mathbf { g } } { d t } + \frac { 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } { 4 n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - \mathit { e } ^ { 2 } } } \mathbf { s } \times \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathbf { g } } F ,
$$

$$
\mathbf { h } = \mathbf { k } + { \frac { \mathbf { g } \times \mathbf { s } } { 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad \mathbf { h } { \cdot } \mathbf { s } = 1 .
$$

The Pffian equation associated with $\boldsymbol { e }$ is $\partial \phi / \partial e = 0$ or

$$
- { \frac { 2 n a ^ { 2 } e } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } { \frac { \bf s \cdot } { 1 + { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } } d t = 0 .
$$

These two equations combined give

$$
{ \frac { d \mathbf { g } } { d t } } = \mathbf { h } \cdot { \frac { ( 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } ) { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } { 2 n a ^ { 2 } e } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } } + { \frac { 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } { 4 n a ^ { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } } \cdot \mathbf { s } \times \operatorname { g r a d } _ { \varepsilon } F ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \epsilon } { d t } = \frac { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } \epsilon } \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } - \frac { ( 1 + { \bf g } ^ { 2 } ) \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 n a ^ { 2 } \epsilon } { \bf h } \cdot { \bf g } \mathrm { r a d } _ { \mathrm { g } } F .
$$

By means of the Gibbs rotation vector the special perturbation of Strömgren's and Musen's methods can be extended to general perturbations.If the reference plane nearly coincides with the osculating orbital plane at the initial epoch, then the components of $\pmb { \mathrm { \pmb { g } } }$ are small. With the undisturbed elements $\mathbf { P } , \mathbf { Q }$ and the disturbed elements $a , e , l$ we write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \bf r } _ { 0 } = { \bf P } a ( \cos \boldsymbol { u } - \boldsymbol { e } ) + { \bf Q } a \sqrt { 1 - \mathrm { \Omega } e ^ { 2 } } \sin \boldsymbol { u } , } } \\ { { \boldsymbol { u } - e \sin \boldsymbol { u } = { l } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then the matrix transforming $\pmb { r } _ { 0 }$ to $\pmb { x }$ is given by

$$
\mathbf { r } = \mathbf { F } { \boldsymbol { \cdot } } \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } , \qquad \mathbf { r } = \mathbf { I } + { \frac { 2 } { 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } } ( [ \mathbf { g } \times \mathbf { I } ] + \mathbf { g } \times [ \mathbf { g } \times \mathbf { I } ] ) ,
$$

or

$$
\mathbf { r } = \frac { 1 - \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } + \frac { 2 } { 1 + \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 } } ( [ \mathbf { g } \times \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } ] + \mathbf { g } ( \mathbf { g } \cdot \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } ) ) .
$$

In terms of Euler’s parameters

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \lambda _ { 1 } = \sin \displaystyle \frac { I } { 2 } \cos \displaystyle \frac { \omega - \Omega } { 2 } , \qquad \lambda _ { 2 } = \sin \displaystyle \frac { I } { 2 } \sin \displaystyle \frac { \omega - \Omega } { 2 } , } } \\ { { \lambda _ { 3 } = \cos \displaystyle \frac { I } { 2 } \sin \displaystyle \frac { \omega + \Omega } { 2 } , \qquad \lambda _ { 4 } = \cos \displaystyle \frac { I } { 2 } \cos \displaystyle \frac { \omega + \Omega } { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } = 1 ,
$$

the Pfaffian is written in the form

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { { \sqrt { \mu a } } \cdot d l + 2 { \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - \ e ^ { 2 } ) } } \cdot ( \lambda _ { 1 } \ d \lambda _ { 2 } - \ \lambda _ { 2 } \ d \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 4 } \ d \lambda _ { 3 } - \ \lambda _ { 3 } \ d \lambda _ { 4 } ) } \\ & { \qquad \ + \ F d t . } \end{array} }
$$

The Pfaffian equations associated with these parameters are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } + 2 d [ \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot \lambda _ { 2 } ] - w \lambda _ { 1 } d t = 0 , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \lambda _ { 2 } } - 2 d [ \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot \lambda _ { 1 } ] - w \lambda _ { 2 } d t = 0 , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \lambda _ { 3 } } - 2 d [ \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot \lambda _ { 4 } ] - w \lambda _ { 3 } d t = 0 , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \lambda _ { 4 } } + 2 d [ \sqrt { \mu a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot \lambda _ { 3 } ] - w \lambda _ { 4 } d t = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

where w is the Lagrangian multiplier with the constraint, or

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 4 a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 2 } } - \lambda _ { 3 } A - \lambda _ { 2 } B \right) - \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 a ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } \lambda _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 2 } } } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 2 } } { d t } = - \frac { 1 } { 4 a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } + \lambda _ { 3 } A - \lambda _ { 1 } B \right) + \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 a ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } \lambda _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } , } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 3 } } { d t } - \frac { 1 } { 4 a ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } + \lambda _ { 3 } A - \lambda _ { 4 } B \right) + \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 a ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } \lambda _ { 4 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 2 } } , } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 3 } } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 4 a ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } - \lambda _ { 4 } A - \lambda _ { 3 } B \right) - \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 a ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } \lambda _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 2 } } { d t } = - \lambda _ { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \partial \widehat { A } _ { 1 } } + \lambda _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 2 } } - \lambda _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 4 } } + \lambda _ { 4 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 3 } } , } \\ &  \ B = \lambda _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \widehat { F } } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } + \lambda _ { 2 } \frac   \end{array}
$$

The disturbed vector $\pmb { x }$ is given by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { r } = \Lambda { \cdot } \left( \begin{array} { c } { a ( \cos u - \epsilon ) } \\ { a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin u } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \circ } & { \left( \begin{array} { c c } { \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } } & { 2 ( \lambda _ { 3 } \lambda _ { 4 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } ) } & { 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 3 } + \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 4 } ) } \\ { - 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 3 } \lambda _ { 4 } ) } & { - \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } } & { 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 4 } - \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 3 } ) } \\ { 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 3 } - \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 4 } ) } & { - 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 4 } + \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 3 } ) } & { - \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } } \end{array} \right) } \end{array}
$$

where the elements of the matrix $\Lambda$ are called Euler's parameters.

Brouck (l969) proposed four iterative methods, modified from Encke, Hill, and Musen, to be implemented on computers for computing perturbations in rectangular coordinates relative to a mean reference orbit.

Popovic (1956，1959,1961，1962） formulated after Milankovitch (1939,194l) the equations for perturbation in vectorial form.He chose $\begin{array} { r } { M + m + \sum _ { \iota } m _ { i } = 1 , k ^ { 2 } = 1 ; } \end{array}$ and

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } \mathbf { r } } { d t ^ { 2 } } } = \mathbf { \nabla } - { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r ^ { 3 } } } + \sum _ { i } m _ { i } \mathbf { F } _ { i } , \qquad \mathbf { F } _ { i } = { \frac { \mathbf { r } _ { i } - \mathbf { r } } { | \mathbf { r } _ { i } - \mathbf { r } | ^ { 3 } } } - { \frac { \mathbf { r } _ { i } } { r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } } + { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r ^ { 3 } } } ,
$$

and expanded

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \bf r } = { \bf r } _ { 0 } + \sum _ { j } m _ { j } { \bf p } _ { 0 j } + \sum _ { j , k } m _ { j } m _ { k } { \bf p } _ { 0 j k } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \bf r } _ { i } = { \bf r } _ { i 0 } + \sum _ { j } m _ { j } { \bf p } _ { i j } + \sum _ { j , k } m _ { j } m _ { k } { \bf p } _ { i j k } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

This gives

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf r } _ { 0 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { { \bf r } _ { 0 } } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } , ~ \frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf r } _ { i 0 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { { \bf r } _ { i 0 } } { r _ { i 0 } ^ { 3 } } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf p } _ { 0 j } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - r _ { 0 } ^ { - 3 } { \bf p } _ { 0 j } + 3 r _ { 0 } ^ { - 5 } ( { \bf r } _ { 0 } { \cdot } { \bf p } _ { 0 j } ) { \bf r } _ { 0 } + { \bf f } ,
$$

$$
\mathbf { f } = | \mathbf { r } _ { j 0 } - \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } | ^ { - 3 } ( \mathbf { r } _ { j 0 } - \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } ) - r _ { j 0 } ^ { - 3 } \mathbf { r } _ { j 0 } + r _ { 0 } ^ { - 3 } \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } .
$$

The equations for $\mathbf { r } _ { 0 } , \mathbf { r } _ { i 0 }$ of the undisturbed motion and $\mathbf { p } _ { 0 j } , \mathbf { p } _ { i j }$ of the first-order perturbation are integrated independently of each other. Next Popovic put the elliptic values

$$
\mathbf { r } _ { 0 } = \mathbf { g } , \qquad | \mathbf { g } | = r _ { 0 } = g , \qquad [ \mathbf { g } \times \dot { \mathbf { g } } ] = \mathbf { c } , \qquad c ^ { 2 } = a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

and

$$
\mathbf { p } _ { 0 j k } = \mathbf { x } , \qquad \mathbf { x } = \xi \mathbf { g } + \eta [ \mathbf { c } \times \mathbf { g } ] + \zeta \mathbf { c } ,
$$

according to Urabe (1958) and Sibuya (l958),and derived the differential equations for $\xi , \eta , \zeta$

Finally, taking the eccentric anomaly $\pmb { u }$ as the independent variable he formed

$$
1 - e \cos u ) ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d u ^ { 2 } } + e \sin u ( 1 - e \cos u ) \frac { d \xi } { d u } + ( 1 - 4 e ^ { 2 } + 3 e \cos u ) \xi =
$$

$$
\left( 1 - e \cos u \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d u ^ { 2 } } - e \sin u \frac { d \zeta } { d u } + \zeta = \alpha ,
$$

where

$$
\alpha = c ^ { - 2 } g ^ { 3 } ( { \bf c \cdot f } ) , \qquad \beta = g ^ { 2 } ( { \bf f \cdot g } ) + 2 \int \left( [ { \bf c \times g } ] \cdot { \bf f } \right) d t .
$$

Popovic obtained the solution by quadrature in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \sin u } { 1 - \epsilon \cos u ) ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \int \frac { ( 1 - \epsilon \cos u ) ^ { 3 } } { \sin ^ { 3 } u } J d u , } \\ & { \frac { 1 } { 2 \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \int \frac { 1 } { ( 1 - \epsilon \cos u ) ^ { 2 } } \left\{ \int \left( [ \mathbf { c } \times \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } ] \cdot \mathbf { f } \right) d t \right\} d t } \\ & { + \frac { 1 } { \epsilon \sqrt { a } \left( 1 - \epsilon \cos u \right) ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \int \frac { ( 1 - \epsilon \cos u ) ^ { 3 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } u } J d u - \frac { 1 } { \epsilon \sqrt { a } } \int \frac { 1 - \epsilon \cos u } { \sin ^ { 2 } u } J d u , } \\ & { \frac { 3 } { 2 } \sin u \displaystyle \int \frac { 1 - \epsilon \cos u } { \sin ^ { 2 } u } \left\{ \int \left( 1 - \epsilon \cos u \right) \sin u \cdot ( \mathbf { c } \cdot \mathbf { f } ) d u \right\} d u , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\lambda = \frac { \sin u } { ( 1 - e \cos u ) ^ { 2 } } , J = \int \frac { \lambda \beta } { 1 - e \cos u } d u .
$$

# 10.5THE HANSEN-TIETJEN METHOD

Hansen's method,slightly modifed by Tietjen,deals with the perturbation in polar coordinates.

Denote the heliocentric polar coordinates of an asteroid by $r , \ l , \ b ,$ those of the disturbing planet by $r _ { 1 } , L _ { 1 } , B _ { 1 } ,$ and the mutual distance by $\pmb { \rho }$ Then,

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { x = r \cos b \cos l = ( r ) \cos l , } & { \quad x _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 } \cos B _ { 1 } \cos L _ { 1 } , } \\ { y = r \cos b \sin l = ( r ) \sin l , } & { \quad y _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 } \cos B _ { 1 } \sin L _ { 1 } , } \\ { z = r \sin b , } & { \quad z _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 } \sin B _ { 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

From the equations of motion we can derive immediately

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle x \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } - y \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } ( x y _ { 1 } - x _ { 1 } y ) K , } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { K = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } ( x y _ { 1 } - x _ { 1 } y ) K = \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } K \cdot ( r ) r _ { 1 } \cos B _ { 1 } \sin ( L _ { 1 } - l ) } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { = \displaystyle \sum U ; } } } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
( r ) ^ { 2 } { \frac { d l } { d t } } = k { \sqrt { \dot { p } _ { 0 } } } + \int \sum U d t ,
$$

where the integration constant is obviously $k \sqrt { { \pmb { \phi } } _ { 0 } } ;$ ， as can be seen by referring to the undisturbed motion.

From the equations of motion we also obtain

$$
x \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( r ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } = \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \Big [ ( x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } ) K - \frac { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \Big ] ;
$$

or

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - ( r ) \left( \frac { d l } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( r ) } { r ^ { 3 } } = ( r ) \sum R - ( r ) \sum w _ { 1 } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \sum R = \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { K r _ { 1 } \cos B _ { 1 } \cos ( L _ { 1 } - l ) } { ( r ) } , } \\ { \displaystyle \sum w _ { 1 } = \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } . } \end{array}
$$

From $r ^ { 2 } = ( r ) ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } { _ { ; } }$ we have

$$
\frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } = \frac { 1 } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } \left[ 1 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { z ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 - \frac { 5 } { 2 } \frac { z ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( r ) ^ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right) \right] .
$$

By employing Encke's function $f$ with the argument $q = z ^ { 2 } / [ 2 ( r ) ^ { 2 } ]$ (Section 9.1),we see that

$$
\frac { ( r ) } { r ^ { 3 } } = \frac { 1 } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { z ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 4 } } \left( \frac { f } { 3 } \right) .
$$

Thus,

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - ( r ) \left( \frac { d l } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } = ( r ) H _ { 2 } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c l } { { \displaystyle H _ { 2 } = \sum R - \sum w _ { 1 } + \Delta \sum R , } } \\ { { \Delta \sum R = \frac { 3 } { 2 } k ^ { 2 } \frac { z ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 5 } } \left( \frac { f } { 3 } \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

The $z \cdot$ coordinate is determined from the equations of motion by

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + [ w ] z = W ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { W = \displaystyle \sum W _ { 1 } + \Delta \sum W , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum W _ { 1 } = \sum k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } K r _ { 1 } \sin B _ { 1 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \Delta \sum W = \frac { 3 } { 2 } k ^ { 2 } \frac { z ^ { 3 } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } \frac { f } { 3 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle [ w ] = \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } + \sum w _ { 1 } . } } \end{array}
$$

The equations (31), (32),and (33) are the fundamental equations of the method; (33) determines the perturbation, while (31） and (32) determine finite quantities.We transform the latter two equations so that each time we can deal with small quantities.

Split $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ into two parts: $l = V + N$ ； such that

$$
( r ) ^ { 2 } { \frac { d N } { d t } } = \int \sum U d t .
$$

Thus,

$$
( r ) ^ { 2 } { \frac { d V } { d t } } = k { \sqrt { p _ { 0 } } } .
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { d l } { d t } = \frac { k \sqrt { \hat { p _ { 0 } } } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } \int \sum U d t ,
$$

or

$$
l = \int { \frac { k { \sqrt { p _ { 0 } } } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } } d t + \int { \frac { 1 } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } } d t \int \sum U d t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

We know that $l _ { 0 } = v _ { 0 } +$ constant for the undisturbed motion.We so arrange that $\iota$ is counted from the ascending node of the undisturbed orbit on the ecliptic; then $\iota$ is the argument of latitude,and the constant of this integration is $\omega _ { 0 }$ - the angular distance of the perihelion and node in the undisturbed orbit.Hence,

$$
l = V + \omega _ { 0 } + \Delta \omega ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \Delta \omega } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } \int \sum U d t .
$$

Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { M = M _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } t + \Delta M , } } \\ { { M = u - e _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } \sin u , } } \\ { { \left( \left( r \right) \right) \sin V = a _ { 0 } \cos \phi _ { 0 } \sin u , } } \\ { { \left( \left( r \right) \right) \cos V = a _ { 0 } ( \cos u - e _ { 0 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
( r ) = ( ( r ) ) ( 1 + \nu ) ,
$$

which defines the perturbation $\nu _ { ; }$ since $( ( r ) )$ is computed on the basis of the undisturbed elements

To obtain the equations for $\Delta M$ and $\pmb { \nu }$ we differentiate twice the equation

$$
( r ) = \frac { \beta _ { 0 } ( 1 + \nu ) } { 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos V } ;
$$

then,

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { k ^ { 2 } \dot { p } _ { 0 } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { ( r ) } { 1 + \nu } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \nu } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { \nu } { 1 + \nu } \cdot
$$

Two terms on the left-hand side are the same as those of (32).To make the two equations coincide we square (31); then,

$$
\frac { k ^ { 2 } \dot { p } _ { 0 } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } = ( r ) \Big ( \frac { d l } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 2 k \sqrt { \dot { p } _ { 0 } } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } \int U ^ { \prime } d t ,
$$

where

$$
\int U ^ { \prime } d t = \left( 1 + { \frac { \int \sum U d t } { 2 k { \sqrt { p _ { 0 } } } } } \right) \int \sum U d t .
$$

Hence,

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - ( r ) \left( { \frac { d l } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { ( r ) } { 1 + \nu } } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \nu } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { \nu } { 1 + \nu } } - { \frac { 2 k \sqrt { / { \nu } _ { 0 } } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } } \int U ^ { \prime } \ d \nu
$$

Eliminate $d ^ { 2 } ( \boldsymbol { r } ) / d t ^ { 2 }$ and $( d l / d t ) ^ { 2 }$ by combining this equation with (32); then,

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \nu } { d t ^ { 2 } } + h \nu = H _ { 0 } ,
$$

where

$$
h = \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { ( r ) ^ { 3 } } - H _ { 0 } ,
$$

$$
H _ { 1 } + H _ { 2 } = H _ { 0 } , H _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 k \sqrt { \rho _ { 0 } } } { ( r ) ^ { 4 } } \int U ^ { \prime } d t .
$$

This is of the same form as (33).

Next we differentiate

$$
\sin V = { \frac { \cos \phi _ { 0 } \sin u } { 1 - e _ { 0 } \cos u } } , M = u - e _ { 0 } \sin u ,
$$

by remembering that $n _ { 0 } = k a _ { 0 } ^ { - 3 / 2 }$ ; then,

$$
\frac { d V } { d M } = \frac { k \sqrt { / p _ { 0 } } } { n _ { 0 } ( ( r ) ) ^ { 2 } } .
$$

From (36) and the relation $( r ) ^ { 2 } d V / d t = k \sqrt { \ p _ { 0 } } ,$ we obtain

$$
k \sqrt { \ p _ { 0 } } = k \sqrt { \ p _ { 0 } } ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 2 } \biggl [ 1 + \frac { 1 } { n _ { 0 } } \frac { d \Delta M } { d t } \biggr ] . \cdot
$$

Hence,

$$
\frac { d \Delta M } { d t } = - n _ { 0 } \nu \sigma ,
$$

where

$$
\sigma = 2 \frac { 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \nu } { ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 2 } } \cdot
$$

Thus the problem is to solve four differential equations, (37), (38), (33),and (34).The time $\Delta t$ for describing the angle ${ \cal V } - v _ { 0 }$ in the undisturbed motion is given by $n _ { 0 } ~ \Delta t = \Delta M _ { : }$ or

$$
\frac { d ~ \Delta t } { d t } = - \sigma \nu .
$$

The Hansen-Tietjen method is suitable for computing the special perturbation of a periodic comet. If the prediction for only a short time interval is needed, then Encke's method is sometimes more convenient.

# HANSEN'S THEORY

# 10.6IDEAL COORDINATES

Hansen published in l83l as his prize essay his research on the mutual perturbation (gegenseitige Storungen) of Jupiter and Saturn; then, in 1838, a paper in Latin (Fundamenta Nova) on the new investigations of the true disturbed orbit of the moon；and in l843,his work on the absolute perturbation of an elliptic orbit with arbitrary eccentricity and inclination.Afterwards he published several papers in the Abhandlungen der mathematische und physikalishe Klasse der K. sächsischen Gesellschaft der Wissenschaften.His treatise on the explanation (Auseinandersetzung) of an adequate (zweckmassige) method for computing the absolute perturbation of an asteroid appeared in the same journal in 1855,1856, l857, and 1859,and its application to the asteroid Egeria in l867.His final treatise, the presentation (Darlegung） of the theoretical computation of the perturbation,and an application of this computation to his Lunar Tables, was published in the same journal in l862 and 1864. The expressions for the coordinates were published in 1874.

One of the characteristic features of his theory is the introduction of ideal coordinates. The longitude of the disturbed planet is counted on the moving instantaneous orbital plane from a certain origin.The motion of the orbital plane is determined separately.His theory is mixed numerical, in contrast to Delaunay's theory,which is purely literal and analytical. He used the eccentric anomaly in place of time as the independent variable.

Consider a coordinate transformation from a space-fixed coordinate $( X , Y , Z )$ to a moving system $( x , y , z )$ . Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x = \alpha X + \alpha ^ { \prime } Y + \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } Z , y = \beta X + \beta ^ { \prime } Y + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } Z , } } \\ { { z = \gamma X + \gamma ^ { \prime } Y + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } Z , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\alpha , \alpha ^ { \prime } , \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } , \beta , \beta ^ { \prime } , \beta ^ { \prime \prime } , \gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ are the direction cosines between the two sets of coordinate axes. If the relations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { X \frac { d \alpha } { d t } + Y \frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime } } { d t } + Z \frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = 0 , \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \displaystyle \ : X \frac { d \beta } { d t } + Y \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime } } { d t } + Z \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = 0 , } } \\ { { \nonumber \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : } } \\ { { \nonumber \ : \ : \ : X \frac { d \gamma } { d t } + Y \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime } } { d t } + Z \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = 0 } } \end{array}
$$

hold in this coordinate transformation, then such a coordinate system $( x , y , z )$ was called an ideal coordinate system by Hansen. Solving this set, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { X = \alpha x + \beta y + \gamma z , \qquad Y = \alpha ^ { \prime } x + \beta ^ { \prime } y + \gamma ^ { \prime } z , } } \\ { { Z = \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } x + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } y + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } z , } } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \alpha \beta + \alpha ^ { \prime } \beta ^ { \prime } + \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } \beta ^ { \prime \prime } = 0 , \qquad \beta \gamma + \beta ^ { \prime } \gamma ^ { \prime } + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } = 0 , } } \\ { { \gamma \alpha + \gamma ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { \prime } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } = 0 , } } \\ { { \alpha ^ { 2 } + \alpha ^ { \prime 2 } + \alpha ^ { \prime \prime 2 } = 1 , \qquad \beta ^ { 2 } + \beta ^ { \prime 2 } + \beta ^ { \prime \prime 2 } = 1 , } } \\ { { \gamma ^ { 2 } + \gamma ^ { \prime 2 } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } = 1 . } } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \cal A } = \beta \frac { d \alpha } { d t } + \beta ^ { \prime } \frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } \frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } , \qquad { \cal B } = \alpha \frac { d \gamma } { d t } + \alpha ^ { \prime } \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal C } = \gamma \frac { d \beta } { d t } + \gamma ^ { \prime } \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then we should have the relations

$$
A y - B z = 0 , \qquad A x - C z = 0 , \qquad B x - C y = 0 .
$$

Hence only two among the three equations (40) are independent. The equations (4l） define the axis of the instantaneous rotation of the coordinates $x , y , z .$ .Therefore the axis of the instantaneous rotation coincides with the radius vector in an ideal coordinate system.In such a coordinate system we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d x } { d t } = \alpha \frac { d X } { d t } + \alpha ^ { \prime } \frac { d Y } { d t } + \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } \frac { d Z } { d t } } , { \displaystyle { \frac { d y } { d t } } = \beta \frac { d X } { d t } + \beta ^ { \prime } \frac { d Y } { d t } + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } \frac { d Z } { d t } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d z } { d t } = \gamma \frac { d X } { d t } + \gamma ^ { \prime } \frac { d Y } { d t } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \frac { d Z } { d t } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus the coordinates $x , y , z$ and the velocity components $d x / d t , \ d y / d t ,$ $d z / d t$ in the disturbed orbit take the same form as in the undisturbed orbit in which the direction cosines $\alpha , \alpha ^ { \prime } , \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } , \beta , \beta ^ { \prime } , \beta ^ { \prime \prime } , \gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ are kept constant. Hence an ideal coordinate system is such that the coordinates and the velocity components, which are to define the osculating elements, are of the same form whether the orbit is disturbed or undisturbed,just like the Keplerian osculating elements (Section 5.19).

Hansen put $z = 0$ .The radius vector is always on the $( x , y )$ -plane. Then $A = 0 _ { ; }$ and

$$
X = \alpha x + \beta y , \qquad Y = \alpha ^ { \prime } x + \beta ^ { \prime } y , \qquad Z = \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } x + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } y ,
$$

and

$$
\frac { d \alpha } { d t } = \frac { \gamma } { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } \frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } , \qquad \frac { d \beta } { d t } = \frac { \gamma } { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { \gamma ^ { \prime } } { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } \frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } , \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { \gamma ^ { \prime } } { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } \cdot
$$

By further differentiation we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \ddot { X } = \alpha \ddot { x } + \beta \ddot { y } + \dot { \alpha } \dot { x } + \dot { \beta } \dot { y } , } \\ & { \ddot { Y } = \alpha ^ { \prime } \ddot { x } + \beta ^ { \prime } \ddot { y } + \dot { \alpha } ^ { \prime } \dot { x } + \dot { \beta } ^ { \prime } \dot { y } , } \\ & { \ddot { Z } = \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } \ddot { x } + \beta ^ { \prime \prime } \ddot { y } + \dot { \alpha } ^ { \prime \prime } \dot { x } + \dot { \beta } ^ { \prime \prime } \dot { y } , } \end{array}
$$

and hence, from (40) and (42),

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \alpha \ddot { X } + \alpha ^ { \prime } \ddot { Y } + \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } \ddot { Z } = \ddot { x } , \qquad \beta \ddot { X } + \beta ^ { \prime } \ddot { Y } + \beta ^ { \prime } \ddot { Z } = \ddot { y } , } } \\ { { \gamma \ddot { X } + \gamma ^ { \prime } \ddot { Y } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \ddot { Z } = ( \gamma \dot { \alpha } + \gamma ^ { \prime } \dot { \alpha } ^ { \prime } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \dot { \alpha } ^ { \prime \prime } ) \dot { x } + ( \gamma \dot { \beta } + \gamma ^ { \prime } \dot { \beta } ^ { \prime } + \gamma ^ { \prime } \dot { \beta } ^ { \prime \prime } ) \dot { y } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } ( \dot { \alpha } ^ { \prime \prime } \dot { x } + \dot { \beta } ^ { \prime } \dot { y } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence the equations of motion in this ideal coordinate system are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial x } , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { y } { r ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial y } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } \frac { d x } { d t } + \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } \frac { d y } { d t } = \gamma ^ { \prime } k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } ,
$$

where

$$
\Omega = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - \frac { X X ^ { \prime } + Y Y ^ { \prime } + Z Z ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \right] ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \Delta ^ { 2 } = ( X - X ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( Y - Y ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( Z - Z ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { r ^ { 2 } = X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } + Z ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

From the third equation of (43) and the relation $x \dot { \alpha } ^ { \prime \prime } + y \dot { \beta } ^ { \prime \prime } = 0$ for an ideal coordinate system, we find

$$
\frac { d \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = - h \gamma ^ { \prime } y \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } , \qquad \frac { d \beta ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = h \gamma ^ { \prime } x \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } ,
$$

where

$$
h = \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { x ( d y / d t ) - y ( d x / d t ) } .
$$

Write

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x = r \cos L , \qquad y = r \sin L , } \end{array}
$$

where the longitude $L$ is counted from the $\pmb { x }$ -axis on the instantaneous orbital plane. Then we obtain the equations of motion in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { r ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } L } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 r \frac { d r } { d t } \frac { d L } { d t } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r \Big ( \frac { d L } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { r ^ { 2 } } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } . } \end{array}
$$

If the right-hand members are zero, then the equations reduce to those for an elliptic motion,and the elements are determined.In the disturbed motion the elements so determined vary in time, but the expressons for the coordinates and momenta in terms of these elements and time are the same for the undisturbed and the disturbed motions (Yarov-Yarovoi, 1960).

# 10.7HANSEN'S METHOD OF INTEGRATION

The solution of (45) with the right-hand members equated to zero is, in Hansen's notation,

$$
\epsilon - e \sin \epsilon = n t + c , ~ \tan { \frac { f } { 2 } } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 + e } { 1 - e } } \tan { \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
L = f + \chi , ~ r = \frac { \rlap / p } { 1 + \varepsilon \cos { ( L - \chi ) } } = \frac { \rlap / p } { 1 + \varepsilon \cos { f } } ,
$$

$$
n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = k ^ { 2 } , ~ \ p = a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

wherefis the true anomaly, $\epsilon$ the eccentric anomaly, and $x$ the longitude of the perihelion in the orbit counted from the $\pmb { x }$ -axis on the instantaneous orbital plane. The arbitrary constants are $a , e , c ,$ and $x$ . These expressions have the same form in the disturbed motion,in which $a , e , c ,$ and $x$ are considered to be variable.

Now the variations of the elements are expressed by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { l a } { l t } = \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } \frac { k a ^ { 3 / 2 } } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left[ S e \sin f + T \frac { \dot { p } } { r } \right] } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d e } { d t } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } k \sqrt { \dot { p } } \left[ S \sin f + T ( \cos e + \cos f ) \right] } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \varpi } { l t } = e ( 1 - \cos I ) \frac { d \Omega } { d t } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } k \sqrt { \dot { p } } \left[ - S \cos f + T \Bigl ( 1 + \frac { r } { \dot { \beta } } \Bigr ) \sin f \right] } } \end{array}
$$

Define

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \varpi = \Omega + \chi - \sigma , } } \\ { { d \sigma = \cos I d \Omega , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\varpi = X N + N P$ $\chi = x M P$ $\Omega = X N _ { ; }$ $\sigma = N x$ (Figure 1). Then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \phi } { d t } = \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } k \sqrt { \dot { p } } \ T r , } \\ { \displaystyle e \frac { d \chi } { d t } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } k \sqrt { \dot { p } } \left[ - S \cos f + \ T \Big ( 1 \ + \frac { r } { \dot { p } } \Big ) \sin f \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
\frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } S = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , \qquad \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 1 + m } T r = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } ,
$$

and

$$
h = { \frac { k } { \sqrt { \dot { p } } } } , \qquad \cos \epsilon = { \frac { \cos f + \epsilon } { 1 + \epsilon \cos f } } , \qquad f + x = L ;
$$

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0002_pages_0041-0080/ocr/images/a9487b3e81ec1abb4e1dc55f02edfa514a80818ee19f068a3bcc9afb01123c8c.jpg)

then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d h } { d t } = - h ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ( h e \sin \chi ) } { d t } = - k ^ { 2 } \cos L \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } + k ^ { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { 1 } { \dot { \rho } } \bigg ) \sin L \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ( h e \cos \chi ) } { d t } = k ^ { 2 } \sin L \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } + k ^ { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { 1 } { \dot { \rho } } \bigg ) \cos L \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } . } \end{array}
$$

Now we abandon the variation of elements. Denote the values of $a , n , e , \phi , c , \chi$ for $t = 0$ respectively by $a _ { 0 } , n _ { 0 } , e _ { 0 } , \ b { \phi } _ { 0 } , c _ { 0 } , \ b { \varpi } _ { 0 }$ .These latter quantities are considered to be constant, such that $n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } a _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } = k ^ { 2 }$ ， $h _ { 0 } = k / \sqrt { \ p _ { 0 } }$ We define the following barred quantities by the equations

$$
\bar { \epsilon } - e _ { 0 } \sin \bar { \epsilon } = n _ { 0 } z + c _ { 0 } ,
$$

$$
\tan \frac { \bar { f } } { 2 } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 + e _ { 0 } } { 1 - e _ { 0 } } } \tan \frac { \bar { \epsilon } } { 2 } , \qquad \bar { r } = a _ { 0 } ( 1 - e _ { 0 } \cos \bar { \epsilon } ) = \frac { \not p _ { 0 } } { 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos { f } } ;
$$

where $_ z$ is written in place of $t$ .The variable $z$ is determined so that

$$
L = \bar { f } + \varpi _ { 0 } .
$$

From the relations

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { r ^ { 2 } { \displaystyle \frac { d L } { d t } } = k \sqrt { \dot { \rho } } , \quad } } & { { d L = d \bar { f } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle r ^ { 2 } { \displaystyle \frac { d \bar { f } } { d t } } = k \sqrt { \dot { \rho } } , \quad } } & { { \bar { r } ^ { 2 } { \displaystyle \frac { d \bar { f } } { d z } } = k \sqrt { \dot { \rho } } , } } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { r ^ { 2 } } { \bar { r } ^ { 2 } } \frac { d z } { d t } = \frac { \sqrt { \hat { p } } } { \sqrt { \hat { p } _ { 0 } } } = \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } .
$$

Hence,

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 2 } } , \quad \quad r = \bar { r } ( 1 + \nu ) ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { 1 } { 1 + \nu } = \frac { \bar { r } } { r } = \bar { r } \frac { 1 + e \cos { ( L - \chi ) } } { \rlap / p } = \bar { r } \frac { 1 + e \cos { ( \bar { f } + \varpi _ { 0 } - \chi ) } } { \rlap / p } .
$$

Now we insert $\boldsymbol { \xi } _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { \eta _ { 1 } }$ defined by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { e \cos { ( \chi - \varpi _ { 0 } ) } = e _ { 0 } + ( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) \xi _ { 1 } , } \\ & { e \sin { ( \chi - \varpi _ { 0 } ) } = ( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) \eta _ { 1 } ; } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\frac { 1 } { 1 \ + \ \nu } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 \ + \ \xi _ { 1 } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \cos \bar { f } + \ \eta _ { 1 } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \sin \bar { f } \right) .
$$

If we substitute this expression in (48), then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d z } { d t } = 1 + \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \left( \frac { \nu } { 1 + \nu } \right) ^ { 2 } + \overline { { W } } , } \\ { \displaystyle \overline { { W } } = \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } - \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } - 1 + \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } \xi _ { 1 } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \cos { \bar { f } } + \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } \eta _ { 1 } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \sin { \bar { f } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\bar { \pmb { r } }$ and $\bar { f }$ are functions of $_ z$ and determined by (47). If we neglect the perturbation, then we have $z = t$ ，The perturbation $\delta z$ of $_ z$ is defined by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { z = t + \delta z , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \delta z } { d t } = \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \left( \frac { \nu } { 1 ~ + ~ \nu } \right) ^ { 2 } + ~ \overline { { W } } . } \end{array}
$$

Using (48), we transform (49a) to

$$
\frac { d \nu } { d t } = \frac { h } { a _ { 0 } } [ \xi _ { 1 } \sin \bar { f } - \eta _ { 1 } ( \cos \bar { f } + e _ { 0 } ) ] ,
$$

where the elliptic value for dr/dt is substituted with reference to

$$
\frac { d \bar { r } } { d z } = h _ { 0 } e _ { 0 } \sin \bar { f } .
$$

Let

$$
\Xi = { \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } } - { \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } } - 1 , \quad \mathrm {  ~ { \cal ~ Y } } = { \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } } \xi _ { 1 } , \quad \mathrm {  ~ \Psi ~ } \Psi = { \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } } \eta _ { 1 } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \nu } { d t } = \frac { n _ { 0 } } { a _ { 0 } \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } [ \Upsilon \sin \bar { f } - \Psi ( \cos \bar { f } + e _ { 0 } ) ] , } \\ { \displaystyle \overline { { W } } = \Xi + \Upsilon \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \cos \bar { f } + \Psi \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \sin \bar { f } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus our final expressons are,in the first order with regard to $\mathbf { \nabla } m ^ { \prime }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \overline { { { W } } } = \Xi + \Upsilon \frac { r } { a } \cos f + \Psi \frac { r } { a } \sin f , } \\ { \quad } \\ { \frac { d \delta z } { d t } = \overline { { { W } } } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\frac { d \nu } { d t } = \frac { n } { a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } [ \Upsilon \sin f - \Psi ( \cos f + e ) ] ,
$$

$$
r \cos f = a ( \cos \epsilon - e ) , \qquad r \sin f = a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin \epsilon ,
$$

$$
\epsilon - e \sin \epsilon = n t + c ,
$$

$$
\nu = - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \Xi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \Upsilon \frac { r } { a } \cos f + \Psi \frac { r } { a } \sin f \right) .
$$

The next problem is to find $\bar { \boldsymbol W }$ .We differentiate (52) and take dh/dt from (46); then,

$$
\frac { d \Xi } { d t } = - h _ { 0 } \bigg ( 1 + \frac { 2 h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \bigg ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } \cdot
$$

Similarly, differentiating (52) and referring to (46) and (49), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \Xi } { d t } = - 3 h \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \Upsilon } { d t } = 2 h \Big [ \Big ( \frac { a } { r } + \frac { 1 } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \Big ) \cos f + \frac { e } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \Big ] \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } + 2 a h \sin f \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \Psi } { d t } = 2 h \Big ( \frac { a } { r } + \frac { 1 } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \Big ) \sin f \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } - 2 a h \cos f \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } . } \end{array}
$$

Here we employ Hansen's artificial device. In order to differentiate with regard to $t ,$ without touching the variable $\pmb { t }$ contained in the osculating elements, but only the variable $t$ outside these osculating elements,Hansen employed different letters for distinguishing these two kinds of differentiation. The time, outside the osculating elements, is denoted by $\pmb { \tau }$ ; and the variables $r , \epsilon , f$ are replaced by $\rho , \eta , \omega ;$ respectively. $_ z$ is replaced by $\boldsymbol { \zeta } ,$ and $\pmb { \nu }$ by $\beta$ Thus $\rho , \omega , \eta , \tau$ are related by

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \eta - e \sin \eta = n \tau + c , } } & { { \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \omega = \cos \eta - e , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \frac { \rho } { a } \sin \omega = \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin \eta , } } & { { \rho = \frac { \dot { \rho } } { 1 + e \cos \omega } , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { W = \Xi + \Upsilon \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \omega + \Psi \frac { \rho } { a } \sin \omega . } \end{array}
$$

The functions $\Xi , \Upsilon , \Psi$ ,and hence $W _ { : }$ ,are zero for $t = 0$ , whatever value $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ may have. When we replace $\tau , \eta , \omega _ { \mathrm { { \rangle } } }$ $\pmb { \rho }$ in $W$ by $t , \epsilon , f , r ,$ respectively, then the function $W$ changes to $\bar { W }$ Differentiating these new expressions with regard to $t$ and substituting (55),we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { d W } { d t } = 2 h \rho \sin \left( f - \omega \right) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } + h \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } \left\{ \frac { 2 \rho } { r } \cos \left( f - \omega \right) - 1 \right. } } \\ { \displaystyle { + \left. \frac { 2 \rho } { \beta } \left[ \cos \left( f - \omega \right) - 1 \right] \right\} } , } \end{array}
$$

in which $W$ is determined so that $W = 0$ for $t = 0$ , whatever values $\tau$ and $\pmb { \omega }$ may have.Then (53) is written

$$
- 2 { \frac { d \nu } { d t } } = \Upsilon { \frac { d \left( { \frac { r } { a } } \cos f \right) } { d t } } + \Psi { \frac { d \left( { \frac { r } { a } } \sin f \right) } { d t } } .
$$

On the other hand, we obtain with these new variables

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial \tau } = \Upsilon \frac { d \left( \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \omega \right) } { d \tau } + \Psi \frac { d \left( \frac { \rho } { a } \sin \omega \right) } { d \tau } .
$$

When the variable $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ in $\partial W / \partial \tau$ is replaced by $t$ , we indicate the resulting expression by putting a bar upon the letter. Then from these two equations we find

$$
\frac { d \nu } { d t } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \overline { { \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \tau } \right) } } .
$$

# 10.8MOTION OF THE ORBITAL PLANE

Let $_ { x M }$ and $x ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime }$ be two consecutive positions of the instantaneous orbital plane, $M$ and $M ^ { \prime }$ being the directions of the radius vector at two epochs $t$ and $t + d t$ Let $N$ be the ascending node of the orbital plane $( x , y )$ . Draw a perpendicular MH from $M$ to the fixed $( X , Y )$ -plane, and a perpendicular NA from $N$ to the orbital plane at $t + d t$ (Figure 2). We have $N N _ { 1 } = d \Omega$ ， $N x = A x ^ { \prime } = \sigma ,$ $N _ { 1 } x ^ { \prime } = \sigma + d \sigma _ { : }$ ， $M \pmb { x } = M ^ { \prime } \pmb { x } ^ { \prime }$ ， $A N _ { 1 } = \cos I d \Omega = d \sigma$ .The angular velocity of the instantaneous rotation is

$$
\omega = \frac { N A } { \sin M N d t } = \frac { \sin I } { \sin \left( L - \sigma \right) } \frac { d \Omega } { d t } ,
$$

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0002_pages_0041-0080/ocr/images/11bd99c8b649446a67d580d18d3caeda75d26ecaf5e9beaa8a8b8a0b454cb2e8.jpg)  
FIGURE 2

the axis of the instantaneous rotation being the radius vector. Here $M N = L - \sigma , H M = b , N H = l - \Omega$ From △MHN we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \cos b \sin ( l - \Omega ) = \cos I \sin ( L - \sigma ) ; } \\ { \cos b \cos ( l - \Omega ) = \cos ( L - \sigma ) , } \\ { \sin b = \sin I \sin ( L - \sigma ) . } \end{array}
$$

Denote the values of $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ and $\Omega$ for $t = 0$ respectively by $I _ { 0 }$ and $\Omega _ { 0 } = \sigma _ { 0 } { \mathrm { : } }$ then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r } { ( l - \Omega _ { 0 } - \Gamma ) = \cos I _ { 0 } \sin ( L - \Omega _ { 0 } ) \qquad } \\ { - \frac { s } { \kappa } [ \sin I _ { 0 } \cos I + \cos I _ { 0 } \sin I \cos ( \sigma - \Omega _ { 0 } ) ] , } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

cos $b$ cos $( { l } - \Omega _ { 0 } - \Gamma ) = \cos { ( L - \Omega _ { 0 } ) } + \frac { s } { \kappa } \sin { I } \sin { ( \sigma - \Omega _ { 0 } ) } ,$

$$
\sin b = \sin I _ { 0 } \sin ( L - \Omega _ { 0 } ) + s ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \kappa = 1 + \cos I _ { 0 } \cos I - \sin I _ { 0 } \sin I \cos { ( \sigma - \Omega _ { 0 } ) } , } \\ & { s = \sin I \sin { ( L - \sigma ) } - \sin I _ { 0 } \sin { ( L - \Omega _ { 0 } ) } . } \end{array}
$$
