If we neglect the quantities of the order $\mathbf { \nabla } m ^ { \prime }$ ,then $\kappa = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 } ,$ $\mathbf { \Gamma } ^ { \Gamma }$ being of the order $\pmb { m } ^ { \prime 2 }$ Hence,

Following Hansen, we define $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ by

$$
P = \sin I \sin ( \sigma - { \Omega } _ { 0 } ) , \theta = \sin I \cos ( \sigma - { \Omega } _ { 0 } ) - \sin I _ { 0 } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { s = Q \sin { ( L - \Omega _ { 0 } ) } - P \cos { ( L - \Omega _ { 0 } ) } , } \\ & { \kappa = \cos { I _ { 0 } } ( \cos { I _ { 0 } } + \cos { I } ) - Q \sin { I _ { 0 } } . } \end{array}
$$

After some calculation we obtain

$$
\frac { d \Gamma } { d t } = \frac { Q \frac { d P } { d t } - P \frac { d Q } { d t } } { \kappa \cos I } = \frac { h r s } { \kappa } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } .
$$

This is of the order $\pmb { m } ^ { \prime 2 }$ ,since $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ are of the order $\mathbf { \nabla } m ^ { \prime }$ ·

Now from the equations for the variation of elements we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d I } { d t } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \mu } \frac { k } { \sqrt { \hslash } } V r \cos { v } , } \\ { \sin { I } \frac { d \Omega } { d t } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \mu } \frac { k } { \sqrt { \hslash } } V r \sin { v } , } \end{array}
$$

where $V$ stands for the component of the disturbing force normal to the instantaneous orbital plane, and $\pmb { v }$ the angle between the ascending node and the radius vector; that is, $\pmb { \upsilon } = L - \pmb { \sigma }$ Hence,

$$
\frac { d I } { d t } = h r \cos { ( L - \sigma ) } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } ,
$$

$$
\sin I \frac { d \Omega } { d t } = h r \sin \left( L - \sigma \right) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \sigma } { d t } = \cos I \frac { d \Omega } { d t } \cdot
$$

Differentiate the equations (57a),defining $P$ and $Q$ ,and combine them with (57b); then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d P } { d t } = h r \cos I _ { 0 } \sin \left( L - \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d Q } { d t } = h r \cos I _ { 0 } \cos \left( L - \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } . } \end{array}
$$

Here we have ${ \pmb { L } } = { \pmb { \bar { f } } } + { \pmb { \varpi } } _ { \bf { 0 } }$ .To the first order we omit the subscripts 0. Then,

$$
( l - \Omega ) = \cos { I } \sin { ( \bar { f } + \varpi - \Omega ) } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { s = Q \sin { ( \bar { f } + \varpi - \Omega ) } - \tilde { P } \cos { ( \bar { f } + \varpi - \bar { \Omega } ) } \mathrm { ~ . ~ } } \end{array}
$$

Further we define

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \tan \frac { r } { a } s = Q \frac { r } { a } \sin \left( \bar { f } + \varpi - \Omega \right) - P \frac { r } { a } \cos \left( \bar { f } + \varpi - \Omega \right) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { R = Q \frac { \rho } { a } \sin \left( \omega + \varpi - \Omega \right) - P \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \left( \omega + \varpi - \Omega \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

The function $R$ depends on both $t$ and $\pmb { \tau }$ and vanishes for $t = 0$ ,whatever value $\pmb { \tau }$ may have.We obtain

$$
\frac { d R } { d t } = h \cos I . \frac { \rho } { a } r \sin \left( \omega - \bar { f } \right) \cdot \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } \cdot
$$

The function $\pmb { R }$ is determined by (60).Denote the value of $\pmb { R }$ for $t = \tau$ by $\bar { R }$ ; then from (59) we find

$$
u = { \bar { R } } .
$$

Finally we transform the independent variable from $\pmb { t }$ to $\epsilon$ ,the eccentric anomaly, according to Hansen. Then,

$$
\epsilon - e \sin \epsilon = n t + c , d t = { \frac { r } { n a } } d \epsilon ,
$$

where $e , n _ { \mathrm { { s } } } , c$ are constants. Put

$$
\frac { d W } { d \epsilon } = T , ~ \frac { d R } { d \epsilon } = U ;
$$

then, combining (56), (60), (62),and referring to the relations

$$
\frac { k } { \sqrt { a } } = n a , \frac { k } { n a } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } , \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial f } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { T \sqrt { 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } = 2 \rho r \sin \left( f - \omega \right) { \cdot } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } \ } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \qquad + \ \left\{ 2 \rho \cos \left( f - \omega \right) - r \qquad } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \qquad + \ \frac { 2 \rho r } { a ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) } \left[ \cos \left( f - \omega \right) - 1 \right] \right\} } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { U \sqrt { 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } = \cos { \cal I } { \cdot } \frac { \rho r ^ { 2 } } { a } \sin \left( \omega - f \right) { \cdot } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } . } } \end{array}
$$

From the relations in an elliptic motion we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \left( \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \epsilon } \right) = a e \sin \epsilon \cdot \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } + \frac { a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { r } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial f } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial f } = \frac { r } { a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \epsilon } \right) - \frac { e r \sin \epsilon } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } \end{array}
$$

where $( \partial \Omega / \partial \epsilon )$ stands for the differentiation of $\Omega$ with respect to $\epsilon$ insofar as $\epsilon$ enters in $\Omega$ through $\boldsymbol { r }$ and $f$ but not through $\scriptstyle { \pmb { r } } ^ { \prime }$ and $f ^ { \prime }$ . Then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { T = M a \Big ( \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \epsilon } \Big ) + N a r \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { U = Q a ^ { 2 } \cos I \cdot \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } . } } \end{array}
$$

In the formulas for $M , \ N , \ Q$ the variables $\boldsymbol { r }$ and $f$ are expressed as functions of $\epsilon$ ,and the variables $\pmb { \rho }$ and $\pmb { \omega }$ are defined by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rho \cos \omega = a ( \cos \eta - \epsilon ) , \qquad \rho \sin \omega = a \sqrt { 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \sin \eta . } \end{array}
$$

Thus finally we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) M = - 3 ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ) + 2 e \cos \epsilon - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \epsilon - 3 e \cos \epsilon } \\ & { \phantom { ( - e ^ { 2 } ) M = - 3 ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ) + } + e ^ { 2 } \cos \left( \eta + \epsilon \right) + ( 4 - e ^ { 2 } ) \cos \left( \eta - \epsilon \right) - \epsilon \cos \epsilon } \\ & { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) N = e \sin \epsilon - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \epsilon - e \sin \eta + e ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \eta + \epsilon \right) } \\ & { \phantom { ( - e ^ { 2 } ) M = - } - ( 2 - e ^ { 2 } ) \sin \left( \eta - \epsilon \right) + e \sin \left( \eta - 2 \epsilon \right) , } \\ & { \phantom { ( - e ^ { 2 } ) M = - } Q = e \sin \epsilon - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \epsilon - \frac { 3 } { 2 } e \sin \eta + ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ) \sin \left( \eta \right. } \\ & { \phantom { ( - e ^ { 2 } ) M = - } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \eta + \epsilon \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e \sin \left( \eta - 2 \epsilon \right) . } \end{array}
$$

As a summary of the formulas we see, from (51) and (57):

$$
\delta z = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \overline { { { W } } } d t , \qquad \nu = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \overline { { { \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \tau } \right) } } } d t .
$$

Since $v = \bar { f } + \varpi , r = \bar { r } ( 1 + \nu )$ , we have by (47)

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \bar { \epsilon } - e \sin \bar { \epsilon } = n t + c + n \delta z , } } \\ { { \bar { r } \cos \bar { f } = a ( \cos \bar { \epsilon } - e ) , \qquad \bar { \bar { r } } \sin \bar { f } = a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin \bar { \epsilon } . } } \end{array}
$$

Then $\pmb { r }$ and $L$ are obtained from

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { r } { 1 + \nu } \cos \left( L - \varpi \right) = a ( \cos \bar { \epsilon } - \epsilon ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { r } { 1 + \nu } \sin \left( L - \varpi \right) = a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin \bar { \epsilon } . } } \end{array}
$$

With

$$
u = \bar { R } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \overline { { \left( \frac { \partial R } { \partial \tau } \right) } } \ d t , \qquad s = \frac { a } { r } u ,
$$

the coordinates are finally derived from

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( l - \Omega ) = \cos I \sin \left( L - \Omega \right) - s \tan I , } } \\ { { ( l - \Omega ) = \cos \left( L - \Omega \right) , } } \\ { { \sin b = \sin I \sin \left( L - \Omega \right) + s ; } } \end{array}
$$

$r , l , b$ are the disturbed polar coordinates.

# 10.9DISTURBING FUNCTION

The next problem is the computation of $( \partial \Omega / \partial \epsilon ) , \ \partial \Omega / \partial r ,$ and $\partial \Omega / \partial z$ · Hansen expanded the disturbing function in the eccentric anomaly $\epsilon$ of the disturbed planet,and in the mean anomaly $g ^ { \prime }$ of the disturbing planet.By counting the position of the radius vector from the common node $\pmb { I }$ of the orbital planes of the disturbed and disturbing planets,write ${ \cal I } M = f + \Pi , { \cal I } M ^ { \prime } = f ^ { \prime } + \Pi ^ { \prime }$ .Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \Omega = \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } } \Delta - m ^ { \prime } \frac { r } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \cos { \cal H } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { r \displaystyle \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } = \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \frac { r ^ { \prime 2 } - r ^ { 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } r } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \cos { \cal H } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } = \displaystyle - m ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime } \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \Big ) \sin { \cal J } \sin { ( { \cal f } ^ { \prime } + \Pi ^ { \prime } ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

cos $H = \cos \left( f + \Pi \right)$ cos $( f ^ { \prime } + \Pi ^ { \prime } ) + \sin { ( f + \Pi ) }$ sin $( f ^ { \prime } + \Pi ^ { \prime } )$ cos $\boldsymbol { J }$


<!-- chunk 0003: pages 81-120 -->
where $J$ denotes the mutual inclination of the two orbital planes. Here we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Delta ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos { H } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = a ^ { 2 } \Big [ D - f _ { 1 } \cos \big ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } - F _ { 1 } \big ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { a ^ { \prime 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } e ^ { \prime 2 } \cos 2 \epsilon ^ { \prime } \Big ] , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\mathcal { D } , f _ { 1 } , F _ { 1 }$ are functions of $\epsilon$ only. Then the expansion of the disturbing function $1 / \Delta$ is carried out in powers of

$$
[ D - f _ { 1 } \cos { ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } - F _ { 1 } ) } ] ^ { - 1 } .
$$

If we write $\epsilon ^ { \prime } - F _ { 1 } = \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \epsilon - ( F _ { 1 } - \epsilon )$ ,then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { [ D - f _ { 1 } \cos \left( \epsilon ^ { \prime } - F _ { 1 } \right) ] ^ { - n } } \\ & { \qquad = \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { ( n ) } + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) } \cos \left( \epsilon ^ { \prime } - F _ { 1 } \right) + 2 \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { ( n ) } \cos \left( 2 \epsilon ^ { \prime } - F _ { 2 } \right) } \\ & { \qquad = \beta _ { 0 } ^ { ( n ) } + 2 \beta _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) } \cos \left( \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \epsilon \right) + 2 \beta _ { 2 } ^ { ( n ) } \cos \left( 2 \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \epsilon \right) } \\ & { \qquad + ~ 2 \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) } \sin \left( \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \epsilon \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

in which $\beta _ { i } ^ { ( n ) } = \alpha _ { i } ^ { ( n ) }$ cos $i ( F _ { 1 } - \epsilon ) , \gamma _ { i } ^ { ( n ) } = \alpha _ { i } ^ { ( n ) } \sin i ( F _ { 1 } - \epsilon )$ ,and ${ \alpha } _ { i } ^ { ( n ) }$ are functions of the Laplace coefficients. The variation of $F _ { 1 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \epsilon$ is limited to a narrow interval. We see that

$$
\beta _ { i } ^ { ( n ) } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } c _ { 0 } + \sum _ { j } c _ { j } \cos j \epsilon + \sum _ { j } s _ { j } \sin j \epsilon .
$$

Hence the expansion is of the form

$$
{ \frac { \alpha } { \bar { \lambda } } } = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } ( i , i ^ { \prime } , c ) \cos { ( i \epsilon - i ^ { \prime } \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) } + \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } ( i , i ^ { \prime } , s ) \sin { ( i \epsilon - i ^ { \prime } \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) } .
$$

The general form $\pmb { F }$ of $\pmb { a } \Omega$ ar $\partial \Omega / \partial r , a ^ { 2 } \partial \Omega / \partial z$ is

$$
\displaystyle { ^ { \mathcal { G } } = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { n ^ { \prime } } \left( i , n ^ { \prime } , c \right) \cos \left( i \epsilon - n ^ { \prime } \epsilon ^ { \prime } \right) + \sum _ { i } \sum _ { n ^ { \prime } } \left( i , n ^ { \prime } , s \right) \sin \left( i \epsilon - n ^ { \prime } \epsilon ^ { \prime } \right) }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { + \ c _ { 0 } + c _ { 1 } \cos \epsilon + c _ { 2 } \cos 2 \epsilon + \cdot \cdot \cdot + s _ { 1 } \sin \epsilon + s _ { 2 } \sin 2 \epsilon + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ & { } & { ( n ^ { \prime } = 1 , 2 , \cdot \cdot . . ) . } \end{array}
$$

Substituting $g ^ { \prime }$ in place of $\acute { \epsilon }$ , we find

$$
= \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } \left( ( i , i ^ { \prime } , c ) \right) \cos \left( i \epsilon - i ^ { \prime } g ^ { \prime } \right) + \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } \left( ( i , i ^ { \prime } , s ) \right) \sin \left( i \epsilon - i ^ { \prime } g ^ { \prime } \right)
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { + c _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + c _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cos \epsilon + c _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \cos 2 \epsilon + \cdot \cdot \cdot + s _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \sin \epsilon + s _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \sin 2 \epsilon + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { ( i = - \infty , \cdot \cdot \cdot , + \infty ; i ^ { \prime } = + 1 , \cdot \cdot \cdot , + \infty ) } } \end{array}
$$

The transformation from $( i , n ^ { \prime } , c )$ and $( i , n ^ { \prime } , s )$ to $( ( i , i ^ { \prime } , c ) )$ and $( ( i , i ^ { \prime } , s ) )$ can be made by using Bessel functions.

Next we eliminate $t$ from e - e sin $\epsilon = N t + c , g ^ { \prime } = N ^ { \prime } t + c ^ { \prime }$ ; then,

$$
g ^ { \prime } = c ^ { \prime } + \frac { N ^ { \prime } } { N } \left( - c + \epsilon - e \sin \epsilon \right) .
$$

The final form of the expansion is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle F = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } \left[ i , i ^ { \prime } , c \right] \cos \left[ ( i - i ^ { \prime } \mu ) \epsilon - \beta \right] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } \left[ i , i ^ { \prime } , s \right] \sin \left[ ( i - i ^ { \prime } \mu ) \epsilon - \beta \right] } . }  \end{array}
$$

It is remarked that $\scriptstyle \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ should be kept constant in the computation of $( \partial \Omega / \partial \epsilon )$

Thus the variable $\eta$ enters in $M$ and $N$ through (64). When computing $_ T$ and $U$ by (63), the quantities $+ \eta , - \eta , 0$ are added in the arguments. Then $W$ and $R$ are computed by (62). $\delta z$ and $\nu$ are derived from (65). Then (66) gives $\pmb { u }$ and $\pmb { S } ,$ Finally the disturbed coordinates are obtained by (67).

Hill (1881) used $\zeta$ instead of $_ z$ of (48), such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \zeta } { d t } = 1 + \frac { \overline { { W } } + \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \nu ^ { 2 } } , } \\ { \displaystyle \overline { { W } } = \Xi + \Upsilon \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \cos { \bar { f } } + \Psi \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \sin { \bar { f } } . } \end{array}
$$

While Hansen assumed that there occurs no small divisor, Olsson $( 1 8 9 6 \mathsf { a } )$ considered the effect of a small divisor,as in Glyden's theory, in which $\delta z$ in the arguments is divided into two parts,one consisting of the long-period terms and the other,of the short-period terms.The former part is eliminated by series expansions,but the latter part remaining in the arguments is integrated by parts.

Newcomb (1882) compared Hansen's theory with Delaunay's. Hansen (1867） himself applied the method to the asteroid Egeria,and then to the perturbation of Jupiter and Saturn. This theory of Hansen on Jupiter and Saturn was completed by Hill (189O). The Tables of Jupiter and Saturn were compiled by Hill (l898) on the basis of Hansen's theory. They are now in practical use for computing the ephemerides. Clemence (1949,l96l） applied the theory with several modifications and correc-tions by facilitating the use ofa computing machine with higher accuracy to the motion of Mars. The long-period inequalities appear with the arguments, among others: $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ (Venus)- 8l (Mars),15l (Mars) - 8l (Earth),l (Mars)- 6l (Jupiter).Clemence (1960) resolved the discrepancy between the theory and observations in the longitudes by taking 1/3499.7 as the mass of Saturn.

Hansen's theory has also been applied to the asteroid Metis by Lesser (1861),to Diana by Wittram (l885),and to Vesta by Le Veau (1880, 1883,1892） and Voronov (1935).Perrotin (1879) studied the motion of Vesta according to the theory of Le Verrier (Chapter 8).Von Zeipel (1901）applied Hansen's theory to the motion of the asteroid Hecuba, one of the characteristic asteroids with mean motion nearly commensurable in the ratio $2 / 1$ to that of Jupiter. Thus Hansen's method has often been employed for computing the absolute perturbations of asteroids. As can be seen from the above derivation,the orbital elements of Hansen's theory are $a _ { 0 } , e _ { 0 } , \varpi _ { 0 } , I _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } ,$ and $c _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ，the values of the elements at the epoch $t = 0 _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ ，but not the osculating elements.The mean elements often employed are those for which all periodic perturbations are taken off and only the secular variations are included.

Hansen later applied his theory to the motion of the moon (1838, 1862,1864),and the Tables of the Moon were published (l857a). In Hansen's Tables there are many parts to be referred to for computing the moon's coordinates.The numerical work is very much complicated.It is to Hansen's credit that he could overcome all numerical computations involved.His Tables were in practical use for the compilation of the nautical almanacs for some time before Brown's Tables.

Charlier (l887) carried out Hansen's expansions and applied them to the motion of the asteroid Thetis. Olsson (1896) expanded the disturbing function to higher orders.He (l896b） further studied the long-period inequalities by Hansen's theory.Klinkerfues (l863） worked out an expansion to be used for moderate eccentricities and inclinations. Recently Rabe (l943） modified Hansen's theory by taking Brendel's angle $\phi$ (Section 10.21) as the independent variable,in order to make the formal convergence more rapid.

Sampson (l92l),when he undertook the theory of the four great satellites of Jupiter, referred not to the method of the variation of elements,but to the method of absolute perturbations based on Hansen's idea of taking Jupiter's moving equatorial plane as the $( x , y )$ -plane.

# I0.I0MUSEN'S MODIFICATION OF HANSEN'S THEORY

Since Hansen's theory is purely numerical and permits the full use of fast computers,Musen (1959,196la,1963) chose this type of theory for the purely numerical computation of the solar perturbation of artificial satellites. The basic idea consists in the introduction of a fictitious satellite describing an auxiliary ellipse of constant shape and size in accordance with Kepler's laws.The position of the real satellite is determined by its deviation from the position of the auxiliary satellite.The position vectors have the same direction,but at different times.Let $\mathbf { r }$ be the position vector of a real satellite and $\overline { { \mathbf { r } } }$ the position vector of the auxiliary satellite. Hansen's developments then depend on the parameter $\nu$ and on the pseudo-time $z$ defined by the vectorial equation (Section 10.7)

$$
\mathbf { r } ( t ) = ( 1 + \nu ) \overline { { \mathbf { r } } } ( z ) .
$$

The pseudo-time $_ z$ represents the time at which the position vector of the auxiliary satellite has the direction possessed by the real satellite at time t.

Musen，however, for the purpose of numerical computation refers rather to Hansen's planetary theory (l857) but uses the fictitious mean anomaly instead of Hansen's function $W _ { : }$ ，and sets up the process of iteration in a convenient form. After completing the process of iteration, he forms the function $\bar { \mathcal { W } }$ and derives the perturbations in the mean anomaly and in the radius vector.

The main differences between Hansen's classical lunar theory and Musen's exposition are the following:

1. The eccentric anomaly of the fictitious satellite is used as the independent variable, instead of time. 2.The existence of fast computing machines permits the replacement of Hansen's development in Maclaurin series by the method of iteration. 3.Of the four parameters which determine the position of the orbital plane, two differ from Hansen's. 4.The rotation matrix is used instead of the development of polar coordinates.

The computation can be carried out to any desired order compatible with the accuracy of the basic data. Hansen's theory permits the easy inclusion of any source of a gravitational nature which might become important in the future,and the same program can be used for all cases. However, it has the drawback that the numerical procedures are not satisfactory if the eccentricity is smaller than O.O5 or larger than 0.6, because of the presence of a small divisor, either the eccentricity $e _ { 0 }$ or $1 \ : - \ : e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } :$ , in the denominator for the expression of the motion of the perigee.

We assume that the auxiliary ellipse is uniformly rotating in the osculating orbital plane with respect to the eccentric anomaly of the fictitious satellite； then vr and $\delta z = z - t$ can be interpreted respectively as perturbations of the radius vector and time. They are determined by a single Hansen function $W$ (Section l0.7). We introduce two systems of rectangular coordinates: one, $( X , Y , Z )$ ，is fixed in space; the other, $( x , y , z )$ , is rigidly connected with the osculating orbital plane. The $_ { x } \cdot$ and $y .$ -axes are in the orbital plane；the $z$ -axis is normal to that plane. The intersection of the $\pmb { x }$ -axis with the celestial sphere is called the departure point.

The motion of both satellites consists of the motion in the osculating plane $( x , y )$ combined with the rotation of that plane about the instantaneous radius vector.In order to obtain the absolute velocity no effect of rotation is to be added, because the instantaneous axis of rotation coincides with the radius vector. The fictitious forces produced by the rotation of the $( { \pmb x } , { \pmb y } )$ -system either disappear or are normal to the orbital plane,and the equation of motion of the satellite with respect to the $( x , y )$ -system takes the simple form

$$
{ \ddot { \mathbf { r } } } + { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r ^ { 3 } } } = \mathbf { g r a d } \ { \Omega } ;
$$

that is, it has the same form as in an inertial system. Such moving systems of coordinates are called ideal by Hansen (Section lO.6).The position of the moving system can be determined by three Eulerian angles: the inclination $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ to the earth's equator, the longitude $\pmb \theta$ of the ascending node,and the angular distance $\pmb { \sigma }$ of the node from the departure point (Section 10.8).

Musen introduced four parameters instead of these Eulerian angles, in order to make all basic arguments linear in time from the outset so as to enable them to be applied to satellite orbits of high inclination.Let $\psi$ be the true orbital longitude of the common node counted from the departure point in the instantaneous orbital plane of the satellite,and $\psi ^ { \prime }$ be the corresponding quantity counted from the departure point in the instantaneous orbital plane of the disturbing planet.Denote the periodic part of $\psi + \psi ^ { \prime }$ by $- 2 N ,$ and the periodic part of $\psi - \psi ^ { \prime }$ by $- 2 K ,$ and let

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \lambda _ { 1 } = \displaystyle { \sin \frac { I } { 2 } \cos N } , ~ \lambda _ { 2 } = \sin \frac { I } { 2 } \sin N , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \lambda _ { 3 } = \displaystyle { \cos \frac { I } { 2 } \sin K } , ~ \lambda _ { 4 } = \cos \frac { I } { 2 } \cos K . } } \end{array}
$$

Euler's parameters $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 3 } , \lambda _ { 4 } ,$ with the condition

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } = 1 ,
$$

which define the periodic perturbations of the orbital plane, are taken as the four parameters for determining the latitude. The four parameters $\lambda _ { 1 } , ~ \lambda _ { 2 } , ~ \lambda _ { 3 } , ~ \lambda _ { 4 }$ are not independent. We can introduce, instead, three independent parameters

$$
\small p = \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { 4 } } = \frac { \cos { N } } { \cos { K } } \tan { \frac { I } { 2 } } , \qquad q = - \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } } { \lambda _ { 4 } } = - \frac { \sin { N } } { \cos { K } } \tan { \frac { I } { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
s = \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } } { \lambda _ { 4 } } = \tan { K } .
$$

These are preferable for the polar satellites.

The perturbations in the orbital plane are determined, according to Hansen as modified by Hill (1881), by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d \bar { \epsilon } } \left( n _ { 0 } \delta z \right) = \displaystyle \frac { \dddot { W } + \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \overbar { W } } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } - \frac { 1 - \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \overbar { W } } \frac { y } { \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } , } \\ { \displaystyle n _ { 0 } \frac { d t } { d \bar { \epsilon } } = \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \frac { 1 - \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \overbar { W } } \left( 1 + \frac { y } { \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \right) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\bar { r } , \bar { f } ,$ and $\bar { \epsilon }$ are the polar coordinates and the eccentric anomaly of the auxiliary satellite in its elliptic motion, and the elements $a _ { 0 } , \epsilon _ { 0 } , \varpi _ { 0 } , e _ { 0 } ,$ $\pmb { n _ { 0 } } ,$ and $t _ { 0 }$ are the values of the osculating elements at $t = 0$ (Section 10.7), and $\overline { { W } } = W | _ { \eta = \overline { { \epsilon } } } ;$ finally, $\pmb { y }$ is defined so that $\bar { f } + \varpi _ { 0 } + y ( \bar { \epsilon } - \epsilon _ { 0 } )$ denotes the polar angle of the fictitious satellite with respect to the departure point,and

$$
\frac { d W } { d \bar { \epsilon } } = N \Lambda r \frac { \partial ( a _ { 0 } \Omega ) } { \partial r } + M \Lambda \frac { \partial ( a _ { 0 } \Omega ) } { \partial \bar { \epsilon } } + \frac { S y } { \sqrt { 1 - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

where

$$
( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) M = \bigg ( \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } [ - 2 + 2 e _ { 0 } \cos \bar { \epsilon } + 2 \cos \left( \eta - \bar { \epsilon } \right)
$$

$$
+ \frac { 1 } { 1 + \nu } [ 2 e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - 2 e _ { 0 } \cos \bar { \epsilon } + e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \cos \left( \eta + \bar { \epsilon } \right)
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { - 1 - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + 2 e _ { 0 } \cos \bar { \epsilon } - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \bar { \epsilon } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) N = \left( \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } [ 2 e _ { 0 } \sin \bar { \epsilon } - e _ { 0 } \sin \eta + e _ { 0 } \sin ( \eta - 2 \bar { \epsilon } ) ]
$$

$$
+ \frac { 1 } { 1 + \nu } [ - 2 e _ { 0 } \sin \bar { \epsilon } - ( 2 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) \sin ( \eta - \bar { \epsilon } )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { + \displaystyle e _ { 0 } \sin \bar { \epsilon } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \bar { \epsilon } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \Lambda = \frac { 1 - \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \bar { W } } \Big ( 1 + \frac { y } { \sqrt { 1 - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \bar { r } } { a _ { 0 } } \Big ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle S = \frac { \rho } { a _ { 0 } } \frac { \partial W } { \partial \eta } - \Big ( W + 1 + \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \Big ) \epsilon _ { 0 } \sin \eta . } } \end{array}
$$

These equations — together with the equations for $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 3 } , \lambda _ { 4 } ,$ which will be given later- constitute the basic system which can be solved by the method of iteration，by starting with $\begin{array} { r } { n _ { 0 } \ \delta z = 0 . } \end{array}$ ， $1 + \nu = 1$ ， $\lambda _ { 1 } = \sin { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } , \ \lambda _ { 2 } = \lambda _ { 3 } = 0 , \ \lambda _ { 4 } = \cos { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) }$ .The program for the multiplication of numerical Fourier series was developed for obtaining the final approximation in a very short time.At each step of iteration we determine $y$ so that no term of the form $\textit { A s i n } \eta$ is present in (67b); otherwise the integration would give rise to a secular term in $W$ and a mixed secular term in $\bar { W }$ The function $W$ has a purely trigonometric form with arguments $i \bar { \epsilon } + j \omega + k \eta , k = - 1 , 0 , + 1$ ; the function $\bar { \boldsymbol W }$ has a similar form with arguments $i \bar { \epsilon } + j \omega _ { ; }$ or

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \overline { { { W } } } = C _ { 0 } + C _ { 1 } \cos \bar { \epsilon } + \displaystyle \sum C \cos \big [ i \bar { \epsilon } + 2 j \omega \big ] } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \sum S \sin \big [ i \bar { \epsilon } + ( 2 j + 1 ) \omega \big ] . } } \end{array}
$$

Substituting this expression for $\bar { \boldsymbol W }$ in the first term of the right-hand side of (67a),we determine $C _ { 0 }$ and $C _ { 1 }$ so that the constant term and the term with $\bar { \epsilon }$ as argument shall disappear. This leaves only periodic terms in $n _ { 0 } \ \delta z$ with no constant term and no term with $\bar { \epsilon }$ as argument. Then $h / h _ { 0 }$ and $\pmb { \nu }$ ,followed by the latitude parameters,are determined.

Let the mean orbital longitude of the pericenter of the disturbed body be $\varpi _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } y t$ ,and its disturbed mean anomaly be $l = g _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } t + n _ { 0 } \delta z$ In place of Hansen's E,Y, $\Psi$ ，Musen made use of $h _ { 0 } / h , \Upsilon , \Psi$ from the standpoint of the iteration process. Higher-order perturbations being taken into account, Hansen's formulas (55) (Section 10.7) are replaced by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d \mathbf { T } } { d t } = n _ { 0 } y \Psi + M _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial l } + N _ { 1 } r \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \Psi } { d t } = - n _ { 0 } y \Big ( \Upsilon + \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } \frac { e _ { 0 } } { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) + M _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial l } + N _ { 2 } r \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \right) = M _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial l } + N _ { 3 } r \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } \end{array}
$$

in which the value of $\scriptstyle n _ { 0 } y$ should be determined so that no purely secular term is present in the expresson for $d \Psi / d t .$ ，and the coefficients are defined by

$$

in which the values of $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ should be determined so that no term linear in $\pmb { t }$ be present in the expressions for $d \lambda _ { 2 } / d t$ and $d \lambda _ { 3 } / d t$ and $\theta = ( \varpi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + n _ { 0 } y ^ { \prime } t - \sigma ^ { \prime } ) - \omega ^ { \prime }$ ，where ${ \pmb { \sigma } } ^ { \prime }$ is the true orbital longitude of the node of the orbital plane of the disturbing body counted on the fixed ecliptic and equinox.

If we denote by $g = g _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } t$ the undisturbed mean anomaly, then the series for $\Psi$ is written, without any additive integration constant, in the form

but with additive integration constants.

In contrast to $\bar { r }$ and ${ \bar { f } } ,$ which depend on the disturbed mean anomaly $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ through

\begin{array} { r } { \bar { r } \cos \bar { f } = a _ { 0 } ( \cos \epsilon - e _ { 0 } ) , \bar { r } \sin \bar { f } = a _ { 0 } \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \sin \epsilon , } \\ { \epsilon - e _ { 0 } \sin \epsilon = l , \qquad } \end{array}

Musen considers

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { W = \Xi + \Upsilon \displaystyle \left( \frac { \rho } { a _ { 0 } } \cos \phi + \frac { 3 } { 2 } e _ { 0 } \right) + \Psi \displaystyle \frac { \rho } { a _ { 0 } } \sin \phi , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \Xi = - 1 - \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } + \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } - 3 \epsilon _ { 0 } \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon \cos { \left( \chi - n _ { 0 } y t - \varpi _ { 0 } \right) } - \epsilon _ { 0 } } { 1 - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { \Upsilon = \displaystyle \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon \cos { \left( \chi - n _ { 0 } y t - \varpi _ { 0 } \right) } - \epsilon _ { 0 } } { 1 - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { \Psi = \displaystyle \frac { 2 h } { h _ { 0 } } \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon \sin { \left( \chi - n _ { 0 } y t - \varpi _ { 0 } \right) } } { 1 - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}

The generalized Hill's (l88l) equation (Section 8.9)

$$

The integration constants $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 }$ must be adjusted so that no secular term and no term with argument $\pmb { g }$ be present in $\eta _ { 0 } \delta z$ .The part

$$
n _ { 0 } [ \stackrel { \_ } { W } _ { 0 } ] + B - \frac { n _ { 0 } y } { \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \bar { \rho } ^ { 2 } } { a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } }

contains the terms of the form $A _ { 1 } + A _ { 2 } \cos \xi$ $_ { g }$ Let $\pmb { \kappa }$ be the coefficient of the term with argument $g$ in $\left( { \bar { \rho } } / a _ { 0 } \right)$ cos $\bar { \phi } + { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } e _ { 0 }$ ; then,

After $c _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { c _ { 2 } }$ are determined we can obtain $\eta _ { 0 } \delta z$ by solving the differential equation (54a).The perturbation of the radius vector is derived from

For the latitude parameters we put

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { } } & { { \displaystyle \lambda _ { 1 } = \sin \frac { I _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( { \cal A } + { \cal B } \right) + [ \lambda _ { 1 } ] , \qquad \lambda _ { 2 } = [ \lambda _ { 2 } ] , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle \lambda _ { 3 } = [ \lambda _ { 3 } ] , \qquad \lambda _ { 4 } = \cos \frac { I _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( { \cal A } - { \cal B } \right) + [ \lambda _ { 4 } ] , } } \end{array}
$$

where $[ \lambda _ { 1 } ] , [ \lambda _ { 2 } ] , [ \lambda _ { 3 } ] , [ \lambda _ { 4 } ]$ are the series obtained by formal integration of the differential equations for $d \lambda _ { 1 } / d t , d \lambda _ { 2 } / d t , d \lambda _ { 3 } / d t , d \lambda _ { 4 } / d t .$ The integration constants $\pmb { A }$ and $B$ are determined by

+ constant part in $\{ ( [ \lambda _ { 1 } ] + [ \lambda _ { 4 } ] ) ^ { 2 } + ( [ \lambda _ { 2 } ] - [ \lambda _ { 3 } ] ) ^ { 2 } \} = 0 ,$

$$
B ^ { 2 } - 2 B \biggl ( \cos \frac { I _ { 0 } } { 2 } - \sin \frac { I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \biggr )
