Let the $\pmb { x } \cdot$ -axis be directed to the mean position of the perihelion on the orbital plane of the disturbing planet, the $\pmb { y }$ -axis be normal to the $\pmb { x }$ -axis on this same plane,and the $_ z$ -axis be normal to both of these axes.With reference to these moving axes we have (Section l0.4)

$$
\mathbf { r } = A _ { 3 } ( \psi ^ { \prime } - { \pmb { \varpi } } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - n _ { 0 } { \pmb { y } } ^ { \prime } t ) \cdot A _ { 1 } ( I ) \cdot A _ { 3 } ( { \pmb { \varpi } } _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } { \pmb { y } } t - { \pmb { \psi } } ) \cdot ( 1 + \nu ) \mathbf { r } _ { 1 } ,
$$

where

$$
4 _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) = \left( \begin{array} { c c c } { { 1 } } & { { 0 } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { \cos \alpha } } & { { - \sin \alpha } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { \sin \alpha } } & { { \cos \alpha } } \end{array} \right) , \qquad A _ { 3 } ( \alpha ) = \left( \begin{array} { c c c } { { \cos \alpha } } & { { - \sin \alpha } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { \sin \alpha } } & { { \cos \alpha } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { 0 } } & { { 1 } } \end{array} \right) ,
$$

$$
\mathbf { r } _ { 1 } = { \binom { { \bar { r } } \cos { \bar { f } } } { \bar { r } \sin { \bar { f } } } } .
$$

Substituting

$$
~ \psi = \psi _ { 0 } + n _ { 0 } ( \beta - \alpha ) - K - N , ~ \psi ^ { \prime } = \psi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - n _ { 0 } ( \beta + \alpha ) + K - N ,
$$

we obtain

$$
{ \bf r } = A _ { 3 } ( - \omega ^ { \prime } ) \cdot \Lambda \cdot A _ { 3 } ( \omega ) \cdot ( 1 + \nu ) { \bf r } _ { 1 } ,
$$

where

$$
\Lambda = { \binom { \lambda _ { 1 1 } } { \lambda _ { 2 1 } } } \lambda _ { 2 2 } \lambda _ { 2 3 } \Biggr ) ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \lambda _ { 1 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } , \qquad \lambda _ { 1 2 } = - 2 ( \lambda _ { 3 } \lambda _ { 4 } + \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { \qquad \lambda _ { 1 3 } = 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 3 } - \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 4 } ) , } } \\ { { \lambda _ { 2 1 } = 2 ( \lambda _ { 3 } \lambda _ { 4 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } ) , \qquad \lambda _ { 2 2 } = - \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \qquad \lambda _ { 2 3 } = - 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 4 } + \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 3 } ) , } } \\ { { \qquad \lambda _ { 3 1 } = 2 ( \lambda _ { 3 } \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 4 } ) , \qquad \lambda _ { 3 2 } = 2 ( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 4 } - \lambda _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 3 } ) , } } \\ { { \qquad \lambda _ { 3 3 } = - \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

The absolute velocity is written, with $f + \chi - \varpi _ { 0 } - n _ { 0 } y t = \bar { f } _ { \ast }$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { { \bf v } = h A _ { 3 } ( - \omega ^ { \prime } ) \cdot \Lambda \cdot A _ { 3 } ( \omega ) \cdot \left( \begin{array} { c } { - \sin \tilde { f } - e \sin \left( \chi - \varpi _ { 0 } - n _ { 0 } y t \right) } \\ { \cos \tilde { f } + e \cos \left( \chi - \varpi _ { 0 } - n _ { 0 } y t \right) } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right) , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } ^ { \prime } = \frac { a _ { 0 } n _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } A _ { 3 } ( - \omega ^ { \prime } ) \cdot \Lambda \cdot A _ { 3 } ( \omega ) \cdot \left( \frac { - \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \sin \bar { f } - \frac { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \Psi } { \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } ( \cos \bar { f } + e _ { 0 } ) + \frac { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \Upsilon } \right) .
$$

With similar formulas Bailie and Bryant (1960) computed the osculating elements from the modified Hansen theory for the motion of an artificial satellite.

Musen (l96la) improved the theory by taking the orbital true longitude instead of the eccentric anomaly as the independent variable in order to make the expansion more rapidly convergent formally. The radius vector and the parameters are expressed in the form of trigonometric polynomials in two arguments, not in the form of truncated infinite series. The two arguments are the mean true anomaly, $c v \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \varpi _ { 0 } .$ and the mean argument of latitude, $g v - \theta _ { 0 }$

Let

$$
u = \frac { 1 } { r } = h ^ { 2 } + e h ^ { 2 } \cos { ( v - \chi ) } , \qquad h ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } ,
$$

$$
x = ( 1 - c ) v + \varpi _ { 0 } + \phi ,
$$

where ${ \pmb v }$ is the orbital'true longitude, $x$ the orbital true longitude of the osculating pericenter reckoned from the departure point, and $\phi$ a purely trigonometric part of the order of perturbation. In contrast to

$$
u = h ^ { 2 } + e h ^ { 2 } \cos { ( c v - \varpi _ { 0 } - \phi ) } ,
$$

write

$$
\bar { u } = h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + e _ { 0 } h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \cos { ( c v - \varpi _ { 0 } ) } , \qquad h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 0 } ( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) } ;
$$

then,

$$
\frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { d v } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { \bar { h } } , \qquad \frac { 1 } { \bar { u } ^ { 2 } } \frac { d v } { d z } = \frac { 1 } { \bar { h } _ { 0 } } , \qquad \frac { d t } { d v } = \frac { \bar { h } } { u ^ { 2 } } \cdot
$$

The perturbation of time $\delta z = z - t$ is derived from

$$
\frac { d ~ \delta z } { d t } = \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \frac { u ^ { 2 } } { \bar { u } ^ { 2 } } - 1 = \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \overline { { W } } } { \bar { u } } + \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \bigg ( \frac { \nu } { 1 + \nu } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } , \qquad 1 + \nu = \frac { \bar { u } } { u } ,
$$

where

$$
\overline { { W } } = - \left( 1 + \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \right) \frac { \bar { u } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } [ 1 + e \cos { ( c v - { \varpi } _ { 0 } - \phi ) } ] .
$$

The corresponding function $W$ becomes

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { W = \displaystyle - \left( 1 + \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \right) ( 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos \varphi ) + 2 \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } [ 1 + e \cos \left( \varphi - \phi \right) ] , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \overrightarrow { W } = W | _ { \varphi = c v - \varpi _ { 0 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The derivative of $W$ to be used in Musen's revised theory is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { l } { = \displaystyle h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial v } \bigg ) \bigg \{ 2 \frac { u } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \cos { \left( c v - { \varpi _ { 0 } } - \varphi \right) } - 1 - \epsilon _ { 0 } \cos { \varphi } } \\ { \qquad \quad + \ : 2 \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \left[ \cos { \left( c v - { \varpi _ { 0 } } - \varphi \right) } - 1 \right] \bigg \} \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } } \\ { \displaystyle - \ : 2 \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial u } \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \sin { \left( c v - { \varpi _ { 0 } } - \varphi \right) } + ( 1 - c ) \left[ \frac { \partial W } { \partial \varphi } - \left( 1 + \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \right) e _ { 0 } \sin { \varphi } \right] . } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

This form does not contain $\textstyle \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } }$ in the denominator,as in the previous theory of Musen. Integrating this equation we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { W = \Xi + \Upsilon \cos \varphi + \Psi \sin \varphi , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \Xi = - 1 - \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } + 2 \displaystyle \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \Upsilon = 2 \displaystyle \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } e \cos \phi - \left( 1 + \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } \right) e _ { 0 } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \Psi = 2 \displaystyle \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } e \sin \phi . } } \end{array}
$$

The equations for $\Xi$ and $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon }$ contain the integration constants in the left-hand members,which should be determined so that neither the constant nor the secular term will be present in $\begin{array} { r l } {  { n _ { 0 } \ \delta z } } \end{array}$ Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } - 1 = - \frac 1 3 \Xi + \frac 2 3 \left[ \left( \frac { h _ { 0 } } h - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { h _ { 0 } } h - 1 \right) ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } = 1 + \frac 1 2 \left( \frac { h _ { 0 } } h - 1 + \Xi \right) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } u = \bar { u } + \frac 1 2 \left( \frac h { h _ { 0 } } - 1 \right) \bar { u } + \frac 1 2 h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \overline { { { W } } } \frac h { h _ { 0 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

These, together with $d W / d v _ { ; }$ , are the basic equations for the problem.

The disturbing function $\Omega$ is expanded with Euler's parameters $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } ,$ $\lambda _ { 3 } , \lambda _ { 4 }$ in the form of finite polynomials,not in the form of truncated trigonometric series.Let $\theta _ { 0 }$ be the mean longitude of the ascending node. In the case of an earth satellite (Section 8.6), the analytic expression for each term of the expansion of the disturbing function is in powers of $J _ { 2 } , J _ { 3 } , \ldots$ ， and the coefficients of the Legendre functions of the geo-potential are trigonometric polynomials in $c v \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \varpi _ { 0 }$ and $g v - \theta _ { 0 }$

Then $\delta z$ and $t$ are derived from

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d \delta z } { d t } = \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \overrightarrow { W } } { \overrightarrow { u } } + \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 ~ - ~ \nu ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 ~ + \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \overrightarrow { u } } ~ \overrightarrow { W } \right) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d t } { d v } = \frac { h _ { 0 } } { \overrightarrow { u } ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 ~ - ~ \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 ~ + ~ \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \overrightarrow { u } } ~ \overrightarrow { W } } , } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \tiny { \begin{array} { l } { \overline { { { \gamma } } } } \\ { \overline { { { \gamma } } } } \end{array} } ( n _ { 0 } \delta 2 ) = ( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } \Big ( \frac { \bar { r } } { \bar { \rho } _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } \overline { { { W } } } + ( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } \Big ( \frac { \bar { r } } { \bar { \rho } _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 4 } \frac { ( u - \bar { u } ) ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } } \\ { - \frac { \bar { r } } { \bar { \rho } _ { 0 } } \ \overline { { { W } } } \frac { d ( n _ { 0 } \delta 2 ) } { d v } } \end{array}
$$

The slowly converging series for $( { \bar { r } } / \phi )$ in the case of a large eccentricity appears only at this last stage of integration.

Write

$$
\mathrm { ~  ~ \bar { ~ } { ~ \theta ~ } ~ } ^ { \prime } = ( 1 - g ) v + \theta _ { 0 } - N - K , \theta = ( 1 - h ^ { \prime } ) v + \theta _ { 0 } - N + K ,
$$

where $\pmb { \sigma }$ denotes the angular distance of the node from the departure point. Then the equations for the Euler parameters are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 1 } } { d v } = \Big ( \frac { h ^ { \prime } + g } { 2 } - 1 \Big ) \lambda _ { 2 } + \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \lambda _ { 2 } } \cos { I } , } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 2 } } { d v } = - \Big ( \frac { h ^ { \prime } + g } { 2 } - 1 \Big ) \lambda _ { 1 } - \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } \cos { I } , } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 3 } } { d v } = \frac { h ^ { \prime } - g } { 2 } \lambda _ { 4 } + \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \lambda _ { 4 } } \cos { I } , } \\ & { \frac { d \lambda _ { 4 } } { d v } = - \frac { h ^ { \prime } - g } { 2 } \lambda _ { 3 } - \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \lambda _ { 3 } } \cos { I } , } \end{array}
$$

which are more elegant than those of the previous theory. The quantities $h ^ { \prime }$ and $g$ are determined so that no constant term appears on the righthand sides of these equations.

The position vector $\pmb { x }$ is determined as in Section 10.4.

Bailie and Fisher (l963） worked out an analytic representation of Musen's revised theory (l96la） of artificial satellites in terms of the orbital true longitude.The solutions include long-period terms and terms with small divisors derived from the $\boldsymbol { J } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ -approximation as well as from the third and fourth harmonics of the earth's potential. For the first approximation they took

$$
u = \bar { u } = h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } [ 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos { ( c v - { \varpi } _ { 0 } ) } ] , \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } = \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } = 1 ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial \varphi } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } = \sin \frac { I _ { 0 } } { 2 } , \qquad \lambda _ { 2 } = \lambda _ { 3 } = 0 , \qquad \lambda _ { 4 } = \cos \frac { I _ { 0 } } { 2 } .
$$

The first approximation to $\pmb { c }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 1 \ - \ c = \ - \frac { 3 } { 4 } J _ { 2 } R ^ { 2 } h _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } ( 1 \ - \ 3 \cos ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

The reciprocal radius vector $\pmb { u }$ and the Euler parameters are all expressed in trigonometric polynomials with arguments $c v \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \varpi _ { 0 }$ and $g v - \theta _ { 0 }$ They gave a long analytical expression for the perturbations of the mean anomaly $\eta _ { 0 } \delta z$ . The appearance of a coupling trm $J _ { 3 } J _ { 4 } / J _ { 2 } ^ { 3 }$ is unexpected. Neither this theory nor those of Brouwer (1959； Section 9.20),Kozai (1959; Section 8.6),or Garfinkel (1959; Section 9.21) can be used for the critical inclination.

Musen's further simplification (1966) of the theory consists in developing a form of the differential equations of a numerical lunar theory which can be used for obtaining the rectangular coordinates of a satellite moving in a highly inclined orbital plane.The integration of these equations is based on solving a certain linear partial differential equation by means of iteration, as Musen had previously suggested (l96la).The arguments of the theory are the linear functions of the true orbital longitudes u of the satellite and $\boldsymbol { v _ { 4 } }$ of the sun; thus they are of Laplacian type. The use of the true longitudes speeds up the formal convergence of the expansion of the disturbing function, as suggested by Brown (1930).Furthermore, Musen adapted Hansen's device for performing the integration, by introducing a fictitious satellite whose true orbital longitude w is considered as a constant until the integration is completed. He splits the perturbation of the satelite into perturbations in the orbital plane and perturbations of the orbital plane as in Hansen's theory (Section 10.8).He obtains the perturbations in the orbital plane by means of a $W .$ -function analogous to Hansen's.

For the real satellite let $1 / h$ be the areal velocity, $h _ { 0 }$ the mean value of $h , \ e$ the osculating eccentricity, $\scriptstyle { e _ { 0 } }$ its mean value, $x$ the true orbital longitude of the pericenter, $\pmb { \varphi }$ its periodic part, $1 \mathrm { ~ - ~ } g _ { 1 }$ the mean motion of $x$ with respect to $v$ ; and let $\upsilon _ { 1 } = g _ { 1 } v - \pi _ { 0 }$ be the mean true anomaly of the real satellite, and $\boldsymbol { w } _ { 1 } = \boldsymbol { g } _ { 1 } \boldsymbol { w } - \boldsymbol { \pi } _ { 0 }$ the mean true anomaly of the fictitious satelite. Musen used the equation

$$
W = \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \left[ 1 + e \cos \left( w _ { 1 } - \varphi \right) \right] - \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } ( 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos w _ { 1 } ) ,
$$

where ${ \pmb w } _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ is considered as a temporary constant.

Hansen's bar-operation in the present case consists in replacing ${ \pmb w } _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ by $\boldsymbol { v } _ { 1 }$ after the integration. This form of $W$ differs from the classical. Applying the bar-operator to $W _ { : }$ ，we obtain for the position vector $\pmb { x }$ of the real satellite:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } = u = \bar { u } + \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \overline { { { W } } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \bar { u } = ( 1 + \nu ) u , \displaystyle ~ \nu = - ( 1 + \nu ) \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \bar { u } } \overline { { { W } } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The differential equation for $W$ is

$$
\frac { d W } { d v } = N \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } + M \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial v } + ( 1 - g _ { 1 } ) \bigg ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial w _ { 1 } } - \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } e _ { 0 } \sin w _ { 1 } \bigg ) ,
$$

where

$$
N = - \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \sin { ( v _ { 1 } - w _ { 1 } ) } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { h _ { 0 } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle { \cal I } = \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \left[ \left( \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } + 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos w _ { 1 } + \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \widehat { W } \right) \cos \left( v _ { 1 } - w _ { 1 } \right) \right. } } } \\ { { { \left. \qquad - \left( \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } + 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos w _ { 1 } \right) \right. } } } \end{array} ,
$$

The motion of the pericenter $1 \phantom { + } - g _ { 1 }$ is obtained by imposing the condition that no term of the form $\pmb { A }$ cos ${ \boldsymbol { w } } _ { 1 }$ appears in this equation.

Denote by $\pmb \theta$ the longitude of the osculating ascending node, by $g _ { 3 }$ the mean motion of the ascending node,and by $\upsilon _ { 3 } = g _ { 3 } \upsilon + \theta _ { 0 }$ the mean longitude of the ascending node.Let $\mathbf { r } ^ { 0 }$ be the unit vector $\pmb { x }$ such that $\mathbf { r } = r { \cdot } \mathbf { r } ^ { 0 }$ ; then,

$$
\mathbf { r } ^ { 0 } = A _ { 3 } ( v _ { 3 } ) \cdot { \boldsymbol { \Lambda } } \cdot { \left( \begin{array} { l } { \cos v _ { 2 } } \\ { \sin v _ { 2 } } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right) } , \qquad A _ { 3 } ( v _ { 3 } ) = { \left( \begin{array} { l l l } { \cos v _ { 3 } } & { - \sin v _ { 3 } } & { 0 } \\ { \sin v _ { 3 } } & { \ \cos v _ { 3 } } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right) } ,
$$

where ${ \boldsymbol { v } } _ { 2 }$ and $v _ { 3 }$ are linear in $v$ such that

$$
v _ { 2 } = g _ { 2 } v + \omega _ { 0 } , \qquad v _ { 3 } = g _ { 3 } v + \theta _ { 0 } ,
$$

and the matrix elements of $\Lambda$ are given in the above, with

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle \lambda _ { 1 } = \sin \frac { I } { 2 } \cos N ^ { \prime } , \qquad \lambda _ { 3 } = \cos \frac { I } { 2 } \sin K ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \lambda _ { 2 } = \sin \frac { I } { 2 } \sin N ^ { \prime } , \qquad \lambda _ { 4 } = \cos \frac { I } { 2 } \cos K ^ { \prime } . } } \end{array}
$$

The function $N ^ { \prime }$ is a purely periodic part of $- ( \theta + \sigma ) / 2$ and $\mathbf { \nabla } K ^ { \prime }$ isa purely periodic part of $( \theta - \sigma ) / 2$ ，if $\pmb { \sigma }$ denotes the distance of the departure point from the ascending node.For the moon, the series for $\lambda _ { 1 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 2 }$ ， $\lambda _ { 3 2 }$ are cosine series and the series for $\lambda _ { 1 2 } , \lambda _ { 2 1 } , \lambda _ { 3 1 }$ are sine series with no constant term.

The derivatives of the disturbing function are formed with respect to the elliptic $v _ { 2 }$ Let $\mathbf { r }$ be the unit vector of the fictitious satellite with respect to the coordinate system associated with the mean orbital plane. This plane is defined as a plane with the real inclination $\pmb { I }$ but with the longitude of the ascending node equal to $v _ { 3 }$ .Let $\overline { { \mathbf { r } } }$ be the unit vector of the real satelite with respect to the mean orbital plane. Now we separate the elliptic and the nonelliptic $v _ { 2 }$ in the disturbing function by temporarily replacing the vectors $\mathbf { r }$ and $\bar { \bf r }$ Let $\Pi$ be the disturbing function so modified.Write

$$
C = \overline { { \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial \Gamma _ { 1 } } } } \lambda _ { 1 3 } + \overline { { \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial \Gamma _ { 2 } } } } \lambda _ { 2 3 } + \overline { { \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial \Gamma _ { 3 } } } } \lambda _ { 3 3 } , \mathbf { R } = \left( \mathbf { r } \times \frac { \partial \mathbf { r } } { \partial w _ { 2 } } \right) _ { w _ { 2 } = 0 } ,
$$

where $\Gamma _ { 1 } , \Gamma _ { 2 } , \Gamma _ { 3 }$ are the components of the vector $\mathbf { r }$ ,that is,

$$
\mathbf { \vec { r } } = \Gamma _ { 1 } \mathbf { \vec { i } } + \Gamma _ { 2 } \mathbf { \vec { j } } + \Gamma _ { 3 } \mathbf { k } .
$$

Then,

$$
\mathbf { r } ^ { 0 } = A _ { 3 } ( v _ { 3 } ) \overline { { \mathbf { T } } } ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d \mathbf { T } } { d v } = ( 1 - { g _ { 2 } } ) \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial w _ { 2 } } + { g _ { 3 } \mathbf { F } \times \mathbf { k } } + \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { R } C \sin { ( w _ { 2 } - v _ { 2 } ) } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d W } { d v } = N \frac { \partial \overline { { \Pi } } } { \partial u } + M \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } \overline { { \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial w _ { 2 } } } } + ( 1 - { g _ { 2 } } ) \bigg ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial w _ { 1 } } - \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } { e _ { 0 } \sin w _ { 1 } } \bigg ) . } \end{array}
$$

We start the iteration with $h / h _ { 0 } = 1$ , that is, with

$$
\Gamma _ { 1 } = \cos w _ { 2 } , \qquad \Gamma _ { 2 } = \cos I _ { 0 } \sin w _ { 2 } , \qquad \Gamma _ { 3 } = \sin I _ { 0 } \sin w _ { 2 } ,
$$

and repeat it up to the final values. The perturbed coordinates are the trigonometric series in four arguments: $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , v _ { 3 } , v _ { 4 } .$

Let $m ^ { \prime }$ be the solar mass and $h ^ { \prime }$ the areal velocity of the sun; then,

$$
\frac { d v _ { 4 } } { d v } = \sqrt { 1 + m ^ { \prime } } \left( \frac { h ^ { \prime } } { h _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 3 } ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } \left( \frac { \bar { u } ^ { \prime } / h ^ { \prime 2 } } { \bar { u } / h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \right) .
$$

If $g _ { 4 }$ is the constant part of the right-hand member, then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d v } = g _ { 1 } \frac { \partial } { \partial v _ { 1 } } + g _ { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial v _ { 2 } } + g _ { 3 } \frac { \partial } { \partial v _ { 3 } } + g _ { 4 } \frac { \partial } { \partial v _ { 4 } } + K \frac { \partial } { \partial v _ { 4 } } , } \\ { \displaystyle K = \left( \frac { h ^ { \prime } } { h _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { \bar { u } ^ { \prime } / h ^ { \prime 2 } } { \bar { u } / h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \bigg ) - g _ { 4 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\kappa$ is a purely periodic series. Thus we reduce the problem to the integration of the partial differential equations

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad g _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial v _ { 1 } } + g _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial v _ { 2 } } + g _ { 3 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial v _ { 3 } } + g _ { 4 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial v _ { 4 } } } \\ & { \quad \quad = N \displaystyle \frac { \partial \overline { { \Pi } } } { \partial u } + M \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \overline { { \partial } } \overline { { \Pi } } } { \partial w _ { 2 } } - K \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial v _ { 4 } } + ( 1 - g _ { 2 } ) \bigg ( \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial w _ { 1 } } - \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } } { h } e _ { 0 } \sin w _ { 1 } \bigg ) , } \\ & { \quad \quad g _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial v _ { 1 } } + g _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial v _ { 2 } } + g _ { 3 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial v _ { 3 } } + g _ { 4 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial v _ { 4 } } } \\ & { \quad \quad = ( 1 - g _ { 2 } ) \displaystyle \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial w _ { 2 } } + g _ { 3 } \mathbf { T } \times \mathbf { k } + \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { h ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { R } C \sin { \bf \Phi } ( w _ { 2 } - v _ { 2 } ) - K \displaystyle \frac { \partial \mathbf { T } } { \partial v _ { 4 } } . } \end{array}
$$

As can be seen, the series $\kappa$ is small of the order $\scriptstyle e _ { 0 } \lambda ,$ ,and the terms $K \partial W / \partial v _ { 4 }$ and $K \partial \mathbf { T } / \partial v _ { 4 }$ are at least of the order $\lambda ^ { 4 }$ ,where $\lambda ^ { 2 } = m ^ { \prime } ( h ^ { \prime } / h _ { 0 } ) ^ { 6 }$ is an analogue of Delaunay's $\mathbf { m }$ and $\alpha = ( h ^ { \prime } / h _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 }$ is the parallactic factor in the present theory (Section 9.2). Consequently the solution of these partial differential equations by means of iteration represents a fast-converging process. The disturbing function $\boldsymbol { \Pi }$ is expressed by

$$
\Pi = \lambda ^ { 2 } h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { \bar { u } ^ { \prime } } { h ^ { \prime 2 } } \bigg ) ^ { 3 } \bigg ( \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \bar { u } } \bigg ) [ \big ( \textstyle { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \textstyle { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \Gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \big )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { + \lambda ^ { 2 } h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \alpha ( 1 + \nu ) ^ { 3 } \Big ( \frac { \bar { u } ^ { \prime } } { h ^ { \prime 2 } } \Big ) ^ { 4 } \Big ( \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \bar { u } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } } \\ & { \times \mathrm { [ } \big ( \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 1 } \Gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \Gamma _ { 1 } \big ) \cos \big ( v _ { 3 } - v _ { 4 } \big ) } \\ & { \qquad + \big ( - \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \Gamma _ { 2 } - \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \Gamma _ { 2 } \big ) \sin \big ( v _ { 3 } } \\ & { \qquad + \big ( \frac { 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } - \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 1 } \Gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \big ) \cos \big ( 3 v _ { 3 } - 3 v _ { 4 } \big ) } \\ & { \qquad + \big ( - \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \Gamma _ { 2 } + \frac { 5 } { 8 } \Gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } \big ) \sin \big ( 3 v _ { 3 } - } \end{array}
$$

+···.

Musen referred to a numerical theory. However, we can solve the equations (7O） and (71） analytically by representing the frequencies $g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , g _ { 3 } , g _ { 4 }$ and $W , \mathbf { r }$ as power series in $\lambda$ .We develop the operator on the left-hand sides of (7O) and (71) into a power series,and dissolve (70) and （7l） into equations integrable by quadratures.

Finally,we derive the disturbed time $_ z$ from

$$
\frac { 1 } { \bar { u } ^ { 2 } } \frac { d v } { \partial z } = \frac { 1 } { h _ { 0 } } \cdot
$$

Put $z = t + \delta z$ ; then,

$$
\frac { d ( n _ { 0 } \ : \delta z ) } { d v } = ( 1 - \ : e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } \biggl ( \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \bar { u } } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } \biggl [ 1 - \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } ( 1 \ : + \ : \nu ) ^ { 2 } \biggr ] .
$$

This method of iteration by Musen was programmed for the development of Hansen's coordinates in the lunar problem by Charnow (1966).

Fisher (l963) computed the osculating elements by Musen's modified Hansen method. The Delaunay variables are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { G = \frac { \mu } { \hbar } = \frac { \mu } { h _ { 0 } } \bigg ( 1 - \frac { \Xi } { 3 } + \frac { 2 } { 2 7 } \Xi ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \bigg ) , } } \\ & { } & \\ & { \ell = e _ { 0 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \Upsilon - e _ { 0 } \Xi ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 4 } ( 4 e _ { 0 } \Xi ^ { 2 } - 4 \Xi \Upsilon + \frac { 3 \Psi ^ { 2 } } { e _ { 0 } } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { } & \\ & { \frac { H } { G } = \cos I = \cos I _ { 0 } \bigg ( 1 + \frac { \Xi } { 3 } + \frac { \Xi ^ { 2 } } { 2 7 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \bigg ) , } \\ & { } & \\ & { \phi = \frac { \Psi } { 2 e _ { 0 } } + \frac { \Psi } { 4 e _ { 0 } } \bigg ( \frac { \Xi } { 3 } - \frac { \Upsilon } { e _ { 0 } } \bigg ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle N = \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } } { \cos { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } + \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } } { \sin { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } - \frac { \Xi } { 1 2 } \cos { I _ { 0 } } \Big [ \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } } { \cos ^ { 3 } { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } - \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 3 } { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } \Big ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle N = \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } } { \cos { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } - \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } } { \sin { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } - \frac { \Xi } { 1 2 } \cos { I _ { 0 } } \Big [ \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } } { \cos ^ { 3 } { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } + \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 3 } { ( I _ { 0 } / 2 ) } } \Big ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

Let $u = 1 / r$ and denote by δ the deviation of an osculating element from its value in the auxiliary ellipse; then,

$$
\delta \left( { \frac { h } { h _ { 0 } } } \right) = { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \Xi , \delta e = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \Upsilon - e _ { 0 } \Xi ) , \delta f = - \phi = - { \frac { \Psi } { 2 e _ { 0 } } } ;
$$

accordingly,

$$
\delta n = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { h _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu } \overline { { W } } + \frac { \Xi } { 6 } \bar { u } , \qquad \overline { { W } } = \Xi + \Upsilon \cos { f } + \Psi \sin { f } .
$$

The periodic parts of the solution of the elements by the two methods agree to the first order in $J _ { 2 }$ . Although differences in the arbitrary constants and arguments of the trigonometric terms do exist, they do not appear in the first-order solutions for the trigonometric parts of the elements. In particular, the mean motions of the argument of the perigee and the longitude of the node are

$$
\frac { d g ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = ( n _ { 0 } ) _ { H } ( g - c ) , \frac { d h ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = ( n _ { 0 } ) _ { H } ( 1 - h ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Terms in $J _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ in these expansions agre if suitable changes of the arbitrary constants are made.

As far as terms of the order $J _ { 2 }$ ，the first-order analytic solutions for $\Xi , \Upsilon , \Psi , \lambda _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 3 } , g , 1 - h ^ { \prime } , g - c$ were obtained by Bailie and Fisher (1963).The differences of the arbitrary constants are tabulated by Fisher (1963）to the first order in $J _ { 2 }$ .Transformations relate the true anomaly of the auxiliary ellipses to the true anomaly of the real satellite.

Differential correction procedures based on Keplerian approximations are not valid for the improvement of some types of reference orbits used in satellite problems.Mulholland （l967） derived correction vectors for use with Hansen-type formulations, such as those modifed by Musen, of satellite motions, in which the intermediary orbit is an ellipse rotating uniformly in the osculating plane.

Musen (1967), following von Zeipel (190la; Section l0.1l),did not consider $W$ as a perturbed element and did not resort to the method of the variation of constants,but obtained $d W / d t$ as the projection of the perturbations in the acceleration vector on a variable vector $\overline { { \bf N } }$ ，selected so that $d W / d t$ is totally integrable and $W$ is linear in $d \nu / d t$ One of the characteristic features of Hansen's theory is the use of $W$ for determining the perturbations $\pmb { \nu }$ and $\delta z .$ ，Musen avoided the expansions in powers of $n _ { 0 } ~ \delta z$ and $\eta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \ \delta z ^ { \prime }$ because of the slow formal convergence.The angular arguments in the periodic series representing the perturbations are linear functions of the mean anomalies of the perturbed and perturbing planets. It is $\bar { W } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ , not the function $W _ { : }$ , that represents the main feature of Hansen's theory. The expansions in series are carried out by electronic computers. The perturbations $\scriptstyle n _ { 0 }$ $\delta z$ and $\eta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \delta z ^ { \prime }$ are the largest,and their determination is associated with the possible appearance in the process of integration of the squares of the small divisors caused by the commensurability of the mean motions.The computation scheme is arranged so that the process of iteration is applicable; thus the programming is homogeneous.

Musen (l967a, l968） further simplifed Hansen's lunar theory by introducing almost ideal coordinates instead of Hansen's ideal coordinates. Let $\mathbf { \delta k }$ be the unit vector normal to the orbital plane of the planet and consider this plane as fixed.Let $\mathbf { P }$ be the unit vector along the ascending node of the mean orbital plane, $\mathbf { R }$ be the normal to this plane,and ${ \bf Q } = { \bf R }$ X P.The mean elements are so chosen that no purely secular or mixed terms are present in the expansions of the rectangular coordinates. The mean orbital plane rotates around $\mathbf { \delta k }$ with constant angular velocity $n _ { 0 } \Psi = n _ { 0 } \gamma \mathbf { k }$ ，where $n _ { 0 }$ is the mean anomalistic motion of the projection of the satellite on the mean orbital plane; this is given in advance. The inclination $\boldsymbol { I }$ of the mean orbital plane toward the orbital plane of the planet is also given in advance. We have

$$
n _ { 0 } { \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } = n _ { 0 } \beta { \bf P } + n _ { 0 } \alpha { \bf Q } , \alpha = \gamma \cos I , \beta = \gamma \sin I .
$$

Let $E$ be the mass of the planet, $m ^ { \prime }$ the mass of the sun, $\pmb { \mathrm { r } }$ the position vector of the satellite, and $\mathbf { r } ^ { \prime }$ that of the sun, and write $\Delta = \left| \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } - \mathbf { r } \right|$ The equations of motion in the rotating frame $( \mathbf { P } , \mathbf { Q } , \mathbf { R } )$ are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } { \bf r } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 n _ { 0 } \gamma \times \frac { d { \bf r } } { d t } = - \frac { E } { r ^ { 3 } } { \bf r } + E \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \bf r } R , } \\ & { } & { R = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { E } \left( \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - \frac { { \bf r } \cdot { \bf r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { E } | \gamma \times { \bf r } | ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Let $\pmb { \ p }$ be the projection of $\mathbf { r }$ on the mean orbital plane,and $\zeta$ be the elevation of the satellite on this plane； then $\mathbf { r } = \boldsymbol { \mathsf { \pmb { \rho } } } + \boldsymbol { \zeta } \mathbf { R }$ ，and the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \underline { { \mathsf { P } } } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \ : 2 n _ { 0 } \alpha \mathbf { R } \times \frac { d \underline { { \mathsf { P } } } } { d t } + \ : 2 n _ { 0 } \beta \mathbf { P } \frac { d \zeta } { d t } = \ : - \frac { E } { r ^ { 3 } } \mathsf { \pmb { \mathsf { P } } } + E \ : \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathsf { p } } \ : R , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \ : 2 n _ { 0 } \beta \mathbf { P } \cdot \frac { d \mathsf { \pmb { \rho } } } { d t } = \ : - \frac { E } { r ^ { 3 } } \ : \zeta + E \frac { \partial R } { \partial \zeta } . \qquad } \end{array}
$$

We choose a second frame rotating with the constant velocity $- \boldsymbol { n } _ { 0 } \alpha$ relative to the frame $( \mathbf { P } , \mathbf { Q } )$ around the $\mathbf { R }$ -vector; then the equation of motion for $p$ takes the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \mathsf { \pmb { \rho } } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { E } { \rho ^ { 2 } } \mathsf { \pmb { \rho } } + E \pmb { \Phi } ,
$$

where, with ${ \bf r } = \xi { \bf P } + \eta { \bf Q } + \zeta { \bf R } , \mathsf { \pmb { \rho } } = \xi { \bf P } + \eta { \bf Q } ,$

$$
n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } a _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } = E , \qquad n _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } a _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 3 } = m ^ { \prime } + E , \qquad \alpha _ { 0 } = a _ { 0 } / a _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } , \qquad m = n _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } / n _ { 0 } ,
$$

we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle a _ { 0 } \Phi = \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \circ } a _ { 0 } \Omega - \frac { 2 \alpha } { n _ { 0 } a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \bf P } \frac { d \zeta } { d t } \tan I } , } \\ { { \displaystyle a _ { 0 } \Omega = \left( 1 - \frac { E } { m ^ { \prime } + E } \right) m ^ { 2 } \frac { a _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } { \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac 1 \Delta - \frac { { \bf r } \cdot { \bf r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \right) + a _ { 0 } \left( \frac 1 r - \frac 1 { \rho } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac 1 2 ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } ) \tan ^ { 2 } I - \eta \zeta \tan I \right] } . } \end{array}
$$

All terms of these last two equations are of higher order except the first term in $\Omega$ , and the equation of motion for p differs from the corresponding equation in a nearly ideal system by terms of higher order. Musen called the second rotating system almost ideal. It has the same external form as the equation of the disturbed planar motion in an inertial or in an ideal system. Hence the process of integration goes formally along the same lines as in the ideal system,as worked out by Hansen. The number of auxiliary constants,such as Euler's parameters,is reduced and the necessity of a literal expansion of the disturbing function is removed and the development is made purely numerical.

The equation of motion for $\zeta$ is transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \left( \zeta / a _ { 0 } \right) } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { E } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \frac { \zeta } { a _ { 0 } } + n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial \left( a _ { 0 } V \right) } { \partial \left( \zeta / a _ { 0 } \right) } + 2 n _ { 0 } \alpha \frac { d \left( \xi / a _ { 0 } \right) } { d t } \tan { \cal I } , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { a _ { 0 } V = a _ { 0 } \Omega + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { a _ { 0 } } { \rho } \right) ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \zeta } { a _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } . \qquad } \end{array}
$$

The coordinates ${ \mathsf { \pmb \rho } } , \ \pmb \zeta , \ \pmb \psi ;$ ，were used by Brendel (1925c),where $\psi$ is proportional to the areal velocity of the projection of the satellite on its mean orbital plane.The mean orbital plane and the constants of integration are so chosen that the expansions of Brendel's coordinates contain only purely periodic terms. Musen (l967a） pointed out the affinity between Hansen's theory and the theory in Brendel's coordinates. Hansen's lunar theory makes use of the osculating orbital plane as reference.The above equations of Musen resemble Brendel's but contain additional terms which reflect the motion of the almost ideal system relative to the inertial system (Section 10.21).

The difficulty of slow formal convergence of the expansions can be removed if numerical values of the parameters involved, especially the parameter m (Section 11.1),are substituted from the outset. An impor-tant advantage of a purely numerical approach as worked out by Musen is that the terms are computed in large groups at each iteration step. For the expansion of the disturbing function,he generalized Cauchy's numbers (Section 5.11） by introducing Gegenbauer polynomials. Thus the theory leads directly to the numerical expansion of the rectangular coordinates.

Hansen's theory as modified by Musen (l963) has been applied to the motion of the tenth satellite of Jupiter by Musen, Maury,and Charnow (1968).The satellite is far from the primary and the orbit is of high inclination.It was lost since l958.Lemekhova (l96l） studied the orbit by referring to Delaunay’s method. Musen and Maury took into account higher-order terms in the inclination than Lemekhova did.The proximity of Jupiter $\mathbf { x }$ to its predicted position in l967 confirmed the theory and showed that electronic computers can be used to perform analytical expansions. This was carried out by Charnow (l966) and by Charnow, Musen,and Maury (l968), leading to Roemer's re-observation of the satellite,as described by Musen (l968).Recently Lubowe (1970) applied Hansen's method for calculating the oblateness perturbation with short computation time.

# I0.II GROUP PERTURBATIONS

For computing the motion of several asteroids as a group with their mean motions nearly equal to $\scriptstyle \mu _ { 0 }$ ，that is,with their mean motions $\mu = \mu _ { 0 } ( 1 - w )$ with a small parameter ${ \pmb w }$ , it is convenient to develop the solution in powers of w around the mean motion $\scriptstyle \mu _ { 0 }$ and compute the coefficients of various powers of w in advance. Bohlin (1896,1903) expanded all quantities and functions in Hansen's theory in powers of ${ \pmb w }$ around $\scriptstyle \mu _ { 0 }$ .The coefficients depend on $\scriptstyle \mu _ { 0 }$ . For example,

$$
T = T _ { 0 } + w T _ { 1 } + w ^ { 2 } T _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\mathbf { \Omega } _ { 1 } =  - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \mu _ { 0 } \frac { \alpha } { \mu } ( \frac { d T } { d \alpha } ) _ { \frac { \mu } { \alpha } = \mu _ { 0 } } , \qquad T _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 9 } \mu _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \frac { \alpha } { \mu } ( - \frac { 1 } { 2 \mu } \frac { d T } { d \alpha } + \frac { \alpha } { \mu } \frac { d ^ { 2 } T } { d \alpha ^ { 2 } } ) _ { \frac { \mu } { \alpha } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { \alpha _ { 0 } } } ,
$$

$$
U = U _ { 0 } + w U _ { 1 } + w ^ { 2 } U _ { 2 } + \cdots .
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \theta = \mu ( \epsilon - e \sin \epsilon ) - g ^ { \prime } } \\ { = \mu [ w n z + ( 1 - w ) n \delta z ] - n ^ { \prime } \delta z ^ { \prime } ; } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\frac { d \theta } { d g } = \mu [ w + ( 1 - w ) \overline { { { W } } } ] - \frac { d ( n ^ { \prime } \delta z ^ { \prime } ) } { d g } .
$$

The expression $\ w + \left( 1 - w \right) \overline { { \cal W } }$ consists of two parts, one depending on $\pmb \theta$ only and the other containing $\epsilon$ explicitly. Denote the two parts by $( 1 - w ) W _ { 1 }$ and $( 1 - w ) V _ { : }$ ,respectively. We write $\theta = \theta _ { 1 } + \mu ( 1 - w ) \zeta$ ； then,

$$
\frac { d \theta _ { 1 } } { d g } = \mu ( 1 - w ) \overrightarrow { W } , \frac { d \zeta } { d g } = \overleftarrow { V } - \frac { 1 } { \mu ( 1 - w ) } \frac { d ( n ^ { \prime } \ : \delta z ^ { \prime } ) } { d g } ,
$$

where $\overrightarrow { W } _ { 1 }$ is independent of $\epsilon$ Also,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \theta _ { 1 } } { d \epsilon } = \mu ( 1 - w ) \overline { { W } } _ { 1 } ( 1 - e \cos \epsilon ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \zeta } { d \epsilon } = \bar { V } ( 1 - e \cos \epsilon ) - \frac { 1 } { \mu ( 1 - w ) } \frac { d ( n ^ { \prime } \delta z ^ { \prime } ) } { d \epsilon } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
n \ : \delta z = - \frac { w } { 1 - w } n z + \frac { \theta _ { 1 } } { \mu ( 1 - w ) } + \frac { n ^ { \prime } \ : \delta z ^ { \prime } } { \mu ( 1 - w ) } + \zeta .
$$

On the other hand,

$$
T = \frac { \partial W } { \partial \epsilon } + \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } \frac { d \theta _ { 1 } } { d \epsilon } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

can be written

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial \epsilon } + \mu ( 1 - \epsilon ) \overline { { { W } } } _ { 1 } \frac { \partial { W } } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } ( 1 - \epsilon \cos \epsilon ) = T ;
$$

or, puting

$$
W = - \frac { w } { 1 - w } + V + W _ { 1 } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \frac { V } { \varkappa } = \left. T - \mu ( 1 - w ) \overline { { W } } _ { 1 } \frac { \partial V } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } ( 1 - \ell \cos \epsilon ) \right. } \\ { \left. \qquad - \mu ( 1 - w ) \overline { { W } } _ { 1 } \frac { \partial W _ { 1 } } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } ( 1 - \ell \cos ^ { 2 } \epsilon ) \right. } \end{array} 
$$

Thus this expression consists of three parts.For each part Bohlin carried out the expansion in powers of ${ \pmb w }$ ,and published numerical tables for the coeffcients of such expansions to facilitate the computation of the group perturbations.

Put

$$
= 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } , ~ \eta = \frac { e } { 2 } , ~ \kappa ^ { \prime } = 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } } , ~ \eta ^ { \prime } = \frac { e ^ { \prime } } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
j ^ { 2 } = \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { J } { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \phi } { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \phi ^ { \prime } } { 2 } ,
$$

where $\phi , \phi ^ { \prime }$ are the eccentric angles; then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \vartheta = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { J } { 2 } \sin \left( f + \Pi \right) \sin \left( f ^ { \prime } + \Pi ^ { \prime } \right) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle V = f - f ^ { \prime } + \Pi - \Pi ^ { \prime } . } } \end{array}
$$

Then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \left( \frac { \Delta } { a } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { \Delta _ { 0 } } { a } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 } { \alpha } \frac { r } { a } \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \vartheta , } & { \alpha = \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \Delta _ { 0 } } { a } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { r } { a } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 2 } { \alpha } \frac { r } { a } \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \cos V . } & \end{array}
$$

The disturbing function is developed according to Gyldén (1881, 1882) in the form (Section 7.5)

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { \alpha } { \Delta _ { 0 } } = \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } C _ { 0 } ^ { ( 1 ) } + 2 \Big ( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \frac { r } { a } C _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \cos V + 2 \Big ( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } \Big ( \frac { r } { a } \Big ) ^ { 2 } C _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \cos 2 V + \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \displaystyle \Big ( \frac { a } { \Delta _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } = \Big ( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } C _ { 0 } ^ { ( 3 ) } + 2 \Big ( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ) ^ { 4 } \frac { r } { a } C _ { 1 } ^ { ( 3 ) } \cos V + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \begin{array} { c } { { \mathrm {  ~ { \scriptstyle { \cal { C } } } } _ { n } ^ { \prime } = \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { 0 \cdot } \ - \ \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 1 \cdot } \ \cdot \mathrm {  ~ { \scriptstyle { \Lambda } } } + \ \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { - } \mathrm {  ~ { \scriptstyle { \Lambda } } } ^ { \prime } \ \cdot \mathrm {  ~ { \scriptstyle { \Lambda } } } ^ { \prime } + \ \cdot \cdot \gamma , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal { C } } _ { n } ^ { ( 3 ) } = \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { 3 , n } - \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { 3 , n } \lambda + \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 3 , n } \lambda ^ { 2 } \ + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot \gamma , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \mathrm {  ~ \Lambda ~ } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ { { \displaystyle \gamma _ { i } ^ { 1 , n } = \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \ \cdot \ \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 i - 1 ) } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \cdot \ \cdot \ \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 i ) } \ \alpha ^ { n + 2 i + 1 } \beta _ { n + i } ^ { ( 2 i + 1 ) } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \beta _ { n } ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 2 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 n } \phi d \phi } { ( 1 \ - \ \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi ) ^ { i / 2 } } , \quad \ \lambda = 1 \ - \ \left( \frac { r } { a } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array} } } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \vartheta _ { i } ^ { m , n } = ( m + 2 n + 4 i ) \gamma _ { i } ^ { m , n } + ( 4 i + 4 ) \gamma _ { i + 1 } ^ { m , n } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \gamma _ { i } ^ { m + 2 , n } = \displaystyle \beta ^ { 2 } \biggl [ \frac { 1 } { m } \vartheta _ { i } ^ { m , n } + \gamma _ { i - 1 } ^ { m + 2 , n } \biggr ] , \qquad \beta ^ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus the final expressions for the disturbing force are expanded in powers of $y = e ^ { \sqrt { - 1 } \epsilon }$ and $\begin{array} { r } { \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } = e ^ { \sqrt { - 1 } \pmb { g } ^ { \prime } } } \end{array}$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { a \Omega = \Re \biggl [ \sum { \cal O } ^ { i } ( n + r , - n + s ) \cdot y ^ { n + r } x ^ { \prime - n + s } \biggr ] , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { a \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } = \Im \biggl [ \sum { \cal P } ^ { i } ( n + r , - n + s ) \cdot y ^ { n + r } x ^ { \prime - n + s } \biggr ] , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { a r \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } = \Re \biggl [ \sum { \cal Q } ^ { i } ( n + r , - n + s ) \cdot y ^ { n + r } x ^ { \prime - n + s } \biggr ] . } \end{array}
$$

These coefficients in their turn are expanded in powers of j $\eta / \kappa ,$ and $\eta ^ { \prime } / \kappa ^ { \prime }$

Von Zeipel (l90la) published the tables of group perturbation for $n ^ { \prime } / n = 1 / 2$ ，that is,for $\mu _ { 0 } = 6 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，the Hecuba group；Bohlin for $n ^ { \prime } / n = 1 / 3$ $\mu _ { 0 } = 9 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，the Hestia group,for which Block (l907）expanded the tables to $7 5 0 ^ { \prime \prime } < \mu _ { 0 } < 9 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ; and later Radyhski (1935) and Wilson (1913） for $n ^ { \prime } / n = 2 / 5$ ， $\mu _ { 0 } = 7 5 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,the Minerva group, for which Commendantov (1935） extended the tables；and Stromberg (1916） for $n ^ { \prime } / n = 2 / 7$ ， $\mu _ { 0 } = 1 0 5 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，the Flora group.Olsson (l900） calculated the expansion in powers of e, $\scriptstyle { e ^ { \prime } }$ ， $\sin ^ { 2 }$ $( I / 2 )$ , and $\pmb { \mu }$ .Leuschner (1910) and his colleagues (1922,1937),and McDonald and Leuschner (1952) published elaborate tables for the motion of asteroids of the Hecuba group.

The motion of the Minerva-group asteroids has been further studied by Behrens (l929,1935),applying the method of Gyldén-Brendel (see Sections 10.18-10.20)．Villemarqué (1928,1928a，1929）studied the motion of the asteroids with mean motions $1 0 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime } < \mu _ { 0 } < 1 1 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,that is, of the Hungaria and the Flora groups by the same method. Osten (1920) discussed the perturbation on such groups of asteroids by Saturn in addition to that by Jupiter. Commendantov (l935,1935a，1935b, 1936,1936a) extended Bohlin's method to the Minerva-group asteroids with mean motion $7 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime } – 8 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$

Further studies on the Hecuba-group asteroids have been made by Neubauer (l938) and Rabe (l937).In particular,Rabe (1942,1943) tried a mixed method of Hansen and Brendel (Brendel's method will be treated in Section 10.21) for a comparative study.

Roure (l939),on the other hand, started with the differential equations in rectangular coordinates,using the combined methods of Hill, Brown,and Andoyer,which will be described in Section l0.33,and introduced Bohlin's idea of group perturbations.

# 10.12 HANSEN'S METHOD APPLIED TO COMETS

Hansen (1856a) devised a method of computing the absolute perturbation of a periodic comet with large eccentricity on the basis of his so-

called partial anomalies, the inferior and the superior, by dividing the orbit into two separate parts. Put

$$
r = a ( 1 - e ) + ( M \sin x + N ) ^ { 2 } ;
$$

then,

$$
\sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { u } { 2 } } = \biggl ( { \frac { M } { \sqrt { 2 a e } } } \sin x + { \frac { N } { \sqrt { 2 a e } } } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $\pmb { u }$ is the eccentric anomaly. Put further

$$
\frac { M } { \sqrt { 2 a e } } = \epsilon \cos \psi , ~ \frac { N } { \sqrt { 2 a e } } = \epsilon \sin \psi .
$$

Let the values of $\boldsymbol { r }$ for $x = \pi / 2$ and $x = 3 \pi / 2$ be $\pmb { r _ { 1 } }$ and $\pmb { r _ { 2 } } .$ , respectively; then,

$$
\displaystyle 1 \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \psi \right) = \sqrt { \frac { r _ { 2 } - a ( 1 - \epsilon ) } { r _ { 1 } - a ( 1 - \epsilon ) } } , \qquad \epsilon = \sqrt { \frac { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } - 2 a ( 1 - \epsilon ) } { 4 a \epsilon } } .
$$

Write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \sqrt { r _ { 2 } - a ( 1 - e ) } = \sqrt { 2 a e } \sin \displaystyle \frac { u _ { 2 } } { 2 } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \sqrt { r _ { 1 } - a ( 1 - e ) } = \sqrt { 2 a e } \sin \displaystyle \frac { u _ { 1 } } { 2 } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\epsilon ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { u _ { 1 } } { 2 } + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { u _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) .
$$

We can express the radius vector $\pmb { r } _ { : }$ ，the true anomaly $f ,$ and time $\scriptstyle t$ as functions of $_ { x }$ ：

$\begin{array} { r l r } {  { r = a ( 1 - e + e \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ) + 2 a \epsilon \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \psi \sin x - a \epsilon \epsilon ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi \cos 2 x } } \\ & { { \mathrm { ~ n ~ } } f = a ( 1 - e - \epsilon ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ) - 2 a \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \psi \sin x } \\ & { } & { + a \epsilon ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi \cos 2 , } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ s ~ } f = 2 a \epsilon \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ( \cos \psi \sin x + \sin \psi ) } \\ & { } & { \times ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \psi \sin x - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi \sin ^ { 2 } x ) ^ { 1 / } } \end{array}$ ，  
rsix，  
r co2，  
$\begin{array} { r } { n d t = \frac { r } { a } \cdot 2 \epsilon \cos \psi \cos x \cdot d x \phantom { \cos \psi \sin x \cdot d } } \\ { \div ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \psi \sin x - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi \sin ^ { 2 } x ) \phantom { \cos \psi \cdot \cos \psi \cos x } } \end{array}$

Next from the analogy in form of

$$
r = a ( 1 - e \cos u ) \quad { \mathrm { a n d } } \quad { \frac { 1 } { r } } = { \frac { 1 + e \cos f } { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } ,
$$

we put

$$
\cos ^ { 2 } { \frac { f } { 2 } } = { \frac { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { 2 e } } ( M ^ { \prime } \sin x + N ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
M ^ { \prime } \sqrt { \frac { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { 2 e } } = \epsilon ^ { \prime } \cos \psi ^ { \prime } , \qquad N ^ { \prime } \sqrt { \frac { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { 2 e } } = - \epsilon ^ { \prime } \sin \psi ^ { \prime } ,
$$

or,

$$
\tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \psi ^ { \prime } \right) = \sqrt { \frac { r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } } . \frac { a ( 1 + e ) - r _ { 1 } } { a ( 1 + e ) - r _ { 2 } } } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } = \left( 1 - \epsilon \right) \frac { a ( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } ) ( 1 + \epsilon ) - 2 r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { 4 e r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } } \\ { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { f _ { 1 } } { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { f _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) , } \end{array}
$$

where $f _ { 1 }$ and $f _ { 2 }$ are the true anomalies corresponding to the radius vectors $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ and $r _ { 2 }$ Hence,

$\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { - e ^ { 2 } } { r } = 1 - e + e \epsilon ^ { 2 } + e \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } - 2 e \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin 2 \psi ^ { \prime } \sin x } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \quad \quad - e \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } \cos 2 ; } \\ & { } & { \quad \cos f = \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } - 1 - 2 \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin 2 \psi ^ { \prime } \sin x } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad - \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } \cos 2 ; } \\ & { } & { \quad \sin f = 2 \epsilon ^ { \prime } ( \cos \psi ^ { \prime } \sin x - \sin \psi ^ { \prime } ) } \\ & { } & { \quad \times ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin 2 \psi ^ { \prime } \sin x - \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } \sin ^ { 2 } x ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \end{array}$ x，x，2，  
$\begin{array} { r l } & { { n d t = - \frac { r _ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \cdot 2 \epsilon ^ { \prime } \cos \psi ^ { \prime } \cos x \cdot d x } } \\ & { ~ \mathrm { ~ } \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { ~ \mathrm { ~ } \quad \quad \quad \quad \div ~ ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \sin 2 \psi ^ { \prime } \sin x - \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \psi ^ { \prime } \sin ^ { 2 } x ) ^ { 1 / 2 } . }  \end{array}$

We divide the whole circumference of the ellipse into four parts :

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { - \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { x = 2 y - \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { 0 < y < \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \displaystyle \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { x = 2 y - \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } < y < \pi , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \displaystyle \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { x = 2 y + \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { \pi < y < \displaystyle \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } < x < \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { x = 2 y + \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ } } & { { \displaystyle \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } < y < 2 \pi . } } \end{array}
$$

The question is now to choose $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ and $r _ { 2 }$ so that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { ^ { 2 } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } - 2 a ( 1 - e ) } { 4 a e } + \frac { 1 - e } { 4 e } \left[ a ( 1 + e ) \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } \right) - 2 \right] } \\ { = \mathrm { m i n i m u m } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus the minimum is attained for $r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } = 2 \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } }$ ，that is，for $\epsilon ^ { 2 } = \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } , \epsilon ^ { 2 } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \left( u _ { 1 } / 2 \right) = 1 -$ tan $( \psi / 2 )$ .This condition determines the points of division of the whole orbit.We use the inferior partial anomaly for the arc $- \pi / 2 < x < \pi / 2$ and the superior partial anomaly for the arc $\pi / 2 < x < 3 \pi / 2$ ,and use the formulas l and 4,or 2 and 3, respectively.

Backlund (1898a) applied this theory of Hansen's to Encke's comet in a series of papers,after having tried to improve the theory (1886,1886a, 1892a).

Gravelius (l889) expressed Hansen's functions in terms of elliptic functions,in accordance with Halphen (1886). If we define

$$
u = \int _ { \wp _ { u } } ^ { \infty } { \frac { d s } { \sqrt { 4 ( s - e _ { 1 } ) ( s - e _ { 2 } ) ( s - e _ { 3 } ) } } } ,
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } ) \sigma ^ { 2 } u + \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } u = \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } u , \qquad ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } ) \sigma ^ { 2 } u + \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } u = \sigma _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } u , } } \\ { { ( e _ { 2 } - e _ { 3 } ) \sigma ^ { 2 } u + \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } u = \sigma _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } u , } } \\ { { ( e _ { 2 } - e _ { 3 } ) \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } u + ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } ) \sigma _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } u = ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } ) \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } u . } } \end{array}
$$

Hansen's $\epsilon$ is expressed by

$$
\sin { \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } } = { \sqrt { e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } } } { \frac { \sigma u } { \sigma _ { 3 } u } } , \qquad \cos { \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } } = { \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } u } } ,
$$

where the determinations of $\epsilon$ and $\pmb { u }$ are so chosen that $\epsilon$ and $\pmb { u }$ vanish simultaneously. We have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \sin \epsilon = 2 \sqrt { e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } } \frac { \sigma u \sigma _ { 1 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } u \sigma _ { 3 } u } , \qquad \cos \epsilon = 2 \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } u } - 1 , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { d \epsilon = 2 \sqrt { e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } } \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } u } d u . } \end{array}
$$

Putting

$$
{ \frac { e _ { 2 } - e _ { 3 } } { e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } } } = { \frac { 2 e } { 1 + e } } = k ^ { 2 } , { \frac { e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } } { e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } } } = 1 - k ^ { 2 } = k ^ { \prime 2 } = { \frac { 1 - e } { 1 + e } } ,
$$

Gravelius obtained

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { r } { a } = \frac { 2 k ^ { \prime 2 } } { 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } } \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } u } , } \\ { \displaystyle d g = \frac { 4 } { 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } } k ^ { \prime 3 } \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } ^ { 3 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } u } d u , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\frac { r } { a } \sin f = \frac { 4 k ^ { \prime 2 } } { 1 - k ^ { \prime 4 } } \frac { d \chi } { d u } , \frac { r } { a } \cos f = \frac { 4 k ^ { \prime 2 } } { 1 - k ^ { \prime 4 } } - 2 \frac { k ^ { \prime 2 } } { k ^ { 2 } } x ^ { 2 } ( u ) ,
$$

where

$$
\chi ( u ) = \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } u } { \sigma _ { 2 } u } \cdot
$$

The function $x$ must satisfy

$$
\left( { \frac { d \chi } { d u } } \right) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - \chi ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - k ^ { \prime 2 } \chi ^ { 2 } ) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \chi } { d u ^ { 2 } } = ( 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } ) \chi - 2 k ^ { \prime 2 } \chi ^ { 3 } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \chi ^ { 2 } } { d u ^ { 2 } } + 2 - 4 ( 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } ) \chi ^ { 2 } + 6 k ^ { \prime 2 } \chi ^ { 4 } = 0 .
$$

In general,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 n } \chi } { d u ^ { 2 n } } = A _ { 0 } ^ { ( n ) } \chi + A _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) } \chi ^ { 3 } + \cdots + A _ { n } ^ { ( n ) } \chi ^ { 2 n + 1 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 n } \chi ^ { 2 } } { d u ^ { 2 n } } = B _ { 0 } ^ { ( n ) } + B _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) } \chi ^ { 2 } + \cdots + B _ { n + 1 } ^ { ( n ) } \chi ^ { 2 n + 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathfrak { I } _ { r } ^ { ( n + 1 ) } = ( 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } ) ( 2 r + 1 ) ^ { 2 } A _ { \tau } ^ { ( n ) } - k ^ { \prime 2 } 2 r ( 2 r - 1 ) A _ { \tau - 1 } ^ { ( n ) } } \\ & { \qquad - ( 2 r + 2 ) ( 2 r + 3 ) A _ { \tau } ^ { ( n ) } } \\ & { \mathfrak { I } _ { r } ^ { ( n + 1 ) } = 4 ( 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } ) r ^ { 2 } B _ { \tau } ^ { ( n ) } - 2 k ^ { \prime 2 } ( r - 1 ) ( 2 r - 3 ) B _ { \tau - 1 } ^ { ( n ) } } \\ & { \qquad - 2 ( 2 r + 1 ) ( r + 1 ) B _ { \tau } ^ { ( n ) } } \end{array}
$$

Thus any odd power, $2 n + 1 , \mathsf { o f } \chi ( u )$ can be expressed as a linear function of $x ( u )$ and its even-order derivatives of order not higher than $2 n$ Any even power, $2 n + 2$ ,of $\chi ( u )$ can be expressed as a linear function of $\chi ^ { 2 } ( u )$ and its even-order derivatives of order not higher than $2 n$ . This property facilitates the computation of the function $( \sigma _ { 3 } u ) / ( \sigma _ { 2 } u )$ . According to Halphen we have

$$
\begin{array} { c }  { \displaystyle { \frac { d \omega } { \pi } k ^ { \prime } \chi ( u ) = 1 + 4 \cdot \sum _ { r } ( - 1 ) ^ { r } \frac { q ^ { r } } { 1 + q ^ { 2 r } } \cos { \frac { r \pi u } { \omega } } , } } \\  { \displaystyle { \left[ \frac { 2 \omega } { \pi } k ^ { \prime } \chi ( u ) \right] ^ { 2 } = 1 - 8 \cdot \sum _ { r } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { r } q ^ { 2 r } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 r } } - 8 \cdot \sum _ { r } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { r } r q ^ { r } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 r } } \cos { \frac { r \pi u } { \omega } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { q = \left( \frac { r } { 2 } \right) + 2 \left( \frac { r } { 2 } \right) ^ { 5 } + 1 5 \left( \frac { r } { 2 } \right) ^ { 9 } + 1 5 0 \left( \frac { r } { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ { r = ( 1 - \sqrt { k ^ { \prime } } ) / ( 1 + \sqrt { k ^ { \prime } } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Let

$$
u = \omega v / \pi ,
$$

then

$$
g = v + 4 { \cdot } \sum _ { r } ( - 1 ) ^ { r } \bigg \{ \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } q ^ { r } } { 1 + q ^ { 2 r } } \left[ 1 + \left( \frac { \pi } { \omega } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { 1 + k ^ { \prime 2 } } \right] \bigg \} \sin r v .
$$

Gravelius says that Hansen's partial anomaly in Hansen's theory of comets is the substitute of an elliptic integral of the first kind for the mean anomaly.

Nacozy (1969） and Skripnichenko (l970） applied the method to Comet Encke and compared these results with the result of numerical integration.

# 10.13OPPOLZER'S LUNAR THEORY

Oppolzer (1886, l888) adapted Hansen's idea to his lunar theory by referring the motion to the moving osculating orbital plane. The differential equations of motion in Oppolzer's lunar theory are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \left( \mu + \mu ^ { \prime } \right) \displaystyle \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } = X , \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \left( \mu + \mu ^ { \prime } \right) \displaystyle \frac { y } { r ^ { 3 } } = Y , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \left( \mu + \mu ^ { \prime } \right) \displaystyle \frac { z } { r ^ { 3 } } = Z , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { \mu }$ and $\mu ^ { \prime }$ are constants. Oppolzer took the coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x _ { 0 } = ( 1 + \gamma ) x , ~ y _ { 0 } = ( 1 + \gamma ) y , ~ z _ { 0 } = ( 1 + \gamma ) z , } } \\ { { { \displaystyle r _ { 0 } = ( 1 + \gamma ) \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } - z ^ { 2 } } = ( 1 + \gamma ) ( r ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } , ~ } } & { { ~ r ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = ( r ) ^ { 2 } \Bigl [ 1 + \Bigl ( \frac { z _ { 0 } } { r _ { 0 } } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } \Bigr ] , } } \end{array}
$$

and the disturbing force with the components

$$

$$

Weiler (l872) proposed to adopt a moving ellipse of fixed shape as the intermediary orbit, following Gyldén's idea,which will be discussed in

Section 10.18.An orbit which is adopted as the first approximate orbit for the actual orbit is called an intermediary orbit.The intermediary orbit adopted in the method of the variation of elements is a Keplerian ellipse having constant values of those elements.Weiler started with Hansen's equations

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { N ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { m } \frac { \partial U } { \partial r } ,
$$$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \overline { { { d t ^ { 2 } } } } = \displaystyle \frac { - \overline { { { r ^ { 3 } } } } + \overline { { { m } } } \overline { { { \partial r } } } } { \overline { { { \partial } } } \overline { { { r } } } } , } } \\  { { \overline { { { ( 1 + m ) ^ { 2 } } } } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { k m m _ { 1 } } { 1 + m } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 r _ { 1 } \cos { \cal H } } } } } \\ { { { } + \displaystyle \frac { k m _ { 1 } } { 1 + m } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + 2 m r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \cos { \cal H } } } } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\frac { d N } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { m } \frac { \partial U } { \partial v } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d v } { d t } + \cos I \frac { d \vartheta } { d t } = \frac { N } { r ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
f = v + \int \cos I \frac { d \vartheta } { d t } d t ,
$$

where $N$ is the variable areal velocity in the orbital plane, $\vartheta$ the longitude of the node, $^ { \pmb { v } }$ the argument of latitude, $f$ the true anomaly,and $\boldsymbol { U }$ the force function. Also,he wrote

$$
\frac { \not p ^ { 2 } } { \not p _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } - 1 = 2 \frac { \not p } { r } ( k \cos f + h \sin f ) ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { d k } { d t } } \cos f + { \frac { d h } { d t } } \sin f = 0 .
$$

For $k$ and $\pmb { h }$ he obtained

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle - m p _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \frac { d k } { d t } = \frac { r } { \beta } \sin f . \bigg ( r \frac { \partial U } { \partial r } + q + P \bigg ) , } \\ { \displaystyle - m p _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \frac { d h } { d t } = \frac { r } { \beta } \cos f . \bigg ( r \frac { \partial U } { \partial r } + q + P \bigg ) , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
P = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \phi } { r } \left( 1 + 2 \frac { \phi _ { 0 } } { \phi } \right) ( \phi - \phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 }
$$

is a quantity of the second order. He derived $q$ from

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d q } { d t } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial r } \frac { d r } { d t } + \frac { \partial U } { \partial H } \frac { d H } { d \upsilon } \frac { N } { r ^ { 2 } } \cdot
$$

Radau (l88l） discussed Oppolzer's theory on the basis of Hansen's. He considered equations of the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + K ^ { 2 } y + L + i M y ^ { 2 } + i ^ { 2 } N y ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0 ,
$$

where $K , L , M , N$ are constants and $_ { i }$ is a small coefficient.

# NEWCOMB'S THEORY

# 10.14LAPLACE's THEORY

Newcomb's theory on the motion of Uranus and Neptune is a modified form of Laplace's classical theory (1878, 1878a). Following Hansen, Newcomb took the eccentric anomaly as the independent variable, because the series converge more rapidly,although merely in a formal sense.

Refer the motion to the moving osculating orbital plane. Let

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x = r \cos L , \qquad y = r \sin L . } \end{array}
$$

The equations of motion are the same as equations (45)- here renumbered -

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r \Big ( \frac { d L } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { r ^ { 2 } } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , } \\ { r ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } L } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 r \frac { d r } { d t } \frac { d L } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( r ^ { 2 } \frac { d L } { d t } \right) = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial L } . } \end{array}
$$

The $z$ coordinate is referred to a fixed orbital plane at the initial epoch

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) z } { r ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } .
$$

Multiply the frst two of the equations (43) and the equation (72c) by $2 \ d x / d t , 2 \ d y / d t ,$ and $2 \ d z / d t$ ,respectively,and add. After integration, we obtain the energy intgral

$$
\left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d z } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } - { \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { r } } = 2 k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \int d ^ { \prime } \Omega + K ,
$$

where $\pmb { K }$ is a constant and

$$
d ^ { \prime } \Omega = \left( \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial x } \frac { d x } { d t } + \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial y } \frac { d y } { d t } + \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } \frac { d z } { d t } \right) d t ,
$$

a differential due to the variation of only the disturbed quantities. Multiply the first two of equations (43) and equation (72c) by $x , y$ and $z$ ， respectively,and add. Then，referring to the energy integral， we obtain

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { r } = 2 K + 2 k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \bigg ( r \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } + 2 \int d ^ { \prime } \Omega \bigg ) .
$$

Laplace (1878) and Pontécoulant (l846) took the equations of motion in polar coordinates

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) \bigg ( { \frac { 1 } { r } } - { \frac { 1 } { a } } \bigg ) = r { \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } } + 2 \Omega - 2 n ^ { \prime } \int { \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \epsilon ^ { \prime } } } d t ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { d v } { d t } } - { \frac { h ( 1 + s ^ { 2 } ) } { r ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { 1 + s ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } \int { \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial v } } d t ,
$$

$$
\frac { l ^ { 2 } } { l ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { r s } { \sqrt { 1 + s ^ { 2 } } } \right) + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { r ^ { 3 } } \frac { r s } { \sqrt { 1 + s ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { \sqrt { 1 + s ^ { 2 } } } { r } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial s } + \frac { s } { \sqrt { 1 + s ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial s }
$$

where $^ { a }$ and $\boldsymbol { h }$ are constants, $s = z / \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } }$ and $v$ is the angle between the projection of $\boldsymbol { r }$ on the $( x , y )$ -plane and the $_ { x }$ -axis. If $\Omega = 0$ ，then $^ { a }$ reduces to the semimajor axis and $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } h$ to the angular velocity in the $( x , y )$ -plane.

With $\boldsymbol { v }$ as the independent variable Laplace (1878a) transformed the equations for his lunar theory to

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { 2 u _ { 1 } } { | v ^ { 2 } } + u _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { h ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } + \frac { s } { h ^ { 2 } u _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial s } - \frac { 1 } { h ^ { 2 } u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { d u _ { 1 } } { d v } \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } - \frac { 2 } { h ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } u _ { 1 } } { d v ^ { 2 } } + u _ { 1 } \right) \int \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } . } \\ & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d v ^ { 2 } } + s = \frac { s } { h ^ { 2 } u _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 + s ^ { 2 } } { h ^ { 2 } u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial s } - \frac { 1 } { h ^ { 2 } u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { d s } { d v } \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } - \frac { 2 } { h ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d v ^ { 2 } } + s \right) \int \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } } \\ & { } & { \frac { d t } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { h u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { h ^ { 2 } } \int \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } \frac { d v } { u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $1 / u _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 }$ is the projection of $\boldsymbol { r }$ on the $( x , y )$ -plane and

$$
F = \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) } { r } + \Omega .
$$

By working from (72a), (72b), (72c) we write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r = r _ { 0 } + \delta _ { 1 } r + \delta _ { 2 } r + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \quad L = L _ { 0 } + \delta _ { 1 } L + \delta _ { 2 } L + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \approx x = x _ { 0 } + \delta _ { 1 } x + \delta _ { 2 } x + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}

where $\boldsymbol { \alpha }$ denotes the sine of the latitude. Then, for the frst-order perturbation,we obtain

$$

where $O ( 2 )$ denotes the terms of the second and higher orders.Eliminating $\pmb { r _ { 0 } } \ \pmb { \delta _ { 1 } } \pmb { r } / r _ { 0 } ^ { 3 }$ from these equations, we obtain

$$
\overline { { { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } \frac { d \delta _ { 1 } L } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( 2 r _ { 0 } \frac { d \delta _ { 1 } r } { d t } + \delta _ { 1 } r \frac { d r _ { 0 } } { d t } \right) - 3 \int d ^ { \prime } \Omega - 2 r _ { 0 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r _ { 0 } } + { \cal O } ( 2 )
$$

Laplace attmpted to solve these equations for $\delta _ { \tt I } r$ and $\delta _ { 1 } L$ together with the third equation:

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r _ { 0 } \ : \delta _ { 1 } z ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 \ : + \ : m ) r _ { 0 } \ : \delta _ { 1 } z } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } .
$$

At first he expanded $1 / r _ { 0 } ^ { 3 }$ in a series of the mean anomaly $M .$ The equation for $\delta _ { \mathbf { 1 } } r$ becomes

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { { } ^ { 2 } \left( r _ { 0 } \ : \delta _ { 1 } r \right) } { d t ^ { 2 } } \ : + n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } r _ { 0 } \ : \delta _ { 1 } r = 2 \int d ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \ : + \ : r _ { 0 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r _ { 0 } } } \\ { \displaystyle - \ : 3 n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } a _ { 0 } r _ { 0 } \ : \delta _ { 1 } r ( \ell \cos { \cal M } \ : + \ : e ^ { 2 } \cos { 2 { \cal M } } \ : + . } \end{array}
$$

A term of the form $\pmb { \alpha }$ cos $( \nu t + N )$ gives rise to a term

$$
\frac { \alpha } { n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \nu ^ { 2 } } \cos \left( \nu t + N \right)
$$

in $\pmb { r _ { 0 } } \delta _ { \mathbf { 1 } } t ,$ if $\alpha , \nu , ( n _ { 0 } \neq \nu )$ ,and $\boldsymbol { x }$ are supposed to be constants. If $\nu = n _ { 0 }$ then we obtain a secular term

$$
{ \frac { \alpha t } { 2 n _ { 0 } } } \sin { ( \nu t + N ) } .
$$

These secular terms are considered to be included in the expressons for the elements.Then $\alpha , \nu ,$ and $N$ become slowly varying quantities and we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \cos \left( \nu t + N \right) d t = \frac { \alpha } { \nu } \sin \left( \nu t + N \right) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \sin \nu t \cdot \left[ - \frac { 1 } { \nu ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ( \alpha \sin N ) } { d t } - \frac { 1 } { \nu ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( \alpha \cos N ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \cdots \right] } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \cos \nu t \cdot \left[ \frac { 1 } { \nu ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ( \alpha \cos N ) } { d t } - \frac { 1 } { \nu ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( \alpha \sin N ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \cdots \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Hence a term $\pmb { \alpha }$ cos $( \nu t + N )$ on the right-hand side of the equation (73a) for $\delta _ { \mathfrak { I } } r$ gives, after integration, a group of terms

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { \alpha } { ; \displaystyle - \nu ^ { 2 } } \cos ( \nu t + N ) + \cos \nu t \cdot \displaystyle [ \frac { 2 \nu } { ( n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ( \alpha \sin N ) } { d t } + \cdots ] } } \\   -
