\sin \nu t \cdot \displaystyle [ \frac { 2 \nu } { ( n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ( \alpha \cos N ) } { d t } + \cdot \cdot \frac { \displaystyle ( \frac { 2 \nu } { \mathrm { e } } ) ^ { 2 } } { d t } ] ( \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { { n } } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { { n } } \overline { n } \overline { { n } } \overline { n } \overline { { n } } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline { n } \overline  \end{array}
$$

If $n _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \nu ^ { 2 }$ is small, then the corresponding term becomes very large. Laplace included this effect by adding the long-period perturbation

$$
\frac { 3 } { \sqrt { a } \sqrt { 1 + m } } \int d ^ { \prime } \Omega
$$

to the mean motion $\pmb { n _ { 0 } }$ fr the elliptic motion in $M = \int n _ { 0 } d t + \epsilon _ { 0 } - \varpi _ { 0 } .$

Laplace's theory was applied to the motion of the moon by Plana (1832) and Damoiseaux (1827). These applications of Laplace's theory were in competition with the theories of Pontecoulant (1846), Poisson (1835),and Lubbock (1831,1832,1834).

# 10.15NEWCOMB'S EQUATIONS OF MOTION

Newcomb's theory on the motion of the four inner planets and of Uranus and Neptune is a modified form of Laplace's theory for simplifying the computation of higher-order perturbations, so that the successive approximations can be carried out quite automatically by repeating similar processes. Newcomb at first published his theory of Neptune (1865)and then of Uranus (l874) and the Tables of Uranus (l898b) and of Neptune (1898c).He began to work on the periodic inequalities (1891), the secular variation (1895a),and the long-period inequalities (1895b) of the four inner planets and finally published the Tables for the motion (1898a).All these Tables are now in practical use for predicting those positions in nautical almanacs each year.

The instantaneous orbital plane containing the sun, the planet, and the velocity vector is taken as the fundamental plane; it rotates around the radius vector. Denote by $v$ the longitude measured on the instantaneous orbital plane from a fixed point on that plane, by v the angular distance of the radius vector from the intersection of the instantaneous orbital plane and that of the disturbing planet,by $\mathbf { v } ^ { \prime }$ the corresponding quantity for the disturbing planet, and by $\gamma$ the mutual inclination of the two orbital planes.Then the disturbing function is

$$
R = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos H } } - \frac { m ^ { \prime } r } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \cos H ,
$$

where cos H = cos V cos $\mathbf { v ^ { \prime } } +$ sin v sin $\mathbf { v } ^ { \prime }$ cos y. Newcomb wrote

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rho = \log r \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \rho ^ { \prime } = \log r ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

Then $\pmb { R }$ becomes a function of $\mathbf { v } , \ \mathbf { v ^ { \prime } } , \ \rho , \ \rho ^ { \prime }$ and $\gamma$ ，The equations of motion are the same as those of Laplace (72):

$$
\begin{array} { r } { r \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d v } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { \mu ( 1 + m ) } { r } = \mu \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } , } \\ { r ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 r \frac { d r } { d t } \frac { d v } { d t } = \mu \frac { \partial R } { \partial \bf v } . } \end{array}
$$

Multiply these two equations by $2 \ d _ { \rho } / d t$ and 2 du/dt,respectively,and add the result. We obtain, after integration, the Laplace integral

$$
\Big ( \frac { d r } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d v } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 2 \mu ( 1 + m ) } { r } = 2 \mu \Big ( C + \int D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R d t \Big ) ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } \frac { d \rho } { d t } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } } \frac { d v } { d t } = D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R ,
$$

and $c$ is the integration constant. Adding this equation to the first equation of (74),we obtain

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ^ { 2 } ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - { \frac { \mu ( 1 + m ) } { r } } = \mu \bigg ( 2 C + 2 \int D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R d t + { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } } \bigg ) .
$$

Consider an $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 0 }$ that satisfies

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { \mu ( 1 + m ) } { r _ { 0 } } = 2 \mu C .
$$

Subtract this equation from (76); then,

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ^ { 2 } - r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - \mu ( 1 + m ) \bigg ( { \frac { 1 } { r } } - { \frac { 1 } { r _ { 0 } } } \bigg ) = \mu \bigg ( 2 \int D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R d t + { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } } \bigg ) .
$$

Put $\rho _ { 0 } = \log r _ { 0 } , \delta \rho = \rho - \rho _ { 0 } $ , and expand in powers of $\delta _ { P }$ ; then, with $\mu ^ { \prime } = \mu ( 1 + m )$ , we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \big ( r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho \big ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { \prime } } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } \big ( r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho \big ) = \mu \bigg ( 2 \int D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R d t + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } \bigg ) } } } \\ { { - \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \big ( r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho ^ { 2 } \big ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { \prime } } { 2 r _ { 0 } } \sigma _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } \equiv \mu Q . } } \end{array}
$$

This equation gives the perturbation of the radius vector.

Suppose that the first approximation to δv, $\delta \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\delta \rho , \delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ is known, and consider how to compute the second approximation. Denote by $\lambda$ the mean angular distance of the planet from the node, that is, the mean value of $\mathbf { v }$ ,by $\pmb { \omega }$ the angle between the node and the perihelion, and by $g$ the mean anomaly. Write $\omega = \lambda - g ,$ $\omega ^ { \prime } = \lambda ^ { \prime } - g ^ { \prime }$ ， ${ \mathfrak { v } } = \log a ,$ ${ \mathfrak { v } } ^ { \prime } = \log a ^ { \prime }$ , in Newcomb's notation. We know that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { { \mathbf { v } } = \lambda + F ( g ) , \qquad { \mathbf { v } } ^ { \prime } = \lambda ^ { \prime } + F ^ { \prime } ( g ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ { \rho = \mathfrak { v } + \phi ( g ) , \qquad \rho ^ { \prime } = \mathfrak { v } ^ { \prime } + \phi ^ { \prime } ( g ^ { \prime } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { F }$ is the equation of center, and $\phi$ is the part of $\pmb { \rho }$ depending on the eccentricity of an elliptic motion. Then the disturbing function $R ( \mathbf { v } , \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime } , \boldsymbol { \rho } , \boldsymbol { \rho } ^ { \prime } , \boldsymbol { \gamma } )$ takes the form $R ( \lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime } , v , v ^ { \prime } , \gamma )$ . In general $R$ has the form

$$
R = \sum { \frac { m ^ { \prime } h } { a _ { 1 } } } \cos \it { N } ; \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad N = i ^ { \prime } \lambda ^ { \prime } + i \lambda + j ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime } + j \omega ,
$$

where ${ \pmb a } _ { 1 }$ is the mean distance of the outer planet and $\pmb { h }$ is a function of $\begin{array} { r } { e , e ^ { \prime } , \alpha = a / a _ { 1 } , } \end{array}$ and $\gamma$ .Newcomb (1891,1895c) worked out the expansions by using Newcomb's operators of Section 7.1l for his theory of the inner planets.Write $\lambda = g + \omega _ { : }$ $\lambda ^ { \prime } = l ^ { \prime } + \omega ^ { \prime }$ ; then,

$$
N = i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } + i g + j ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime } + ( i + i ^ { \prime } + j ) \omega .
$$

Let the first-order part of $Q$ be $Q _ { 0 }$ . Then,

$$
Q _ { 0 } = 2 \int D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R _ { 0 } d t + \frac { \partial R _ { 0 } } { \partial \rho _ { 0 } } .
$$

Since

$$
D _ { t } ^ { \prime } R = - \frac { m ^ { \prime } i h n } { a _ { 1 } } \sin N ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\int { \cal D } _ { t } ^ { \prime } R d t = \frac { m ^ { \prime } i h \nu } { a _ { 1 } } \cos { \cal N } , \qquad \nu = \frac { n } { i ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } } \frac { \ d n } { \ d t + i n } .
$$

Hence,

$$
Q _ { 0 } = { \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { a _ { 1 } } } [ k _ { c } \cos N _ { 1 } + k _ { s } \sin N _ { 1 } ] , \qquad N _ { 1 } = i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } + i g ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { k _ { c } = \displaystyle \sum k \cos { [ j ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime } + ( i ^ { \prime } + i + j ) \omega ] } , } \\ & { k _ { s } = \displaystyle \sum k \sin { [ j ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime } + ( i ^ { \prime } + i + j ) \omega ] } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus $\pmb { R }$ becomes a known function of $t _ { : }$ ,and hence $Q$ ,the right-hand side of (77),is a known function of $\pmb { t } .$

Let the two independent solutions of (77） for $Q = 0$ be $\pmb { x _ { 1 } }$ and $\pmb { x _ { 2 } }$ Then the solution of the nonhomogeneous equation with $Q \neq 0$ is

$$
r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho = \frac { \mu } { x _ { 1 } \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } - x _ { 2 } \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } } \bigg [ x _ { 2 } \int x _ { 1 } Q d t - x _ { 1 } \int x _ { 2 } Q d t \bigg ] .
$$

Suppose that $\pmb { x _ { 1 } }$ and $\pmb { x _ { 2 } }$ are the two rectangular coordinates. Then, for an elliptic motion with $Q = 0$ ,we have

$$
x _ { 1 } \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } - x _ { 2 } \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = \sqrt { a \mu ( 1 + m ) } \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu ( 1 + m ) \sqrt { 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } { a \pi } .
$$

Put $x _ { 1 } = a \xi$ ， $x _ { 2 } = a \eta \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } }$ ; then $\pmb { \xi }$ and $\pmb { \eta }$ ，being functions of the eccentricity and the mean anomaly,are expanded in Fourier series with multiples of the mean anomaly as arguments. Then

$$
r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho = \frac { a ^ { 3 } n } { 1 + m } \left[ \eta \int \xi Q d t - \xi \int \eta Q d t \right]
$$

Denote by $\pmb { r _ { 1 } }$ the value of $\pmb { r _ { 0 } }$ when the mean distance of the planet is put equal to unity; $\pmb { r _ { 1 } }$ is a function of the eccentricity and the mean anomaly, similarly to $\xi$ and $\pmb { \eta }$ .Let $\pmb { u }$ and $v$ be respectively the eccentric and the true anomaly. Obviously,

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \xi = \cos u - e = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j _ { i } } \cos i g , } } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \eta = \sin u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { q _ { i } } \sin i g } } } & { { ( i = - \infty , . . . , + \infty ) . } } \end{array}
$$

${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \mathfrak { i } }$ and $\pmb { q _ { i } }$ can be expanded in powers of the eccentricity:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \tan \beta _ { - i } = \displaystyle \frac { i ^ { i - 2 } e ^ { i - 1 } } { ( i - 1 ) ! 2 ^ { i - 1 } } [ 1 - ( { \displaystyle \frac { i + 2 } { 1 } } ) \displaystyle \frac { i e ^ { 2 } } { 2 ^ { 2 } ( i + 1 ) }  } } \\ { {  + ( { \displaystyle \frac { i + 4 } { 2 } } ) \displaystyle \frac { i ^ { 3 } e ^ { 4 } } { 2 ^ { 4 } ( i + 1 ) ( i + 2 ) ( i + 3 ) } - \cdot \cdot \cdot  } } \\ { {   q _ { i } = \displaystyle \frac { i } { e } \int \phi _ { i } d e . }  }  \end{array}
$$

Substituting these expressions and (79) in the equation (80) for $\delta \rho ,$ we come to integrate a series consisting of terms of the form sis $( N _ { 1 } \pm i g )$ Denote the coefficient of $\pmb { t }$ in $N$ by $\pmb { \mu }$ ； then the coefficient of $\pmb { t }$ in the argument of this term is $\mu + i n$ Put $\nu _ { i } = n / ( \mu + i n )$ ; then the solution of (80) is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \hat { \delta } \rho = \frac 1 8 \frac { m ^ { \prime } a r _ { 1 } ^ { - 2 } } { a _ { 1 } ( 1 + m ) } ^ { - \infty \cdots + \infty } ( \gamma _ { \imath } q _ { i - \imath } + \gamma _ { i - \imath } q _ { i } ) ( \nu _ { \imath } - \nu _ { \imath - i } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \quad \times \ [ k _ { c } \cos { ( N _ { 1 } + u g ) } + k _ { s } \sin { ( N _ { 1 } + u g ) } ] _ { \jmath } } } \end{array}
$$

where the sum is to be taken over $u = i + j , i - j , - i + j , - i - j .$ In the summation those terms with such combinations of $_ i$ and $j$ that make the corresponding coefficients of $t$ in the arguments vanish are excluded. These are secular and are treated separately. Denote such terms in $Q$ by $( m ^ { \prime } / a _ { 1 } ) ( k _ { c } ^ { ( u ) } \cos u g + k _ { s } ^ { ( u ) } \sin u g )$ ; then the secular terms are

$$
r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } a n t } { a _ { 1 } ( 1 + m ) } \left[ \eta \sum _ { u } \rlap / { p } _ { u } k _ { c } ^ { ( u ) } - \xi \sum _ { u } q _ { u } k _ { s } ^ { ( u ) } \right] .
$$

These two parts (8l) and (8la) constitute the second approximation we required.

In the following approximation, the latter class of terms give rise to terms in $Q$ with the factor $\pmb { t }$ outside the sine or cosine of the argument. Denote such a term by

$$
Q = { \frac { m ^ { \prime } n t } { a _ { 1 } } } ( k _ { c } \cos N + k _ { s } \sin N ) .
$$

Write the coefficient of $\pmb { t }$ in $\pmb { N }$ by $\mu ^ { \prime }$ ,and let

$$
\nu _ { i } = \frac { n } { \mu ^ { \prime } + i n } , \qquad \nu _ { i } ^ { 2 } k _ { s } + \nu _ { i } k _ { c } n t = c _ { i } , \qquad \nu _ { i } ^ { 2 } k _ { c } - \nu _ { i } k _ { s } n t = s _ { i } .
$$

$$
\delta \rho = \frac { 1 } { 8 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } a r _ { 1 } ^ { - 2 } } { a _ { 1 } ( 1 + m ) } et { } { ' } \sum _ { i , u } ^ { - \infty \cdots + \infty } ( \phi _ { i } q _ { u - i } - \phi _ { u - i } q _ { i } )
$$

To these we add the terms in which $\nu _ { i }$ is infinity, that is,

$$
\frac { 1 } { 4 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } a r _ { 1 } ^ { - 2 } n ^ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } } { a _ { 1 } ( 1 + m ) } \left[ \eta \sum \rlap { p } _ { u } k _ { c } ^ { ( u ) } - \xi \sum \rlap { p } _ { u } k _ { s } ^ { ( u ) } \right] ,
$$

where $k _ { c } ^ { ( u ) }$ and $k _ { s } ^ { ( u ) }$ are respectively the coefficients of $( m ^ { \prime } / a _ { 1 } ) n t$ cos ug and $( m ^ { \prime } / a _ { 1 } ) n t$ sin ug in $Q$ .The equations (8l) and (8la),or (82) and (82a), solve our problem.

# 10.16PERTURBATION IN LONGITUDE

From (82) we have

$$
{ \frac { d v } { d t } } = { \frac { \mu } { r ^ { 2 } } } \left[ \int { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } } } d t + C \right] .
$$

For the undisturbed motion,

$$
\frac { d v _ { 0 } } { d t } = \frac { a ^ { 2 } n \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu C } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Put $\delta \boldsymbol { v } = \boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } _ { 0 } ,$ then we obtain

$$
\frac { d \ : \delta \boldsymbol { v } } { d t } = \frac { \mu } { r ^ { 2 } } \int \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } } \ : d t + \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \bigg ) a ^ { 2 } n \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

or

$$
r ^ { 2 } \frac { d \delta v } { d t } = \frac { a n ^ { 2 } } { 1 + m } ( 1 - 2 \ : \delta \rho ) \int \frac { \partial R } { \partial \bf { v } } \ : d t - 2 n \sqrt { 1 - \ : e ^ { 2 } } ( \delta \rho - \ : \delta \rho ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

correct to the second order.

When we know the first approximation, those first approximate values are substituted in $\delta R$ ， $\delta ( \partial R / \partial \mathbf { v } )$ ， $\delta ( \partial R / \partial \rho )$ , the variations due to δv, $\delta \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\delta \rho , \delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \delta \gamma .$ .The constant terms of $\delta \rho$ ， $\delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ are included in the expressions for the mean distance, so that δv, $\delta \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\delta \rho$ ， $\delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ consist only of periodic terms.First we obtain $\delta \rho$ from (81） and (8la),using the value of $Q _ { 0 }$ given by (79). Then, using (83), we derive $\delta \rho$ from

$$
r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \frac { d \hat { s } v } { d t } = \frac { a n ^ { 2 } } { 1 + m } \int \frac { \partial R _ { 0 } } { \partial \bf v } d t - 2 n \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \hat { s } \rho .
$$

Then we obtain $\delta R , \delta ( \partial R / \partial \mathbf { v } ) , \delta ( \partial R / \partial \rho )$ from this value of $\delta \rho$ by

$$
{ \delta R = \frac { \partial R } { \partial { \bf v } } \delta { \bf v } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial { \bf v } ^ { \prime } } \delta { \bf v } ^ { \prime } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } \delta \rho + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho ^ { \prime } } \delta \rho ^ { \prime } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \gamma } \delta \gamma , }
$$

for example. From $\delta R$ we compute $D _ { t } ^ { \prime } \delta R$ by differentiation.Then the difference of the value of $Q$ given by (77) and the value of $Q _ { 0 }$ given by (79) is computed by

$$
\delta Q = 2 \int D _ { t } ^ { \prime } \delta R d t + \delta \frac { \partial R } { \partial \rho } - \frac { 1 } { \mu } \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \delta \rho ^ { 2 } ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \delta \rho ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 0 } } \cdot
$$

The second approximation is then obtained from

$$
\frac { d } { d t } = a n ^ { 2 } \bigg [ \int \delta \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } } d t - 2 ~ \delta \rho \int \frac { \partial R _ { 0 } } { \partial \mathbf { v } } d t \bigg ] - 2 n \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ~ ( \delta \rho - \delta \rho ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

# 10.17 MOTION OF THE ORBITAL PLANE

We decompose the instantaneous rotation around the radius vector into two parts. One of the axes is to be taken as the common node of the two orbital planes. The sense of the rotation is considered positive if we see it as anticlockwise in looking toward the origin.Denote by dq the instantaneous rotation around the $\pmb { x }$ -axis,and by dp that around the $y$ -axis. Let $d _ { \eta }$ be the instantaneous rotation around the ascending node of the orbital plane of the disturbing planet relative to that of the disturbed;and let $d k$ be the instantaneous rotation around the secondary axis (Figure 3).

The formulas for the variation of the inclination $\phi ^ { \prime }$ and of the longitude $\theta ^ { \prime }$ of the node are

$$
\frac { d \theta ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { a ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } } \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } , \frac { d \phi ^ { \prime } } { d t } = - \frac { a ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } } \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } .
$$

If $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ is the angular distance between the node of the disturbed orbit and the common node, then,

$$
\frac { \partial \lambda ^ { \prime } } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } = - \sin \kappa ^ { \prime } \cot \gamma , \qquad \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \partial \omega } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } = - \sin \kappa ^ { \prime } \cot \kappa \cot \gamma ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { \partial \lambda ^ { \prime } } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } } = { \frac { 1 } { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } } = \cos \kappa ^ { \prime } \cot \gamma ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } } = { \frac { 1 } { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { \partial \omega } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } } = \cos \kappa ^ { \prime } \cos \mathrm { e c } \gamma ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial \gamma } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } = - \cos \kappa ^ { \prime } , \frac { 1 } { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial \gamma } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } = - \sin \kappa ^ { \prime } .
$$

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0004_pages_0121-0160/ocr/images/66ab0ae8666c18b80add19c0f62ac29753e6209695430c7b2aa19bfabd888c6c.jpg)  
FIGURE 3

Consider a term $( m h / a ^ { \prime } )$ cos $N$ from the disturbing function $\pmb { R }$ ; then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \partial R } { \partial \lambda } \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \omega } \frac { \partial \omega } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \lambda ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial \lambda ^ { \prime } } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \gamma } \frac { \partial \gamma } { \partial \phi ^ { \prime } } } \\ { = \frac { m } { a ^ { \prime } } h \sin \kappa ^ { \prime } \sin N [ ( i ^ { \prime } + j ^ { \prime } ) \cot \gamma + ( i + j ) \sec \gamma ] } \end{array}
$$

$$
- \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { m } { a ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \gamma } \cos \kappa ^ { \prime } \cos N ,
$$

$$
\frac { 1 } { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } = - \frac { m } { a ^ { \prime } } h \cos \kappa ^ { \prime } \sin N [ ( i ^ { \prime } + j ^ { \prime } ) \cot \gamma + ( i + j ) \cos \epsilon \coth \gamma ]
$$

We exchange the letters with primes and those without. Then, corresponding to a term $\left( m ^ { \prime } h / a _ { 1 } \right)$ cos $N$ in $R$ ，we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d \eta } { d t } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } a n } { a _ { 1 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \cos { ( \gamma / 2 ) } } \left[ \frac { \iota h } { 2 \sigma } + ( i + j ) \sigma h \right] \sin { N } , } \\ & { \frac { d k } { d t } = - \frac { m ^ { \prime } a n \cos { ( \gamma / 2 ) } } { 2 a _ { 1 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \sigma } \cos { N } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
i ^ { \prime } + i + j ^ { \prime } + j = - \imath , \sigma = \sin \frac { \gamma } { 2 } \cdotp
$$

Denote the longitude of the ascending node of the disturbing planet on the orbit of the disturbed planet by $\pmb \theta$ ; then,

$$
\frac { d q } { d t } = \cos \theta \frac { d \eta } { d t } - \sin \theta \frac { d k } { d t } , \frac { d \phi } { d t } = \cos \theta \frac { d k } { d t } + \sin \theta \frac { d \eta } { d t } .
$$

Now,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \delta \mathrm { v } = \cot \gamma \cdot \delta k - \sec \gamma \cdot \delta k ^ { \prime } , \qquad \delta \mathrm { v } ^ { \prime } = \sec \gamma \cdot \delta k - \cot \gamma \cdot \delta k ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \delta \gamma = \delta \eta ^ { \prime } - \delta \eta . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left( \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } } \cot \gamma + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime } } \sec \gamma \right) \delta k - \left( \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } } \cos \epsilon + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime } } \cot \gamma \right) \delta k ^ { \prime } \qquad } \\ { + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \gamma } ( \delta \eta ^ { \prime } - \delta \eta ) . } \end{array}
$$

Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { R = \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } h } { 2 a _ { 1 } } \left[ \iota \cot \frac { \gamma } { 2 } \cdot \left( \delta k - \delta k ^ { \prime } \right) + ( i + j - i ^ { \prime } - j ^ { \prime } ) \tan \frac { \gamma } { 2 } \cdot \left( \delta k + \delta k ^ { \prime } \right) \right] \sin \frac { \delta k } { \sin \theta } } \\ { + \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } \cos \left( \gamma / 2 \right) } { 2 a _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \sigma } \left( \delta \eta ^ { \prime } - \delta \eta \right) \cos \theta } \end{array}
$$

Since we have counted the longitude from the fixed point on the moving orbital plane, the fixed point should be determined by the condition that the instantaneous rotation of the orbital plane should be always zero around the line perpendicular to this plane. Hence the direction of motion of the reference fixed point must always be perpendicular to the instantaneous orbital plane. The instantaneous rotation is zero in the first approximation but not necessarily so in the second approximation. Thus the fixed point moves due to the motion of the plane.

Next,we transform to the longitude on the ecliptic.Let dr be the rotation around the axis perpendicular to the line of the node.Then the orbital longitude increases by $d l = d r$ tan $( \phi / 2 )$ owing to the rotation cos $\theta \cdot d \phi - \sin \theta \cdot d q = d r _ { \mathrm { { z } } }$ where $\phi$ is the inclination of the actual instantaneous orbital plane relative to the orbital plane at the initial epoch.Owing to this rotation the distance of the fixed points at these two epochs increases by $d r ( \cos \epsilon c \phi - \cot \phi ) = d r .$ tan $( \phi / 2 )$ . Thus the longitude increases by $d l = d r \cdot \tan \left( { \phi } / 2 \right)$ . Denote the integrated values of $d { \boldsymbol { p } }$ and dq by $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ and $q$ ,respectively. Then,

$$
d l = \frac { \cos { \phi } } { 1 + \cos { \phi } } ( \tan { q } \cdot d p - \tan { p } \cdot d q ) ,
$$

because

$$
\sin \theta = \frac { \tan \phi } { \tan \phi } , \qquad \cos \theta = \frac { \tan \eta } { \tan \phi } ;
$$

or approximately

$$
\delta l \approx \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int { ( q d \phi - \phi d q ) } .
$$

It can be seen that this term is unnecessary.

Denote by $\pmb { \tau }$ the longitude of the orbital node on the ecliptic counted on the orbital plane from the point from which $v$ is counted; then

$$
\tan { ( L - \theta ) } = \cos { \phi } \tan { ( \mathbf { v } - \boldsymbol { \tau } ) } ,
$$

where $L$ is the longitude on the ecliptic. Let

$$
L = { \bf v } + \theta - \boldsymbol { \tau } + D ;
$$

then

$$
D = - \tan ^ { 2 } { \frac { \phi } { 2 } } \sin 2 ( v - \tau ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { 4 } { \frac { \phi } { 2 } } \sin 4 ( v - \tau ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Substituting the expressions for dp/dt,dq/dt,dp'/dt,dq'/dt in the expressions for $d \theta / d t , d \phi / d t , d \tau / d t ,$ we obtain

$$
{ \frac { d L } { d t } } = { \frac { d \mathbf { v } } { d t } } + { \frac { d D } { d t } } - \tan { \frac { \phi } { 2 } } \left[ \cos \tau { \frac { d \phi } { d t } } - \sin \tau { \frac { d q } { d t } } + \cos \theta { \frac { d \phi ^ { \prime } } { d t } } - \sin \theta { \frac { d q } { d t } } \right]
$$

or

$$
{ \cal L } = { \bf v } + { \cal D } + \tan \frac { \phi } { 2 } \left[ \cos \theta \cdot ( \delta k + \delta k ^ { \prime } ) - \sin \theta \cdot \left( \delta \eta + \delta \eta ^ { \prime } \right) \right] .
$$

The latitude is obtained from

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \sin \beta = \sin \phi \sin ( v - \tau ) , } \\ { v = l + 2 e \sin g + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

The variation

$$
\delta p = - a _ { s } \sin { N } - a _ { c } \cos { N } , \qquad \delta q = - a _ { s } ^ { \prime } \sin { N } + a _ { c } ^ { \prime } \cos { N } ,
$$

leads to an analytic expression

$$
\delta \beta = \sin \mathbf { v } \cdot \delta q - \cos \mathbf { v } \cdot \delta \boldsymbol { \beta } .
$$

The reduction of the observations has been made since by Morgan and Scott (1939) and Spencer Jones (1939) for the sun,by Clemence (1943) for Mercury, by Ross (1917) and Clemence (1949) for Mars, and by Eckert, Brouwer, Clemence (1951） and Clemence (1954) for the outer planets except Pluto.

# GYLDEN'S THEORY

# 10.18GYLDEN's INTERMEDIARY ORBIT

The usual procedure in the method of the variation of elements is to take a Keplerian ellipse as a first approximation and to study the deviation from this Keplerian motion. Gyldén's new approach was to adopt an orbit different from a Keplerian ellipse as his first approximation.Such an orbit is called an intermediary orbit, (Gyldén, l88l, 1882). He studied the intermediary orbits of a comet (l877,1884) and of the moon (l885).Gyldén amalgamated,in the intermediary orbit,the secular and the long-period terms in the perturbation of the Keplerian motion and called such an orbit an absolute orbit.He expressed the motion in such an intermediary orbit in a purely trigonometrical series which he mistakenly thought to be convergent. The deviations from such an intermediary orbit turned out to be purely periodic fluctuations.However,even for the intermediary orbit, the solution was very complicated. He made use of elliptic functions.His works are summarized in two volumes published in 1893 and 1909.

A space curve contained between two concentric spheres and with its concave side facing always toward the common center of the two spheres is said to be periplegmatic. The radius vector of a periplegmatic curve is of length between the radii of the two spheres.At the maxima and minima of the radius vector the curve touches respectively the larger or smaller of the two limiting spheres.The radial distance between such a maximum and minimum is called the diastem. The general equation of a periplegmatic curve is represented by

$$
y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - \Pi , \qquad \Pi > 0 .
$$

Gyldén tried to solve the differential equations for a planetary motion by means of purely trigonometric functions with linear functions of $t$ as arguments.A term in his theory which corresponds to a periodic inequality in ordinary pertrubation theory is called a coordinate term. A term which does not vanish even when the disturbing mass is put equal to zero and which reduces to a term in an elliptic motion of the disturbed planet around the sun is called an elementary term.This idea was reviewed by Wolf (1891)，Backlund (1889)，and Olsson (l891).In Gyldén's process of integration,terms appear with the disturbing mass in the denominator,that is, terms which become infinite when the disturbing mass vanishes, although they appear only in the process of integration but not in the final result. Such terms are called hyper-elementary. Terms of long period arising from the presence of the so-called small divisors are called characteristic, sometimes semi-elementary.

Another aspect of Gyldén's theory is the use of the true anomaly as the independent variable in place of time, where Hansen and Newcomb used the eccentric anomaly. Then the series converge more rapidly, although only in a formal sense.Gyldén obtained the solution in terms of purely trigonometric series by referring to the works of Hermite and Lamé on elliptic functions.Gyldén thought such series as he employed to be convergent.He called his method of solving the nonlinear differential equation the heuristic method.However,Poincaré proved that Gyldén's series are not uniformly convergent (Chapter l5).

# 10.19HEURISTIC METHOD

The coordinate axes are referred to the moving instantaneous orbital plane in the sense of Hansen. The origin of the longitude is taken to be the line on this moving orbital plane such that the locus of this line, which is a curved surface, is always perpendicular to the orbital plane. This is the fixed $_ { x }$ -axis of Hansen. The longitude counted from this line on the moving orbital plane is called the true longitude and denoted by $v$ Take $v$ in place of $t$ as the independent variable and write

$$
r ^ { 2 } \frac { d v } { d t } = \frac { k \sqrt { a ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } } { 1 + S } ,
$$

where $\eta$ is a quantity which reduces to the eccentricity and $s$ a quantity which reduces to zero in an elliptic motion;both being unknown functions of $v$ .It is arranged so that $\eta$ contains the secular inequalities of the eccentricity. Gylden called $^ { a }$ the protometer.

Put

$$
P = m ^ { \prime } r ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } , ~ Q = m ^ { \prime } \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { a ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial v } ,
$$

and

$$
r = \frac { a ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } { 1 + \rho } ,
$$

where $\pmb { \rho }$ is a new unknown function of $v$ . Then the equations of motion are transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \rho = 2 S - P - \frac { d \rho } { d v } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } \frac { d \eta ^ { 2 } } { d v } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + S } \frac { d S } { d v } \right) + S ^ { 2 } - ( 2 S + S ^ { 2 } ) P } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } - ( 1 + \rho ) \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta ^ { 2 } } { d v ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { d \eta ^ { 2 } } { d v } \right) ^ { 2 } \right. } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \left. \qquad - \frac { 1 } { 1 + S } \frac { d S } { d v } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } \frac { d \eta ^ { 2 } } { d v } \right] , \quad { \scriptstyle ( 8 7 a ) } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
- \frac { 1 } { 1 + S } \frac { d S } { d v } = ( 1 + S ) ^ { 2 } Q + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } \frac { d \eta ^ { 2 } } { d v } ,
$$

$$
\frac { k } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } \frac { d t } { d v } = \frac { ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } { ( 1 + \rho ) ^ { 2 } } ( 1 + S ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \varkappa } { d v ^ { 2 } } + \varkappa = - ( 1 + S ) ^ { 2 } Q \frac { d \varkappa } { d v } + m ^ { \prime } \frac { r ^ { 3 } ( 1 + S ) ^ { 2 } } { a ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } - \varkappa \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial r } \right) ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle \Delta ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos H , } } & { { ~ z = r z , \nonumber } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \Omega = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - \frac { r \cos H } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } , } } & { { ~ H = v - v ^ { \prime } . } } \end{array}
$$

Gylden expanded the disturbing function in the form (Section 7.3)

$$
a \Omega = 2 \sum { ( - 1 ) ^ { \nu } \Omega ( n , s , s ^ { \prime } ) _ { \nu \nu ^ { \prime } } } { \rho ^ { s } \rho ^ { \prime } s ^ { \prime } \eta ^ { 2 \nu } \eta ^ { \prime 2 \nu ^ { \prime } } } \cos \ n H ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Omega ( n , 0 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } = \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { \left( n \right) } , } \\ & { \Omega ( n , 1 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } = - n \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { \left( n \right) } - 2 \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \left( n \right) } , } \\ & { \Omega ( 0 , 0 , 1 ) _ { 0 0 } = ( n + 1 ) \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { \left( n \right) } + 2 \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \left( n \right) } , } \\ & { \Omega ( n , 0 , 0 ) _ { 1 0 } = - n \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { \left( n \right) } + 2 \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \left( n \right) } , } \\ & { \qquad \quad \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \bf \Gamma } _ { \mathrm { 2 3 } ^ { \prime } + 1 } ^ { \mathrm { \prime \prime } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } H } { ( 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } H ) ^ { ( 2 s + 1 ) / 2 } } d H , } \\ { ~ \gamma _ { s } ^ { \mathrm { \prime \prime } } = \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 s - 1 ) } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 s ) } \alpha ^ { n + 2 s + 1 } \beta _ { 2 s + 1 } ^ { ( n + s ) } } \\ { = \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 s - 1 ) } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 s ) } \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 n + 2 s - 1 ) } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( 2 n + 2 s ) } \alpha ^ { n + 2 s + 1 } } \\  ~ \times F \Big ( s + \frac { 1 } { 2 } , n + s + \frac { 1 } { 2 } , n + s + 1 , { \mathrm { \prime } } , { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } } { \mathrm { \prime } }  \ \end{array}
$$

There are similar expansions for $P$ and $Q$ ; they have been tabulated by Masal (1889), Harzer (1886,1886a, 1891),and Olsson (1897).

Put $k / a ^ { 3 / 2 } = n$ in (87c); then,

$$
\frac { n d t } { d v } = \frac { ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } { ( 1 + \rho ) ^ { 2 } } ( 1 + S ) .
$$

Let $\left( \rho \right)$ be the part of $\rho$ which is independent of the factor $m ^ { \prime }$ and of the order of the eccentricity. Let

$$
( \rho ) = \eta \cos { ( v - \varpi ) } .
$$

But the perihelion moves with the mean motion on. Since the longitude of the perihelion is now $\varpi + \sigma v$ ,we have

$$
\mathbf { \phi } ( \rho ) = \eta \cos \mathbf { \phi } ( v - \sigma v - \varpi ) ;
$$

$\left( \rho \right)$ was called the elementary part of $\rho$ by Gyldén. The variables $\eta$ and $\boldsymbol { \varpi }$ contain the remaining part of the secular inequality, which is written in the form of terms of very long period.Put

$$
\rho = ( \rho ) + R ,
$$

where $\scriptstyle { R }$ is periodic and vanishes with $m ^ { \prime }$ ，,and is called the diastematic inequality. Gylden called $\eta$ the diastematic function, and $( 1 - \sigma ) v - \varpi$ the diastematic argument.

If we put further

$$
n t = n \zeta + W ,
$$

$$
\frac { n d \zeta } { d v } = \frac { ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } { [ 1 + ( \rho ) ] ^ { 2 } } ;
$$

then $\zeta$ is called the reduced time and $\mathbf { \Omega } _ { n } W$ the time reduction. Substituting (89), we see that

$$
{ \frac { d \zeta } { d v } } = 1 + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } i B _ { i } \cos i ( v - \sigma v - \varpi ) ,
$$

where $B _ { i }$ are functions of $\eta$ .Integrate this equation; then, we obtain

$$
n t + \Lambda = v + \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } \sin i ( v - \sigma v - \varpi ) + W .
$$

Similarly,for the disturbing planet,

$$
n ^ { \prime } t + \Lambda ^ { \prime } = v ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma ^ { \prime } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } ^ { \prime } \sin i ( v ^ { \prime } - \sigma ^ { \prime } v ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) + { \cal W } ^ { \prime } .
$$

In the integration of (90) the variables $\eta$ and $\eqcirc$ are supposed to be constants. Since Gylden included also the very long-period terms in $\eta$ and $\varpi , R$ consists of terms of short period.Hence $\eta$ and $\eqcirc$ must be supposed to be variable,and the integration should be carried out in such a way that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle { \int } } \eta \cos { ( v - \sigma v - \varpi ) } d v = \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } } \eta \cos { \varpi } { \sin { ( v - \sigma v ) } } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } - \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } } { \int } \sin { ( v - \sigma v ) } d ( \eta \cos { \varpi } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } - \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } } \eta \sin { \varpi } \cos { ( v - \sigma v ) } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } + \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } } { \int } \cos { ( v - \sigma v ) } d ( \eta \sin { \varpi } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
X = 2 \int \cos { ( v - \sigma v ) } d ( \eta \sin { \varpi } ) - 2 \int \sin { ( v - \sigma v ) } d ( \eta \cos { \varpi } ) ,
$$

and neglect $\pmb { \sigma }$ in comparison with unity; then,

$$
n t + \Lambda = v + \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } \sin i ( v - \sigma v - \varpi ) + W - X .
$$

# Similarly

$$
n ^ { \prime } t + \Lambda ^ { \prime } = v ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma ^ { \prime } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } ^ { \prime } \sin i ( v ^ { \prime } - \sigma ^ { \prime } v ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) + W ^ { \prime } - X ^ { \prime } .
$$

Write

$$
\mu = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { \pi } , \qquad \Delta = \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \mu \Lambda , \qquad U = \mu ( W - X ) - ( W ^ { \prime } - X ^ { \prime } ) ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { v ^ { \prime } = \mu v + \Delta + U + \displaystyle \frac { \mu } { 1 - \sigma } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } \sin i ( v - \sigma v - \varpi ) \qquad } \\ { \displaystyle - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma ^ { \prime } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } ^ { \prime } \sin i ( v ^ { \prime } - \sigma ^ { \prime } v ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { H = v - v ^ { \prime } = w - \displaystyle \frac { \mu } { 1 - \sigma } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } \sin i \mathrm { v } } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma ^ { \prime } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { i } ^ { \prime } \sin \left( i w - i \mathrm { v ^ { \prime } } - \frac { i \mu } { 1 - \sigma } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { j } \mathrm { s i } \mathrm { s i } \right) } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { v - \sigma v - \varpi = \mathbf v , \qquad v - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } v ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime } = \mathbf v ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { ( 1 - \mu ) v - \Delta - U = w . } } \end{array}
$$

With this value of $\pmb { H }$ we can expand cos nH and sin nH. Substitute these in $\Omega , P ,$ and $Q$ ; then we obtain trigonometric series with

$$
\vartheta = - \alpha ^ { \prime } w - \beta \mathbf { v } - \beta ^ { \prime } \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime }
$$

as the arguments, where $\alpha ^ { \prime } , \beta , \beta ^ { \prime }$ are positive or negative integers.

Our next problem is to study the elementary part $\rho$ in the form (89) by integrating the equations (87a) and (87b). In the first approximation we have

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho } { d v ^ { 2 } } + \rho = 2 S - P ,
$$

$$
\frac { d S } { d v } = - Q .
$$

The terms with arguments $\mathbf { v }$ and $\mathbf { v ^ { \prime } }$ in $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ become larger after the integration of this equation $( 8 7 \mathbf { a } ^ { \prime } )$ for $\pmb { \rho }$ In order to avoid this inconvenience Gylden wrote

$$
\rho = \eta \cos { \bf v } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \rho ^ { \prime } = \eta ^ { \prime } \cos { ( w - \bf v ^ { \prime } ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { H = \cos w - \mu \eta \cos \left( w - \mathbf { v } \right) + \mu \eta \cos \left( w + \mathbf { v } \right) \quad } \\ { + \mathbf { \eta } ^ { \prime } \cos \left( 2 w - \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime } \right) - \eta ^ { \prime } \cos \mathbf { v } } \end{array}
$$

and substituted them in the expressions for $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ .Thus he obtained

$$
\begin{array} { l } { P = m ^ { \prime } ( P _ { 0 } + P _ { 1 } \eta \cos { \bf v } + P _ { 2 } \eta ^ { \prime } \cos { \bf v ^ { \prime } } ) , } \\ { Q = m ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 } \eta ^ { \prime } \sin { \bf v ^ { \prime } } . } \end{array}
$$

If we insert these expressions in $( 8 7 \mathrm { b ^ { \prime } } )$ and integrate, by supposing that $\eta ^ { \prime }$ and $\scriptstyle { \overline { { \pmb { \varpi } } } } ^ { \prime }$ are constant, then,

$$
S = \frac { m ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 } } { 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { \prime } \cos { \bf v ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Substitute this in $( 8 7 \mathsf { a } ^ { \prime } )$ ; this gives an equation of the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho } { d v ^ { 2 } } + \rho = - m ^ { \prime } P _ { 0 } - m ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } \eta \cos { \bf v } - m ^ { \prime } \Big ( P _ { 2 } - \frac { 2 Q _ { 2 } } { 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } } \Big ) \eta ^ { \prime } \cos { \bf v ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Subtract the constant term from the right-hand member and let it be included in $\rho ,$ and write $\eta$ Cos $\mathbf { v }$ for $\pmb { \rho }$ ; this gives an equation of the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( \rho ) } { d v ^ { 2 } } + ( 1 - \beta ) ( \rho ) = \gamma \eta ^ { \prime } \cos { [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) { v } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } .
$$

Gyldén solved this equation by his heuristic method.

We know (Section 8.13) that the secular inequality of the disturbing planet is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { e ^ { \prime } \sin \omega ^ { \prime } = e ^ { \prime } \sin \left( \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } v + \Gamma ^ { \prime } \right) + \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \sin \left( \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } v + \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { e ^ { \prime } \cos \omega ^ { \prime } = e ^ { \prime } \cos \left( \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } v + \Gamma ^ { \prime } \right) + \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cos \left( \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } v + \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } \end{array}
$$

Write $\omega ^ { \prime } = \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } v + \varpi ^ { \prime }$ and $\eta ^ { \prime }$ in place of $e ^ { \prime }$ ; then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \eta ^ { \prime } \cos { ( \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ) } = \kappa ^ { \prime } + \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cos { [ ( \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v + \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ] } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { \eta ^ { \prime } \sin { ( \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ) } = \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \sin { [ ( \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v + \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ] } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { ~ o s ~ } [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] = \kappa ^ { \prime } \cos { [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ] } } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ } + \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cos { [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ] } + \cdot \cdot } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ i n ~ } [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] = \kappa ^ { \prime } \sin { [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ] } } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ } + \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \sin { [ ( 1 - \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ] } + \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

Substituting these expressions in (92),we obtain the following differential equation with constant coefficients :

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( \rho ) } { d v ^ { 2 } } + ( 1 - \beta ) ( \rho ) = \gamma \kappa ^ { \prime } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma ^ { \prime } \right] } \\ & { \qquad + \gamma \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } \end{array}
$$

The general solution is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm {  ~ \gamma ~ } _ { 0 } ) = \kappa _ { 0 } \cos \left[ v \sqrt { 1 - \beta } - \Gamma \right] + \mathbf { a } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma ^ { \prime } \right] \mathrm {  ~ \gamma ~ } } \\ { + \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) v - \Gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right] + \mathbf { \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { a } [ ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { 2 } - ( 1 - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } ] = \gamma \kappa ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } [ ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { 2 } - ( 1 - \mu \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } ] = \gamma \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \qquad \quad \cdot \cdot . . } \end{array}
$$

We should take ${ \sqrt { 1 - \beta } } = 1 - \sigma$ 、The quantity $\kappa$ is called the diastematic modulus; $\mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma }$ is the absolute longitude of the perihelion at the epoch; ${ \sigma } ^ { \prime } , { \sigma } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots$ are of the order of the disturbing mass.The series obtained for $\rho$ is called the heuristic function.

Similarly we obtain for the third coordinate $z$ the solution of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { z = ( z ) + Z , } \\ & { ( z ) = I \sin \left[ ( 1 - \tau ) v - \Theta \right] + I _ { 1 } \sin \left[ ( 1 - \tau _ { 1 } ) v - \Theta _ { 1 } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } \end{array}
$$

Write this equation in the form

$$
( z ) = J \sin { [ ( 1 - \tau ) v - \Omega ] } .
$$

Gylden called $\boldsymbol { J }$ the anastematic function and $( 1 - \tau ) v - \Omega$ the anastematic argument.

# 10.20 CHARACTERISTIC TERMS

When the ratio $\mu$ of the mean motions is nearly equal to a simple fraction, that is, when the mean motions are nearly commensurable, the corresponding terms called the characteristic appear.An argument of the form $( 1 - \delta ) v - \Delta$ is called the short-period characteristic and an argument of the form $\delta v - \Delta ,$ ，the long-period characteristic. In contrast to these terms,an argument $( 1 - \sigma ) v - \Gamma$ is called the short-period elementary and an argument $\sigma v - \Gamma$ the long-period elementary.

Write the equation (93) in the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( \rho ) } { d v ^ { 2 } } + ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { 2 } ( \rho ) = Y ;
$$

then the solution is obtained in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { ( \rho ) = \kappa _ { 0 } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \sigma ) v - \Gamma \right] } \\ { \displaystyle - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \sigma ) v \right] \int Y \sin \left[ ( 1 - \sigma ) v \right] d v } \\ { \displaystyle + \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma } \sin \left[ ( 1 - \sigma ) v \right] \int Y \cos \left[ ( 1 - \sigma ) v \right] d v . } \end{array}
$$

Consider the case of an asteroid of the Hecuba group. We have $\mu = n ^ { \prime } / n \approx 1 / 2$ Put $1 - 2 \mu = \delta$ ; then,

$$
\psi = \frac { \sigma + \delta } 2 v - \Delta + \frac { \Gamma } 2 + R _ { 2 } ,
$$

where $R _ { 2 }$ denotes the group of long-period terms in the right-hand member of (9l),so that (91) is written in the form

$$
v ^ { \prime } = \mu v + \Delta + R _ { 2 } + \mathrm { s h o r t  – p e r i o d e l e m e n t a r y ~ t e r m s } .
$$

Thus $\psi$ contains in itself the long-period elementary and the long-period characteristic terms. Consider $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathbf { \vec { Y } * } }$ ,a term from $\boldsymbol { Y }$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle Y ^ { * } = - \gamma _ { 0 } \cos \left[ ( 1 - \sigma ) v + 2 \psi - \Gamma \right] } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \gamma _ { 0 } = \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } } { m _ { 1 } } P ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } + \frac { 8 + 4 \delta + \delta ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \delta } \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { m _ { 1 } } Q ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } } ; } \end{array}
$$

then the solution of (93a) is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { = - \displaystyle \frac { \gamma _ { 0 } } { 2 ( 1 - \sigma ) } \cos { [ ( 1 - \sigma ) v - \Gamma ] } \int \overline { { { \sin 2 \psi } } } d v } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \frac { \gamma _ { 0 } } { 2 ( 1 - \sigma ) } \sin { [ ( 1 - \sigma ) v - \Gamma ] } \int \overline { { { \cos 2 \psi } } } d } } \end{array}
$$

where $\rho ^ { * }$ is, similarly,a part of $\rho$ ,and a bar over an expression indicates the aggregate of terms of arguments $\sigma v$ and the constant terms in that expression. To this part $\rho ^ { * }$ ， we add the elementary part

$$
\rho = \eta \cos { [ ( 1 - \sigma ) v - \varpi ] } .
$$

The long-period part of $\left( \rho \right)$ is obtained from

$$
\frac { d ( \rho ) } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { 2 } } \Big ( \frac { d Y } { d v } - \frac { d ^ { 3 } \rho } { d v ^ { 3 } } \Big ) ,
$$

where we can neglect $d ^ { 3 } \rho / d v ^ { 3 }$ in the first approximation. After computation,using the above expression for $\rho ^ { * }$ plus the elementary part, we obtain the principal part of $\pmb { \rho }$ which is of long period. Thus

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d ( \rho ) } { d v } = - \left[ \gamma _ { 1 } + \frac { 3 \mu \gamma _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 3 } } { 8 \mathbf { a } ( 1 - \sigma ) } z \right] \eta \eta ^ { \prime } \sin \left[ \varpi ^ { \prime } - \varpi - ( \sigma - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v \right] } \\ { \displaystyle ~ - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \frac { d \eta ^ { 2 } } { d v } - \gamma _ { 2 } \eta \sin \left( 2 \psi + \varpi - \Gamma \right) } \\ { \displaystyle ~ - \gamma _ { 3 } \eta ^ { \prime } \sin \left[ 2 \psi - ( \sigma - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) v + \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Gamma \right] , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \gamma _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { m _ { 1 } } \left[ - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { Q ( 1 , 1 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } } { 1 - \sigma ^ { \prime } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mu \frac { Q ( 1 , 0 , 1 ) _ { 0 0 } } { 1 - \sigma } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } Q ( 1 , 1 , 1 ) _ { 0 0 } \right] , } \\ { \gamma _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { m _ { 1 } } \left[ \frac { 1 2 \mu Q ( 2 , 0 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } } { 1 - \sigma } - 3 Q ( 2 , 1 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } \right] , } \\ { \gamma _ { 3 } = \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { m _ { 1 } } \left[ - 3 \frac { Q ( 1 , 0 , 0 ) _ { 0 0 } } { 1 - \sigma ^ { \prime } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } Q ( 1 , 0 , 1 ) _ { 0 0 } \right] . } \end{array}
$$

The function $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } }$ is a trigonometric series of long-period elementary terms; a is approximately equal to $\frac { 1 } { 4 } ( \delta + \sigma ) ^ { 2 }$ . In the case of an exact commensurability, ${ \mathbf { a } } / \varkappa$ approaches a finite limit for a vanishing value of $\delta + \sigma ,$ and $3 \mu \gamma _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 3 } { \boldsymbol { \approx } } / [ 8 \mathbf { a } ( 1 - \sigma ) ]$ is of the first order,while in the case of an approximate commensurability, the latter fraction is of the second order. In the case of a noncommensurability the terms in the first line of (93b) are all of the second degree.

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { \beta } { a } \cos 2 \Theta = - \frac { 4 \gamma _ { 4 } } { \gamma _ { 0 } } \eta \cos ( \varpi - \Gamma ) - \frac { 4 \gamma _ { 5 } } { \gamma _ { 0 } } \eta ^ { \prime } \cos [ \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Gamma - ( \sigma - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } )  } } \\ { { \displaystyle  \frac { \beta } { a } \sin 2 \Theta = - \frac { 4 \gamma _ { 4 } } { \gamma _ { 0 } } \eta \sin ( \varpi - \Gamma ) - \frac { 4 \gamma _ { 5 } } { \gamma _ { 0 } } \eta ^ { \prime } \sin [ \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Gamma - ( \sigma - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } )  } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\gamma _ { 4 } = - \frac { \mu \gamma _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 2 } } { 2 { \bf a } } + 3 \mu \frac { \gamma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 8 { \bf a } ( 1 - \sigma ) } , \qquad \gamma _ { 5 } = - \mu \frac { \gamma _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 3 } } { 2 { \bf a } } .
$$

Thus $\left( \beta / \mathbf { a } \right) \mathbf { \Lambda } _ { \mathbf { s i n } } ^ { \mathsf { c o s } } 2 \Theta$ are expressed as trigonometric series of long-period elementary terms.

We must now determine the long-period elementary characteristic terms. From the definition of $\psi$ ,the differential equation for $\psi$ takes the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \psi } { d v ^ { 2 } } = - \beta \sin { ( 2 \psi + \Theta ) } + X , } } \\ & { } & \\ & { X = - 2 \mu \bigg [ \gamma _ { 1 } + \frac { 2 \mu \gamma _ { 0 } \gamma _ { 3 } } { 8 \mathbf { a } ( 1 - \sigma ) } \mathfrak { a } \bigg ] \eta \eta ^ { \prime } \sin { [ \varpi ^ { \prime } - \varpi - ( \sigma - \mu \sigma ^ { \prime } ) \mathfrak { v } ] } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

Gyldén (1869,1871,1874,1876,1877,1882,1896) solved this equation with elliptic functions,as will be shown in Section 12.7.

The equation (94) is the equation for a spherical pendulum, if $\beta$ and @ are constants and $X$ is zero. If $\beta$ and @ are variable and $X$ is not zero, the integration gives two forms,corresponding respectively to the case of a rotating pendulum and the case of an oscillating pendulum. The first form appears for an approximate commensurability of the mean motions, and the second for an exact commensurability. Gyldén's method of integration (l874,1885) is to put the solution in the form

$$
\psi + \Theta = \mathrm { a m } \Big ( \nu v + F , \sqrt { \frac { 2 \beta } { \gamma + \beta } } \Big ) ,
$$

where the value of the coefficient of $v$ in

$$
\psi + \Theta = \frac { \pi } { 2 { \bf K } } ( \nu v + F ) + \frac { 2 q } { 1 - q ^ { 2 } } \sin 2 \frac { \pi } { 2 { \bf K } } ( \nu v + F ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

should be equal to that in

$$
\psi + \Theta = \frac { \ S + \sigma } { 2 } v - \Delta + \frac { \Gamma } { 2 } + R _ { 2 } + \Theta .
$$

Both $\mathbf { \delta x }$ and $q$ are the known constants in the theory of elliptic functions, and $R _ { 2 }$ and $\Theta$ do not contain the terms linear in $v$ ; that is,

$$
{ \frac { \pi } { 2 \mathbf { K } } } \nu = { \frac { \delta + \sigma } { 2 } } \cdotp
$$

Here we recall that the inversion of an elliptic integral

$$
{ \frac { 2 \mathbf { K } } { \pi } } x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } { \frac { d \phi } { \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } }
$$

is defined by

$$
\theta = \operatorname { a m } { \frac { 2 \mathbf { K } } { \pi } } x \qquad { \mathrm { ( m o d ~ } } k { \mathrm { ) } } , \qquad 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = \Delta \operatorname { a m } { \frac { 2 \mathbf { K } } { \pi } } x ,
$$

where $\mathbf { \delta K }$ is the complete elliptic integral of the first kind. If we expand in series form,we obtain

$$
\cos 2 n \bigg ( \mathrm { a m } \frac { 2 \bf K } { \pi } x \bigg ) = \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { ( 2 n ) } + 2 \Gamma _ { 2 } ^ { ( 2 n ) } \cos 2 x + 2 \Gamma _ { 4 } ^ { ( 2 n ) } \cos 4 x + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\sin 2 n \bigg ( \mathrm { a m } \frac { 2 \bf K } { \pi } x \bigg ) = 2 \Sigma _ { 2 } ^ { ( 2 n ) } \sin 2 x + 2 \Sigma _ { 4 } ^ { ( 2 n ) } \sin 4 x + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Hence we obtain, with $k ^ { 2 } + k ^ { \prime 2 } = 1$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { + \ 1 6 \bigg ( \frac { \pi } { 2 k \mathbf { K } } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } \bigg \{ q \sin \bigg [ 2 \frac { \pi } { 2 \mathbf { K } } \left( \nu v + F \right) - 2 \Theta \bigg ] } \\ & { \qquad - \ q ^ { 3 } \sin \bigg [ 2 \frac { \pi } { 2 \mathbf { K } } \left( \nu v + F \right) + 2 \Theta \bigg ] \ + \ \cdots } \\ & { \qquad + \ 2 q ^ { 2 } \sin \bigg [ 4 \frac { \pi } { 2 \mathbf { K } } \left( \nu v + F \right) - 2 \Theta \bigg ] } \\ & { \qquad - \ 2 q ^ { 6 } \sin \bigg [ 4 \frac { \pi } { 2 \mathbf { K } } \left( \nu v + F \right) + 2 \Theta \bigg ] \ + \ \cdots \bigg \} , } \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf { E }$ is the complete elliptic integral of the second kind and $q = \exp \left\{ - \pi \mathbf { K } ^ { \prime } / \mathbf { K } \right\}$ .Also,

$$
\delta = - \frac { 4 \mathbf { a } } { \beta } \left[ \frac { 2 \mathbf { E } / \mathbf { K } - \mathbf { \beta } 1 - k ^ { \prime 2 } } { k ^ { 2 } } \right] .
$$

Let the mean motion of the perihelion of the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ th planet be $\pmb { \sigma } ^ { ( \mathfrak { p } ) }$ and its phase be $\Gamma ^ { ( p ) }$ . Put

$$
B ^ { ( p ) } = ( \sigma ^ { ( p ) } - \sigma ) v + \Gamma ^ { ( p ) } - \Gamma ;
$$

then the solution is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \eta \cos { \left( \varpi - \Gamma \right) } = \kappa _ { 0 } + \kappa _ { 1 } \cos { B ^ { ( 1 ) } } + \kappa _ { 2 } \cos { B ^ { ( 2 ) } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { \eta \sin { \left( \varpi - \Gamma \right) } = \kappa _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \sin { B ^ { ( 1 ) } } + \kappa _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \sin { B ^ { ( 2 ) } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

Thus $\left( \beta / \mathbf { a } \right)$ s 20 and $\ll$ are trigonometric series of the same form. Put $k = 2 \sqrt { k _ { 1 } } / ( 1 + k _ { 1 } )$ ; then $q$ is expanded in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { q = \frac { 1 } { 4 } k _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } k _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 7 } { 5 1 2 } k _ { 1 } ^ { 5 } + \cdots . } \end{array}
$$

The equation for $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ can be similarly integrated.

This is the outline of the method by which Harzer (1886,1886a,1891), Backlund (1887,1892,1897a,1897b,1897c,1898a,1898b,1902,1904), Andoyer (1886),Ludendorff (1897,1899,1900,1901),Iwanow (1899, 1900,190l),Donner (1882),and Kramer (1901,1902,1907） studied the motion of the characteristic asteroid 108 Hecuba；Brendel (1890, 1889,1897),Boda (1928,1930),and Olsson (1893）- the asteroid 46 Hestia; Buchholz (1902,l905)- the asteroid Hilda;Krassowski (1916) - the asteroid Thule；and Callandreau (l882） and Schultz-Steinheil (1898)- the asteroid lO3 Hera of mean motion around $8 0 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ . Weiler (1895;cf.,1884,1889,1891) challenged the work of Harzer (1895),and there were frequent discussions among Harzer, Brendel (l895),and Weiler. Callandreau (1894） criticized the work of Weiler. Recently Behrens (1929,l935) treated the case of commensurability 5/2.Baillaud (1876) applied the method to the perturbations of comets.

Backlund，among others,modified Gyldén's method. By working from Laplace's theory of Jupiter's satellites he took $\tau = n t + \psi$ as the independent variable in place of Gyldén's true anomaly.Like Gyldén, he wrote the true longitude as $w = n t + \psi + y + \Lambda _ { : }$ ，where $\Lambda$ isa constant, $\psi$ a long-period function,and $y$ a small quantity in powers of which the disturbing function is expanded. The unknown functions are $w , \varkappa ,$ and $\rho$ contained in the expression

$$
r ^ { 2 } = a ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \Theta + \rho ) , n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ) ,
$$

where O is a constant of the order $m ^ { \prime }$ to be determined so that $\rho$ contains only purely periodic terms. Backlund's equations are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + \rho = R _ { 0 } + R _ { 1 } , \frac { d w } { d \tau } = 1 + W _ { 0 } + W _ { 1 } , } } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \varkappa } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + \varkappa = Z _ { 0 } + Z _ { 1 } , } } } \end{array}
$$

where $R _ { 0 } , R _ { 1 } , W _ { 0 } , W _ { 1 } , Z _ { 0 } , Z _ { 1 }$ are expanded in powers of $\Theta , \rho , y , d \rho / d \tau ,$ $d \psi / d \tau ,$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } }$ .The quantities $R _ { 1 } , W _ { 1 } ,$ and $Z _ { 1 }$ are multiplied by $m ^ { \prime }$ ,but not $R _ { 0 } , \ W _ { 0 } \qquad $ and $Z _ { 0 }$ .He worked out the solution for an asteroid of the $5 / 2$ type,and Iwanow published the tables.

Brendel (1887,1898-1929)，with Hartner (1928)，developeda theory of asteroids according to Gyldén's theory and extended it to the major planets (1933).He also developed a lunar theory (l905, 1925a). Curiously enough,Brendel (1897,1898) believed,on theoretical grounds, that there should be a gap in the mean motion of the Hecuba-group asteroids.Let the ratio of the osculating mean motions be $\mu$ and the ratio of the mean mean motions be $\mu _ { 1 }$ . The actual mean motion ratio is approximately equal to a simple fraction $1 / 2$ ，so that we can write

$$
\mu = ( 1 - \delta ) / 2 , \mu _ { 1 } = ( 1 - \delta _ { 1 } ) / 2 ,
$$

Sand $\delta _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ being very small. Let the coeffcient of the short-period characteristic term in $R$ be $\beta _ { 1 }$ ，which satisfies a cubic equation of the form $\beta _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } + \phi \beta _ { 1 } + q = 0$ .From this equation Brendel concluded that the root $\pmb { n _ { 1 } }$ cannot lie between $5 9 3 \% 3$ and 603"8, and hence that there is a gap in the mean motion,or that there are two solutions for $\pmb { n }$ near 607"2, so that the motion is unstable. This view of Brendel's was erroneous, as was pointed out by Poincaré (l902; Section 18.17).

Gyldén's method (l88la) was first published in l88l and was simplified by Backlund (1882,i889,1902, 1904） and Callandreau (1882a, 1890).Criticisms were raised by Thiele (1882)，Radau (1882)，and Charlier (1904). Buchholz (1903) gave warning that Gyldén's method was false,and Backlund (l9l2) discussed Gylden's heuristic differential equation.

Gyldén himself (1885)，Shdanow (1885)，Andoyer (1887)，and Tisserand (l888) tried to apply the method to the moon,and Shdanow (1885a) to Comet Faye.Gyldén wrote several papers on the integration of his differential equation by means of elliptic functions by refering to Hermite's work on the Lamé equation (see Chapter l2； Hagihara, 1970),and claimed (1881b,1882a,1887,1892,1893a) to have proved the convergence of the series which he obtained by his heuristic method. Schmidt (1888,1889）discussed it. Poincaré (1904,1904a,1905,1889) argued against Gyldén and proved that the series are not uniformly convergent. According to Poincaré,Gyldén's method presents unnecessary complications by introducing several unfamiliar variables and is only of historical interest.In the usual method of successive approxima-tions we first integrate a differential equation by neglecting some of the terms,and then integrate again,by substituting this first approximate solution in the neglected terms. In using this method it is necessary that the terms neglected in the first approximation should be small. Poincareé proved that this is not necessarily so in Gylden's treatment.He even pointed out several mistakes in Gylden's memoirs.

# I0.2IBRENDEL'S MODIFICATION

After several years working on Gyldén's theory, Brendel (1930, 1933, 1935）arrived at the following modification.

Consider the expansion of the disturbing function

$$
( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos { \cal H } ) ^ { - n / 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = - \infty } ^ { \infty } b _ { n } ^ { ( i ) } \cos i { \cal H } \left( \alpha = \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } \right) \cdot
$$

In order to construct an expansion which converges more rapidly Brendel transformed to the variable $\phi$ ， the angle subtended by the sun and the disturbed planet at the disturbing planet for $e = e ^ { \prime } = 0 _ { \mathrm { \scriptscriptstyle ~ \cdot } }$ ，such that

$$
\frac { \Delta } { a ^ { \prime } } \sin \phi = \sin H .
$$

Put

$$
\vartheta = \frac { \Delta } { a ^ { \prime } } = \sqrt { 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos { \cal H } } , \qquad \Delta \phi = \sqrt { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { l l r } { { \displaystyle \vartheta \sin \phi = \sin H , \qquad } } & { { \displaystyle \vartheta = \Delta \phi - \propto \cos \phi , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \vartheta \cos \phi = \cos H - \alpha , \qquad } } & { { \displaystyle \vartheta ^ { - 1 } = \frac { \Delta \phi + \alpha \cos \phi } { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\vartheta ^ { 2 } = 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \phi - 2 \alpha \cos \phi \cdot \Delta \phi .
$$

Expanding in powers of $\phi$ ,we obtain

$$
\vartheta ^ { - 2 n } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { n } ^ { ( i ) } \cos 2 i \phi + \Delta \phi \cdot \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { n } ^ { ( i ) } \cos { ( 2 i - 1 ) \phi } ,
$$

$$
\vartheta ^ { - ( 2 n + 1 ) } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { n } ^ { ( i ) } \cos { \left( 2 i + 1 \right) } \phi + \Delta \phi \cdot \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { n } \beta _ { n } ^ { ( i ) } \cos { 2 i \phi } .
$$

Write

$$
r = \frac { \phi } { 1 + \rho } , \qquad \phi = a ( 1 - \psi ) , \qquad r ^ { 2 } \frac { d v } { d t } = \sqrt { M / } ;
$$

then the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d \psi } { d v } = \frac { 2 ( 1 - \psi ) } { 1 + \rho } m ^ { \prime } Q , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho } { d v ^ { 2 } } + \rho = m ^ { \prime } \Big ( P - 2 \frac { d Q } { d v } - \frac { Q } { 1 + \rho } \frac { d \rho } { d v } - \frac { 2 } { 1 + \rho } m ^ { \prime } Q ^ { 2 } \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P = \displaystyle \frac { r ^ { 3 } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \left( \frac { r ^ { \prime 3 } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - 1 \right) \displaystyle \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \cos { \cal H } , } } \\ { { Q = \displaystyle - \left( \frac { r ^ { \prime 3 } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - 1 \right) \displaystyle \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \partial } { \partial v } \cos { \cal H } , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
n \frac { d t } { d v } = \frac { ( 1 + \psi ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } { ( 1 + \rho ) ^ { 2 } } , \qquad n = \frac { \sqrt { M } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } \cdot
$$

We limit ourselves to the consideration of the first-order perturbation for $\boldsymbol { e } = \boldsymbol { e } ^ { \prime } = 0$ .Then the equations of motion reduce to

$$
\frac { d \psi } { d v } = 2 m ^ { \prime } Q , \frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho } { d v ^ { 2 } } + \rho = m ^ { \prime } \Bigl ( P - 2 \frac { d Q } { d v } \Bigr ) ,
$$

$$
n \frac { d t } { d v } = 1 - 2 \rho - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \psi ,
$$

$$
H = v - v ^ { \prime } = ( 1 - \mu ) ( v - B ) , ~ \mu = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } ,
$$

$$
\Delta ^ { 2 } = a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 a a ^ { \prime } \cos { \cal H } , \qquad B = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Furthermore, we have to this degree of approximation,

$$
Q = ( \vartheta ^ { - 3 } - 1 ) \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin { \cal H } , \qquad P = \alpha ^ { 3 } \vartheta ^ { - 3 } - ( \vartheta ^ { - 3 } - 1 ) \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos { \cal H } .
$$

Since

$$
\frac { d v } { d H } = \frac { 1 } { 1 ~ - ~ \mu } , ~ \frac { d H } { d \phi } = \frac { \vartheta } { \Delta \phi } , ~ \frac { d v } { d \phi } = \frac { \vartheta } { \left( 1 ~ - ~ \mu \right) \Delta \phi } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { d \psi } { d \phi } = \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - \mu ) \Delta \phi } ( \vartheta ^ { - 1 } - \vartheta ^ { 2 } ) \sin \phi .
$$

The solution is

$$
\psi = - \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } \alpha } { 1 - \mu } ( \vartheta ^ { - 1 } + \vartheta \Delta \phi ) + c _ { 1 } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \mathit { \Pi } _ { j } ^ { \prime } = - \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \mu } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \alpha ( 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } ) } \Delta \phi \right. } } \\ { \displaystyle { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } } } + \frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } } \cos \phi - \left. \sin \phi \cos \phi - \alpha \sin ^ { 2 } \phi \right] + c _ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
c _ { 2 } = c _ { 1 } - \frac { 2 m ^ { \prime } \alpha } { 1 - \mu } \cdot
$$

Put

$$
\rho = g \cos v + h \sin v ;
$$

then from the equation for $\rho$ of (95) we derive

$$
\frac { d g } { d v } = - m ^ { \prime } ( P \sin v + 2 Q \cos v ) , \frac { d h } { d v } = m ^ { \prime } ( P \cos v - 2 Q \sin v ) .
$$

Write

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { P _ { 1 } = \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos { H } , } } & { { P _ { 2 } = - \alpha ^ { 2 } \vartheta ^ { - 2 } \cos { \phi } , \nonumber } } \\ { { Q _ { 1 } = - \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin { H } , } } & { { Q _ { 2 } = \alpha ^ { 2 } \vartheta ^ { - 2 } \sin { \phi } , \nonumber } } \\ { { P = P _ { 1 } + P _ { 2 } , } } & { { Q = Q _ { 1 } + Q _ { 2 } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then we obtain, corresponding to each part of $P$ and $Q$ ,the equations

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d g _ { 1 } } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } [ 3 \sin { ( H - v ) } + \sin { ( H + v ) } ] } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d h _ { 1 } } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } [ 3 \cos { ( H - v ) } - \cos { ( H + v ) } ] } . } } \end{array}
$$

The solution is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle g _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } \biggl [ \frac { 3 } { \mu } \cos { ( H - v ) } - \frac { 1 } { 2 - \mu } \cos { ( H + v ) } \biggr ] , } } \\ { { \displaystyle h _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } \biggl [ \frac { 3 } { \mu } \sin { ( H - v ) } + \frac { 1 } { 2 - \mu } \sin { ( H + v ) } \biggr ] , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\rho _ { 1 } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } ( 3 - 2 \mu ) } { \mu ( 2 - \mu ) } \cos H .
$$

Similarly,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d g _ { 2 } } { d \phi } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \mu } \frac { \vartheta ^ { - 1 } } { \Delta \phi } [ 3 \sin { ( v - \phi ) } - \sin { ( v + \phi ) } ] , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d h _ { 2 } } { d \phi } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \mu } \frac { \vartheta ^ { - 1 } } { \Delta \phi } [ 3 \cos { ( v - \phi ) } - \cos { ( v + \phi ) } ] . } \end{array}
$$

Consider, for example, the case of the small divisor for the motion of the asteroid Hecuba. Write $1 / ( 1 - \mu ) = 2 + \epsilon , | \epsilon | < \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ ；then, $v = ( 2 + \epsilon ) H + B$ .Write $H = \phi - \Pi$ ， $v = 2 \phi + \epsilon H + B - 2 \Pi$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \sin \Pi = \alpha \sin \phi , } & & { \sin 2 \Pi = 2 \alpha \sin \phi \Delta \phi , } \\ & { \cos \Pi = \Delta \phi , } & & { \cos 2 \Pi = 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } + \alpha \cos 2 \phi , } \end{array}
$$

and the equation for $g _ { 2 }$ becomes

$$
\frac { d g _ { 2 } } { d \phi } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 ~ - ~ \mu } \frac { \vartheta ^ { - 1 } } { \Delta \phi } \left[ L _ { 1 } \sin { ( \epsilon H + B ) } + L _ { 2 } \cos { ( \epsilon H + B ) } \right] ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r } { L _ { 1 } = ( 3 \sin \phi - \sin 3 \phi ) \sin 2 \Pi + ( 3 \cos \phi - \cos 3 \phi ) } \\ { L _ { 2 } = [ 3 \sin \phi - \sin 3 \phi ) \cos 2 \Pi - ( 3 \cos \phi - \cos 3 \phi ) } \end{array}
$$

From the relation

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { - 1 } } { \Delta \phi } = \frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 + \frac { \alpha \cos \phi } { \Delta \phi } \right) ,
$$

we compute

$$
( 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } ) \frac { \vartheta ^ { - 1 } } { \Delta \phi } \stackrel { \cos } { \sin } 2 \Pi ,
$$

and substitute in the differential equation. Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { \varepsilon _ { 2 } } { l \phi } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \mu ) } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \times \left[ \left( M _ { 1 } + \frac { M _ { 3 } } { \Delta \phi } \right) \sin { ( \epsilon H + B ) } + \left( M _ { 2 } + \frac { M _ { 4 } } { \Delta \phi } \right) \cos { ( \epsilon H + B ) ^ { 2 } } \right] } } \end{array}
$$

$$
M _ { 3 } = { \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( 9 \alpha - 7 \alpha ^ { 2 } ) - ( 3 \alpha - 4 \alpha ^ { 3 } )
$$

For integrating this equation we expand

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta \phi } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } = A _ { 0 } + 2 \cdot \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { ( - 1 ) ^ { n } A _ { n } \cos 2 n \phi } ,
$$

$$
A _ { n } = k ^ { n } ( 1 + k ) \beta _ { n } , k = { \frac { 2 } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } } ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } } ) - 1 ,
$$

$$
\beta _ { n } = \frac { 2 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 n } \chi d \chi } { \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \chi } } ,
$$

where $k$ is of the order $\alpha ^ { 2 } / 4$ . This series converges rapidly; for example,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Delta \phi } = 1 . 1 2 2 9 - 0 . 1 3 3 7 \cos 2 \phi + 0 . 0 1 1 9 \cos 4 \phi } } \\ { { - 0 . 0 0 1 2 \cos 6 \phi + 0 . 0 0 0 1 \cos 8 \phi \ . } } \end{array}
$$

Substitute $H = \phi - \Pi$ ， $\Pi = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \alpha \sin \phi \right)$ in sin $( \epsilon H + B )$ and cos $( \epsilon H + B )$ , and expand sin ${ \epsilon } \Pi$ and cos $\epsilon \Pi$ again in series proceeding with the multiples of $\phi$ ; then we obtain finally

$$
: = \sin ( \epsilon \phi + B ) \bigg ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \alpha _ { n } \cos n \phi \bigg ) + \cos ( \epsilon \phi + B ) \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \beta _ { n } \sin n \phi
$$

The integral is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { = \displaystyle - \cos { ( \epsilon \phi + B ) } \biggl ( \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n \beta _ { n } - \epsilon \alpha _ { n } } { n ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \cos { n \phi } \biggr ) } } \\ { { + \sin { ( \epsilon \phi + B ) } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n \alpha _ { n } - \epsilon \beta _ { n } } { n ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \sin { n \phi } } } \end{array}
$$

Similarly

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle - \sin { ( \epsilon \phi + B ) } \bigg ( \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n \beta _ { n } - \epsilon \alpha _ { n } } { n ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \cos n \phi \bigg ) } } \\ { { - \cos { ( \epsilon \phi + B ) } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n \alpha _ { n } - \epsilon \beta _ { n } } { n ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \cos n \phi . } } \end{array}
$$

Referring back to $\epsilon \phi + B = v - ( 2 H - \epsilon \Pi )$ ,we find

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { = - \cos { ( 2 H - \epsilon \Pi ) } \displaystyle { \Bigg ( \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } } { 2 } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n \beta _ { n } - \epsilon \alpha _ { n } } { n ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \cos { n \phi } \Bigg ) } } } \\ { { - \sin { ( 2 H - \epsilon \Pi ) } \displaystyle { \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n \alpha _ { n } - \epsilon \beta _ { n } } { n ^ { 2 } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \sin { n \phi } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\boldsymbol { H }$ and $\Pi$ are to be replaced by their expressions in terms of $\phi$ The coefficient $\alpha _ { 0 }$ depends on $\textstyle e , e ^ { \prime } , d \varpi / d t ,$ and $d \varpi ^ { \prime } / d t$

Numerically we obtain

$$
\rho _ { 2 } = \cos { ( 2 H - \epsilon \Pi ) } \biggl ( - \frac { 1 . 3 2 7 0 } { \epsilon } + 1 . 3 9 6 5 \cos { \phi } + 0 . 6 1 4 0 \cos { 2 \phi }
$$

- sin(2H - eII)(0.0582 sin $\phi - 0 . 2 4 9 9$ sin $2 \phi - 0 . 0 3 4 9$ sin $3 \phi$ $+ \ 0 . 0 4 8 6$ sin $4 \phi - 0 . 0 0 7 5$ sin $5 \phi - 0 . 0 0 1 5 \sin 6 \phi$ $+ \ 0 . 0 0 0 4$ sin $7 \phi + 0 . 0 0 0 1$ sin $8 \phi + \cdots )$

For the asteroid Hecuba we have $\epsilon = - 0 . 0 6$ and the first term is large in magnitude.

Boda (1930a,1933) wrote

$$
