\begin{array} { l } { { \Delta ^ { - n } = ( r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos { H } ) ^ { - n / 2 } } } \\ { { \ = ( r + r ^ { \prime } ) ^ { - n } ( 1 - x ) ^ { - n / 2 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle x = \frac { 2 r r ^ { \prime } } { ( r + r ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } ( 1 + \cos { H } ) , \qquad | x | < 1 . } } \end{array}
$$

But the convergence is still slow.

Trousset (1933) applied Brendel's method to the motion of asteroids. Shen (1940,1941,1957) made a further modification by putting

$$
\Delta ^ { - 3 / 2 } = \frac { 1 } { ( 1 - \alpha ) ^ { 3 } } \left( c ^ { ( 0 ) } + 2 \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } c ^ { ( n ) } \cos n H \right) { . }
$$

Write $X = 1 + e \cos f , f = v - \varpi$ ; then,

$$
\frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha } = \frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha _ { 0 } X ^ { \prime } / X } = \bigg ( \frac { 2 X E } { \alpha _ { 0 } e ^ { \prime } } \bigg ) ^ { 3 } ( 1 + E ^ { 2 } - 2 E \cos f ^ { \prime } ) ^ { - 3 } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \displaystyle \frac { 2 e } { 1 + e - \alpha _ { 0 } } = k ^ { 2 } , } & { { } } & { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { f } { 2 } = D , \qquad \displaystyle \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } e ^ { \prime } } { 1 + e - \alpha _ { 0 } } = \epsilon ^ { \prime } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon ^ { \prime } } { D } = \displaystyle \frac { 2 E } { 1 + E ^ { 2 } } , } & { { } } & { 0 < E < 1 , \qquad 0 < \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon ^ { \prime } } { D } < \displaystyle \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } \epsilon ^ { \prime } } { 1 - e - \alpha _ { 0 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { - \ \alpha ) ^ { 3 } } = \bigg ( \frac { 2 X E } { \alpha _ { 0 } e ^ { \prime } } \bigg ) ^ { 3 } \bigg [ F ( 3 , 3 , 1 , E ^ { 2 } ) + \sum _ { j ^ { \prime \prime } = 1 } ^ { \infty } 4 ( - E ) ^ { j ^ { \prime \prime } } \Big ( _ { j ^ { \prime \prime } } ^ { - 3 } \Big ) } \\ { \displaystyle \times F ( 3 + j ^ { \prime \prime } , 3 , 1 + j ^ { \prime \prime } , E ^ { 2 } ) \cos { j ^ { \prime \prime } } \bigg ] } \end{array}
$$

Denote by $\Sigma$ the angle between the $_ { x }$ -axis on the moving orbital plane and its node on the fixed orbital plane. Writing

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { P } { m ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \alpha ^ { 3 } - \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos H } { ( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos H ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } + \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos H , } \\ & { \frac { Q } { m ^ { \prime } } = \bigg [ \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos H ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \alpha ^ { 2 } \bigg ] \bigg ( - \frac { \partial \cos H } { \partial v } \bigg ) , } \\ & { \frac { Z ^ { ( 1 ) } } { m ^ { \prime } } = \bigg [ \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos H ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \alpha ^ { 2 } \bigg ] \cos H , } \\ & { \frac { Z ^ { ( 2 ) } } { m ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos H ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \alpha ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

Shen obtained the equations

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin I . \left\{ \left[ 1 + \cos 2 ( f + \varpi - \Sigma ) \right] \frac { Q } { X } - \sin 2 ( f + \varpi - \Sigma ) \frac { Z ^ { ( 1 ) } } { X } \right\} } \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin I ^ { \prime } \cdot \left\{ \sin \left( f + f ^ { \prime } + \varpi + \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Sigma - \Sigma ^ { \prime } \right) \right. } \ ~ }  \\  { \displaystyle ~ \left. - \sin \left( f - f ^ { \prime } + \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Sigma + \Sigma ^ { \prime } \right) \frac { Z ^ { ( 2 ) } } { X } \right\} } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c }  { I { \displaystyle { \frac { d \Sigma } { d v } } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sin I \cdot \{ \sin 2 ( f + \varpi - \Sigma ) { \frac { Q } { X } }  } } \\  {  - { \frac { \displaystyle { [ 1 - \cos 2 ( f + \varpi - \Sigma ) ] { \frac { Z ^ { ( 1 ) } } { X } } } \} } } } \\ { {  + { \frac { \displaystyle { 1 } } { \displaystyle { 2 } } } \sin I ^ { \prime } \cdot \{ \cos ( f - f ^ { \prime } + \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } - \Sigma + \Sigma ^ { \prime } )  } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\frac { d ( \Omega - \Sigma ) } { d v } = \frac { 1 - \cos I } { \cos I } \frac { d \Sigma } { d v } .
$$

In the integration he put

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } { { \frac { f } { 2 } } = { \frac { v \ - \varpi } { 2 } } = \displaystyle { \mathrm { a m } } { \frac { 2 \bf K } { \pi } } { \frac { \theta } { 2 } } } & { \ ( \mathrm { m o d } \ k ) , } \\ { \displaystyle } & { \ = \theta + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { 4 q ^ { n } \sin n \theta } { n ( 1 \ + \ q ^ { 2 n } ) } } . } \end{array} }
$$

We obtain

$$
D ^ { - j } = k ^ { \prime - 2 j } \biggl [ \mathrm { d n } \frac { 2 \bf K } { \pi } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) \biggr ] ^ { 2 j } ,
$$

$$
\mathrm { d n } \frac { 2 \mathbf { K } } { \pi } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) = \frac { \pi } { 2 \mathbf { K } } + \frac { 2 \pi } { \mathbf { K } } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n } q ^ { n } } { 1 + q ^ { 2 n } } \cos \pi \theta ,
$$

where

$$
\epsilon = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan { \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \psi \right) } , \sqrt { k ^ { \prime } } = \tan { \psi } = \bigg ( \frac { 1 - e - \alpha _ { 0 } } { 1 + e - \alpha _ { 0 } } \bigg ) ^ { 1 / 4 } ,
$$

$$
q = \epsilon + 2 \epsilon ^ { 5 } + 1 5 \epsilon ^ { 9 } + 1 5 0 \epsilon ^ { 1 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Let

$$
\frac { \sin i f } { D ^ { j - 1 / 2 } } = \frac { \sin 2 i \tan \frac { 2 \mathbf { K } } { \pi } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { \left( \mathrm { d n } \frac { 2 \mathbf { K } } { \pi } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 j - 1 } } = 2 \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } H _ { k } ^ { ( i , j ) } \sin k \theta ,
$$

and

$$
G = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } G _ { j } \sin j f = 2 \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } g _ { k } \sin k \theta ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { G _ { j } = ( - 1 ) ^ { j } \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { j } \left( \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \right) ^ { j } ( 1 + j \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) , } \\ { g _ { k } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } G _ { i } H _ { k } ^ { ( i , 1 / 2 ) } ; } \end{array}
$$

then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \theta = n _ { 0 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) + \Lambda - \varpi } } \\ { { \displaystyle \ = \tau - 2 \cdot \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } h _ { n } \sin n \theta + W , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\tau = n _ { 0 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) + \Lambda - \varpi , h _ { n } = g _ { n } + \frac { 2 q ^ { n } } { n ( 1 + q ^ { 2 n } ) } \cdot
$$

Shen (l957) applied his method to the motion of Thule.

# I0.22 BRENDEL'S LUNAR THEORY

The main feature of Brendel's lunar theory (l925b) is the taking of the sun as the principal attracting body and the earth as a disturbing body, although his former theory (l905） was founded on that of Gyldén.The zero-th approximation to the motion of the moon is the Keplerian motion of the center of mass of the moon and earth around the sun.

Denote the coordinates of the sun referred to the earth by $- a x ^ { \prime }$ ， $- a y ^ { \prime } , \ - a z ^ { \prime } , \ - a r ^ { \prime }$ ，those of the center of mass of the moon and earth referred to the sun by $a x , a y , a z , a r ,$ and those of the moon referred to the earth by $A X , A Y , A Z , A \Delta ,$ and the distance between the moon and sun by $a \rho$ .Then,

$$
a x ^ { \prime } = a x - { \frac { m } { m + m ^ { \prime } } } A X , \qquad a y ^ { \prime } = a y - { \frac { m } { m + m ^ { \prime } } } A Y ,
$$

and the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { A \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } X } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { m + m ^ { \prime } } { A ^ { 3 } \Delta ^ { 3 } } A X = \displaystyle \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } r ^ { \prime } \mathrm { { } } ^ { 3 } } a x ^ { \prime } - \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } \rho ^ { 3 } } ( a x ^ { \prime } + A X ) , } } \\ { { A \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } Y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { m + m ^ { \prime } } { A ^ { 3 } \Delta ^ { 3 } } A Y = \displaystyle \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } r ^ { \prime } \mathrm { { } } ^ { 3 } } a y ^ { \prime } + \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } \rho ^ { 3 } } ( a y ^ { \prime } + A Y ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
= \frac { m } { m + m ^ { \prime } } , ~ \alpha = \frac { \cal { A } } { a } , ~ n = \frac { \sqrt { m _ { 0 } + m + m ^ { \prime } } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } , ~ N = \frac { \sqrt { m + m ^ { \prime } } } { { \cal { A } } ^ { 3 / 2 } }
$$

Let $v$ be the true longitude of the center of mass as seen from the sun, and let

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \xi = X \cos v + Y \sin v , \qquad x = r \cos v , \qquad r = x \cos v + y \sin v , } } \\ { { \eta = - X \sin v + Y \cos v , \qquad y = r \sin v , \qquad 0 = - x \sin v + y \cos v } } \end{array}
$$

then the equations become

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 2 } { t ^ { 2 } } - \eta \frac { d \eta } { d t } \frac { d v } { d t } - \xi \Big ( \frac { d v } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \eta \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d t ^ { 2 } } } } \\ & { } & { = \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } } ( \frac { r } { c } - \mathbf { m } \xi ) ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } ) - \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } \rho ^ { 3 } } \xi - \frac { m + m } { A ^ { 3 } \Delta ^ { 3 } } } \\ & { } & { \frac { 2 \eta } { t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d \xi } { d t } \frac { d v } { d t } + \eta \Big ( \frac { d v } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \xi \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d t ^ { 2 } } \qquad } \\ & { } & { = - \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } } \mathbf { m } \eta \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \Big ) - \frac { m _ { 0 } } { a ^ { 3 } \rho ^ { 3 } } \eta - \frac { m + m } { A ^ { 3 } \Delta ^ { 3 } } } \end{array}
$$

So far, $_ n$ and $N _ { : }$ as well as $^ { a }$ and $\pmb { A }$ ,are still undetermined.Write $\mu = n / N$ and $\mathbf { m ^ { \prime } } = ( m + m ^ { \prime } ) / ( m _ { 0 } + m + m ^ { \prime } )$ ， $\mu _ { 1 } = \mu ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \mathbf { m } ^ { \prime } )$ ， $L = N t + \Lambda$ ， $l = n t + \lambda _ { ; }$ $N = d L / d t .$ ， $n = d l / d t .$ $\Delta ^ { 2 } = \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } = 1 + R$ .Then the equations reduce to

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d L ^ { 2 } } - 2 \mu \frac { d v } { d l } \frac { d \eta } { d L } - \mu ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d v } { d l } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \xi - \mu ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d l ^ { 2 } } \eta - \frac { 2 \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \xi = - \frac { \xi } { ( 1 + R ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } , } \\ & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d L ^ { 2 } } + 2 \mu \frac { d v } { d l } \frac { d \xi } { d L } + \mu ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d v } { d l } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \eta + \mu ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d l ^ { 2 } } \xi + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \eta = - \frac { \eta } { ( 1 + R ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Brendel made a further transformation:

$$
1 - \phi = \xi \cos w + \eta \sin w , \qquad q = - \xi \sin w + \eta \cos w ,
$$

with $w = l - L = ( 1 - \mu ) L - B , B = \lambda - \mu \Lambda , R = - 2 \phi + \phi ^ { 2 } + q ^ { 2 } ;$ and obtained the equations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \phi } { d L ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d q } { d L } - 3 \phi = P + S , } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } q } { d L ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d \phi } { d L } = Q + T , } } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\frac { 1 } { ( 1 + R ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } = 1 - \frac { 3 } { 2 } R + \Phi = 1 + 3 \rlap / p + \Psi ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Phi = \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } R ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 5 } { 1 6 } R ^ { 3 } + \frac { 3 1 5 } { 1 2 8 } R ^ { 4 } - \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { \Psi = \Phi - \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( \rlap / p ^ { 2 } + \rlap / q ^ { 2 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
P = A + B , \qquad S = - C ( 1 - \rlap / p ) + D \frac { d q } { d L } + B \Bigl ( \frac 1 { r ^ { 3 } } - 1 \Bigr ) + F q ,
$$

$$
Q = A ^ { \prime } + B ^ { \prime } , \qquad T = C q - D { \frac { d \phi } { d L } } + B ^ { \prime } \biggl ( { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } } - 1 \biggr ) + F ( 1 - \phi ) ,
$$

$$
A = \Psi - \rlap / p \left[ \frac 1 { ( 1 + R ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } - 1 \right] , A ^ { \prime } = - q \left[ \frac 1 { ( 1 + R ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } - 1 \right] ,
$$

$B = - { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } [ 1 - \phi + 3 ( 1 - \phi )$ cos $2 w - 3 q \sin 2 w ]$ ，$B ^ { \prime } = { \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } [ q - 3 ( 1 - p ) ]$ sin $2 w - 3 q \cos 2 w ]$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { C = \displaystyle 2 \mu \Big ( \frac { d v } { d l } - 1 \Big ) + \mu ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d v } { d l } - 1 \Big ) ^ { 2 } , \ } } & { { D = - 2 \mu \Big ( \frac { d v } { d l } - 1 \Big ) , \nonumber } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { F = - \mu ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d l ^ { 2 } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $C , D , F , ( 1 / r ^ { 3 } ) - 1$ are of the order of the eccentricity of the earth's orbit around the sun, and $A , A ^ { \prime }$ are of the order of the eccentricity of the moon's orbit around the earth.Let $e$ and $e ^ { \prime }$ be something like the eccentricities of these orbits in elliptic motions, and expand $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ and $q$ in powers of $e$ and $e ^ { \prime }$ ，so that $\begin{array} { r } { p = \sum _ { i , j } \ p _ { i j } . } \end{array}$ $\begin{array} { r } { q = \sum _ { i , j } q _ { i j } } \end{array}$ where $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i j }$ and $q _ { i j }$ are of the order of $e ^ { i } e ^ { \prime j }$

For $i = j = 0$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \phi } { d L ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d q } { d L } - 3 \phi = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \cos 2 w , } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } q } { d L ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d \phi } { d L } = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 w . } } } \end{array}
$$

The solution is obtained by the method of indeterminate coefficients

$$
p _ { 0 0 } = b _ { 0 } + b _ { 1 } \cos 2 w , \qquad q _ { 0 0 } = c _ { 1 } \sin 2 w ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { 3 b _ { 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , } \\ { { } } \\ { { [ 4 ( 1 - \mu ) ^ { 2 } + 3 ] b _ { 1 } - 2 ( 1 - \mu ) c _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { 4 ( 1 - \mu ) ^ { 2 } c _ { 1 } - 2 ( 1 - \mu ) b _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Substitute these solutions for $\pmb { \mathit { \phi } } _ { 0 0 }$ and $\scriptstyle q _ { 0 0 }$ in the expressions for $A , A ^ { \prime } , B .$ $B ^ { \prime }$ , etc.and write the solution as far as the order $\mu ^ { 4 }$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \dot { p } = b _ { 0 } + b _ { 1 } \cos { 2 w } + b _ { 2 } \cos { 4 w } , \qquad q = c _ { 1 } \sin { 2 w } + c _ { 2 } \sin { 4 w } , } \end{array}
$$

and so on.

The intermediary orbit is taken so that the center of mass describes an elliptic orbit around the sun, and the moon also describes an elliptic orbit around the earth.Expanding $v \mathrm { ~ - ~ } l , \mathbf { v } - L ,$ and $\Delta$ in terms of the true anomaly $f$ with powers of $e$ and $e ^ { \prime }$ as coefficients,where v is the true anomaly of the moon in its motion around the earth,we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { } & { = e \cos f + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \cos 2 f - 2 e \ell ^ { \prime } \cos \left( f - f ^ { \prime } \right) + 2 e \ell ^ { \prime } \cos \left( f + f ^ { \prime } \right) } \\ { } & { = \ell ^ { \prime 2 } - e ^ { \prime 2 } \cos 2 f ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 } \sin 2 f - e \ell ^ { \prime } \sin \left( f - f ^ { \prime } \right) } \\ { } & { = 2 e \sin f - 2 e ^ { \prime } \sin f ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 } \sin 2 f - e e ^ { \prime } \sin \left( f - f ^ { \prime } \right) } \\ { } & { \phantom { = } + e e ^ { \prime } \sin \left( f + f ^ { \prime } \right) - \frac { 5 } { 4 } e ^ { \prime 2 } \sin 2 f ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 } \sin 2 f ^ { \prime } + . } \end{array}
$$

We obtain the intermediary orbit if we put $m _ { 0 } = 0$ ， $m ^ { \prime } = 1$ , and hence $\mu _ { 1 } = 0$ .Neglecting $e ^ { \prime }$ ,we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \dot { \mathcal { P } } _ { 1 0 } } { d L ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d q _ { 1 0 } } { d L } - \left( 3 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) \dot { \mathcal { P } } _ { 1 0 } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \dot { q } _ { 1 0 } } { d L ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d \dot { \mathcal { P } } _ { 1 0 } } { d L } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } q _ { 1 0 } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

The solution is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \dot { p } = e \cos f , } } & { { \quad q = 2 e \sin f , } } \\ { { \dot { f } = ( 1 - \sigma ) L - \pi , \quad } } & { { \sigma = \frac { 5 } { 4 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 8 9 } { 3 2 } \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

Substitute this solution in $\mathcal { P }$ and $Q$ on the right-hand sides of the expressions for $d ^ { 2 } \phi / d L ^ { 2 }$ and $d ^ { 2 } q / d L ^ { 2 }$ ； then the equations to be solved for the intermediary orbit are of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \dot { p } _ { 1 0 } } { d L _ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d q _ { 1 0 } } { d L } - 3 \dot { p } _ { 1 0 } = P _ { 1 0 } = P _ { 1 } \dot { p } _ { 1 0 } + P _ { 2 } q _ { 1 0 } , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } q _ { 1 0 } } { d L ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d \dot { p } _ { 1 0 } } { d L } = Q _ { 1 0 } = Q _ { 1 } \dot { p } _ { 1 0 } + Q _ { 2 } q _ { 1 0 } . } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r } { \dot { p } _ { 1 0 } = e \cos f + g _ { \perp 0 } , \qquad q _ { 1 0 } = 2 e \sin f + h _ { 1 0 } , } \\ { f _ { 1 } = f - w = ( \mu - \sigma ) L - \pi + B ^ { * } ; } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { = ( b _ { 5 } - 1 ) e \cos { ( f _ { 1 } + w ) } + b _ { 7 } e \cos { ( f _ { 1 } + 3 w ) } } \\ & { \qquad + b _ { 9 } e \cos { ( f _ { 1 } + 5 w ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + b _ { 6 } e \cos { ( f _ { 1 } - w ) } } \\ & { \qquad + b _ { 8 } e \cos { ( f _ { 1 } - 3 w ) } + b _ { 1 0 } e \cos { ( f _ { 1 } - 5 w ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ & { = ( c _ { 5 } - 2 ) e \sin { ( f _ { 1 } + w ) } + c _ { 7 } e \sin { ( f _ { 1 } + 3 w ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ & { \qquad + c _ { 6 } e \sin { ( f _ { 1 } - w ) } + c _ { 8 } e \sin { ( f _ { 1 } - 3 w ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

These functions $g _ { 1 0 }$ and $h _ { 1 0 }$ satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } g _ { 1 0 } } { d L ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d h _ { 1 0 } } { d L } - 3 g _ { 1 0 } = K _ { 1 0 } + P _ { 1 } g _ { 1 0 } + P _ { 2 } h _ { 1 0 } , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } h _ { 1 0 } } { d L ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d g _ { 1 0 } } { d L } = L _ { 1 0 } + Q _ { 1 } g _ { 1 0 } + Q _ { 2 } h _ { 1 0 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { K _ { 1 0 } = ( 2 \sigma + \sigma ^ { 2 } ) e \cos f + P _ { 1 } e \cos f + 2 P _ { 2 } e \sin f , } } \\ { { L _ { 1 0 } = - 2 ( \sigma - \sigma ^ { 2 } ) e \sin f + Q _ { 1 } e \cos f + 2 Q _ { 2 } e \sin f . } } \end{array}
$$

Substitute the expressions for $P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } , Q _ { 1 } , Q _ { 2 }$ and for $g _ { 1 0 } , h _ { 1 0 }$ on the righthand sides; then they are of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { ~ : ~ } ( f _ { 1 } + w ) + a _ { 7 } \ell \cos { ( f _ { 1 } + 3 w ) } + \cdots } \\ & { \qquad + a _ { 6 } \ell \cos { ( f _ { 1 } - w ) } + a _ { 8 } \ell \cos { ( f _ { 1 } - 3 w ) } + \cdots , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \cdot \left( f _ { 1 } + w \right) + a _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } e \sin \left( f _ { 1 } + 3 w \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ & { \phantom { \cdot \ } + a _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } e \sin \left( f _ { 1 } - w \right) + a _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } e \sin \left( f _ { 1 } - 3 w \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

where $a _ { 5 } , a _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } , a _ { 6 } , a _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots$ contain unknown coefficients $b _ { n }$ and $c _ { n }$

Substitute the above expressions for $g _ { 1 0 }$ and $h _ { 1 0 }$ on the left-hand sides of the differential equations and denote the factor of $L$ in the argument by $s _ { n }$ ; then,we obtain

$$
b _ { n } = - \frac { a _ { n } } { s _ { n } ^ { 2 } - 1 } - \frac { 2 a _ { n } ^ { \prime } } { s _ { n } ( s _ { n } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } , ~ c _ { n } = - \frac { 2 a _ { n } } { s _ { n } ( s _ { n } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } - \frac { ( s _ { n } ^ { 2 } + 3 ) a _ { n } ^ { \prime } } { s _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( s _ { n } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } .
$$

The expressions for the coefficients $a _ { n }$ and $b _ { n }$ have a small divisor $\sigma _ { n }$ Since $\sigma _ { n }$ is the coefficient of $L$ in the corresponding arguments and varies very slowly with time, the corresponding terms in $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ and $q$ are of long periods. Such long-period terms do not appear in the terms multiplied by $\pmb { \ell } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ,but they do in the terms multiplied by $e ^ { 2 }$ and $e ^ { \prime }$ , where $\sigma _ { n }$ is of the order $\mu$ ; such terms become large by further integration.

If $s _ { n }$ is approximately equal to unity, then the corresponding coefficients become large.The corresponding terms of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ and $q$ have periods of the order of the moon's revolution period,which is called of shortperiod.

In such a manner Brendel repeated the computation of the terms with factors $e ^ { 2 } , e ^ { 3 } , e ^ { \prime } , e e ^ { \prime } , e ^ { 2 } e ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 }$

# BROWN'S THEORY

# 10.23EQUATIONS OF MOTION FOR A TROJAN ASTEROID

Brown (1923,1925；Brown and Shook 1933), following Hansen, took the moving orbital plane of an asteroid as the reference plane. Denote the rectangular coordinates of the asteroid referred to the sun in this rotating reference plane by $x _ { 1 } , ~ x _ { 2 } , ~ x _ { 3 }$ ； the velocity components by $y _ { 1 }$ ， $y _ { 2 } , y _ { 3 }$ ; and the speeds of the instantaneous rotation of the reference axes around these three axes by $\omega _ { 1 } , \omega _ { 2 } , \omega _ { 3 }$ The $x _ { 1 }$ -axis is chosen to point to the instantaneous position of the asteroid. Then,

$$
y _ { 1 } = \frac { d r } { d t } , \qquad y _ { 2 } = \omega _ { 3 } r , \qquad y _ { 3 } = - \omega _ { 2 } r .
$$

With $\delta x _ { 1 } = \delta r$ ， $\delta x _ { 2 } = r \delta \theta _ { 3 }$ ， $\delta x _ { 3 } = - r \delta \theta _ { 2 } ,$ Brown's equations of motionare

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r ( \omega _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( \omega _ { 3 } r \right) + \omega _ { 1 } \omega _ { 2 } r + \omega _ { 3 } \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle - \frac { d } { d t } \left( \omega _ { 2 } r \right) - \omega _ { 2 } \frac { d r } { d t } + \omega _ { 1 } \omega _ { 3 } r = - \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Let $\boldsymbol { I }$ be the inclination of the moving orbital plane on Jupiter's fxed orbital plane, $\theta$ be the angular distance of the ascending node of the asteroidal orbit on Jupiter's orbital plane or the ecliptic,and $v - \theta$ be the heliocentric angular distance of the asteroid counted from the common node of the two orbital planes. Then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \delta \theta _ { 1 } = \cos { ( v - \theta ) } \delta I + \sin { I } \sin { ( v - \theta ) } \delta \theta , } \\ & { \delta \theta _ { 2 } = - \sin { ( v - \theta ) } \delta I + \sin { I } \cos { ( v - \theta ) } \delta \theta , } \\ & { \delta \theta _ { 3 } = \delta v - ( 1 - \cos { I } ) \delta \theta . } \end{array}
$$

Since the $x _ { 3 }$ -axis is perpendicular to the orbital plane, we should have

$$
\omega _ { 2 } = 0 , \frac { d \theta _ { 2 } } { d t } = 0 , \frac { d \theta _ { 1 } } { d t } = \omega _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { \omega _ { 3 } r ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 2 } } .
$$

Hence, from the equations of motion,we obtain

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } , \qquad \frac { d } { d t } \left( r ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } \right) = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 3 } } ,
$$

$$
\omega _ { 3 } = \frac { d r } { d t } - \left( 1 - \cos I \right) \frac { d \theta } { d t } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d I } { d t } } \sin \left( v - \theta \right) - { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } \sin I \cos \left( v - \theta \right) = 0 \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d I } { d t } } \cos \left( v - \theta \right) + { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } \sin I \sin \left( v - \theta \right) = - { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 2 } } } } } \end{array}
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \partial F = \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \delta x _ { 1 } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 2 } } \delta x _ { 2 } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 3 } } \delta x _ { 3 } } } } \\ { ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle { \quad = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } \delta r + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 2 } } \delta \theta _ { 2 } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 3 } } \delta \theta _ { 3 } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \quad = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } \delta r + \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } \delta v + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } \delta \theta + \frac { \partial F } { \partial I } \delta I } , } } \end{array}

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 3 } } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } , \qquad - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta _ { 2 } } \sin { ( v - \theta ) } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial I } ,

$$

$$
\left[ { \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } } + ( 1 - \cos I ) { \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } } \right] \sin { ( v - \theta ) } + { \frac { \partial F } { \partial I } } \sin { I } \cos { ( v - \theta ) } = 0 .
$$

Thus the equations of motion for a Trojan asteroid are transformed to

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } , \qquad \frac { d } { d t } \left( r ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } \right) = \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \Gamma } { d t } = - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } } \left( \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } + \Gamma \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } \right) , \frac { d \theta } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Gamma } ,
$$

$$
\omega _ { 3 } = { \frac { d v } { d t } } - \Gamma { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } , \Gamma = 1 - \cos { I } .
$$

The disturbing function

$$
{ \cal F } = \frac { M } { r } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \Delta } - \frac { m ^ { \prime } r } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \cos { \cal H } , \qquad \Delta ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos { \cal H } ,
$$

cos $H = \cos { ( v - \theta ) }$ cos $( v ^ { \prime } - \theta ) + \cos I \sin ( v - \theta ) \sin ( v ^ { \prime } - \theta )$

$$
= \left( 1 - { \frac { \Gamma } { 2 } } \right) \cos { ( \upsilon - \upsilon ^ { \prime } ) } + { \frac { \Gamma } { 2 } } \cos { ( \upsilon + \upsilon ^ { \prime } - 2 \theta ) } ,
$$

has the property

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial v ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } = 0 ,
$$

where $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime }$ and $v ^ { \prime } - n ^ { \prime } t - \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ are functions of $n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ ， or functions of $n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ and $\scriptstyle { \overline { { \pmb { \varpi } } } } ^ { \prime }$ , if we neglect the perturbation of Jupiter's orbit itself. Hence,

$$
\frac { 1 } { n ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \varpi ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } = 0 .
$$

Accordingly,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d F } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } \frac { d r } { d t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } \frac { d v } { d t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } \frac { d \theta } { d t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Gamma } \frac { d \Gamma } { d t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial t } } \\ & { \qquad = \frac { \partial F } { \partial r } \frac { d r } { d t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial v } \left( \frac { d v } { d t } - n ^ { \prime } \right) + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } \frac { d \theta } { d t } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Gamma } \frac { d \Gamma } { d t } - n ^ { \prime } \Big ( \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \varpi ^ { \prime } } \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

or, if we substitute (96),

$$
{ \frac { d F } { d t } } = \left( { \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - r \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \right) { \frac { d r } { d t } } + \left( \omega _ { 3 } - n ^ { \prime } \right) { \frac { d } { d t } } \left( r ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } \right) - n ^ { \prime } \left( { \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } } + { \frac { \partial F } { \partial \varpi ^ { \prime } } } \right) .
$$

Integration of this equation gives

\frac { d r } { d t } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } ( \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 2 n ^ { \prime } \omega _ { 3 } ) = 2 { \cal F } + 2 n ^ { \prime } \int \left( \frac { \partial { \cal F } } { \partial \theta } + \frac { \partial { \cal F } } { \partial \varpi ^ { \prime } } \right) d t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

From the second and third equations of (96) we obtain

$$

Then (97) becomes

$$

Brown introduced the rational coordinates $r _ { 1 } , \ r _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ \Delta _ { 1 }$ and $t _ { 1 }$ ，such that

$$
r = a ^ { \prime } r _ { 1 } , \qquad r ^ { \prime } = a ^ { \prime } r _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \qquad \Delta = a ^ { \prime } \Delta _ { 1 } , \qquad n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } = t _ { 1 } ,

then (96), (97), (98) remain of the same form, the only difference being that $\scriptstyle { n ^ { \prime } }$ is replaced by unity. Since ${ \pmb F }$ enters in the equations of motion only through its derivatives, we take

These equations are satisfied for $t = v \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ by $1 / u = \Delta = r ^ { \prime } = 1$ ， $\Gamma = 0$ .This is the equilateral triangular equilibrium solution described in Section 3.4.If the asteroid is disturbed from this equilibrium solution, we obtain a solution with six arbitrary constants.Two of the integration constants - the mean longitude at the epoch and the mean motion - are replaced by the amplitude and the phase of the long-period free oscillation. This period depends on the period and the mass of Jupiter and also on the amplitude of the oscillation. Brown took this free oscillation in the case of zero eccentricity as the intermediary orbit for a Trojan asteroid.This period is about l2 times that of Jupiter's revolution.Then the solution is expanded in powers of the eccentricity. These terms are of period 1, $1 / 2 , 1 / 3 , \ldots$ of the period of Jupiter's revolution, and are of short period compared with the principal oscillation.

# 10.24BROWN'S INTERMEDIARY ORBIT

If $\scriptstyle e ^ { \prime } = 0$ ，then the solution is: $u = 1$ ， $t = v \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ } ;$ $\Gamma = 0$ Put $u = 1 + \delta u$ ， $t = v \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ } + \delta t ,$ and write the differential equations for the variations $\delta \boldsymbol { u }$ and $\delta t .$ The solution is an oscillation with two periods; the one is nearly equal to Jupiter's period corresponding to the principal elliptic term in the lunar theory (Section 9.2) and the other is of long period.As was discussed in Brown's theory of libration for the case $s = 1$ in Section 9.8,one of the periods is of the order unity,and the other of long period is of the order $\sqrt { m }$ Since $\delta \boldsymbol { u }$ is of the order $\sqrt { m }$ ， $_ { D u }$ is of the order m and $D ^ { 2 } u$ of the order $m ^ { 3 / 2 }$ . Thus $_ { D q }$ and $\pmb { D } \pmb { \theta }$ of $( 1 0 0 ) -$ (103)are of the order $m$ .We neglect quantities of the order $_ m$ ; then we have $u = q$ ，As the orbital inclination is moderate,we must take $\boldsymbol { \Gamma }$ into consideration.Then

$$

$$

$$

The constant $c$ fixes the amplitude,and the constant on the left-hand side fixes the phase of the free oscillation.

Under the above limitation, we now have

$$

The next approximation is to compute the terms depending on the first power of $e$ For that purpose we expand $R$ up to higher-order terms. Write

where $\rho$ is of the order $\sqrt { m }$ and $\tau , y$ contain only long-period terms. For $e = e ^ { \prime } = 0$ we have $y = \rho = 0 ; y$ / does not contain any term of the order $\sqrt { m }$ .We obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { R r ^ { \prime } = R _ { 0 } + \rho S - y T + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \rho ^ { 2 } A - \rho y B + \frac { 1 } { 2 } y ^ { 2 } C + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
R = m \biggl ( \frac { 1 } { \Delta } + \frac { \Delta ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - u - \frac { 1 } { 2 u ^ { 2 } } \biggr ) ,
$$

$$
\Delta ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { \cos H } { u } + 1 , \cos H = \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \Gamma } \right) \cos \tau ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad \mathcal { C } = \pi \Big ( \frac { 1 } { - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \delta } \boldsymbol { \cdot } \ln \boldsymbol { \cdot } \boldsymbol { \cdot } \boldsymbol { } \alpha \Big ) , } \\ & { \quad T = m \Big ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } \Big ) \Big ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \boldsymbol { \cdot } \boldsymbol { \Sigma } \Big ) \sin \tau , } \\ & { \quad A - m \Big ( - \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - 2 + \Delta ^ { 2 } \Big ) , } \\ & { \quad R = m \Big ( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - 1 \Big ) \Big ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \boldsymbol { \cdot } \boldsymbol { \Sigma } \Big ) \sin \tau , } \\ & { \quad C = m \Big ( - \frac { 3 \Gamma } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } + \frac { 2 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta } + 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Delta ^ { 3 } \Big ) , } \\ & { \quad V - m \frac { \Gamma } { 2 } \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { \tau } \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta } + 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Delta ^ { 2 } \Big ) . } \\ & { \quad W - m \Big ( - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \Delta ^ { 2 } \Big ) . } \end{array}
$$

Now we evaluate the derivatives of $\scriptstyle { R }$ ，not for $u = 1 , \Gamma = \gamma ;$ but for $u = u _ { 0 } , \Gamma = \Gamma _ { 0 } ,$ .Up to the terms of the order $m ^ { 2 }$ ,let $R$ be

$$
R = R _ { 0 } + u _ { 1 } S - t _ { 1 } T = R _ { 0 } + q _ { 0 } E S - 2 ( 1 - x ) D E \cdot T .
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { Q = q _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 4 } ( 1 + 2 C + \frac { 1 } { 2 } V ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { P = D ^ { 2 } Q + q _ { 0 } A + \frac { 4 - 6 x } { q _ { 0 } } C + 2 D B + 2 \frac { V } { q _ { 0 } } ; } } } \end{array}
$$

then the first equation of (110) is now written

$$
D ^ { 2 } ( E Q ) + ( E Q ) ( 1 - P ) = 0 .
$$

To the order $\pmb { m }$ ,the solution is

$$
E = e \cos { ( v - \varpi ) } ,
$$

$$
e = { \frac { \eta } { Q } } \biggl ( 1 + { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } P \biggr ) = \eta q _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 4 } \biggl ( 1 + { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } A - C \biggr ) ,
$$

$$
\varpi = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } D ^ { - 1 } P + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

where $\eta$ is the integration constant.

The second equation of (110) is written

$$
D q _ { 1 } = ( 4 T - 2 B ) E + 4 C D E = ( 2 B - 4 T ) D ^ { 2 } E + 4 C D E .
$$

The solution is

$$
q _ { 1 } = 4 C E + ( 2 B - 4 T ) D E .
$$

Hence,

$$
u _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } + / p _ { 0 } q _ { 0 } E = ( q _ { 0 } + S + 4 C ) E + ( 2 B - 4 T ) D E .
$$

Similarly we integrate the second and the following equations of (110). The terms depending on $e ^ { \prime }$ are also computed in a similar manner. The result is obtained up to the order ${ \sqrt { m } } ,$ ,in particular up to the order $\pmb { m }$ for the secular part of the motion of the perihelion. Let $q _ { 0 }$ be the part consisting of the long-period terms and the constant terms of $q$ ; then,

$$
q = q _ { 0 } ( 1 + E ) , ~ D t = \frac { 1 } { q _ { 0 } ^ { 3 / 2 } ( 1 + E ) ^ { 2 } } \cdot
$$

The variable $\pmb { \tau }$ defined by $D t _ { 0 } = 1 - D \tau = 1 - x$ contains the longperiod and the constant terms of $\pmb { t }$ Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { t = v - \tau + ( 1 - x ) [ - 2 e \sin { ( v - \varpi ^ { \prime \prime } ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] , } \\ { u = ( 1 + \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ) ( 1 + E ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

In an elliptic motion,

$$
u = \frac { 1 } { a } \frac { 1 + e \cos { ( v - \varpi ) } } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } , \qquad n \frac { d t } { d v } = \frac { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } { [ 1 + e \cos { ( v - \varpi ) } ] ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Hence, in our case, the expression is in the same form as in an elliptic motion,but the major axis is $1 + { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } x$ and the mean motion is $1 \ : - \ : x .$ The difference is that all short-period terms in the expression for the mean longitude in terms of the true longitude are multiplied by the factor $1 - x .$

# I0.26PLANETARY PERTURBATION

We consider only the frst-order perturbations due to other planets. Put ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } ( 1 + E ) = { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } }$ ， $u = { \mathbf { p } } q = { \mathbf { p } } ( 1 + E ) q$ ; then the equations (100) are written

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \cal D } ^ { 2 } { \bf p } + { \bf p } = 1 + \frac { \partial { \cal R } } { \partial u } + 2 { \cal D } \Big ( \frac 1 u \frac { \partial { \cal R } } { \partial v } \Big ) + \frac { { \cal D } u } u \cdot \frac 1 u \frac { \partial { \cal R } } { \partial v } , } } \\ { { { \cal D } q = - \frac { 2 } { { \bf p } ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial { \cal R } } { \partial v } , } } \\ { { { \cal D } t = q ^ { - 3 / 2 } { \bf p } ^ { - 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

From (112) and (l13) we obtain

$$
\frac { D ^ { 2 } t } { D t } = \frac { 3 } { \bf p } \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } - 2 \frac { D { \bf p } } { \bf p } .
$$

An important property of $\partial R / \partial u$ and $\partial R / \partial v$ in (111), (112),and (114) is that they contain $\boldsymbol { r } , \boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime } , \boldsymbol { v } , \boldsymbol { v } ^ { \prime }$ only in the combinations of $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime } \pmb { u }$ and $\boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } ^ { \prime }$ ， if the short-period terms containing $2 \theta$ are neglected.

Suppose that there are additional terms $\alpha R _ { \mathbf { p } }$ and $\alpha R _ { q }$ in (111） and (112),respectively,and an additional factor $1 + \alpha R _ { t }$ to (113), where $\propto$ is a parameter. The quantity $D t$ is a function of $\mathbf { p }$ and $q$ only. Let the subscript $\pmb { \alpha }$ and the prefix δ indicate the additions due to these modifications. Then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( D ^ { 2 } + 1 ) { \bf p } _ { \alpha } = \delta \frac { \partial R } { \partial u } + 2 D \ : \delta \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \bigg ) + \frac { D u } { u } \delta \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \bigg ) \quad \quad } \\ { + \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \delta \frac { D u } { u } + \alpha R _ { \bf p } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle { D q _ { \alpha } = - \frac { 2 q } { \mathbf { P } } \delta \Big ( \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \Big ) - \frac { 2 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \frac { \partial q } { \mathbf { P } } + \alpha R _ { \alpha } } } \\ & { \displaystyle { \frac { D t _ { \alpha } } { D t } = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q } - 2 \frac { \mathbf { P } _ { \alpha } } { \mathbf { P } } + \alpha R _ { t } } } \\ & { \displaystyle { \delta \frac { D ^ { 2 } t } { D t } = \frac { 3 } { \mathbf { P } } \delta \Big ( \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \Big ) - \frac { 3 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \frac { \mathbf { P } _ { \alpha } } { \mathbf { P } ^ { 2 } } - 2 \delta \frac { D \mathbf { p } } { \mathbf { P } } } } \\ & { \qquad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad + \alpha D R _ { t } - \frac { 3 \alpha } { 2 } \frac { \partial R } { q } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \delta \frac { \partial R } { \partial u } = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R } { \partial u ^ { 2 } } u _ { \alpha } - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R } { \partial u \partial v } t _ { \alpha } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \delta \Big ( \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \Big ) = \frac { \partial } { \partial u } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \Big ) u _ { \alpha } - \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R } { \partial v ^ { 2 } } t _ { \alpha } . } } \end{array}
$$

We classify groups of terms:

(i）Terms with period nearly equal to Jupiter's period $2 \pi / n ^ { \prime }$ . The operation $D ^ { 2 } + 1$ gives rise to a small divisor. Hence $\mathbf { P } \alpha$ and $t _ { \alpha }$ have the same small divisor. Since $D t - 1$ and $\mathbf { p } - 1$ are multiplied by a small divisor,the principal part of（ll7）becomes $D t _ { \alpha } = - 2 \mathbf { p } _ { \alpha }$ As the first approximation,we obtain $D t _ { \alpha } = - 2 \mathbf { p } _ { \alpha } .$ $t _ { \alpha } = 2 D \mathbf { p } _ { \alpha }$ . The equation for $\mathbf { p } _ { \alpha }$ is

$$
{ \cal D } ^ { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { Q } { \hbar } { \bf p } _ { \alpha } \bigg ) + ( 1 - P ) \bigg ( \frac { Q } { \hbar } { \bf p } _ { \alpha } \bigg ) = { \mathrm { a k n o w n f u n c t i o n o f } } v .
$$

The solution is $Q \mathbf { p } _ { \alpha } / \mathbf { \nabla } \phi$ . Thus the short-period terms can be treated similarly to those in the ordinary planetary theory.

(ii） Terms with period longer than Jupiter's.These terms are further divided into two subclasses: those with periods of the same order as the libration,and those with periods longer than the libration.

The first approximation to $t _ { \alpha }$ is obtained from (118), (119):

$$
D ^ { 2 } t _ { \alpha } = - 3 \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R } { \partial v ^ { 2 } } t _ { \alpha } + \alpha D R _ { t } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \alpha R _ { q } - 2 \alpha D R _ { \mathbf { p } } .
$$

In the ordinary planetary theory, $\partial ^ { 2 } R / \partial v ^ { 2 }$ contains no constant term and hence is neglected. But here, since $\mathcal { D }$ is of the order $\sqrt { m } , \ \partial ^ { 2 } R / \partial v ^ { 2 }$ has a constant part. After $t _ { \alpha }$ is obtained,(l16) gives $q _ { \alpha }$ . The principal term of $3 ~ \partial ^ { 2 } R / \partial v ^ { 2 }$ is $\nu ^ { 2 }$ ,where the libration period is denoted by $2 \pi / \nu$ . If a periodic term $\pmb { c }$ sin $( s v + s _ { 0 } )$ on the right-hand side of this equation is due to the disturbing force,then

$$
( D ^ { 2 } + \nu ^ { 2 } ) t _ { \alpha } = c \sin { ( s \upsilon + s _ { 0 } ) }
$$

is the approximation,and the solution is

$$
t _ { \alpha } = \frac { c } { \nu ^ { 2 } - s ^ { 2 } } \sin { ( s v + s _ { 0 } ) } .
$$

If $s / \nu$ is small enough, the divisor becomes $\nu ^ { 2 }$ for $s \to 0$ ,and the terms of very long period do not give rise to any term with a very large coefficient,as the period becomes longer.As $t _ { \alpha }$ is an addition to the difference of the heliocentric longitudes of Jupiter and the asteroid,the action of Jupiter gives rise to terms of very long period in the motion of the asteroid;moreover, it prevents any term of a very long period from being produced by the direct action of other planets.If $s$ and ${ \pmb \nu }$ are of the same order of magnitude, then the small divisor becomes nearly zero as $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ tends to $\pm \nu$

Since the expansion of $\partial ^ { 2 } R / \partial v ^ { 2 }$ contains terms of the period $2 \pi / \nu$ due to the libration,we must take into account these periodic terms with argument $\phi _ { 0 } = \nu v + v _ { 0 }$ 、Thus the right-hand member is a combination of two kinds of term,each with $s v + s _ { 0 }$ and $\phi _ { 0 }$ as arguments.The longitude ofa planet other than Jupiter is expressed in terms of $t = v - \tau ,$ $\tau = \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ } + \delta \tau$ ，and is expanded in powers of $\delta \tau$ with arguments sv + so + i𝜙o,i = O,±1,±2,....We compute the effect of such terms by referring to the equation for $t _ { \alpha }$ .Put $\tau + Z$ in place of $t$ in $D ^ { 2 } \tau +$ $3 ( \partial R / \partial v ) = 0$ and retain only the first power of $z$ ； then we obtain an equation of the form

$$
D ^ { 2 } Z + 3 C Z = c \sin { ( s v + s ^ { \prime } \tau + s _ { 0 } ) } .
$$

The solution can be computed in the form

$$
\tau = \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ } + \tau _ { 0 } + \tau _ { 1 } \cos \phi _ { 0 } + \tau _ { 2 } \cos 2 \phi _ { 0 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Let the two particular solutions of the homogeneous equation with ${ \mathfrak { c } } = 0$ be

$$
Z = \frac { \partial \tau } { \partial b } = Z _ { 1 } , \qquad Z = \frac { \partial \tau } { \partial \nu _ { 0 } } = \frac { \partial \tau } { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } = Z _ { 2 } ,
$$

where $^ { b }$ is the coefficient of the term for the libration. Then the equations for the variation of the constants $^ { b }$ and $\nu _ { 0 }$ for obtaining the solution of the nonhomogeneous equation with $c \neq 0$ are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { D b = - \frac { c } { s ^ { \prime } B \nu } \frac { \partial } { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } \cos { ( s v + s ^ { \prime } \tau + s _ { 0 } ) } , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { D \nu _ { 0 } = \frac { c } { s ^ { \prime } B \nu } \frac { \partial } { \partial b } \cos { ( s v + s ^ { \prime } \tau + s _ { 0 } ) } , } } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
B = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial b } \left( \sum _ { i } i \tau _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot
$$

The solution is expanded in the form

$$
( s v + s ^ { \prime } \tau + s _ { 0 } ) = \sum _ { i } c _ { i } \cos { ( s v + i \phi _ { 0 } + s _ { i } ) }
$$

$$
( i = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

If we substitute this expansion in the differential equations, then we see that the terms with argument $s v + s _ { 0 }$ disappear completely in the solution for $b$ ，Hence we reach a very conspicuous conclusion that the amplitude of the libration is never increased by the presence of terms of very long period due to the action of other planets.This conclusion is very important from the point of view of the stability of the motion.

Brouwer (l933) worked out Brown's theory for Achilles, and Eckert (1933) for Hector.

# 10.27BROWN'S REVISED THEORY

Brown (l929） later published a revised method of his theory of a Trojan asteroid after his experience of working on the Trojan problem.

Let v be the orbital true longitude counted on the instantaneous orbital plane in the sense of Hansen. This time Brown took v as the independent variable; $\theta$ is the longitude of the ascending node of the instantaneous orbital plane on the fixed plane,and $v - \theta$ is the argument of latitude. Then,

$$
\frac { d \mathbf { v } } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( \boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { \theta } \right) + \cos I \frac { d \boldsymbol { \theta } } { d t } = \frac { d \boldsymbol { v } } { d t } - \boldsymbol { \Gamma } \frac { d \boldsymbol { \theta } } { d t } ,
$$

and

$$
{ \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { q } } } = r ^ { 2 } { \frac { d \mathbf { v } } { d t } } .
$$

Write $D = d / d \mathbf { v }$ .The equations of motion now take the form

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { D q = - \displaystyle \frac { 2 q ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } , \qquad ( D ^ { 2 } + 1 ) u - q = q \Bigl ( \displaystyle \frac { \partial R } { \partial u } - \displaystyle \frac { D u } { u ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \Bigr ) , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { D t = \left( \displaystyle \frac { q } { \mu } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } , \qquad } } & { { D v = 1 + \Gamma D \theta , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { D ( \Gamma q ^ { - 1 / 2 } ) = - \displaystyle \frac { q ^ { 1 / 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \partial R } { \partial \theta } , \qquad } } & { { D \theta = \displaystyle \frac { q } { u ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \partial R } { \partial \Gamma } . } } \end{array}
$$

From (122) we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } D \bigg [ \frac { ( D u ) ^ { 2 } + u ^ { 2 } } { q } - 2 u \bigg ] = \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } + D u \frac { \partial R } { \partial u } .
$$

These equations (l22)-(l25) are those of the former method,in which we now put $\Gamma D \theta = 0$ in the factor $1 - \Gamma D \theta$ and replace $v$ by $\mathbf { v }$ on the left-hand side.

The latitude $B$ on the fixed plane depends only on $\mathbf { v }$ , such that

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } \left( \sin B \right) = { \frac { \partial } { \partial \mathbf { v } } } \left( \sin B \right) \cdot { \frac { d \mathbf { v } } { d t } } .
$$

Since

$$
\sin B = \sin I \sin ( v - \theta ) = \sin I \sin \left( v - \int \cos I d \theta \right) ,
$$

we have

$$
D ( \sin B ) = \sin I \cos { ( v - \theta ) } ,
$$

or, referring to (120),

$$
\begin{array} { r } { D ^ { 2 } ( \sin B ) = - \sin I \sin ( v - \theta ) ( 1 - \cos I { \cdot } D \theta ) \phantom { \cos ( v - \theta ) } } \\ { + \cos I \cos ( v - \theta ) { \cdot } D I . } \end{array}
$$

From $D ( \sin B ) = \partial ( \sin B ) / \partial \mathbf { v }$ we obtain

$$
D I \cdot \cos I \sin { ( v - \theta ) } - \cos I \sin I { \cdot } D \theta \cdot \cos { ( v - \theta ) } = 0 .
$$

Eliminating $_ { D I }$ from these equations, we obtain

$$
\left( D ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \sin B = { \frac { \sin I \cos I } { \sin \left( v - \theta \right) } } D \theta = { \frac { \sin I \cos I } { \sin \left( v - \theta \right) } } { \frac { q } { u ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \Gamma } } .
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { P = \displaystyle { \frac { \partial R } { \partial u } } - \frac { D u } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } , \qquad Q = - \frac { q } { u ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { Z = \displaystyle { - \frac { q } { u ^ { 2 } \sin { ( v - \theta ) } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \Gamma } } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then the equations of motion are transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { r } { D q = 2 q Q , \qquad ( D ^ { 2 } + 1 ) u = q ( 1 + P ) , \qquad D t = \left( \frac { q } { \mu } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { D v = 1 - ( 1 - \cos I ) Z \sin ( v - \theta ) , } } \\ { { ( D ^ { 2 } + 1 ) \sin B = ( \cos I \sin I ) Z . } } \end{array}
$$

The equations (l24) are used only for determining the second approximation for $u , v , B$ .The equations (130) and (l31) are used for computing the planetary perturbations.

For $P = Q = Z = 0 _ { : }$ we have

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { q } } = a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) , \qquad u = q + q e \cos { ( \bf v - \varpi \big ) } ,
$$

$$
\sin B = \sin I \sin ( \mathbf { v } - { \boldsymbol { \theta } } ) ,
$$

$$
I , \theta = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad v = { \bf v } ,
$$

and

$$
D t = \left( \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \mu } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } { [ 1 + e \cos { ( \bf v - \varpi \big / z ) ] ^ { 2 } } } \cdot
$$

When $\pmb { n }$ is determined by

$$
n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = \mu ,
$$

the equation (l33) is writen

$$
\boldsymbol { n } t + \epsilon = \mathbf { v } - E _ { f } ,
$$

where $E _ { f }$ denotes the equation of center expressed in terms of the true anomaly ${ \bf v } - \varpi$ Thus,

$$
n t + \epsilon - \varpi = M - \epsilon \sin M , \tan \frac { M } { 2 } = \Bigl ( \frac { 1 - \epsilon } { 1 + \epsilon } \Bigr ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \tan \frac { \bf v - \varpi } { 2 } \cdot
$$

The arbitrary constants are the Keplerian elements for an elliptic motion. The integration constant for $D v = 1$ is determined so that $v = \mathbf { v }$

If we substitute these elliptic values in $\pmb { P }$ ， $Q$ ,and $z$ ,then these perturbation terms contain $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime }$ and $v ^ { \prime }$ besides $u , v , \Gamma$ and $\pmb \theta .$ The elliptic values are taken for $\scriptstyle { \pmb { r } } ^ { \prime }$ and $v ^ { \prime }$ ，then $r ^ { \prime } / a ^ { \prime }$ and $v ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ are functions of $e ^ { \prime }$ and $g ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ . Written as functions of $\mathbf { v }$ by (134）or (135） we obtain

$$
g ^ { \prime } = f _ { 1 } - \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } E _ { f } = f _ { 1 } - \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } ( 2 e \sin f + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) ,
$$

where

$$
f = { \mathbf v } - \varpi , ~ f _ { 1 } = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } \left( { \mathbf v } - \epsilon \right) + \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime } .
$$

Since ${ \pmb v } = { \pmb v }$ in the first approximation,we have $v = f + \varpi$ and $1 / r = q + q e \sin f .$ Hence $P , Q , Z$ are expressed in the form of sine or cosine series with multiples of $f$ and $f _ { 1 }$ as arguments,and their coefficients depend on the longitudes of the perihelion and the node,on the eccentricity and the inclination,and on the ratio of the mean distances.

The right-hand members of (130) and (131) are functions of v only, because $D f = 1$ and $D f _ { 1 } = n ^ { \prime } / n$ .The integral of the first equation of (122) for $q$ is

$$
q = q _ { 0 } + D ^ { - 1 } ( 2 q Q ) = q _ { 0 } + \delta q ,
$$

where $q _ { 0 }$ is the arbitrary constant. If we substitute this expression for $q$ in the second equation of (122) for ${ \pmb u } .$ ,and integrate, then

$$
{ u } = { u } _ { 0 } + \delta { u } ,
$$

$$
u _ { 0 } = q _ { 0 } + q _ { 0 } e _ { 0 } \cos { ( \mathrm { v } - \varpi _ { 0 } ) } , \qquad \delta u = \frac { 1 } { D ^ { 2 } + 1 } ( \delta q + q _ { 0 } P ) ,
$$

where $\scriptstyle { e _ { 0 } }$ and $\varpi _ { 0 }$ are arbitrary constants. Next we substitute these expressions for $\boldsymbol { u }$ and $q$ in the first equation of (123) for $D t$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { D t = \left( \frac { q _ { 0 } } { \mu } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { 1 } { u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \delta q } { q _ { 0 } } - 2 \frac { \delta u } { u _ { 0 } } \right) } \\ & { \quad = \left( \frac { q _ { 0 } } { \mu } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { 1 } { u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { q _ { 0 } } { \mu } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { 1 } { u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \delta q ( 1 + e _ { 0 } \cos f _ { 0 } ) - 2 \delta u \right) . } \end{array}
$$

The solution of the second equation of (131) is

$$
v = { \bf v } - \Gamma _ { 0 } D ^ { - 1 } Z \sin { ( v - \theta ) } ,
$$

where the additive arbitrary constant is equated to zero. Finally we obtain

$$
\sin B = \sin I _ { 0 } \sin ( { \bf v } - { \bf \nabla } \theta _ { 0 } ) - \cos I _ { 0 } \sin I _ { 0 } \frac { 1 } { D ^ { 2 } + 1 } Z ,

where $I _ { 0 }$ and $\theta _ { 0 }$ are the arbitrary constants.

10.28BROWN'S THEORY OF JUPITER'S EIGHTH SATELLITE

The eighth satellite of Jupiter was discovered in 1908 and the orbit was immediately calculated by Crommelin (l908).Later, Cowell and Crommelin (1908b) and then Cowell, Crommelin,and Davidson (1909) computed the motion by numerical integration of the rectangular coordinates (Section lO.1) based on the observations near the oppositions of 1908 and l909.However,the computed ephemerides were not in accord with the observations of 1910.Crommelin (l91O） recomputed the ephemerides.The satellite was not observed in the years l9l9-1921, but was rediscovered in l922.In 1922 Miss Boeva (1933，1936） of Leningrad recomputed the ephemerides for l932 and 1935 by Numerov's method of integration (Section l0.1).Trousset (1913) began work on the theory in l9l3.The diffculty is that the satellite,similarly to J IX, J XI, and J XII, is greatly influenced by the solar attraction, being far from Jupiter, and also has high eccentricity and high inclination to Jupiter's orbital plane, not to mention its retrograde motion. Jackson (1913) had recourse to Delaunay's theory but abandoned it because of the slow convergence.Trousset (Section 10.32） used Andoyer's method (Section l0.30),a modification of Hill's lunar theory,by starting with rectangular coordinates. Still the satellite was lost again. Recently, Grosch (1948),Koulikov (1950),and Proskurine (1950) computed the motion.

Brown (1930) applied his revised theory (Section l0.27) to the motion of this J VIII.

Let $m ^ { \prime }$ be the solar mass and $\pmb { \mu }$ be the sum of the masses of Jupiter and the satellite.We have $n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = \mu , n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } = m ^ { \prime } + \mu$ Consider the equations (132),(133),and (134) of Section 10.27,where

$$
\mu R = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } r ^ { 2 } \biggr ( \frac { 3 } { 2 } S ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \biggr ) + \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 4 } } r ^ { 3 } \biggr ( \frac { 5 } { 2 } S ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } S \biggr ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
S = \cos { H } = \Big ( 1 - \frac { \Gamma } { 2 } \Big ) \cos { ( v - v ^ { \prime } ) } + \frac { \Gamma } { 2 } \cos { ( v + v ^ { \prime } - 2 \theta ) } .
$$

The frst term $m ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime }$ in the expression (136a） for $\pmb { R }$ is dropped.The third term is small compared with the second and is neglected.In the expressions (136b） for $S ^ { 2 }$ the terms containing $\pmb \theta$ are neglected. The functions of $\gamma ^ { \prime }$ and $v ^ { \prime }$ involve the two angles $- n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ and $- n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ The terms containing the second angle are neglected, which amounts to neglecting the solar eccentricity.Thus the part of $\scriptstyle { R }$ we retain at first is

When $\pmb { t }$ is taken as the independent variable, we must consider the two angles $2 ( n t + \epsilon ) - 2 ( n ^ { \prime } t - \epsilon ^ { \prime } )$ and $n t + \epsilon - \varpi _ { 1 } n t - \varpi _ { 0 }$ under the above limitation,where $\varpi _ { 1 }$ and $\varpi _ { 0 }$ are constants. We have

Owing to the presence of terms whose periods are long compared with the period of revolution, small divisors appear and the approximating process is slow. Brown and Brouwer (l937),and Brouwer (1955), had to invent several devices for facilitating the procedure. The coefficients of the terms of shorter period should be computed before the coefficients of the terms of longer period are determined,in contrast to ordinary planetary theory, because the relative effect of the short-period terms on the long-period terms is,according to Brouwer, much greater than the inverse effect.

The disturbing function

$$
T = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \mu } { \mathrm { { U } } ^ { - 4 } } \mathrm { { } } \mathrm { { u } } ^ { - 4 } \biggl [ \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } S ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) + q ^ { - 1 / 4 } { \mathrm { U } } ^ { - 1 } \frac { a ^ { 4 } } { r ^ { \prime 4 } } \left( \frac { 5 } { 2 } S ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } S \right) \biggr ]
