is written in the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \begin{array} { r l } & { { \it { 1 } = { \it { \Psi } } \mathrm { { 1 } } \mathrm { { } } _ { 0 } + { \it { \Psi } } \mathrm { { 1 } } _ { \theta } + { \it { \Psi } } \mathrm { { 1 } } _ { \alpha } , } } \\ { { { \frac { \mu } { m ^ { \prime } } } \mathrm { { } } \mathrm { { } } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \frac { { } } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \frac { } { } } { \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \frac { } { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \frac { } { } } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \left[ \left( { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } - { \mathrm { ~ } \Gamma } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Gamma ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( { \mathrm { 1 } } - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \mathrm { } } \Gamma \right) ^ { 2 } \cos { ( 2 v - 2 v ^ { \prime } ) } \right] , } } \end{array} }  \\ { { { \frac { \mu } { m ^ { \prime } } } \mathrm { { } } \mathrm { } } \mathrm { { } } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } } \mathrm { { } } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { }  \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \Omega  _ { \mathrm { { } } } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } { } \mathrm { } \left\{ \Gamma  { \bigl ( 1 - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Gamma \bigr ) { \mathrm { } } { \mathrm { } } \Gamma { \bigl [ \cos { ( 2 v - 2 \theta ) } + \cos { ( 2 v ^ { \prime } - 2 \theta ) } \bigr ] } \right. } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \overline { { { \tau } } } ^ { 4 } T _ { \alpha } = q ^ { - 1 / 4 } { \bf u } ^ { - 1 } \displaystyle \frac { \alpha ^ { 4 } } { r ^ { \prime 4 } } [ \frac 5 8 ( 1 - \frac 1 2 { \Gamma } ) ^ { 3 } \cos ( 3 v - 3 v ^ { \prime } )  } } } \\ { { {  \qquad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad +  ( \frac 5 8 - \frac { 3 3 } { 1 6 } { \Gamma } + \frac { 7 5 } { 3 2 } { \Gamma } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 4 5 } { 6 4 } { \Gamma } ^ { 3 } ) \cos ( v - v   } } } \end{array}
$$

The terms depending on $\pmb \theta$ are obtained from

$$
D Z = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma _ { 0 } } \frac { \partial T _ { \theta } } { \partial \theta _ { 0 } } = \frac { 2 - \Gamma _ { 0 } } { \mathrm { \bf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } } \left[ k _ { 1 } \sin \left( 2 v _ { 0 } - 2 \theta _ { 0 } \right) + k _ { 2 } \sin \left( 2 v _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - 2 \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right]
$$

$$
\mathrm { \sf ~ Q } _ { \theta } ^ { \prime } = \frac { 4 - 2 \Gamma _ { 0 } } { \mathrm { \bf ~ U } _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } } k _ { 1 } \sin { ( 2 v _ { 0 } - 2 \theta _ { 0 } ) } + \frac { \Gamma _ { 0 } } { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Gamma _ { 0 } } D \mathrm { \sf ~ Q } _ { 0 } . \cal Z ,
$$

$$
{ \bf u } _ { \theta } ^ { \prime } = - 2 { \bf u } _ { 0 } T _ { \theta } + 2 { \bf u } _ { 0 } \Gamma _ { 0 } \frac { \partial T _ { 0 } } { \partial \Gamma _ { 0 } } Z , \qquad { \bf \nabla } { u } _ { \theta } ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 - \Gamma _ { 0 } } { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Gamma _ { 0 } } T _ { \theta } .
$$

The series are of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \mathsf { \Gamma } _ { \mathrm { s i n } } ^ { \cos } \left( 2 F + 2 j \xi + i l \right) } & { } & { \binom { j = 0 , \pm 1 , \cdots , \pm 4 } { i = 0 , \pm 1 , \cdots , \pm 8 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $2 F$ is the value of $2 v _ { 0 } - 2 \theta _ { 0 }$ without its periodic terms,and $2 \xi$ ， $\mathbf { \xi } _  l , $ and $2 F$ are expressed as linear functions of $\mathbf { v }$ ,and $\pmb \theta$ and $v$ as sums of linear functions of $\mathbf { v }$ and periodic terms.

The parallactic terms are obtained by taking the part $T _ { \alpha }$ from the disturbing function. They are expanded in series with the argument $\psi = v _ { 0 } - n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime }$

The solar inequalities with the factor $e ^ { \prime }$ are obtained by taking the part $T _ { \eta }$ depending on $\psi$ and $g ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t - \epsilon ^ { \prime } + \varpi ^ { \prime }$ .They have arguments of the form $2 j \xi + i l + k l ^ { \prime } ( j = 0 , 1 , \ldots ; i = 0 , \pm 1 , \ldots \pm 6 ; k = \pm 1 _ { r }$ ).

Exactly the same method was applied to the motion of the ninth satellite of Jupiter by Hori (1957, i957a, 1958). He had to carry out several approximations over and over again. The theory is more complicated than that of J VIII, because $\textbf { J } \mathbf { I X }$ is even farther from Jupiter than J VIII. He compared his result with the elements given by Nicholson (1944). On the other hand,Koulikov (l950) applied a numerical method.

The Hill-Brown lunar theory (Chapter 11） was applied to the motion of J VII by Tokmalayeva (1956), to the motion ofJ VIII by Proskurine (1949,1950),and to the asteroid Ceres by Proskurine (1952,1962). Proskurine (1955）also discussed the irregularity of the motion of J VI caused by the sun. Lemekhova (l96l) studied the motion of J X, using Delaunay's method.Musen，Maury，and Charnow (Section 10.10) greatly improved the theory of the motion, by the use of the modified Hansen method. The programming of the computation has been published by Charnow, Musen, and Maury (1968).

# 10.29KOVALEVSKY's NUMERICAL METHOD

As already stated,Brown (l930) had his own method of numerical harmonic analysis；further he improved it with Brouwer (Brown and Brouwer，1937)．Kovalevsky (1958，1959,1959a) invented a more refined method and applied it to the motion of J VIII.

Let $f ( t )$ be a certain function of time, tabulated in a time interval $t _ { 0 } - t _ { 1 }$ and represented by the Fourier series

$$
f ( t ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { i j k } \cos { ( i x + j y + k z ) } ,
$$

where $x = n _ { x } t + x _ { 0 } , y = n _ { y } t + y _ { 0 } , z = n _ { z } t + z _ { 0 } .$ Suppose that we know which terms are important and hence cannot be neglected. Let the number of such important terms be $\mathbf { x }$ ； we must find their coefficients. We give, for that purpose, $\mathbf { P }$ values to $t$ in the interval $t _ { 0 } ~ - ~ t _ { 1 }$ ,and compute $\mathbf { P }$ values of $f ( t _ { \mathfrak { p } } )$ by interpolation of the table. Then we can compute N unknown coefficients $A _ { i j k }$ by solving $\mathbf { P }$ equations

$$
\sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \sum _ { k } A _ { i j k } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } = f ( t _ { p } )
$$

by the method ofleast squares, where $a _ { p } ^ { i j k }$ is the value of cos $( i x + j y + k z )$ for $t = t _ { p }$

If $x , y , z$ are incommensurable, then cos $( i x + j y + k z )$ are linearly independent for all combinations of $i , j , k$ ； and the determinant of the linear system does not vanish. If there is a relation $\begin{array} { r } { i _ { 0 } x + j _ { 0 } y + k _ { 0 } z = 0 . } \end{array}$ ， then

$$
( i x + j y + k z ) = \cos { [ ( i + \lambda i _ { 0 } ) x + ( j + \lambda j _ { 0 } ) y + ( k + \lambda k _ { 0 } ) z ] } .
$$

If two of such combinations are taken as the unknown quantities, then there would be indeterminacy. Let $\alpha _ { 1 }$ and $\alpha _ { 2 }$ be two such arguments; then the corresponding determinant has for its general term

$$
\sum _ { p } ^ { ( \mathrm { P } ) } \left( \cos \alpha _ { 1 } \right) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } \qquad \mathrm { a n d } \qquad \sum _ { p } ^ { ( \mathrm { P } ) } \left( \cos \alpha _ { 2 } \right) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } .
$$

Since cos $\alpha _ { 1 } = \cos \alpha _ { 2 } ,$ these are identical and the determinant is zero. Suppose that $( \cos \alpha _ { 1 } ) _ { p }$ and $( \cos \alpha _ { 2 } ) _ { p }$ are nearly equal for all observations. This occurs when these arguments are very nearly equal to $t _ { 0 }$ ,and when the time interval $t _ { 1 } ~ - ~ t _ { 0 }$ is small compared with the period of ${ \boldsymbol { \alpha } } _ { 1 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } { \boldsymbol { \alpha } } _ { 2 }$ Then two columns of the determinant are nearly equal. By subtracting these two columns one from the other the determinant is equal to a determinant of which one column is

$$
\sum _ { p } ^ { \left( \mathrm { P } \right) } \left( \cos \alpha _ { 1 } - \cos \alpha _ { 2 } \right) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } ,
$$

and is small, of the order of sin $( \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha _ { 2 } ) _ { p }$ .Hence the problem is indeterminate.

We can generalize this to the case of a mixed series

$$
{ \mathrm { \Omega } } ^ { \prime } \rangle = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \sum _ { k } \begin{array} { l } { { \sum _ { i j k } { \cos { \left( i x + j y + k z \right) } } + B _ { i j k } \sin { \left( i x + j y + k z \right) } } } \end{array} .
$$

Four columns of the determinant corresponding to the two arguments ${ \pmb { \alpha _ { 1 } } }$ and $\alpha _ { 2 }$ have general terms of the form

$$
( \cos \alpha _ { 1 } ) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } , \quad \sum _ { p } ^ { \mathrm { ( P ) } } ( \sin \alpha _ { 1 } ) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } , \quad \sum _ { p } ^ { \mathrm { ( P ) } } ( \cos \alpha _ { 2 } ) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } , \quad \sum _ { p } ^ { \mathrm { ( P ) } } ( \sin \alpha _ { 2 } ) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k }
$$

Suppose that $\alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha _ { 1 } + \phi$ where $\phi$ is constant. The third column has general terms of the form

$$
\sum _ { p } ^ { ( \mathrm { P } ) } \left( \cos \alpha _ { 1 } \right) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } \cos \phi - \sum _ { p } ^ { ( \mathrm { P } ) } ( \sin \alpha _ { 1 } ) _ { p } a _ { p } ^ { i j k } \sin \phi ,
$$

and is a linear combination of the first two. The determinant is still zero.

Thus two columns are very nearly equal if we have $\alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha _ { 1 } + \epsilon + \phi ;$ where $\epsilon$ is a term which increases very slowly with $t$ and gives a very long period to $t _ { 1 } ~ - ~ t _ { 0 } ,$ and hence the determinant is very small.

Hence,a necessary condition for the possibility of such harmonic analysis is that the interval $t _ { 1 } ~ - ~ t _ { 0 }$ is not small compared with the period of the reserved terms and with the periods obtained by taking the difference of any pair of the reserved arguments.We suppose that those periods are shorter than $t _ { 1 } - t _ { 0 }$

It is known that the motion of a satellite disturbed by the sun is represented by Fourier series depending on four independent angular parameters,apart from linear functions of time. We take these four parameters as the mean anomaly $\iota$ of the satellite, the mean anomaly ${ \mathrm { \Omega } } l ^ { \prime }$ of the sun, the mean angular distance $\pmb { D }$ of the satellite from the sun, and the mean angular distance $\pmb { F }$ of the satellite from the ascending node. Then,

$$
{ \cal D } = l + \overline { { { \varpi } } } - l ^ { \prime } - \overline { { { \varpi } } } ^ { \prime } , F = l + \overline { { { \varpi } } } - \overline { { { \Omega } } } .
$$

Kovalevsky chose the osculating elements, because they are represented by Fourier series with even multiples of $\pmb { F }$ as arguments, because the double integration introducing small divisors is avoided except for $M ,$ and because an approximation for $\Omega , \omega ,$ and $M$ is obtained by calculating the linear part $\overline { { \Omega } } , \overline { { \varpi } } - \overline { { \Omega } } .$ and $l _ { : }$ ,respectively.

Consider

$$
F ( t ) = \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \sum _ { k } A _ { h i j k } \cos { ( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F + k l ) } .
$$

In order to avoid the computation of so large a number of terms as 300, Kovalevsky wrote

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \vec { \tau } } ( t ) = \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \left( \sum _ { k } A _ { h i j k } \cos k l \right) \cos \left( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F \right) } } \\ { { - \displaystyle { \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \left( \sum _ { k } A _ { h i j k } \sin k l \right) \sin \left( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F \right) } } } \end{array}
$$

and defined a new function $\Phi ( \tau , l )$ of two variables $\pmb { \tau }$ and $\iota$ by writing $l = n _ { l } \tau + c _ { 0 }$ Then $\mathbf { } F ( t ) = \Phi ( \tau = t , t )$ . Give a fixed value $l _ { 0 }$ to $l ,$ and the successive values $t _ { 0 } + ( 2 \pi \lambda / n _ { l } )$ to $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ Put

$$
C _ { h i j } ( l _ { 0 } ) = \sum _ { k } A _ { h i j k } \cos k l _ { 0 } , ~ S _ { h i j } ( l _ { 0 } ) = \sum _ { k } A _ { h i j k } \sin k l _ { 0 } .
$$

For integral values of $\lambda _ { z }$ ,the function $\Phi ( t _ { 0 } + [ 2 \pi \lambda / n _ { l } ] , t )$ takes the same value

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \Phi ( t _ { 0 } , t ) = \Phi _ { l _ { 0 } } ( t ) } \\ { = \displaystyle \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } C _ { h i j } ( l _ { 0 } ) \cos { ( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F ) } } \\ { \displaystyle - \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } S _ { h i j } ( l _ { 0 } ) \sin { ( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F ) } . } \end{array}
$$

The new function $\Phi _ { l _ { 0 } } ( t )$ depends on $t$ only through $D , \ l ^ { \prime }$ ,and $F ,$ and is developable in a Fourier series. Since $F ( t ) = \Phi ( t , t )$ ,we have

$$
\Phi _ { l _ { 0 } } \Big ( t + \frac { 2 \pi \lambda } { n _ { l } } \Big ) = \Phi \Big ( t _ { 0 } + \frac { 2 \pi \lambda } { n _ { l } } , t _ { 0 } + \frac { 2 \pi \lambda } { n _ { l } } \Big ) = F \Big ( t _ { 0 } + \frac { 2 \pi \lambda } { n _ { l } } \Big ) .
$$

Thus there are as many values of $\Phi _ { l _ { 0 } }$ as the different integral values of $\lambda$ in the interval of the tabulation of ${ \pmb F }$ Let $\mathbf { P }$ be the number of these values. We must multiply the formula (138) $\mathbf { P }$ times.

Similarly,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \displaystyle } & { = \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } C _ { h i j } ( l _ { 0 } ) \cos { ( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F ) } } & \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad + \sum _ { h } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } S _ { h i j } ( l _ { 0 } ) \sin { ( h D + i l ^ { \prime } + j F ) } . } & \end{array}
$$

This gives P new values of $F ( - t _ { 0 } - [ 2 \pi \lambda / n _ { l } ] )$

If n is the number of combinations of $h , i , j ,$ then there are $\bf { 2 \sigma _ { N } }$ equations for $2 \mathbf { N }$ unknown quantities, to which the method of least squares should be applied. If further a similar calculation is made for $\mathbf { Q }$ different values of $l _ { 0 }$ then $\mathsf Q$ values of the coefficients $C _ { h i j } ( l )$ and $S _ { h i j } ( l )$ are ob-tained. Hence we can calculate $A _ { h i j k }$ by solving these $2 \mathsf Q$ equations (137) by the method of least squares.

In this manner Kovalevsky avoided the tedious computation of so large a number of terms. As Delaunay pointed out, the principal terms can be arranged in groups in a sequence of four or eight successive integral values of $k$ .The number of such principal terms to be computed in the frst step is divided by six at most. We can take $Q = 1 3$ and try to find in the second part the terms for $k = - 8 , . . . , + 8$

Thus we have replaced an inversion of matrices of order 6n by inversions of matrices of order N. In the next stage we have only one matrix for all the coefficients；the work is simplified if $\mathbf { Q }$ values of $l _ { 0 }$ are in arithmetic progression.

It is now possible to program electronic computers for literal analytic calculations,such as addition,multiplication,differentiation and in-tegration of polynomials, rational fractions, and truncated trigonometric series. In celestial mechanics we often meet algebraic operations on expressions of the form

$$
\sum _ { i _ { 1 } , \cdots , i _ { n } } R _ { i _ { 1 } \cdots i _ { n } } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { k } ) \operatorname { s i n } _ { \cos } { ( i _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdots + i _ { n } y _ { n } ) } ,
$$

where $R _ { i _ { 1 } \cdots i _ { n } }$ is a generalized polynomial of $k$ literal parameters $\boldsymbol { x } ,$ and $y _ { j } = n _ { j } ( t - t _ { j } )$ are the arguments.Barton (l967； Section 9.2） applied his method of programming to expand the disturbing function for the moon with $k = 8$ ， $n = 6$ ， $| i | \leq 6 3$ . Chapront, Mangeney-Ghertzman, and Kovalevsky of the Bureau des Longitudes studied the motion of the moon up to the sixth order of the parameters with $k = 5$ ， $n = 4$ Herget and Musen (l959) of Cincinnati, Davis of the Triangle Universities,Deprit of the Boeing Research Laboratory, Sconzo of IBM, Collet of the Meudon Observatory,and Griffth of Greenwich each invented methods for the programming.Such methods ofliteral analytic operations with computers enable us to cultivate quite a new field of approach for attacking various problems in celestial mechanics in the near future. See the proceedings of the IAU Colloquium on the use of electronic computers for analytic developments in celestial mechanics, published in the Astronomical Journal 73 (1968), 195.

Morando (l967） obtained a seminumerical solution for the general planetary theory such that no secular term appears in the semimajor axis.For Vesta he operated with literal analytic programs for trigonometric series by means of computers.In place of secular terms he found periodic terms whose periods were of the order of magnitude of the revolution time of the node and perihelion,and compared his results with those of Le Veau (Section l0.9),Perrotin (Section 8.3),and Herget (1950).

Morando referred to Delaunay's variables. The equations for longperiod terms are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d G } { d t } = \frac { \partial R } { \partial g } , \qquad \displaystyle \frac { d H } { d t } = \frac { \partial R } { \partial h } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d g } { d t } = - \frac { \partial R } { \partial G } , \qquad \displaystyle \frac { d h } { d t } = - \frac { \partial R } { \partial H } , } \end{array}
$$

Since $\partial P _ { 1 1 } / \partial G$ is large because of the presence of the divisor $e$ he neglected for computing the long-period terms allcoeffcients $P _ { j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } }$ except $P _ { \bf { 1 1 } }$ ,and obtained

$$
\frac { d G } { d t } = 0 , \frac { d H } { d t } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \overline { { { d t } } } ^ { \mathrm { ~ ~ -- ~ } } \overline { { { d t } } } ^ { \mathrm { ~ ~ -- ~ } \overline { { { d t } } } ^ { \mathrm { ~ ~ ~ } } } = \mathrm { ~ } ^ { \mathrm { ~ o ~ } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d g } { d t } = - \frac { \partial N ( L _ { 0 } , G , H ) } { \partial G } - \frac { \partial P _ { 1 1 } ( L _ { 0 } , G , H ) } { \partial G } \cos { \left( g + h \right) } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \frac { d h } { d t } = - \frac { \partial N ( L _ { 0 } , G , H ) } { \partial H } . } } \end{array}
$$

Put $\theta _ { 1 } = g + h , \theta _ { 2 } = 2 h$ and tan $( \theta _ { 1 } / 2 ) = u ,$ $\alpha = a / a ^ { \prime }$ ; then,

$$
\frac { d u } { d t } = n _ { 0 } \alpha m ^ { \prime } ( 0 . 0 7 6 8 7 5 + 0 . 1 5 3 6 0 3 u ^ { 2 } ) \equiv \lambda ( A ^ { 2 } + u ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\lambda = n _ { 0 } \alpha m ^ { \prime } \times 0 . 1 5 3 6 0 3 , \quad \quad A = 0 . 7 0 7 4 4 5 ;
$$

or, with $q = ( A - 1 ) / ( A + 1 ) = - 0 . 1 7 1 3 4 1 ,$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \theta _ { 1 } = \phi + 2 q \sin \phi + q ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \phi + \frac { 2 } { 3 } q ^ { 3 } \sin 3 \phi + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { \theta _ { 2 } = - 0 . 4 8 8 7 4 2 n _ { 0 } \alpha m ^ { \prime } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi$ is linear in t. Morando solved for the long-period terms by iteration，and then proceeded to the computation of short-period inequalities.

Chapront and Mangeney (1968,l969) constructed a completely literal analytic solution of the equations of motion for the lunar theory by successive approximations, in anticipation of handling the development on an electronic computer with the program of the Bureau des Longitudes (Kovalevsky, 1968).

First we explain their method of solution with Delaunay's equations for the variation of elements

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \xi } { d t } = \epsilon \sum _ { j } A _ { j } ( \epsilon , \xi ) \sin j \eta , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \eta } { d t } = N _ { 0 } + \frac { \epsilon } { \xi } \sum _ { j } B _ { j } ( \epsilon , \xi ) \cos j \eta , } \end{array}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small parameter which is $( n ^ { \prime } / n ) ^ { 2 }$ in the lunar theory, $N _ { 0 }$ is a constant, and $A _ { j } , B _ { j }$ are polynomials in $\xi$ ：

$$
A _ { j } , B _ { j } = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { p } a _ { k } ^ { j } \xi ^ { k } .
$$

The variables $\xi , \ \eta$ are, for example, the eccentricity and the mean anomaly. We integrate these differential equations by starting with a solution

$$
\xi = \xi _ { 0 } , \qquad \eta = \eta _ { 0 } + \left[ N _ { 0 } + \frac { \epsilon B ( \xi _ { 0 } ) } { \xi _ { 0 } } \right] t .
$$

The differential equations

$$
\frac { d \xi } { d t } = A ( \xi , \eta ) , ~ \frac { d \eta } { d t } = n + B ( \xi , \eta ) ,
$$

can be written, with $z = \xi \exp ( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \eta ) = x + { \sqrt { - 1 } } y ,$ in the form

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = \sqrt { - 1 } \ : n z + \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \xi } \Big . A + \sqrt { - 1 } \ : B \Big ) z ,
$$

where $\pmb { n }$ is given by

$$
\frac { d n } { d t } = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { n } { a } \frac { d a } { d t } \cdot
$$

The variable $\pmb { n }$ is decomposed into a constant part $\pmb { \nu }$ of the order O with respect to $( n ^ { \prime } / n ) ^ { 2 }$ and a small but constant and periodic part $\delta \boldsymbol { n }$ with the small parameter as factor, i.e., $\pmb { n } = \pmb { \nu } + \delta \pmb { n } .$ Then

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } - \sqrt { - 1 } \nu z = \sigma , \sigma = \left[ \frac { 1 } { \xi } A + \sqrt { - 1 } ( \delta n + B ) \right] z ,
$$

where $\pmb { \sigma }$ contains all terms depending on ${ \pmb n } ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 }$ or

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \nu ^ { 2 } z = \sqrt { - 1 } \nu \sigma + \frac { d \sigma } { d t } \cdot
$$

Let $z _ { 1 } = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \nu t ) }$ ， $z _ { 2 } = \exp { ( - \sqrt { - 1 } \nu t ) }$ be two particular solutions with the right-hand member equated to zero;then

$$
z _ { 1 } \frac { d z _ { 2 } } { d t } - z _ { 2 } \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } = - 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \nu ,
$$

and the general solution is

$$
2 { \sqrt { - 1 } } \nu z = k _ { 1 } z _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } z _ { 2 } + \int ^ { \tau = t } \left( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \nu \sigma + { \frac { d \sigma } { d t } } \right) ( z _ { 1 } z _ { 2 } ^ { * } - z _ { 2 } z _ { 1 } ^ { * } ) d t
$$

with arbitrary constants $k _ { 1 }$ and $k _ { 2 }$ . The variable $z _ { i } ^ { * }$ indicates the function $z _ { i }$ in which $t$ is replaced by $\pmb { \tau }$ according to Hansen's artifice (Section 10.7). We find

$$
z _ { 1 } z _ { 2 } ^ { * } - z _ { 2 } z _ { 1 } ^ { * } = 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \sin \nu ( t - \tau ) ,
$$

if

$$
z = z _ { 0 } - \frac { 1 } { \nu } \int ^ { \tau = t } \left( \sqrt { - 1 } \nu \sigma + \frac { d \sigma } { d t } \right) \sin \nu ( t - \tau ) d t ,
$$

or, integrating by parts,

$$
z = z _ { 0 } + \int ^ { \tau = t } \sigma \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } \nu ( \tau - t ) \right] d t .
$$

This is a formulation of Encke's method (Section 10.1).

More generally we consider

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } + \nu y \lambda ( x , y ) = X , \frac { d y } { d t } - \nu x \lambda ( x , y ) = Y ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } + \nu \Phi = Z , \qquad Z = X + \sqrt { - 1 } ~ Y , \qquad \Phi = - \sqrt { - 1 } ~ \lambda ( x , y ) z .
$$

Then $_ z$ is written

$$
z = z _ { 0 } + \int ^ { \tau = t } z _ { 1 } ^ { * } \bar { z } _ { 1 } [ Z + \sqrt { - 1 } \left( \lambda - \lambda _ { 1 } \right) z ] d t ,
$$

where $z _ { 1 } ^ { * } = z _ { 1 } ( t = \tau )$ ， $z _ { 1 } \bar { z } _ { 1 } = 1$ ,and $\lambda _ { 1 }$ is the value of $\lambda$ at the previous approximation. If $\lambda$ is a constant $\lambda _ { 0 }$ ,then $\lambda = \lambda _ { 1 } - \lambda _ { 0 } ,$ ,and the equation reduces to the former case.The difference $\lambda - \lambda _ { 1 }$ should be known in the approximation we consider,as will be shown immediately.

Let $\pmb { R }$ be the disturbing function

$$
R = n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } \biggl ( \frac { 1 } { \Delta } - \frac { r } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \Theta \biggr ) ;
$$

then

$$
\frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \Theta } = n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 } \frac { a ^ { \prime 3 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } Z , ~ \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r } \frac { \partial R } { \partial r } = n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 } \frac { a ^ { \prime 3 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \Big [ \Big ( \Theta - \frac { r } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ) Z - 1 \Big ] ,
$$

$$
Z = \left( \frac { r ^ { \prime 3 } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - 1 \right) \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { r } .
$$

The function $z$ can be expanded in power series of $\rho = r / r ^ { \prime }$ with the derivatives of Legendre's polynomials as coefficients. Put

$$
a { \frac { \partial R } { \partial r } } = n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 } \Phi , { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \Theta } } = n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 \Psi } , \gamma = \sin { \frac { I } { 2 } } ,
$$

with Delaunay's elements (Section 9.2).

It is known that

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { r } } = { \frac { 1 } { a } } \sum _ { j , r } \xi ^ { | j | + r } \cos j \eta .
$$

With $x = \xi \cos \eta , y = \xi \sin \eta ,$ we find the equations

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } + \nu \lambda y = \sum _ { \alpha , r , j } a \xi ^ { | \alpha | + r } \sin { ( \alpha \eta + \psi _ { j } ) } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d y } { d t } - \nu \lambda x = \sum _ { \beta , r , j } b \xi ^ { | \beta | + r } \cos { ( \beta \eta + \psi _ { j } ) } ,
$$

where $\psi _ { j }$ is the angular part of the solution other than $\eta$ .Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { z _ { 1 } = \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } \eta ( t ) ] } , \qquad \bar { z } _ { 1 } = \exp { [ - \sqrt { - 1 } \eta ( t ) ] } , } \\ & { \qquad z _ { 1 } ^ { * } = \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } \eta ( \tau ) ] } ; } \end{array}
$$

then an approximate solution $\zeta$ of $z$ is expressed by

$$
\zeta = \sum _ { p , q \geq 0 } \beta _ { p , q } z _ { 1 } ^ { p } \bar { z } _ { 1 } ^ { q } ,
$$

where $\beta _ { p , q }$ are real. Substituting this expansion in the right-hand members of the differential equations for $_ x$ and $y$ we obtain

$$
z = z _ { 0 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \int ^ { \tau = t } \sum \gamma _ { p , q } z _ { 1 } ^ { p } \bar { z } _ { 1 } ^ { q } z _ { 1 } ^ { * } \bar { z } _ { 1 } d t .
$$

$\begin{array} { r } { \mathbb { f } \pmb { \mathscr { p } } - \mathscr { q } - 1 = 0 } \end{array}$ and $\psi _ { j } = 0$ , then a secular term

$$
z _ { t } = \left[ \sum \gamma _ { p , p - 1 } \right] z _ { 1 } ( t ) \times t
$$

appears in the course of integration.For eliminating this term we must modify $\delta \nu$ step by step at each stage of approximation. This is in accord with Newcomb's idea (Sections 10.15-l0.17).The determination of such secular terms appears in Gylden's theory.

Chapront and Mangeney took, for the variables

$$
\begin{array} { c } { m = { n ^ { \prime } } / { n } , \qquad x = \epsilon \cos l , \qquad y = \epsilon \sin l , \qquad p = \gamma \cos { ( v + g ) } , } \\ { q = \gamma \sin { ( v + g ) } , \qquad r _ { 0 } = m _ { 0 } \cos { ( v + g + h - v ^ { \prime } ) } , \qquad } \\ { s _ { 0 } = m _ { 0 } \sin { ( v + g + h - v ^ { \prime } ) } , \qquad } \end{array}
$$

the equations

$$
\frac { d m } { d t } = 3 m \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } } { n } \left[ e ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \Phi \sin v + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \Psi \frac { \partial \Theta } { \partial g } \right] ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { a x } { d t } = - n y + X = - n y + \frac { n \cdots } { n } G _ { 1 } \Phi + \frac { n \cdots } { n } G _ { 2 } \Psi \frac { \sigma \Psi } { \partial g } , } \\ & { \frac { d y } { d t } = n x + Y = n x + \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } } { n } H _ { 1 } \Phi + \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } } { n } H _ { 2 } \Psi \frac { \partial \Theta } { \partial g } , } \\ & { \frac { d \hat { \rho } } { d t } = - n \lambda q + P } \\ & { \quad = - n \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } q - ( 1 - \hat { p } ^ { 2 } - 2 q ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } n _ { 0 } \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } ( q r _ { 0 } - \hat { t } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d q } { d t } } = n \lambda \psi + Q } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = n \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } p - \hbar q ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } n _ { 0 } \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } ( q r _ { 0 } - \rlap / p s _ { 0 } ) \Psi , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d r _ { 0 } } { d t } } = \Big ( - n \lambda + \frac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } \Big ) s _ { 0 } + R } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = ~ \Big [ - n \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } \Big ] s _ { 0 } + 2 q s _ { 0 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } n _ { 0 } \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } ( q r _ { 0 } - \rlap / p s _ { 0 } ) \Psi , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \frac { { \bf \Pi } _ { S _ { 0 } } ^ { \prime } } { l t } = \left( n \lambda - \frac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } \right) r _ { 0 } + S \qquad } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = \left[ n \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } \right] r _ { 0 } - 2 q r _ { 0 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } n _ { 0 } \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } \left( q r _ { 0 } - { / } s _ { 0 } \right) \mathbb { Y } } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
G _ { 1 } = 2 \frac { r } { a } y + \sin { ( v - l ) } + \frac { 1 } { e ^ { 2 } } [ ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } - 1 ] e x \sin { v } + e y \cos { v } ,
$$

and similar expressions for $G _ { 2 } , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 }$ .The right-hand members are expanded in the form

$$
\sum _ { i } C \left( \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } e , e ^ { \prime } , \gamma , \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } \right) \stackrel { \cos } { \sin } \phi _ { i } , \qquad \phi _ { i } = i _ { 1 } D + i _ { 2 } F + i _ { 3 } l + i _ { 4 } l ^ { \prime } .
$$

The integration of (A) gives $m = m _ { 0 } ( 1 + \delta m _ { p } )$ , where $m _ { 0 } = n ^ { \prime } / n _ { 0 }$ and $\delta m _ { p }$ is purely periodic. Writing

$$
n = n _ { 0 } ( 1 + \delta n ) , m = n ^ { \prime } / [ n _ { 0 } ( 1 + \delta n ) ] = m _ { 0 } ( 1 + \delta m ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
1 + \delta n = ( 1 + \delta m ) ^ { - 1 } ,
$$

by which $\delta n$ is calculated from δm.But $\delta { \boldsymbol { n } }$ is not purely periodic. Let the constant part of $\pmb { n }$ obtained from this equation be $n _ { 0 } ( 1 \ + \ \epsilon )$ ；then $n = n _ { 0 } - n _ { 0 } \delta \nu _ { l } + \delta n _ { \mu }$ where $\delta \nu _ { l }$ is an indeterminate constant depending on $m , a / a ^ { \prime } , \gamma , e ^ { \prime }$ .Thus

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \delta n = n _ { 0 } \delta \nu _ { l } + n _ { 0 } \epsilon + n _ { 0 } \delta n _ { p } , \qquad n = \nu + \delta n . } \end{array}
$$

The additions to the previous approximation for the solutions of (B) and (C) are

$$
\Delta x = \int \left( X - y \ \delta n \right) \cos \left( l _ { 1 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) d t + \int \left( Y + x \ \delta n \right) \sin \left( l _ { 1 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) d
$$

$$
\Delta y = \int \left( Y + x \ \delta n \right) \cos \left( l _ { 1 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) d t - \int \left( X - y \ \delta n \right) \sin \left( l _ { 1 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) d
$$

In the first approximation we substitute $e = e _ { 0 } , \gamma = \gamma _ { 0 } , a = a _ { 0 }$ and $\boldsymbol { l } = l _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { F } = F _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { D } = D _ { 1 }$ in the right-hand members of the equations (B) and (C).Then $x = e _ { 0 } \cos l _ { 1 } , y = e _ { 0 } \sin l _ { 1 }$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { \Psi } _ { * } x = - e _ { 0 } \sin l _ { 1 } \cdot \displaystyle \int \delta n \cdot d t + \int \left[ X \cos \left( l _ { 1 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) + Y \sin \left( l _ { 1 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) \right] d t . } \end{array}
$$

The secular term in $\Delta x$ is obtained after computation as

$$
- e _ { 0 } \sin l \cdot \int n _ { 0 } \delta \nu _ { l } \cdot d t + e _ { 0 } \sin l \cdot \int \frac { \tau } { 4 } n _ { 0 } m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } d t .
$$

For eliminating the secular term we determine $\delta \nu _ { l }$ by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { n _ { 0 } \delta \nu _ { l } - \frac { 7 } { 4 } n _ { 0 } m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Thus the secular part in the first approximation is $\begin{array} { r } { ( \delta \nu _ { 1 } ) _ { 1 } = \frac { \eta } { 4 } m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$

The equations $( \mathbf { D } )$ and (E) and the equations $( \mathbf { F } )$ and $( \mathbf { G } )$ are similarly treated.

# ANDOYER'S THEORY

# 10.30 ANDOYER'S LUNAR THEORY

Andoyer's theory (1899,1901,1902,1907,1926,1926a,1926b,1928, 1928a) consists of formulating the equations of motion in rectangular coordinates and of substituting integral expressions directly in the differential equations for obtaining literal and numerical expressions for the solution. In 1899 he considered only powers of $\pmb { e }$ and $_ m$ and pointed out some errors in Delaunay's theory. Then he computed step by step the terms in powers of $e ^ { 2 }$ ,the terms in $e ^ { \prime }$ ,and the secular acceleration.His theory was completed in 1926-1928.Caubet (1912,1913) included the orbital inclination.

Andoyer took out from the disturbing function

$$
: = k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \bigg \{ \frac { m _ { 1 } + m _ { e } } { r } + m ^ { \prime } \bigg [ ( a ^ { \prime 2 } + r ^ { 2 } - 2 r a ^ { \prime } \cos H ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } - \frac { r \cos H } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \bigg ] \bigg \}
$$

the part

$$
k ^ { 2 } m _ { 1 } \bigg [ { \frac { n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { r } } + { \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { 4 } } ( 1 + 3 \cos 2 H ) \bigg ]
$$

containing the lunar coordinates only and independent of $\tau$ and $r ^ { \prime } / a ^ { \prime }$ ， where $n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } ( 1 + \tau ) = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime }$ ， $m ^ { \prime }$ being the solar mass, $m _ { e }$ the earth's mass, and $m _ { 1 }$ the lunar mass. Then the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - r \left( \frac { d v } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 2 } } - \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } r } { 2 } \left( 1 + 3 \cos 2 H \right) = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d } { d t } \left( r ^ { 2 } \frac { d v } { d t } \right) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } n ^ { \prime 2 } r ^ { 2 } \sin 2 H = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and the integral due to Laplace is

$$
\left( { \frac { d r } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \left( { \frac { d v } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } - { \frac { 2 n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { r } } - { \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 } } \int d [ r ^ { 2 } + 3 r ^ { 2 } \cos 2 H ] = 0 ,
$$

where $d [ \ ]$ is a differential due to the variation of the lunar coordinates only. If we combine this equation with the first, then,

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } ( r ^ { 2 } ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { r } - 2 n ^ { \prime 2 } r ^ { 2 } - 6 n ^ { \prime 2 } \bigg ( r ^ { 2 } \cos 2 H - n ^ { \prime } \int r ^ { 2 } \sin 2 H \cdot d t \bigg ) = 0
$$

Noting the relation

$$
[ d ( r ^ { 2 } \cos 2 H ) ] = d ( r ^ { 2 } \cos 2 H ) - 2 n ^ { \prime } r ^ { 2 } \sin 2 H \cdot d t ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { r ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d v } { d t } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } n ^ { \prime 2 } \int r ^ { 2 } \sin 2 H \cdot d t = 0 , } \\ { \displaystyle \left( \frac { d v } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { r } \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 } \left( 1 + 3 \cos 2 H \right) = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

These three equations are the fundamental equations of Andoyer's theory.

Let $N = n t + v _ { 0 }$ and $N ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + v _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ be respectively the longitudes of the moon and the sun, with arbitrary constants $\pmb { n }$ and $v _ { 0 }$ Put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { I = v - N ^ { \prime } , \qquad v = N + \lambda , \qquad r = a ( 1 + \rho ) , \qquad K = N - N ^ { \prime } , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
G = g n t + \varpi ,
$$

where $\eqcirc$ is a constant and $\pmb { g }$ is a quantity to be determined. Then $\lambda$ and $\pmb { \rho }$ can be expanded in the form

$$
\lambda = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \lambda _ { i j } \sin { ( i K + j G ) } , \qquad \rho = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \rho _ { i j } \cos { ( i K + j G ) } ,
$$

where we have $\lambda _ { - i , - j } = - \lambda _ { i , j } , \ \rho _ { - i , - j } = \rho _ { i , j }$ due to symmetry. The indeterminate coefficients $\lambda _ { i j }$ and $\rho _ { i j }$ are determined successively by the recurrence relations obtained through the substitution of the series in the differential equations,so that, if for $n ^ { \prime } = 0$ the moon describes a Keplerian ellipse with its eccentricity $e _ { \perp }$ ,then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \lambda _ { 0 1 } = e - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 3 } + \frac { 5 } { 1 9 2 } e ^ { 5 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

$\pmb { e }$ being an arbitrary constant.

Put

$$
x = { \frac { r } { a } } \cos \lambda = 1 + \sum _ { p } x _ { p } \cos \mathrm { v } _ { p } ,
$$

$$
y = \frac { r } { a } \sin { \lambda } = \sum _ { p } y _ { p } \sin { \mathrm { v } _ { p } } , \qquad \mathbf { v } _ { p } = i K + j G ,
$$

and write $d { \boldsymbol { x } } / d t = n { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime }$ ， $d ^ { 2 } x / d t ^ { 2 } = n ^ { 2 } x ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,...; then the equations of motion are transformed to, with $m = n ^ { \prime } / n$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { x ^ { \prime \prime } - 2 y ^ { \prime } - x \Big ( 1 + \frac { m ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \Big ) + x \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } x \cos 2 K } } \\ { { \qquad + \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } y \sin 2 K = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
+ \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } x \sin 2 K = 0 .
$$

If we substitute for the quantities their Fourier series expansions with the arguments $\mathbf { v } _ { p }$ and equate to zero the coefficients of cos $\mathbf { v } _ { p }$ or sin $\mathbf { v } _ { p }$ ， then we can derive the recurrence relations for various coefficients in the Fourier expansions.

Eliminating $a ^ { 3 } / r ^ { 3 }$ from (142), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x y ^ { \prime \prime } - y x ^ { \prime \prime } + 2 ( x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } [ 2 x y \cos 2 K + ( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) \sin 2 K ] = } \end{array}
$$

Multiply the two equations (142） by $x ^ { \prime }$ and $y ^ { \prime }$ ，respectively,and add, then integrate.Next multiply respectively by $_ x$ and $y$ and add. Then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { x x ^ { \prime \prime } + y y ^ { \prime \prime } - 2 ( x y ^ { \prime } - x ^ { \prime } y ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x ^ { \prime 2 } + y ^ { \prime 2 } ) - \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } - \frac { 9 } { 4 } m ^ { 2 } [ ( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) \cos 2 K - 2 x y \sin 2 K ] } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } ( n - n ^ { \prime } ) \displaystyle \int \left[ 2 x y \cos 2 K + ( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) \sin 2 K \right] d t = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

These two equations (l43) and (l44) are homogeneous and of the second degree with respect to $_ x$ and $y$ and their derivatives. Andoyer took these two equations as his second fundamental equations.

Let $\lambda _ { p }$ be the coefficient $\lambda _ { i j }$ of a term with the argument $\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { v } _ { p } = i K + j G _ { ; } } \end{array}$ and let the coefficient of $t$ in this same argument be $n k _ { p }$ .The coefficient $k _ { p } ,$ ,as well as the coefficient $g$ of $t$ in the argument $G$ ,can be expanded in even integral powers of $e$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { r } { k _ { p } = k _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) } + k _ { p } ^ { ( 2 ) } e ^ { 2 } + k _ { p } ^ { ( 4 ) } e ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ { g = g ^ { ( 0 ) } + g ^ { ( 2 ) } e ^ { 2 } + g ^ { ( 4 ) } e ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

Write

$$
w _ { p } = x _ { p } + y _ { p } , \qquad w _ { - p } = x _ { p } - y _ { p } ,
$$

also

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \alpha _ { p } = \sum \frac { 1 } { 2 } k _ { p _ { 1 } } k _ { p _ { 2 } } ( w _ { p _ { 1 } } w _ { - p _ { 2 } } + w _ { - p _ { 1 } } w _ { p _ { 2 } } ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \beta _ { p } = \sum \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( k _ { p _ { 1 } } - k _ { p _ { 2 } } ) ( w _ { p _ { 1 } } w _ { - p _ { 2 } } - w _ { - p _ { 1 } } w _ { p _ { 2 } } ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \gamma _ { p } = \sum \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( w _ { p _ { 1 } } w _ { - p _ { 2 } } + w _ { - p _ { 1 } } w _ { p _ { 2 } } ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \delta _ { p } = [ 1 ] _ { 0 } + 2 w _ { p } + 2 \sum \frac { 1 } { 2 } w _ { p _ { 1 } } w _ { p _ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $[ 1 ] _ { 0 } = 1$ for $\mathbf { v } _ { p } = 0$ and $[ 1 ] _ { 0 } = 0$ otherwise.

Take $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { 1 } }$ and $\pmb { \hat { p } _ { 2 } }$ such that we have $\mathbf { v } _ { p _ { 1 } } + \mathbf { v } _ { p _ { 2 } } \leq \mathbf { v } _ { p }$ The result of substituting these Fourier series expansions in (l43) and (l44) gives the relations between the coefficients :

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { k _ { p } ( k _ { p } ^ { 2 } - 1 + \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } ) y _ { p } + \alpha _ { p } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( k _ { p } ^ { 2 } - 1 + \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } ) \beta _ { p } } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { 2 } } { k _ { p } } \left( k _ { p } ^ { 2 } + 3 k _ { p } + 1 + 2 m + \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } \right) \delta _ { p } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { + \frac { 3 } { 4 } \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { 2 } } { k _ { p } } \left( k _ { p } ^ { 2 } - 3 k _ { p } + 1 + 2 m + \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } \right) \delta _ { p ^ { \prime } } = 0 , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { x _ { p } + \displaystyle \frac { k _ { p } } { 2 } y _ { p } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \beta _ { p } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \gamma _ { p } - \frac { 3 } { 8 } \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { 2 } } { k _ { p } } \delta _ { p ^ { \prime } } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } \displaystyle \frac { m ^ { 2 } } { k _ { p } } \delta _ { p ^ { \prime } } = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

where we take the subscripts $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } ^ { \prime }$ and $\pmb { \phi } ^ { \prime \prime }$ such that $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { v } _ { p ^ { \prime } } = \mathbf { v } _ { p } - 2 K , } \end{array}$ ${ \mathrm { v } } _ { p ^ { \prime \prime } } = - { \mathrm { v } } _ { p } - 2 K$

Solving the equations (l45) by successive approximations, we obtain $x _ { p } , y _ { p } ,$ and $\lambda _ { i j } , \omega _ { i j } , \zeta _ { i j }$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { r ^ { 2 } = a ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \omega ) = a ^ { 2 } \Big [ 1 + \sum \omega _ { i j } \cos { ( i K + j G ) } \Big ] , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d v } { d t } = n ( 1 + \zeta ) = n \Big [ 1 + \sum \zeta _ { i j } \cos { ( i K + j G ) } \Big ] , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \zeta _ { i j } = [ i ( 1 - m ) + j g ] \lambda _ { i j } . } } \end{array}
$$

Since $k _ { p }$ and $g _ { p }$ are expanded in even powers of $e$ , we have

$$
k _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) } = i ( 1 - m ) + j g ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad k _ { p } ^ { ( 2 ) } = j g ^ { ( 2 ) } , \qquad .
$$

and

$$
\zeta _ { p } ^ { ( h ) } = \sum _ { h _ { 1 } + h _ { 2 } = h } \ : k _ { p } ^ { ( h _ { 1 } ) } \lambda _ { p } ^ { ( h _ { 2 } ) } .
$$

In place of $\pmb { \rho }$ Andoyer took $\mu = \log { ( 1 + \omega ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } = \log { ( r / a ) }$ and

$$
\mu _ { p } = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \omega _ { p } - { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { 2 } { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \omega _ { p _ { 1 } } \omega _ { p _ { 2 } } + \sum { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 6 } } \omega _ { p _ { 1 } } \omega _ { p _ { 2 } } \omega _ { p _ { 3 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where ${ \ p } _ { 1 } , { \ p } _ { 2 } , { \ p } _ { 3 } , \ldots ,$ ，are such that $\mathrm { v } _ { p _ { 1 } } + \mathrm { v } _ { p _ { 2 } } + \mathrm { v } _ { p _ { 3 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = \mathrm { v } _ { p }$ Thus he computed the coefficients $\lambda _ { i j } ^ { ( h ) } , \omega _ { i j } ^ { ( h ) } , \mu _ { i j } ^ { ( h ) } , x _ { i j } ^ { ( h ) } , y _ { i j } ^ { ( h ) }$ of $e ^ { h }$ in the series for $\lambda _ { i j } , \omega _ { i j } , \mu _ { i j } , x _ { i j } , y _ { i j } ,$ arranged in powers of $\pmb { m }$

Then Andoyer (1902） took the solar eccentricity $e ^ { \prime }$ into account. The differential equations of motion are now, in place of (l39） and (140),

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \left( r ^ { 2 } \right) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { r } - 2 n ^ { \prime 2 } r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 3 } - 6 n ^ { \prime 2 } r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 3 } \cos 2 H } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \left( r ^ { 2 } \right) } { d t ^ { 2 } } - } + n ^ { \prime 2 } \displaystyle \int \left[ r ^ { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 3 } + 3 r ^ { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 3 } \cos 2 H \right. } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \left( r ^ { 2 } \right) } { d t } - } \left. + 6 r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 3 } \frac { d { v ^ { \prime } } } { d t } \sin 2 H \right] d t = 0 , } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \left( r ^ { 2 } \right) } { d t } - \frac { d { v ^ { \prime } } } { d t } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } } } \\ &  \phantom { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \left( r ^ { 2 } \right) } { d t } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } n ^ { \prime 2 } \displaystyle \int r ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 3 } \sin 2 H \cdot d t = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

The series are now expanded with three arguments $K , \ G ,$ and $G ^ { \prime } =$ $N ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ in the form

$$
= \sum \lambda _ { i j j ^ { \prime } } \sin { ( i K + j G + j ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } ) } , ~ \rho = \sum \rho _ { i j j ^ { \prime } } \cos { ( i K + j G + j ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } ) }
$$

We have, this time,

$$
\zeta _ { i j j ^ { \prime } } = [ i ( 1 - m ) + j g + j ^ { \prime } m ] \lambda _ { i j j ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Thus Andoyer obtained analytical expressions for the coefficients in positive integral powers of $_ m$ ,and corrected some mistakes of Delaunay.

# 10.31PALOQUE'S THEORY OF A TROJAN ASTEROID

Paloque (l925) undertook to base the theory of the motion of a Trojan asteroid on the equations of motion referred to the rectangular rotating coordinates, in accordance with Hill's lunar theory (Chapter l1).

Take the rectangular coordinates $X , Y , z$ with the sun as origin and Jupiter's orbital plane as the $( X , Y )$ -plane. Write

$$
x = { \frac { X } { r ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad y = { \frac { Y } { r ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad z = { \frac { Z } { r ^ { \prime } } } ,
$$

where $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime }$ is the heliocentric distance of Jupiter. Put

$$
\rho ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + e ^ { \prime } \cos v ^ { \prime } } , \qquad h ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } } ,
$$

and take Jupiter's true anomaly $v ^ { \prime }$ as the independent variable; then we have

$$
X = a ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } x , \qquad Y = a ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } y , \qquad Z = a ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } z ,
$$

$$
\frac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { \rho ^ { \prime 2 } h ^ { \prime 3 } } , \frac { d X } { d t } = \frac { a ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } } { h ^ { \prime } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { \prime } } \frac { d x } { d v ^ { \prime } } - \frac { d ( 1 / \rho ^ { \prime } ) } { d v ^ { \prime } } \right] ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } X } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { a ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime 2 } } { \rho ^ { \prime 3 } h ^ { \prime 4 } } \left[ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d v ^ { \prime 2 } } - x \rho ^ { \prime } \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( 1 / \rho ^ { \prime } ) } { d v ^ { \prime 2 } } \right] = \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 2 } } { \rho ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime 2 } } \frac { \partial V } { \partial x } ,
$$

and the identity

$$
\rho ^ { \prime } \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( 1 / \rho ^ { \prime } ) } { d v ^ { \prime 2 } } = \rho ^ { \prime } - 1 .
$$

The equations of motion are of the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d v ^ { \prime 2 } } = \frac { \partial U _ { 1 } } { \partial x } , \qquad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d v ^ { \prime 2 } } = \frac { \partial U _ { 1 } } { \partial y } , \qquad \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d v ^ { \prime 2 } } = \frac { \partial U _ { 1 } } { \partial z } ,
$$

where

$$
U _ { 1 } = \rho ^ { \prime 2 } h ^ { \prime 2 } V + \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \rho ^ { \prime } - 1 ) \rho ^ { 2 } , \qquad \rho ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } .
$$

Jupiter's eccentricity $e ^ { \prime }$ can be easily taken into account, because it is absorbed in $\pmb { \rho } ^ { \prime }$

We rotate the axes by an amount $\upsilon ^ { \prime } + \psi .$ ，where $\psi$ is a constant. Then the coordinates of the asteroid are $r ^ { \prime } ( 1 + \xi ) , - \sqrt { - 1 } r ^ { \prime } \eta , \sqrt { - 1 } r ^ { \prime } \zeta ,$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x = ( 1 + \xi ) \cos { ( v ^ { \prime } + \psi ) } + \sqrt { - 1 } \eta \sin { ( v ^ { \prime } + \psi ) } , } \\ & { y = ( 1 + \xi ) \sin { ( v ^ { \prime } + \psi ) } - \sqrt { - 1 } \eta \cos { ( v ^ { \prime } + \psi ) } , } \\ & { z = - \sqrt { - 1 } \zeta . } \end{array}
$$

With the independent variable $\tau = { \sqrt { - 1 } } v ^ { \prime }$ ， the equations of motion are transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d \eta } { d \tau } = - ( 1 + \xi ) - \frac { \partial U _ { 1 } } { \partial \xi } = - \frac { \partial U } { \partial \xi } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d \xi } { d \tau } = - \eta + \frac { \partial U _ { 1 } } { \partial \eta } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial \eta } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \partial U _ { 1 } } { \partial \zeta } = \zeta + \frac { \partial U } { \partial \zeta } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
U = \rho ^ { \prime 2 } h ^ { \prime 2 } V + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \rho ^ { \prime } \rho ^ { 2 } , \qquad \rho ^ { 2 } = ( 1 + \xi ) ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } .
$$

Now,

$$
k ^ { 2 } m _ { 0 } = ( 1 - \mu ) n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } , \qquad k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } = \mu n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } , \qquad \mu = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { m _ { 0 } + m ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { U = ( 1 - \mu ) \rho ^ { \prime } \displaystyle \left( \frac { 1 } { \rho } + \frac { \rho ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) + \mu \rho ^ { \prime } \displaystyle \left( \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { \Delta } + \frac { \rho ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \rho \cos { \cal H } \right) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \Delta ^ { 2 } } { \gamma ^ { \prime 2 } } = 1 + \rho ^ { 2 } - 2 \rho \cos { \cal H } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\rho \cos { H } = ( 1 + \xi ) \cos { \psi } + \sqrt { - 1 } \eta \sin { \psi } ,
$$

where $\Delta$ is the distance between the asteroid and Jupiter, $\boldsymbol { H }$ the angle between the vectors $r$ and $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { r } } ^ { \prime }$ ， $m _ { 0 }$ the mass of the sun, and $m ^ { \prime }$ the mass of Jupiter.

If we take $\psi = \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ ，then $\xi , \ \eta , \ \zeta$ are small, and the right-hand members of the equations of motion can be expanded in powers of these small quantities. Write $\chi = - \sqrt { - 3 }$ for $\psi = + 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ and $\chi = \sqrt { - 3 }$ for $\psi = - 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ ; then,

$$
2 \rho \cos { H } = 1 + \xi - \chi \eta , \frac { \Delta ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } = 1 + \xi + \chi \eta + \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } ,

and

U = ( 1 - \mu ) \rho ^ { \prime } \Bigl [ ( 1 + \sigma ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 + \sigma } { 2 } \Bigr ] + \mu \rho ^ { \prime } \Bigl [ ( 1 + \omega ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } + \frac { \omega } { 2 } \Bigr ] ,

$$
\sigma = 2 \xi + \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } , ~ \omega = \xi + \chi \eta + \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } ;

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal J } = ( 1 - \mu ) \rho ^ { \prime } \Big [ \frac { 1 \cdot 3 } { 2 \cdot 4 } \sigma ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 } \sigma ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \cdot 8 } \sigma ^ { 4 } - \cdot \cdot \cdot \Big ] } } \\ { { + \mu \rho ^ { \prime } \Big [ \frac { 1 \cdot 3 } { 2 \cdot 4 } \omega ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 } \omega ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 } { 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \cdot 8 } \omega ^ { 4 } - \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

The Lagrangian solution corresponds to $\xi = \eta = \zeta = 0 .$ or

$$
\omega = \sigma = { \frac { \partial U } { \partial \xi } } = 0 .
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { U = U _ { 0 } + R , } } \\ { { \displaystyle U _ { 0 } = \left( 3 - \frac 9 4 \mu \right) \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } } 2 + \frac 3 4 \mu \chi \xi \eta - \frac 9 4 \mu \frac { \eta ^ { 2 } } 2 ; } } \end{array}
$$

then the equations of motion take the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d \eta } { d \tau } + \bigg ( 3 - \frac { 9 } { 4 } \mu \bigg ) \xi + \frac { 3 } { 4 } \mu \chi \eta + \frac { \partial R } { \partial \xi } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d \xi } { d \tau } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \mu \chi \xi + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \mu \eta - \frac { \partial R } { \partial \eta } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } - \zeta - \frac { \partial R } { \partial \zeta } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

If we neglect $R$ , then the equations (146) are linear and homogeneous with constant coefficients.We try the solution

$$
\xi = \alpha \exp \{ f \tau \} , \qquad \eta = \beta \exp \{ f \tau \} , \qquad \zeta = \gamma \exp \{ f \tau \} .

This equation has two real roots if $1 - 2 7 \mu ( 1 - \mu ) > 0 .$ or if approximately $\mu < 1 / 2 5$ .Then the solution is periodic and there is a libration. The two roots $f _ { 1 }$ and $f _ { 2 }$ are

$$

$$

For obtaining the general solution we put

$$
\epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \epsilon ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \exp \{ \tau \} , \qquad \epsilon _ { - 1 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \epsilon ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \exp \{ - \tau \} ,

so that

$$

$$

We then expand $\partial R / \partial \xi , \partial R / \partial \eta , \partial R / \partial \zeta$ in powers of $\xi , \eta , \zeta , \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \epsilon _ { - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ .There is no term containing only $\pmb { \epsilon } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\epsilon _ { - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ in these equations. The quantity $\zeta$ enters in $\partial R / \partial \xi$ and $\partial R / \partial \eta$ in even degrees, and in $\partial R / \partial \zeta$ in odd degrees.

Consider three arguments $F , G , H$ of the form

$$
F = f v ^ { \prime } + F _ { 0 } , \qquad G = g v ^ { \prime } + G _ { 0 } , \qquad H = h v ^ { \prime } + H _ { 0 } ,

then the coefficients of $\tau$ in each exponential are $f , g , h , - f , - g , - h .$ Now suppose that $\xi , \eta , \zeta$ can be developed in powers of eight quantities $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ : \alpha _ { - 1 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { - 1 } , \gamma _ { 1 } , \gamma _ { - 1 } , \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \epsilon _ { - 1 } ^ { \prime } ,$ ，where there is no term containing only $\pmb { \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } }$ and $\epsilon _ { - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ . # 10.32TROUSSET'S THEORY OF J VIII

Let $x , \ y , \ z$ be the rectangular coordinates of the satellite J VIII referred to Jupiter with fixed direction in space, the $( x , y )$ -plane being

the orbital plane of Jupiter. With $n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = k ^ { 2 } M _ { 0 } ,$ $k ^ { 2 } = n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 }$ and an arbitrary constant $\pmb { \omega }$ ,Trousset (1913) defined

\xi = \frac { x + \sqrt { - 1 } y } { a } \exp \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \mathrm { { n } } \} , { } \qquad \eta = \frac { x - \sqrt { - 1 } y } { a } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 }

$$

$$
m = { \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n } } , \qquad \sigma = \exp { \{ { \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( { \bf n } - { \bf \sigma } { \bf n } ^ { \prime } ) } \} } , \qquad { \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } } = \alpha .
$$

The equations of motion are

$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d  { \mathbf { N } } ^ { 2 } } + m ^ { 2 } \zeta + \frac { \zeta } { \rho ^ { 3 } } = \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial \zeta } ,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { m ^ { 2 } ( \rho ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 3 } - 1 ) ( \frac { 1 } { 4 } \xi \eta - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \zeta ^ { 2 } ) + m ^ { 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 3 } ( \frac { 3 } { 8 } \xi ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } \exp \{ - 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } \} } } \\ { { \qquad + \ \& m ^ { 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 2 } \exp \{ 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } \} ) } } \\ { { \qquad + \ \alpha m ^ { 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 4 } [ ( \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \xi ^ { 2 } \eta - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \xi \zeta ^ { 2 } ) \sigma \exp \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda ^ { \prime } \} } } \\ { { \qquad + \ ( \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \xi \eta ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \eta \zeta ^ { 2 } ) \sigma ^ { - 1 } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda ^ { \prime } \} } } \\ { { \qquad + \ ( \frac { 5 } { 1 6 } \xi ^ { 3 } \sigma ^ { 3 } \exp \{ - 3 \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda ^ { \prime } \} } } \\ { { \qquad + \ \frac { 5 } { 1 6 } \eta ^ { 3 } \sigma ^ { 3 } \exp \{ + \frac { 5 } { 1 6 } \eta ^ { 3 } \sigma ^ { - 3 } \exp \{ 3 \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda ^ { \prime } \} ) ] } } \end{array}
$$

The Laplace equation is written

$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \epsilon _ { 1 } = \epsilon \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( \mathrm { n } - G ) \} } , \qquad \epsilon _ { 2 } = \epsilon \exp { \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( \mathrm { n } - G ) \} } , } \\ & { \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \epsilon ^ { \prime } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( \mathrm { n } ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { \Delta } w ^ { \prime } ) \} } , ~ \gamma _ { 1 } = \gamma \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( \mathrm { n } - H ) \} } , } \\ & { _ { 2 } = \gamma \exp { \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( \mathrm { n } - H ) \} } , ~ \epsilon _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \epsilon ^ { \prime } \exp { \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( \mathrm { n } ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { \varpi } w ^ { \prime } ) \} } . } \end{array}

$$

$$
M _ { p } = \alpha ^ { q } \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { r _ { 1 } } \epsilon _ { 2 } ^ { r _ { 2 } } { \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } ^ { r _ { 1 } } { \epsilon _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } ^ { r _ { 2 } } { \gamma _ { 1 } ^ { s _ { 1 } } } \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { s _ { 2 } } .
$$

Put

$$
r = \rho a = a e ^ { \mu } ;
$$

then we have, the longitude being $v = \mathbf { v } + \lambda _ { : }$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \xi = \exp \left\{ \mu + \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda \right\} \cos \thinspace s , \qquad \eta = \exp \left\{ \mu - \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda \right\} \cos \thinspace s , \nonumber } } \\ { { \zeta = \exp \left\{ \mu \right\} \sin \thinspace s . } } \end{array}
$$

Furthermore write

$$
\begin{array} { c c c } { \mu = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log \omega + M , } & { } & { \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda = L , \sqrt { - 1 } s = S , } \end{array}
$$

where $M , L , S$ are small quantities. We expand $\omega ^ { 1 / 3 } \xi$ ， $\omega ^ { 1 / 3 } \eta$ ， $\omega ^ { 1 / 3 } \sqrt { - 1 } \zeta ,$ $\omega ^ { - 2 / 3 } \rho ^ { - 3 } \xi _ { ; }$ $\omega ^ { - 2 / 3 } \rho ^ { - 3 } \eta$ ， $\bar { \omega } ^ { - 2 / 3 } \rho ^ { - 3 } \sqrt { - 1 } \ \zeta$ ， $\omega ^ { - 1 / 3 } \rho ^ { - 1 }$ ， $\omega ^ { 2 / 3 } \rho ^ { 2 }$ in integral powers of $M , L , S .$ Then we substitute these expansions in the differential equations (l47) and the Laplace equation,equate various coefficients, and determine the coefficients by successive approximations. This is the way Andoyer treated the lunar theory (Section 10.30).

# 10.33ROURE'S THEORY OF PLANETS

In 1939 Roure （l939） combined the theories of Hill,Brown，and Andoyer and devised a method for computing the general perturbation of the motion of asteroids with nearly the same mean motions, such that the mean motions are expressed by $n = n _ { 0 } ( 1 + w )$ ,according to Bohlin's group-perturbation theory (Section 10.11).He (l94O) also discussed the motion of Pluto with the rectangular coordinates.Roure (l947, 1948) then treated the motion of a Trojan asteroid,by referring to Hill's equations of motion in the lunar theory (Section 11.1). Finally he (1946,1947a,1950) applied the same Hill-Brown theory to the motion of the major planets.

Take the moving axes $X _ { 1 }$ ， $Y _ { 1 } , Z _ { 1 }$ rotating with the mean longitude of the disturbing planet, $\mathbf { v } ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + \lambda _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .$ ， $n ^ { \prime } = d \mathbf { v } ^ { \prime } / d t$ . All powers of the mass are taken into account from the first approximation,and the secular terms are eliminated.Let

$$
\mathbf { N } = n t + \lambda _ { 0 } , \qquad T = { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ ( \mathbf { n } - \mathbf { N } ^ { \prime } ) , \qquad m = { \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { n - n ^ { \prime } } } ;
$$

then the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { D ^ { 2 } X _ { 1 } + 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \ m D X _ { 1 } + m ^ { 2 } X _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { ( n - n ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial X _ { 1 } } = 0 , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { D ^ { 2 } Y _ { 1 } - 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \ m D X _ { 1 } + m ^ { 2 } Y _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { ( n - n ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial Y _ { 1 } } = 0 , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { D ^ { 2 } Z _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { ( n - n ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial Z _ { 1 } } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

$$
U = k ^ { 2 } \frac { M _ { 0 } + M } { r } + k ^ { 2 } M ^ { \prime } \bigg [ \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos { H } } } - \frac { r \cos { H } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \bigg ] ,
$$

$$
k ^ { 2 } ( M _ { 0 } + M ) = \kappa ( n - n ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } ,
$$

where $M _ { 0 } , M , M ^ { \prime }$ are respectively the masses of the sun, the disturbed planet and the disturbing planet.

Write

$$
x = X _ { 1 } + { \sqrt { - 1 } } Y _ { 1 } , \qquad a y = X _ { 1 } - { \sqrt { - 1 } } Y _ { 1 } , \qquad a z = { \sqrt { - 1 } } Z _ { 1 } ,
$$

and

$$
= { { \bf { n } } ^ { \prime } } + \frac { { { l ^ { \prime } } } } { { { \sqrt { - 1 } } } } , \qquad s ^ { \prime } = \frac { { { \sigma ^ { \prime } } } } { { \sqrt { - 1 } } } , \qquad v = { { \bf { n } } } + \frac { { { l } } } { { \sqrt { - 1 } } } , \qquad s = \frac { { \sigma } } { { \sqrt { - 1 } } }
$$

$$
\rho = { \frac { a } { r } } , \qquad \rho ^ { \prime } = { \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad \alpha = { \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad 2 \nu = { \frac { M ^ { \prime } } { M _ { 0 } + M ^ { \prime } } } ;

\begin{array} { r } { D ^ { 2 } x + 2 m D x + ( 1 - 2 \nu ) m ^ { 2 } x + \cfrac { 3 } { 2 } \nu m ^ { 2 } \beta ( x + y ) - \kappa \rho ^ { 3 } x + 2 \frac { \partial F } { \partial y } = 0 , } \\ { D ^ { 2 } y - 2 m D y + ( 1 - 2 \nu ) m ^ { 2 } y + \cfrac { 3 } { 2 } \nu m ^ { 2 } \beta ( x + y ) - \kappa \rho ^ { 3 } y + 2 \frac { \partial F } { \partial x } = 0 , } \\ { D ^ { 2 } z - ( \kappa \rho ^ { 3 } + \nu m ^ { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } ) z - \cfrac { \partial F } { \partial z } = 0 , } \end{array}

where $\beta = 1 + \cos 2 s ^ { \prime } .$ $\beta ^ { \prime } = 3 \cos 2 s ^ { \prime } - 1$ ; and $\boldsymbol { v } , \boldsymbol { s }$ and $v ^ { \prime } , s ^ { \prime }$ are the true longitude and the latitude of the disturbed and disturbing planet, respectively.

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F = 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { 4 } \displaystyle \frac { z } { \sqrt { - 1 } } ( x + y ) \sin 2 x ^ { \prime } - 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 8 } ( x y + 2 z ^ { 2 } ) \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } - 1 \big ) } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { 4 } \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } \varepsilon ^ { - 2 \nu } - 1 \big ) x ^ { 2 } + 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } \varepsilon ^ { ( 2 \nu } - 1 ) y ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } ( x y + 2 z ^ { 2 } ) \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } - 1 \big ) + \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } \varepsilon ^ { - 2 \nu } - 1 \big ) x ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } \varepsilon ^ { 2 \nu } - 1 \big ) y ^ { 2 } \big ] \cdot } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { 4 } \displaystyle \frac { z } { \sqrt { - 1 } } x \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } \varepsilon ^ { - \nu } - 1 \big ) \sin 2 x ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + 2 \nu m ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { 4 } \frac { z } { \sqrt { - 1 } } y \big ( \rho ^ { \mathrm { s } } \varepsilon ^ { - \nu } - 1 \big ) \sin 2 x ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + \cdots } } \\ { { \phantom { F = } + \cdots } } \end{array}

If the planet describes a circle on the ecliptic, the equations are satisfied by

$$

under the condition that

where $U , V , Z$ are the terms of the second and higher degree in $\delta x , \delta y$ and $_ z$ . Combining the first two equations,we obtain

We solve these equations by successive approximations.

$$

The solution is expanded in series of the form

\ d { \ b x } = \sum _ { n } \ d { x } _ { n } \ d { M } _ { n } , \qquad \ b y = \sum _ { n } \ d { y } _ { n } \ d { M } _ { n } , \qquad \dotsc ,

$$

Now $M _ { n }$ can be written

# COMETARY PERTURBATION

# 10.34 SPHERE OF ACTIVITY

Let $x , y , z , r$ be the heliocentric coordinates of a comet; $\pmb { x } ^ { \prime } , \pmb { y } ^ { \prime } , \pmb { z } ^ { \prime } , \pmb { r } ^ { \prime }$ the heliocentric coordinates of Jupiter; and $\xi , \eta , \zeta , \rho$ the jovicentric coordinates of the comet.Denote the masses of the sun, Jupiter, and the comet by $M , m ^ { \prime }$ ,and $_ m$ ,respectively.The equations of motion are

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + k ^ { 2 } M \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \bigg ( \frac { x ^ { \prime } - x } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { x ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \bigg ) , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x ^ { \prime } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + k ^ { 2 } ( M + m ^ { \prime } ) \frac { x ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } = 0 , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

Hence, approximately we have

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d t ^ { 2 } } + k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \frac { \xi } { \rho ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } M \bigg ( \frac { x ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } - \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } \bigg ) , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

Put

$$
R ^ { \prime } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } M } { r ^ { 2 } } , \qquad F = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \sqrt { \sum \left( \frac { x ^ { \prime } - x } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { x ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
R ^ { \prime } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } } { \rho ^ { 2 } } , F ^ { \prime } = k ^ { 2 } M \sqrt { \sum \left( \frac { x ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } - \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

where the summation is over the $x \textprime , y \textprime$ and $_ z$ components.The relation

$$
{ \frac { F } { R } } = { \frac { F ^ { \prime } } { R ^ { \prime } } }
$$

represents a surface on which the comet may be considered either as a planet moving around the sun or a satellite moving around Jupiter. The surface is approximately expressed for $m ^ { \prime } \ll M$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \rho } { r ^ { \prime } } = \left( \frac { ( m ^ { \prime } / M ) ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 1 + 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } \right) ^ { 1 / 5 } \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ { + \frac { 2 } { 5 } \cos \theta \left( \frac { ( m ^ { \prime } / M ) ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 1 + 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } \right) ^ { 2 / 5 } \frac { 1 + 6 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } { 1 + 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } + \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\frac { x ^ { \prime } \xi + y ^ { \prime } \eta + z ^ { \prime } \zeta } { \rho r ^ { \prime } } = \cos \theta .
$$

This is the equation of the surface approximately of a sphere.It is called the sphere of activity of Jupiter. Outside the sphere we have $F / R < F ^ { \prime } / R ^ { \prime }$ and the comet can be considered as a planet. Inside the sphere we have $F / R > F ^ { \prime } / R ^ { \prime }$ and the comet can be considered as a satellite. The radii of the spheres of activity for Mercury, Venus, Earth,Mars, Jupiter, Saturn,Uranus, Neptune,are,respectively, 0.00l, 0.004, 0.006, 0.004, 0.322, 0.363,0.339, 0.576 astronomical units.

When a comet approaches Jupiter, the orbit, initially an ellipse with large eccentricity, is altered by a considerable degree due to the perturbation by Jupiter. The orbit of the comet that appeared in 1770 could be represented well enough by an ellipse with period 5.5 years.But it has been lost since.Lexell thought that the comet，having approached Jupiter during l767 and 1779,was describing an elliptic orbit in 1767 and again returned to an elliptic orbit in 1779.According to Burkhardt the semimajor axis was 5.06 A.U. with perihelion distance 2.96 A.U. before 1770, but changed to 6.37 A.U.with perihelion distance 3.33 A.U. after 1779 (Le Verrier, l857). Comet Wolf approached Jupiter in 1875 and again in l922.By a strange chance the second encounter almost exactly reversed the effect of the first encounter and the orbit was changed back again into practically the original orbit.Before 1875 and after 1922 the orbit had a perihelion distance of 2.5 A.U.,whereas the temporary orbit from l875 to l922 had a perihelion distance of only about 1.5 A.U. The aphelion distance remained more or less unchanged at 5.5 A.U., a value quite close to that of Jupiter's orbit. Brooks’ comet (1889 V) spent over two days within the sphere of activity ofJupiter and meanwhile described $3 1 3 ^ { \circ }$ of longitude around Jupiter at a distance of

2.28 times the radius of Jupiter without producing detectable perturbations on any of the satellites,but as a result the period of the comet's orbit was changed from 29 to 7 years. Comet d'Arrest approached Jupiter in 186l.Comet Tempel I (l867 II） has a very small relative velocity in its approach to Jupiter；hence it stays a long time in the neighborhood of Jupiter. Some doubt whether the comet has been captured to the solar system by Jupiter's action.The eccentricity has decreased,but after reaching its minimum it will increase again and the orbit might become hyperbolic. The eccentricity of Comet De Vico-Swift was O.617 in l844, 0.572 in l894,and further decreased to 0.516 in its approach to Jupiter in l897; it was then lost,but reappeared in 1965.Comet Biela has been lost since l852. Recently Marsden and Aksnes (1967） found that the period of Comet l963 VIII changed from about 5O to 9 years and the perihelion distance from 4.3 to 2.2 A.U.,during the close approach to Jupiter in 1961.

The question whether a comet now appearing after its approach to Jupiter is the same as the one that appeared formerly can be judged by the Jacobi integral. Take the orbital plane of Jupiter as the $( x , y )$ -plane. Suppose that Jupiter's orbit is circular,and denote its longitude and the radius by $l ^ { \prime }$ and $\boldsymbol { a ^ { \prime } }$ ,respectively. Then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } x } { r ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \Big ( \frac { a ^ { \prime } \cos l ^ { \prime } - x } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { \cos l ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \Big ) , } \\ & { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } y } { r ^ { 3 } } = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \Big ( \frac { a ^ { \prime } \sin l ^ { \prime } - y } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { \sin l ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \Big ) , } \\ & { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } z } { r ^ { 3 } } = - k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \frac { z } { \rho ^ { 3 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \rho ^ { 2 } = ( x - a ^ { \prime } \cos { l ^ { \prime } } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - a ^ { \prime } \sin { l ^ { \prime } } ) ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { l ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + c , d l ^ { \prime } / d t = n ^ { \prime } . } } \end{array}
$$

From these equations we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } - y \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } ( x \sin l ^ { \prime } - y \cos l ^ { \prime } ) \displaystyle \biggl ( \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { a ^ { \prime 3 } } \biggr ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d x } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d z } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { d r } { d t } } } \\ { { = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \biggl [ \displaystyle \frac { a ^ { \prime } \cos l ^ { \prime } - x } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \displaystyle \frac { d x } { d t } + \frac { a ^ { \prime } \sin l ^ { \prime } - y } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \displaystyle \frac { d y } { d t } - \frac { z } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \displaystyle \frac { d z } { d t } } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \left( \frac { d x } { d t } \cos l ^ { \prime } + \frac { d y } { d t } \sin l ^ { \prime } \right) \biggr ] . } } \end{array}
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { - \frac { x \cos l ^ { \prime } + y \sin l ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { 2 } } \Big ] } \\ & { \quad = \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \left[ ( a ^ { \prime } \cos l ^ { \prime } - x ) \Big ( \frac { d x } { d t } + n ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } \sin l ^ { \prime } \Big ) \right. } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \left. + \left( a ^ { \prime } \sin l ^ { \prime } - y \right) \Big ( \frac { d y } { d t } - n ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } \cos l ^ { \prime } \Big ) - z \frac { d z } { d t } \right] } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad - \frac { 1 } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \left( \frac { d x } { d t } \cos l ^ { \prime } + \frac { d y } { d t } \sin l ^ { \prime } \right) + \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \left( x \sin l ^ { \prime } - y \cos l ^ { \prime } \right) , } \end{array}
$$

the equation (152) gives

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { { \frac { d } { d t } } \left[ \left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d z } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } - { \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } } { r } } \right] } \\ & { \qquad = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } { \frac { d } { d t } } \left[ { \frac { 1 } { \rho } } - { \frac { x \cos l ^ { \prime } + y \sin l ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } } \right] } \\ & { \qquad \quad \qquad \quad + k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } ( x \sin l ^ { \prime } - y \cos l ^ { \prime } ) \left( { \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } } - { \frac { \dot { z } } { a } } \right) } \end{array} }
$$

Combining this last equation with (l5l), we obtain the integral

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { d x } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { r } - m ^ { \prime } \bigg ( x \frac { d y } { d t } - y \frac { d x } { d t } \bigg ) } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \bigg [ } - k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { \rho } - \frac { x \cos l ^ { \prime } + y \sin l ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \bigg ) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } . } \end{array}
$$

This is called the Jacobi integral.

Let us apply this integral to a comet supposed to have been influenced to a considerable degree due to Jupiter's perturbation. We have

$$
\left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \ \left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \ \left( { \frac { d z } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 2 k ^ { 2 } } { r } } - { \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { a } } ,
$$

$$
x \frac { d y } { d t } - y \frac { d x } { d t } = k \sqrt { \dot { p } } \cos I , \qquad n ^ { \prime } = k / a ^ { \prime 3 / 2 } .
$$

Denote the values of $a , p ,$ $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ for the two epochs $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 1 }$ by $a _ { 0 } , p _ { 0 } , I _ { 0 } ;$ and $a _ { 1 } , ~ p _ { 1 } , ~ I _ { 1 }$ ，respectively. The Jacobi integral applied at these two epochs gives

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { \circ } + \frac { 2 \sqrt { \dot { p } _ { 0 } } \cos I _ { 0 } } { a ^ { \prime } ^ { 3 / 2 } } + 2 m ^ { \prime } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 0 } } - \frac { x _ { 0 } \cos l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + y _ { 0 } \sin l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \bigg ) } } \\ { \displaystyle { = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 } } + \frac { 2 \sqrt { \dot { p } _ { 1 } } \cos I _ { 1 } } { a ^ { \prime } ^ { 3 / 2 } } + 2 m ^ { \prime } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 1 } } - \frac { x _ { 1 } \cos l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + y _ { 1 } \sin l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \bigg ) } } \end{array}
$$

Let $t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 }$ be the two epochs when the comet entered and left the sphere of activity. We can take $\rho _ { 0 } = \rho _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 1 } - x _ { 0 } , y _ { 1 } - y _ { 0 } , z _ { 1 } - z _ { 0 }$ to be small. Hence the criterion is

$$
\frac { 1 } { \alpha _ { 0 } } + \frac { 2 \sqrt { \dot { \rho } _ { 0 } } \cos I _ { 0 } } { a ^ { \prime 3 / 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \frac { 2 \sqrt { \dot { \rho } _ { 1 } } \cos I _ { 1 } } { a ^ { \prime 3 / 2 } } = \alpha .
$$

This expression was obtained by Tisserand (l889） and was called the invariant of a comet by Callandreau. Callandreau (1892） and then Matukuma (19l7） extended the criterion to the case when the eccentricity of Jupiter's orbit is taken into account. The criterion is

$$
\frac { 1 } { a } + \frac { 2 \sqrt { a ^ { \prime } } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \sqrt { \dot { p } } + \frac { 2 e e ^ { \prime } } { \sqrt { \dot { p } a ^ { \prime } } } \sin { M ^ { \prime } } \sin { ( l ^ { \prime } - \varpi ) } = \mathrm { i n v a r i a n t } ,
$$

where $M ^ { \prime }$ is the mean anomaly of Jupiter. Heinrich (19l2,1916,1918) also extended the idea of the Jacobi integral in order to apply it to the motion of characteristic asteroids,by taking Jupiter's eccentricity into account.
