Ovenden and Roy (l96l） obtained formal expressions for the Jacobi integral and the angular-momentum integrals of the elliptical restricted three-body problem in terms of auxiliary functions.Let $G$ be the center of mass of $M$ and $m ^ { \prime } = \mu M .$ Take a nonuniformly rotating axis around the $G \zeta .$ -axis with a variable angular velocity $\dot { \phi }$ ,such that $G \xi$ is always in the direction of $m ^ { \prime } G M ,$ and the $G \xi \eta$ -plane contains the instantaneous velocities of $M$ and $m ^ { \prime }$ .Let $G M = r _ { 1 } = \mu r _ { 2 }$ and $G m ^ { \prime } = r _ { 2 }$ at $t = 0$ Then,

$$
\dot { \phi } ^ { 2 } = \frac { M + m ^ { \prime } } { ( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } ( 1 + \ell ^ { \prime } \cos v ^ { \prime } ) , \qquad r _ { 1 } = \mu r _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu a ^ { \prime } ( 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } ) } { 1 + e ^ { \prime } \cos v ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

where $v ^ { \prime }$ is the true anomaly of the orbit for $m ^ { \prime }$ . Ovenden and Roy obtained the extended Jacobi integral in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \zeta } ^ { 2 } \quad \quad \quad } \\ { \quad \quad = \dot { v } ^ { 2 } \big ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } \big ) + 2 \bigg ( \frac { M } { \rho _ { 1 } } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \rho _ { 2 } } \bigg ) - \frac { 2 e ^ { \prime } ( M + m ^ { \prime } ) } { h ( 1 + \mu ) } F + \kappa ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial \hat { F } } { \partial \kappa ^ { \prime } } ; } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
( \eta \dot { \xi } - \dot { \eta } \xi ) - \dot { v } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { L } { 1 + \mu } = C ,
$$

and $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ is the integration constant. $\boldsymbol { F }$ and $L$ are unknown auxiliary functions of time,defined by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \dot { F } = \left[ m ^ { \prime } \xi \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } \Big ) - m ^ { \prime } r _ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { \mu } { \rho _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } \Big ) - \frac { 2 h L } { ( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } \right] \sin v , \ } } \\ { { \dot { L } = - m ^ { \prime } ( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } ) \eta \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } \Big ) . \ } } \end{array}
$$

The so-called Jacobi constant $\kappa _ { e }$ in the elliptic case is related to the Jacobi constant $\pmb { \kappa }$ in the circular case, where $\dot { v } = n$ by

$$
\kappa _ { e } = \kappa ^ { \prime } + 2 C \dot { v } - \frac { 2 e ^ { \prime } ( M + m ^ { \prime } ) } { h ( 1 + \mu ) } F \equiv \kappa ( 1 + e ^ { \prime } A ) ,
$$

$$
{ \cal A } = { \frac { 2 } { e ^ { \prime } \kappa } } ( \dot { v } - n ) C - { \frac { 2 ( M + m ^ { \prime } ) } { h ( 1 + \mu ) \kappa } } F .
$$

At an initial epoch $t = 0$ the constants $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ and $C$ may be evaluated from these equations by taking the functions $\pmb { F }$ and $L$ equal to zero. The surface defined by the left-hand member of the above extended Jacobi integral varies with time, since $\dot { v }$ ， $r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } $ and $\pmb { F }$ vary with time. The zero-velocity curve of Hill (Section 9.13),which serves to restrict the domain of motion, is only for the circular restricted three-body problem. No information of the behavior of $\pmb { F }$ is yet available. Hence, if the eccentricity of the orbit of $m ^ { \prime }$ around $M$ is taken into account, then any conclusion similar to the conclusion drawn from the Jacobi integral in the circular case cannot be obtained for a long-range effect.

The orbits of comets can hardly be determined accurately enough and sometimes are thought to be parabolic or hyperbolic.The periodic comets are roughly classified into the long-period and short-period groups. Comets belonging to the short-period group are of smaller inclination to the ecliptic. It may be conjectured that Jupiter's action may have altered the orbits so much that they are now of short period. Several comets of this group are sometimes lost.For example, Brorsen's comet of 1846 of period 5.5 years has not been seen since l879; Holmes's comet of 1892 of period 7 years was not found in l919 or in l928, but was observed in l964.Even Encke's comet was sometimes missed through unfavorable circumstances, though it was rediscovered later.Because of their close dynamical association with Jupiter,these comets are called

Jupiter'sfamily.Kazimircak-Polonskaya (1967) discussed various methods of computing cometary perturbations for comets of the Jupiter group and studied the perturbation of Comet Wolf I due to Jupiter during its approach to Jupiter in 1922,and the evolution of its orbit from 1660 to 2060.

Comets of longer periods, between about 100 to l000 years, show no such close relation with Jupiter's orbit; none of these comets comes close enough to Jupiter. There are comets of much longer periods, say 10,000 years. On the other hand, there are comets which come very near the sun in very elongated orbits,and which may be disrupted by the sun's heat. Indeed, the l882 comet was disrupted after passing very near the sun,forming a group of four comets with periods 670, 770, 880,and 960 years.

The distribution of the aphelia shows a peculiar character and suggests their grouping; they may be conjectured to be of the same origin. Some have thought the distribution to be due to the perturbation of unknown trans-Neptunian planets.

Some comets show progressive shortening of the period. The period of Encke's comet decreased from 3.3 years, its value of l8l8,by the amount of 2.5 days in l944. The rate of the decrease is not regular.A similar circumstance occurs for the orbits of meteoric swarms studied by the Harvard College Observatory. Kamienski (l959） and Kazimircak-Polonskaya （196l，1962）studied Comet Wolf I,Makover（1955） and Makover and Bokhan (196l） Comet Encke-Backlund.Kastel (1965) studied the close approach of Comet Brooks II to Jupiter in l886 by taking into account the perturbations by five major planets from Venus to Saturn and the oblateness of Jupiter.From his result he is in favor of the capture hypothesis of short-period comet.

The great comet of 1882 II approached the sun. It is one of the group of six comets with extremely small perihelion passages. This shows that a parent comet survived such a close perihelion passage at least once in the past and split up into a number of components that have since endured. Three members of this group - Comets 1880 I, 1882 II, and 1887 I- with periods of nearly a thousand years returned within an interval of ten years (Whipple 1961, 1962,1963).Comet 1882 II has five distinct nuclei.Nebulous matter with a considerable escape velocity around the nuclei was observed.The best-known examples of earlier disruptions resulting in independently observed nuclei occurred in Comet Biela l946 II,Comet Taylor l96l I,and the Southern Comet 1947 XII. Comet Brooks II consists of four distinct nuclei.Two separate nuclei of Comet Wirtanen l957 VI were observed for more than two years. The splitting occurred at a heliocentric distance of well over 4 A.U.A number of comets have passed within a perihelion distance of 0.1 A.U. from the sun. Fading in the brightness of a comet also occurs, for example, Comet Ensor l926 III, Comet Pajdusakova 1954 II, and Periodic Comet Westphal 1913 VI. Sudden changes in brightness have often been observed in periodic Comet Schwassmann-Wachmann (1) 1925 II, for example,a sudden outburst occurred after a long period of quiescence (Roemer,1963).

Roemer (196l) discussed the residuals of the acceleration in the motion of periodic comets, especially Comet l884 III (Wolf I). Brandt (1961) discussed the motion of periodic Comets $1 9 5 4 \mathrm { ~ h ~ }$ (Baade) and $1 9 5 4 \ \mathrm { k }$ (Haro-Chavira） in connection with the interaction of comet tails with the interplanetary medium.The observed motions of knots in comet tails have been explained by Biermann (l951) in terms of the outward streaming of the interplanetary plasma from the sun.Brandt showed that Chamberlain's model (1960,196l),but not Parker's model (1958, 1960),of the solar corona causing this streaming is consistent with these observations. Thus the motion of a comet might give a clue for studying the interplanetary solar stream of plasma.

Since Oort (l95O） raised a hypothesis on the origin of short-period comets (Oort and Schmidt,195l),Barteneva (1955),Galibina (1958, 1963,1964)，Makover (1955a,l964),Bilo and van de Hulst (1960), Bilo and van Houten-Groeneveld (1960),Pels (1960),and Pels-Kluyver (1960） computed the original orbits of several comets.Galibina (1963) gave the distribution of the original and future semimajor axes. These works show that—for these comets， at least—the capture scheme, rather than the ejection hypothesis,is favored in celestial mechanics. Brady (l965） showed by studying nearly parabolic orbits that a large majority of the original orbits were elliptic before the comet entered the region of the planets. These comets were members of the solar system and their velocities were increased by the planetary perturbations as they approached the sun.His computation shows that over 75 percent of his 30 comets pick up energy and show increased eccentricity after passing through the planetary system,and that after a sufficiently long interval of time during which they pass through the planetary system those comets will attain hyperbolic velocity and be ejected. Sekanina (1966, 1967) computed the future orbits of ten comets in his General Catalogue of Original and Future Comet Orbits (1966a). Sekanina studied the non-gravitational effects on cometary motions and on a model of an arbitrarily rotating comet nucleus $( 1 9 6 7 \mathsf { a } )$ ，and discussed Whipple's suggestion (1950) on the interaction of escaping matter and the rotating nucleus that affects the daily mean motion and the orbital elements, the change being sudden in character. Recently Sekanina and Vanysek (l967) and Brady and Carpenter (l967) discussed the irregularities in the motion of Comet Halley l910 (Lyttleton, 1953).

Chebotarev (1963，1964)，Chebotarev and Bozkova (1960，1962, 1963),and Chebotarev and Volkov (l962) studied the motion of a body of infinitesimal massin the outer region of the solar system under the perturbation by the galactic nucleus,and showed that a stable motion is possible at a distance of 230,0oO A.U. from the sun in the case of direct motion and of 100,0oo A.U. in the case of retrograde motion. The orbital elements are seen to be subject to considerable perturbation.Chebotarev (1966,l968) again attacked the same problem.He showed that the stable motion of a comet with $e _ { 0 } = 0 . 6$ is possible at a distance of 80,000 A.U. from the sun and that the boundaries of the cloud of comets are approximately 60,000-100,000 A.U.Dukhnovski (1958) discussed the influence of the disintegration of comets by assuming that the distribution of the perihelia of long-period comets is proportional to the perihelion distance from the sun,and pointed out that the absence of short-period comets with retrograde motion is due to their small perihelion distances.

Marsden (1968,1969a，1970a） investigated the nongravitational forces acting on comets, that is, the difference between the nongravitational effects detected in the motions of the short- and the long-period comets, in the former case the transverse component of the forces and in the latter case the radial component. The radial component seems to depend on the inverse square of heliocentric distance and the transverse component,on a much higher power of inverse heliocentric distance. Marsden discussed the problem of systematic residuals in the orbit of the periodic Comet P/Encke. There is a diffculty when a comet strongly affected by nongravitational forces makes a relatively closed approach to Jupiter. Marsden studied the cases of P/Whipple,P/Forbes,P/Honda-Mrkos-Pajdusaková,Comet 1960 II (Burnham)，Comet l957 III (Arend-Roland)， P/Perrine-Mrkos， P/d'Arrest, P/Pons-Winnecke, P/Daniel, P/Wirtanen, P/Arend,P/Oterma,P/Schaumasse,and P/Perrine.He established that the secular variation in the motion of P/Pons-Winnecke changed sign around the turn of the century,a fact that is presumably connected with this comet's repeated encounters with Jupiter.

# 10.35 CAPTURE OF A COMET BY JUPITER

Let $M _ { 0 }$ and $M _ { 1 }$ be respectively the points at which a comet enters and leaves the sphere of activity. Referring to Figure 4, we suppose

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0006_pages_0201-0240/ocr/images/8562826922014636ba0a7ef7ca4fb3bd3366738415c7238e804c4f17df172fd4.jpg)  
FIGURE 4

that approximately $\angle S J M _ { 0 } = 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ and that the orbital velocity of the comet is in the direction $M _ { 0 } J .$ . We have

$$
v ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { 2 } { r } - \frac { 1 } { a } \Big )
$$

for an elliptic motion. Denote by $v _ { 0 }$ the velocity of the comet at infinity. We suppose approximately $r = S M _ { 0 } = S J = r ^ { \prime }$ Then we have $v _ { 0 } = k \sqrt { 2 / r ^ { \prime } }$ For $\scriptstyle e ^ { \prime } \ = 0$ the velocity $\boldsymbol { v } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ of Jupiter is directed to the prolongation of $M _ { 0 } J _ { : }$ and $v _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = k / \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } }$

In the motion around $\boldsymbol { J }$ we have

$$
V _ { 0 } = v _ { 0 } - v _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = k \frac { \sqrt { 2 } - 1 } { \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } } } .
$$

Denote by $R$ and $\pmb { A }$ respectively the radius vector and the semimajor axis in the relative motion of the comet around Jupiter; then,

$$
V ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { 2 } { R } - \frac { 1 } { A } \right) .
$$

Put $R = \rho _ { ; }$ $V = V _ { 0 }$ ; then,

$$
\frac { 1 } { m ^ { \prime } } ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { 2 } \frac { \rho } { r ^ { \prime } } = 2 - \frac { \rho } { A } \cdot
$$

Substituting the actual value for Jupiter on the left-hand side,we obtain approximately $A = - \rho / 9 < 0$ .Hence the jovicentric motion of the comet is hyperbolic.

The point $M _ { 1 }$ at which the comet leaves the sphere of activity is near the point $M _ { 0 }$ . Since $R = \rho$ ,we have

$$
V _ { 1 } = k \frac { 1 - \sqrt { 2 } } { \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } } } .
$$

Combining this value $V _ { 1 }$ with $\pmb { v _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } .$ ，we obtain the absolute velocity $v _ { 1 }$ of the comet at the epoch of leaving the sphere of activity:

$$
\upsilon _ { 1 } = k \frac { 2 - \sqrt { 2 } } { \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } } } .
$$

Put $v = v _ { 1 } , r = r _ { 1 } , a = a _ { 1 }$ in (154). This gives the elliptic orbit after the comet has passed across the sphere of activity. Then, since $\gamma ^ { \prime } \approx 5 . 2 0 3$ ，

$$
a _ { 1 } = r ^ { \prime } { \frac { { \sqrt { 2 } } + 1 } { 4 } } = 3 . 1 4 .
$$

This applies to Comet Brorsen and Comet Winnecke.

Tisserand (1889） considered the effect of the deviation from $\pmb { \pi }$ of the angle $H _ { 0 }$ between the initial relative velocity $V _ { 0 }$ and the jovicentric radius vector.Let the jovicentric polar coordinates of the comet be $\scriptstyle { R }$ and $\Theta$ , then, since $R = \rho _ { \mathrm { * } }$ ， $\Theta = \Theta _ { 0 }$ at $t _ { 0 }$ and again $R = \rho , \Theta = \Theta _ { 1 }$ at $t _ { 1 }$ ， we have

$$
P = { \frac { V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { k ^ { 2 } m ^ { ' } } } \rho ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } H _ { 0 } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { E \sin { ( \Theta _ { 0 } - \Pi ) } = \displaystyle \frac { V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } } \rho \sin { H _ { 0 } } \cos { H _ { 0 } } , } \\ { E \cos { ( \Theta _ { 0 } - \Pi ) } = \displaystyle \frac { V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { k ^ { 2 } m ^ { \prime } } \rho \sin ^ { 2 } { H _ { 0 } } - 1 , } \end{array}
$$

where $\Pi$ is the jovicentric longitude of the comet's perijove, $\mathcal { P }$ the perijove distance, and $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ the eccentricity. According to the sign of sin $H _ { 0 }$ ， the relative motion is direct or retrograde. Since $x J M _ { 0 } = - \Theta _ { 0 } .$ $A J M _ { 0 } = \Pi - \Theta _ { 0 } ,$ and $A J M _ { 1 } = \Theta _ { 1 } - \Pi = A J M _ { 0 } ,$ we have

$$
\Theta _ { 0 } + \Theta _ { 1 } = 2 \Pi ,
$$

$X = R \cos \Theta$ $Y = R \sin \Theta$ and $R = P / [ 1 + E$ cos $( \Theta - \pi ) ]$ . Denote by $\scriptstyle \gamma ^ { \prime }$ and $\chi \prime$ the heliocentric coordinates of Jupiter; then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 k } { \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } } } \left[ \left( \frac { d Y } { d t } \right) _ { 0 } \cos l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - \left( \frac { d Y } { d t } \right) _ { 1 } \cos l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - \left( \frac { d X } { d t } \right) _ { 0 } \sin l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \left. \qquad + \ \left( \frac { d X } { d t } \right) _ { 1 } \sin \left( \frac { d Y } { d t } \right) _ { 0 } \right] _ { } \sin 2 l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } } \end{array}
$$

because $V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ . From the equations

$$
v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \Bigl ( { \frac { 2 } { r _ { 0 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { a _ { 0 } } } \Bigr ) , \qquad v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \Bigl ( { \frac { 2 } { r _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 } } } \Bigr ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle _ { \mathrm { \Lambda } ^ { - } } - \frac { 1 } { a _ { 0 } } = \frac { 2 } { k \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } } } \left[ \left( \frac { d Y } { d t } \right) _ { 0 } \cos { l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } - \left( \frac { d Y } { d t } \right) _ { 1 } \cos { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } - \left( \frac { d X } { d t } \right) _ { 0 } \sin { l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } \right. } \\ { \displaystyle \left. + \ \left( \frac { d X } { d t } \right) _ { 1 } \sin { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } \right] + 2 \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \right) \cos { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } . } \end{array}
$$

or, substituting $d X / d t , d Y / d t ,$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { a _ { 0 } } \bigg ) = \sqrt { \frac { m ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime } } { P } } \sin \bigg ( \frac { l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } - \Pi \bigg ) \sin \bigg ( \Theta _ { 0 } - \Pi + \frac { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \bigg ) } } \\ { \displaystyle { + E \sqrt { \frac { m ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime } } { P } } \sin \bigg ( \frac { l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } - \Pi \bigg ) \sin \frac { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } + \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r _ { 0 } } \bigg ) } } \end{array}
$$

We suppose that approximately $l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = 0$ .Then the initial orbit is parabolic in this approximation and we have

$$
S = \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { 4 a _ { 1 } } = \sqrt { \frac { m ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime } } { P } } \sin { ( l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - \Pi ) } \sin { ( \Theta _ { 0 } - \Pi ) } .
$$

Put

$$
\lambda = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { k } \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \beta = \frac { 1 } { m ^ { \prime } } \frac { \rho } { r ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \phi _ { 0 } = l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - \Theta _ { 0 } ;
$$

then,after some calculation, we can rewrite:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { ; H _ { 0 } [ ( 1 - \beta \lambda ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } H _ { 0 } ) \sin \phi _ { 0 } - \beta \lambda ^ { 2 } \sin H _ { 0 } \cos H _ { 0 } \cos \phi _ { 0 } ] } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad \div } & { [ 1 + \beta ^ { 2 } \lambda ^ { 4 } \Big ( 1 - \frac { 2 } { \beta \lambda ^ { 2 } } \Big ) \sin ^ { 2 } H _ { 0 } ] . } \end{array}
$$

Let $9 0 ^ { \circ } + \sigma ^ { \prime }$ be the angle between the absolute velocity at $M _ { 0 }$ and the prolongation of SJ. We have $\lambda ^ { 2 } = 1 + l ^ { 2 } - 2 l \cos \sigma ^ { \prime }$ ， $l = \sqrt { 2 r ^ { \prime } / r _ { 0 } }$ In

our approximation we have assumed that $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 0 } = \boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime }$ ; hence $l = { \sqrt { 2 } } .$ Then $s$ can be put in a simpler form.Let

$$
u = \beta \lambda ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - \frac { 2 } { \beta \lambda ^ { 2 } } } \sin H _ { 0 } ;
$$

then,

$$
{ \cal S } = [ l \cos \sigma ^ { \prime } - 1 + u l \sin \sigma ] \div [ 1 + u ^ { 2 } ] .
$$

From this equation we can compute $s .$ ,and accordingly $\pmb { a _ { 1 } }$ ,in terms of ${ \pmb \sigma } ^ { \prime }$ and $\pmb { u }$ If $\pmb { \sigma } ^ { \prime } = \pmb { u } = 0$ ，then $\bar { S = l } - 1 = \sqrt { 2 } - 1$ . This is what we obtained at first.The eccentricity of the hyperbola is $E = \sqrt { 1 + u ^ { 2 } }$

S has its maximum value $S _ { 1 } = \overline { { ( l \sqrt { 1 + u ^ { 2 } } } - 1 ) } / ( 1 + u ^ { 2 } )$ at $u = \tan \sigma ^ { \prime }$ for a fixed value of $\pmb { u }$ ,and a maximum $S _ { 2 } = 1 / 2$ for $\boldsymbol { u } = 1$ and $\sigma ^ { \prime } = 4 5 ^ { \circ }$ ， $a _ { 1 } = r ^ { \prime } / 2$ .Hence, for a periodic comet we cannot have $a _ { 1 } > 3 . 1 4$ For the periodic Comet Barnard we have $a _ { 1 } = 2 . 9 8$ .The function $s$ can reach at its upper limit $\sqrt { 2 } - 1$ . In order that this formula for $s$ be satisfied for a real value of ${ \pmb \sigma } ^ { \prime }$ with a fixed $\pmb { u } ,$ it is necessary that

$$
\frac { 1 - S - \sqrt { 1 - 2 S } } { S ^ { 2 } } < 1 + u ^ { 2 } < \frac { 1 - S + \sqrt { 1 - 2 S } } { S ^ { 2 } } .
$$

There should be an upper limit and a lower limit of $\pmb { u }$ for each value of $s$ The lower limit is zero if $( 1 - S - \sqrt { 1 - 2 S } ) / S ^ { 2 } < 1$ ，that is,if $S < \sqrt { 2 } - 1$ . The upper limit is the upper limit of the eccentricity of the jovicentric hyperbolic orbit.

Tisserand (1889) also saw that

$$
\sqrt { 1 - \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } = 2 \sqrt { S } ( \sqrt { 2 } \cos \sigma ^ { \prime } - 2 S ) , a _ { 1 } = \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { 4 S } ,
$$

should be less than unity; for example, $e _ { 1 } > 0 . 6 9 3$ for $a _ { \mathrm { 1 } } = 3 . 0$ and $e _ { 1 } > 0 . 5 2 0$ for $a _ { 1 } = 3 . 8$ ，The quantities $\pmb { \ell _ { 1 } }$ and ${ \pmb a } _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ are obtained from ${ \pmb { \sigma } } ^ { \prime }$ and $S _ { : }$ or ${ \pmb { \sigma } } ^ { \prime }$ and $s$ are computed from the known values of ${ \pmb a } _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ and $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 1 }$

Bosler (1931） extended this discussion of Tisserand.Schulhof (1891) studied the large perturbation of a periodic comet by Jupiter by transforming to its jovicentric motion.

# 10.36THE POINCARE-SCHWARZSCHILD THEOREM

Poincaré (l9l2) had the idea that the probability of a capture is infinitely small. Schwarzschild (l896) considered after Poincaré the motion of molecules of an incompressible liquid.He proved the theorem that it is impossible for a finite mass of liquid arriving from a point at infinity to remain at a finite distance for a long interval of time in the future. Thus any comet approaching the sun in a parabolic or a hyperbolic orbit,although it may once acquire an elliptic orbit by the perturbation of Jupiter, should recede far from the sun in a parabolic or a hyperbolic orbit.

Take the sun as the origin. Let $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 )$ be the coordinates and velocity components of a comet; let its equations of motion under the action of Jupiter be

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = X _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 ) ,
$$

where $X _ { 4 } , X _ { 5 } , X _ { 6 }$ depend on $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 3 }$ only, and $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 }$ are equal to ${ x _ { 4 } } , { x _ { 5 } } , { x _ { 6 } } ,$ respectively. Hence,

$$
\sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0 .
$$

For simplicity, consider an equation of the third order:

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } } = X _ { 1 } , \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = X _ { 2 } , \frac { d x _ { 3 } } { d t } = X _ { 3 } ,
$$

with

$$
\frac { \partial X _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial X _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } + \frac { \partial X _ { 3 } } { \partial x _ { 3 } } = 0 ,
$$

where $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 }$ are periodic with the common period $_ { \pmb { T } }$ Suppose that $x _ { 1 } , ~ x _ { 2 } , ~ x _ { 3 }$ represent the rectangular coordinates in a three-dimensional space.Let the initial values of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ ${ \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 2 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 3 }$ be $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , a _ { 3 }$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ . The solution of the equations of motion represents the motion of the particle initially at $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , a _ { 3 }$ as a function of $t .$ If we vary $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , a _ { 3 }$ ，then we obtain the orbits of different particles. The last equation is the equation of continuity, that is, the condition for incompressibility. Since we suppose that $X$ is periodic with the period $T$ in $t _ { : }$ ，the state of motion at $t = t _ { 0 }$ is repeated after the lapse of the period $T _ { : }$ ，that is,at the times $t _ { 0 } + T ,$ $t _ { 0 } + 2 T , t _ { 0 } + 3 T , . . . .$

For the proof of the theorem we consider a finite mass of liquid that approaches the coordinate origin once.Take a small but finite volume $v$ around the origin. Suppose that a liquid molecule that was outside $\pmb { v }$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ enters the volume $v$ at $t = t _ { 1 }$ . Assume that the molecule, once having entered $v$ ，would remain inside $v$ all the time after $t _ { 1 }$ . Since the state of motion is periodic, the same finite amount of liquid should enter the volume $v$ again and again at $t = t _ { 1 } + T _ { : }$ $t _ { 1 } + 2 T , \dots$ .By our assumption these masses are different from the mass that entered $v$ at first. Thus the volume $v$ would be congested with the masses entering into U successively in each interval of $_ { T }$ But the liquid is incompressible. Hence any mass entering the volume $v$ should leave the volume in some finite time.

We can extend the theorem easily to six dimensions. The volume $v$ is limited by

$$
\phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 6 } ) < C .
$$

From the relation

$$
\frac { \partial X _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial X _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial X _ { 6 } } { \partial x _ { 6 } } = 0 ,
$$

the integral

$$
\int d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { 6 }
$$

is an integral invariant (Sections 4.l7-4.22). Hence the volume of the liquid remains unaltered during the whole motion.

For the motion of a comet, we limit the volume by

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } < r ^ { 2 } , \qquad x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } < v ^ { 2 } .
$$

Von Zeipel (l905） proved that, if the radius vector of a comet has only a finite number of maxima and minima, then a capture is only a temporary phenomenon.

Let $\xi , \eta$ be the rectangular coordinates of a comet referred to a rotating system，the $O \xi .$ -axis always pointing from the sun toward Jupiter, which is supposed to be revolving around the sun with uniform angular velocity. Put

$$
V = \frac { M } { r } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { \rho } , ~ F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \zeta } ^ { 2 } ) + \dot { \xi } \eta - \xi \dot { \eta } - V ;
$$

then the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { \displaystyle { \frac { d \xi } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \dot { \xi } } , \quad } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { d \eta } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \dot { \eta } } , \quad } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { d \zeta } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \dot { \zeta } } } , } \\ { \displaystyle { \frac { d \dot { \xi } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \dot { \xi } } , \quad } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { d \dot { \eta } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \eta } , \quad } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { d \dot { \zeta } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \dot { \zeta } } } . } \end{array}
$$

We have the Jacobi integral

$$
F = { \tt c o n s t a n t . }
$$

Consider a six-dimensional space $\xi , \ \eta , \ \zeta , \ \dot { \xi } , \ \dot { \eta } , \ \dot { \zeta } .$ A maximum or minimum of the radius vector occurs if $\Phi = \xi \dot { \xi } + \eta \dot { \eta } + \zeta \dot { \zeta } = 0 .$ This

surface $\Phi = 0$ constitutes a five-dimensional subspace imbedded in the six-dimensional space. The surface $\Phi = 0$ is divided into two parts: $\Phi _ { u }$ where $\dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \zeta } ^ { 2 } + r ( \partial V / \partial r ) > 0 _ { : }$ and $\Phi _ { v }$ where

$$
\dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \zeta } ^ { 2 } + r \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } < 0 .
$$

In order to prove the theorem it is suffcient to show that any trajectory in the six-dimensional space with a hyperbolic branch does not cross the surface $\Phi _ { u }$ or $\Phi _ { v }$ infinitely often.

Let $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { - }$ be the domain constituted by the set of the first minimum points of the radius vector on the sheet of hyperbolic trajectories for $t = - \infty$ ,let $U _ { \mathbf { 2 } } ^ { - }$ be the domain of the set of the second minimum points, and so on. Let $U _ { 1 } ^ { + } , U _ { 2 } ^ { + } , \ldots$ be respectively the domains constituted by the set of the last, the last but one,...minimum points on the sheet of hyperbolic trajectories for $t = + \infty$ . The domain $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { - }$ has no common point with either of $U _ { 2 } ^ { - } , U _ { 3 } ^ { - } , \ldots$ ，but may have a common point with $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ ，because there may be trajectories that are hyperbolic both for $t = - \infty$ and $t = + \infty$ and with their minimum radius vector occurring once or twice or .... Let $\overline { { U _ { 1 } ^ { - } } }$ be the part of $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { - }$ that has no common part with $U _ { 1 } ^ { + }$ so that $U _ { 1 } ^ { - } = \overline { { { U _ { 1 } ^ { - } } } } + U _ { - } ^ { 1 }$ . There should be trajectories which have their first minimum and then their first maximum after the first minimum.Let the set of such points be $V _ { 1 } ^ { - }$ ; this set is in $\Phi _ { v }$ .There may be trajectories that have their second minimum after having their first minimum and their first maximum.Let the set of such points be $U _ { \mathbf { 2 } } ^ { - }$ .Let $U _ { - } ^ { 2 }$ be the part of $U _ { \pmb { 2 } } ^ { - }$ that has a common part with $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ Let $\overline { { U _ { 2 } ^ { - } } }$ be the remaining part, such that $U _ { 2 } ^ { - } = \overline { { U _ { 2 } ^ { - } } } + \bar { U } _ { - } ^ { 2 }$ . The set of points that have a second maximum after having their first minimum, their first maximum,and their second minimum constitute $V _ { 2 } ^ { - }$ , and so on.Thus any trajectory which is hyperbolic for $t = - \infty$ and has the first $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ minima and the first $s - 1$ maxima successively and alternately, passes successively through the domains

$$
\overline { { U _ { 1 } ^ { - } } } , V _ { 1 } ^ { - } , \overline { { U _ { 2 } ^ { - } } } , V _ { 2 } ^ { - } , . . . , \overline { { U _ { s - 1 } ^ { - } } } , V _ { s - 1 } ^ { - } , U _ { - } ^ { s } ,
$$

where any domain in the row contains the next following domain. After that, the radius vector increases indefinitely and the trajectory becomes hyperbolic for $t = + \infty$ .We shall show that $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ cannot be infinite.

Let $\Phi ( F )$ be an arbitrary function of ${ \pmb F }$ in (155). According to Poincaré (Sections 4.17-4.21),

$$
\zeta = \int \Phi ( F ) \left| { \frac { 1 } { \zeta } } \left[ { \dot { \xi } } ^ { 2 } + { \dot { \eta } } ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { \xi { \dot { \xi } } + \eta { \dot { \eta } } } { \zeta } } \right) ^ { 2 } + r { \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } } \right] \right| d \xi d \eta d \zeta d { \dot { \xi } } d { \dot { \eta } }
$$

is an integral invariant, if the integral is extended over the succesive images of a domain $\pmb { A }$ on the surface $\Phi$ ，where $\xi \dot { \xi } + \eta \dot { \eta } + \zeta \dot { \zeta } = 0$ Denote respectively by $I _ { k } ^ { - }$ ， $\overline { { I _ { k } ^ { - } } } , I _ { - } ^ { k }$ the integrals (158) over the domains $U _ { \kappa } ^ { - } , \overline { { U _ { \kappa } ^ { - } } } , \bar { U } _ { - } ^ { \kappa }$ . It will soon be proved that the arbitrary function $\Phi ( F )$ can be so chosen that the integrals $I _ { k } ^ { - }$ and $\overline { { I _ { k } ^ { - } } } ( k = \dot { 0 } , 2 , \ldots )$ are all finite.From the definition of the domains $U _ { \kappa } ^ { - } , \overline { { U _ { \kappa } ^ { - } } } , U _ { - } ^ { \kappa }$ ，we see that

$$
I _ { 2 } ^ { - } = \overline { { { I _ { 2 } ^ { - } } } } + I _ { - } ^ { 2 } , \qquad I _ { 3 } ^ { - } = \overline { { { I _ { 3 } ^ { - } } } } + I _ { - } ^ { 3 } , \qquad . . . , \qquad I _ { n } ^ { - } = \overline { { { I _ { n } ^ { - } } } } + I _ { - } ^ { n }
$$

or

$$
\overline { { { I _ { 1 } ^ { - } } } } = ( I _ { - } ^ { 2 } + I _ { - } ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + I _ { - } ^ { n } ) + \overline { { { I _ { n } ^ { - } } } } .
$$

Now, if each point $( \xi , \eta , \zeta , \dot { \xi } , \dot { \eta } , \dot { \zeta } )$ of a domain $\pmb { A }$ corresponds to a point $( \xi , - \eta , - \zeta , - \dot { \xi } , \dot { \eta } , \dot { \zeta } )$ of another domain $B$ , and vice versa, then we say that the domains $\pmb { A }$ and $\pmb { B }$ are the images of one another. If a point $( \xi , - \eta , - \zeta , - \dot { \xi } , \dot { \eta } , \dot { \zeta } )$ of a domain $c$ corresponds to another point $( \xi , \eta , \zeta , \dot { \xi } , \dot { \eta } , \dot { \zeta } )$ of the same domain $C _ { i }$ ,then the domain is said to be the image of itself. The domains $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { - }$ and $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ have a common part defined by $U _ { - } ^ { 1 }$ or $U _ { + } ^ { 1 }$ . Each of $U _ { + } ^ { 1 }$ or $U _ { - } ^ { \bf 1 }$ is the image of itself; also, $\overline { { U _ { 1 } ^ { + } } }$ and $\overline { { U _ { 1 } ^ { - } } }$ are the images of one another. Hence the integrals $\overline { { I _ { 1 } ^ { + } } }$ and $\overline { { I _ { 1 } ^ { - } } }$ are equal, and we obtain in the place of (l59) the relation

$$
\overline { { { I _ { 1 } ^ { + } } } } = ( I _ { - } ^ { 2 } + I _ { - } ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + I _ { - } ^ { n } ) + \overline { { { I _ { n } ^ { - } } } } .
$$

Furthermore, we can prove that the integral $\boldsymbol { I }$ is finite when extended over the domain $\Phi _ { v }$ ; we call it $I _ { v }$ .Since the domains $V _ { 1 } ^ { - } , V _ { 2 } ^ { - } , \ldots$ are in the domain $\Phi _ { v }$ and no two of them have a common part, we obtain from (157) the inequality $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \overline { { I _ { n } ^ { - } } } } < I _ { v } . } \end{array}$ Hence,

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \overline { { { I _ { n } ^ { + } } } } = 0 .
$$

Now the domains $U _ { - } ^ { 1 } , U _ { - } ^ { 2 } , \dots .$ are all contained in $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ ,and $U _ { - } ^ { 1 }$ coincides with the part $U _ { + } ^ { \bf 1 }$ of $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ . Consequently the set of all $U _ { - } ^ { 2 } , U _ { - } ^ { 3 } , \dots$ fills up the domain $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ . If a trajectory of a comet that is hyperbolic for $t = + \infty$ passes through $U _ { \mathbf { 1 } } ^ { + }$ ,then it also passes through one of the domains $U _ { - } ^ { 1 } , \ U _ { - } ^ { 2 } , . . . , \ U _ { - } ^ { s } , . . . ,$ provided that the trajectory is not exceptional. If the trajectory passes only through the domain $U _ { - } ^ { s }$ ， then the comet has just $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ minima of the radius vector and is hyperbolic for $t = - \infty$ .Conversely，any orbit hyperbolic for $t = - \infty$ is also hyperbolic for $t = + \infty$ , provided that it is not exceptional.

It remains to prove that we can choose $\Phi ( F )$ so that the integral (158) over $\Phi _ { v } ,$ that is,over the domain

$$
\dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { \xi \dot { \xi } + \eta \dot { \eta } } { \zeta } \right) ^ { 2 } + r \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } < 0 ,
$$

is finite. Put

$$
{ \dot { p } } = \dot { \xi } \eta - \xi \dot { \eta } , \qquad q = \dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
h = F = ( q r ^ { 2 } - \phi ^ { 2 } ) { \frac { 1 } { 2 \zeta ^ { 2 } } } + \phi - V ,
$$

$$
\dot { p } ^ { \prime } = h + V + \frac { r } { 2 } \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } , \qquad \rho ^ { 2 } = \dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
\Psi ( h , \xi , \eta , \zeta ) = \int \frac { \ d \rho - \rlap { / } p \cdot \nabla } { \sqrt { 2 \rho ^ { 2 } ( h + \ V - \rlap / p ) - \rlap / p ^ { 2 } } } d \rlap { / } p ,
$$

$$
\Pi ( h ) = \int \int \int \frac { 1 } { r } \cdot \Psi ( h , \xi , \eta , \zeta ) d \xi d \eta d \zeta ;
$$

then the integral is written

$$
I _ { v } = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \Phi ( h ) \Pi ( h ) d h .
$$

The integration over $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is limited over

$$
- \rho ^ { 2 } + \sqrt { \rho ^ { 4 } + 2 \rho ^ { 2 } ( h + V ) } > \rho ^ { \prime } > - \rho ^ { 2 } - \sqrt { \rho ^ { 4 } + 2 \rho ^ { 2 } ( h + V ) }
$$

and

$$
- \rho ^ { 2 } - \sqrt { \rho ^ { 4 } + 2 \rho ^ { 2 } ( h + V ) } \geq \beta ^ { \prime } .
$$

Von Zeipel proved after some calculation that $\Pi ( h )$ is finite if $\boldsymbol { h }$ is neither zero nor infinite. $\Pi ( h )$ may become infinite for some isolated values of $h .$ 、In that case we can choose $\Phi ( h )$ so that it vanishes at those isolated points.Hence $I _ { v }$ is finite.

Von Zeipel also proved that the probability for an orbit that is hyperbolic for $t = - \infty$ to have at least $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ minimum values of the radius vector tends to zero as $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ increases to infinity.

Maitre (l937） repeated the arguments of Schwarzschild and von Zeipel.Chazy (1924, 1933) obtained similar results on the basis of his work (l922)on the regularization of the three-body problem at infinity, which will be discussed in Sections 22.19-22.25.Recently Merman came to a different conclusion,as we shall discuss in Sections 22.26-22.32.

# 10.37INTERPLANETARY TRAJECTORIES

A moon probe encloses the moon and earth inside its orbit,describing an oval or a figure-eight trajectory.Huang (l96l,l962) and Huang and Wade (l963) computed such orbits numerically.In order to obtain an analytical series expansion for such an orbit,Lagerstrom and Kevorkian (1963,l964) tried to match two different asymptotic solutions,one for a moon satellite and the other for an earth satellite, just in the same manner as in fluid mechanics,and also in atomic physics,when we fit two asymptotic solutions (one for the inside of an atom and the other for a point far from the atomic nucleus) in quantum mechanical computation of the wave functions for the electron configuration of an atom.In celestial mechanics, the method is to approximate the orbit by matching two conics,one around the earth and the other around the moon.This is called the matched conic method of Egorov,although any mathematical proof of its legitimacy cannot be expected (see Hunter, 1967).

Denote by $\mu \ll 1$ the mass of the moon and by $1 \ : - \ : \mu$ the mass of the earth.The solution is expanded in series arranged in powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ Take the units so that the distance between the moon and earth and the constant of gravitation are both equal to unity. Assume for a first approach to the problem that the two bodies are fixed in space,and consider a two-dimensional problem.Denote the coordinates of the probe by $x , y ;$ ， those of the earth by $\xi , \eta ,$ and those of the moon by $\xi _ { m } , \eta _ { m } ,$ and put

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r ^ { 2 } = ( x - \xi ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - \eta ) ^ { 2 } , \qquad r _ { m } ^ { 2 } = ( x - \xi _ { m } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - \eta _ { m } ) ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \mu = { \frac { m _ { m } } { m _ { m } + m _ { e } } } . } } \end{array}
$$

While the probe is influenced predominantly by the attraction of the earth the equations of motion are

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \partial } { \partial x } \bigg ( \frac { 1 - \mu } { r } + \frac { \mu } { r _ { m } } \bigg ) , \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \partial } { \partial y } \bigg ( \frac { 1 - \mu } { r } + \frac { \mu } { r _ { m } } \bigg ) .
$$

We have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \xi = - \mu \cos { ( t - \psi ) } , } & & { \xi _ { m } = ( 1 - \mu ) \cos { ( t - \psi ) } , } \\ & { \eta = - \mu \sin { ( t - \psi ) } , } & & { \eta _ { m } = ( 1 - \mu ) \sin { ( t - \psi ) } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\psi$ is the initial direction of the straight line joining the earth and moon. For the two-fixed-force-center problem, we have also $\xi = \eta = 0 \mathrm { ; }$ $\xi _ { m } = 1$ ， $\eta _ { m } = 0$

The energy integral gives

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left[ \left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - { \frac { 1 - \mu } { r } } - { \frac { \mu } { r _ { m } } } = h = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Denote by $M = x ( d y / d t ) - y ( d x / d t )$ the angular momentum relative to the earth, and by $M _ { m } = ( x - 1 ) ( d y / d t ) - y ( d x / d t )$ the angular momentum relative to the moon; then,

$$
\frac { d M } { d t } = \ - \frac { \mu y } { r _ { m } ^ { 3 } } , \frac { d M _ { m } } { d t } = \ - \ \frac { ( 1 \ - \ \mu ) y } { r ^ { 3 } } ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { x } { r } \right) = - \frac { y M } { r ^ { 3 } } , ~ \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { r _ { m } } \right) = - \frac { y M _ { m } } { r _ { m } ^ { 3 } } .
$$

Hence we obtain an integral

$$
M M _ { m } + \left( 1 - \mu \right) \frac { x } { r } - \mu \frac { x - 1 } { r _ { m } } = g = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

The problem can be solved by these two integrals. But for our present purpose we proceed by the method of perturbation. Thus asymptotic expansions are studied for the case of a very small $\pmb { \mu }$ .We suppose that the probe starts very near the earth. Instead of specifying the position and velocity at $t = 0$ as the initial condition we take $\pmb { h }$ as the prescribed quantity and consider that

$$
x = 0 , \quad y = 0 , \quad { \frac { d y } { d x } } = - \mu c ,
$$

at $t = 0$ ，where $\pmb { c }$ fixes the initial slope of the orbit. If $\pmb { h }$ is sufficiently large, the probe willmove along the straight line joining the earth to the moon.

When the probe is not near the moon,and the earth's attraction is dominant, we call the expansion in powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ the outer expansion and the limit $\mu = 0$ the outer limit. When the probe is sufficiently near the moon and passes within a distance from the moon of the order $\pmb { \mu }$ ,and the moon's attraction is dominant, we call the expansion the inner expansion. In the latter case, the variables are transformed by

$$
x ^ { * } = \frac { x - 1 } { \mu ^ { \alpha } } , \qquad y ^ { * } = \frac { y } { \mu ^ { \alpha } } , \qquad t ^ { * } = \frac { t - \tau } { \mu ^ { \beta } } ,
$$

where $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \tau } }$ is the time elapsed for the probe to reach $x = 1$ . Then the moon-centered equations of motion are

$$
\frac { x ^ { * } } { * ^ { * } } = - \frac { ( 1 - \mu ) \mu ^ { 2 \beta - \alpha } ( 1 + \mu ^ { \alpha } x ^ { * } ) } { [ 1 + 2 \mu ^ { \alpha } x ^ { * } + \mu ^ { 2 \alpha } ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) ] ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \mu ^ { 1 - 3 \alpha + 2 \beta } \cdot \frac { x ^ { * } } { ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) }
$$

and a similar one for $d ^ { 2 } y ^ { * } / d t ^ { * 2 }$

When the moon is revolving around the earth in a circular orbit with unit mean motion, the equations of motion (Kevorkian, 1962) are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \alpha - 2 \beta \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * 2 } } = 2 \mu ^ { \alpha - \beta } \displaystyle \frac { d y ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * } } + \mu ^ { \alpha } x ^ { * } + 1 - \mu } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \frac { ( 1 - \mu ) ( \mu ^ { \alpha } x ^ { * } + 1 ) } { [ 1 + 2 \mu ^ { \alpha } x ^ { * } + \mu ^ { 2 \alpha } ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) ] ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \displaystyle \frac { \mu ^ { 1 - 2 \alpha } x ^ { * } } { ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) } } } \end{array}
$$

If the moon's gravitation acting on the probe is of the same order as the centrifugal acceleration due to the orbital motion of the moon around the earth, then the exponent of $\pmb { \mu }$ in the coefficients of the corresponding two terms of this equation must be equal, giving $\alpha = 1 - 2 \alpha .$ If, furthermore, the Coriolis and the centrifugal accelerations due to the moon's motion around the earth are comparable,then the exponent $\alpha - \beta$ of the Coriolis acceleration term must be equal to $\pmb { \alpha }$ .These two conditions define the values $\alpha = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }$ and $\beta = 0$ . If we require a milder condition that the moon's gravitation is by far the dominant term, then we must have $1 - 2 \alpha = 0$ and $\alpha - 2 \beta = 0$ ,which give $\alpha = 1 / 2$ and $\beta = 1 / 4$

Lagerstrom and Kevorkian, in the two-fixed-center problem (Section 2.24),stressed those orbits that start at finite distances from the moon and pass close to the moon with finite velocity measured in the outer variables,and worked out the matching of the outer and inner solution, which requires the velocity in the inner coordinates to be of the same order as that in the outer coordinates. Thus $d { x ^ { \prime } } / d t ^ { \prime } = O ( d { x } / d t ) = O ( 1 )$ Hence $\alpha = \beta$ and $3 \alpha - 2 \beta = 1$ ; s $\mathbf { \alpha } _ { 3 } \propto \mathbf { \alpha } = \beta = 1$ ·

Thus the inner variables are taken as

$$
x ^ { * } = { \frac { x - 1 } { \mu } } , \qquad y ^ { * } = { \frac { y } { \mu } } , \qquad t ^ { * } = { \frac { t - \tau } { \mu } } .
$$

Then the equations (l60) are transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * 2 } } } = - \frac { ( 1 - \mu ) \mu ( 1 + \mu x ^ { * } ) } { [ 1 + 2 \mu x ^ { * } + \mu ^ { 2 } ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) ] ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \frac { x ^ { * } } { ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } y ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * 2 } } } = - \frac { ( 1 - \mu ) \mu y ^ { * } } { [ 1 + 2 \mu x ^ { * } + \mu ^ { 2 } ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) ] ^ { 3 / 2 } } - \frac { y ^ { * } } { ( x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } } . }  \end{array}
$$

For $\mu = 0$ -that is, close to the moon -the orbit of the probe is, in the first approximation,Keplerian relative to the moon. The nonuniformity of the outer solution is expected to occur in some neighborhood of the moon.Hence it is more convenient to choose the distance coordinate as the independent variable.We consider $y$ and $t$ as functions of $\pmb { x }$ and indicate the derivatives with respect to $_ x$ by primes; then (160) becomes

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { - \frac { t ^ { \prime \prime } } { t ^ { \prime 3 } } = - \left( 1 - \mu \right) \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } - \mu \frac { x - 1 } { r _ { m } ^ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { \frac { y ^ { \prime \prime } } { t ^ { \prime 2 } } - \frac { t ^ { \prime \prime } y ^ { \prime } } { t ^ { \prime 3 } } = - \left( 1 - \mu \right) \frac { y } { r ^ { 3 } } - \mu \frac { y } { r _ { m } ^ { 3 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The outer expansion is now in the form

$$
t ( x ) = t _ { 0 } ( x ) + \mu t _ { 1 } ( x ) + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) , ~ y ( x ) = \mu y _ { 1 } ( x ) + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

The inner expansion is

$$
t ( x ^ { * } ) \ : - \ : \tau = \mu t _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( x ^ { * } ) \ : + \ : O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) , \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : y ( x ^ { * } ) = \mu y _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( x ^ { * } ) \ : + \ : O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Since $t$ and $y$ , as we have remarked,are of the order $\pmb { \mu }$ near the moon, the leading terms in (167) also are of the order $\pmb { \mu }$

Now consider an exact solution of (l6O) depending on the parameter $\mu$ ，whose trajectory passes within a distance of the order $\pmb { \mu }$ from the moon. As $\pmb { \mu }$ tends to zero in the outer limit, the limiting trajectory passes through the position of the moon, that is, $x = 1 , y = 0 .$ . The trajectory before and after this point is called thefirst and second branch,respectively, of the outer solution to the order unity.The first branch of the outer solution gives a certain velocity at the position of the moon. According to the matching principle,this velocity is the apparent velocity at infinity for the motion around the moon given by the inner solution,which is hyperbolic. To determine the inner solution we need the angular momentum relative to the moon.When this angular momentum is known, the change in velocity direction due to the moon is known, and the second branch of the outer solution is determined. From the firstorder outer solution we obtain the apparent velocity at infinity for the hyperbolic trajectory around the moon; this gives us the slope of the first asymptote of this trajectory. For determining the angular momentum we need the distance of the moon to this asymptote, which is of the order $\mu$ in the outer variables. Thus we need the distance to the order $\mu$ from the moon to the trajectory. Hence we must compute the first branch of the outer solution to the order $\mu$ ,thatis,compute the first-order perturbation to the Keplerian orbit relative to the earth.Then the change in the velocity direction due to the moon is given by the leading term of the inner solution.Finally,the first-order inner solution determines the second branch of the outer solution.

Substituting the expansions (166) in (165), we obtain

$$
\frac { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 3 } } = \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } , \qquad - \frac { t _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 3 } } + \frac { 3 t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } t _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 4 } } = \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { ( 1 - x ) ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } } - \frac { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 3 } } = - \frac { y _ { 1 } } { x ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

The solution is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \qquad t = t _ { 0 } + \mu t _ { 1 } + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { \sqrt { 2 } ~ t _ { 0 } = \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \rho x ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) - \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 2 } } [ x ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } x ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } , } \\ & { \sqrt { 2 } ~ t _ { 1 } = - \frac { h _ { 1 } } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \Big \{ \frac { [ x ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } x ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } } { \rho } + \frac { 1 } { \rho } \Big ( \frac { x } { 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } x } \Big ) ^ { 1 / 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 \rho ^ { 2 } } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \rho x ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) \Big \} } \\ & { \qquad - \frac { 2 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \rho x ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) + \frac { 2 - \rho ^ { 2 } } { \rho ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) } \Big ( \frac { x } { 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } x } \Big ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \qquad ( 1 } \\ &  \qquad + \frac { 1 } { 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \log \frac { 1 } { + \left( 1 - 2 \rho ^ { 2 } \right) x - 2 [ x ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } x ) ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ] } \end{array}
$$

$$
y _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } \rho [ x ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } x ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } + c _ { 2 } \rho ^ { 2 } x ,
$$

with integration constants $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 } .$ where

$$
h = h _ { 0 } + \mu h _ { 1 } + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) , ~ h _ { 0 } = - \rho ^ { 2 } > - 1 .
$$

From the initial condition (163) the equation (165) must be $y _ { 1 } ( x ) = - c x$ Since we consider only motions that are elliptic to the order unity relative to the earth,we obtain $h _ { 0 } < 0$

Next we must compute the inner solution. The inner equations (164) possess two integrals for $\mu = 0$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle h ^ { * } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { d x ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \frac { 1 } { [ x ^ { * 2 } + y ^ { * 2 } ] ^ { 1 / 2 } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle M ^ { * } = x ^ { * } \frac { d y ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * } } - y ^ { * } \frac { d x ^ { * } } { d t ^ { * } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } . } } \end{array}
$$

On the other hand, we have

$$
M ^ { * } = c [ 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

because the distance between the asymptote and the moon obtained by putting $x = 1$ in the outer solution is $\mu c + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } )$ and the velocity at

$x = 1$ is $[ 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ from the energy integral. With the inner variable we have

$$
M ^ { * } = \frac { x - 1 } { \mu } \frac { d y } { d t } - \frac { y } { \mu } \frac { d x } { d t } = \left( x - 1 \right) \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } - y _ { 1 } \frac { d x } { d t } .
$$

But

$$
\frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { t ^ { \prime } } \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d x } = - \frac { c } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } [ 1 + { \cal O } ( \mu ) ] , \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { t ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } [ 1 + { \cal O } ( \mu ) ] .
$$

Hence,

$$
M ^ { * } = c [ 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

because

$$
t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ( 1 ) = [ 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { - 1 / 2 } .
$$

Similarly,

$$
h ^ { \ast } = h _ { 0 } + 1 = 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } > 0 .
$$

Thus the orbit in the neighborhood of the moon is hyperbolic.The approach velocity at $x ^ { * } = - \infty$ along one of the asymptotes is parallel to the $x ^ { * }$ -axis,and the asymptote is at the distance $\pmb { c }$ below the $x ^ { * }$ -axis for $c > 0$

Let $\pmb \theta$ be the angle between the $x ^ { * }$ -axis and the $\scriptstyle { \pmb x } ^ { \prime }$ -axis. Then from the geometry of the hyperbola we obtain

$$
\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
x ^ { \prime } = x ^ { * } \cos \theta - y ^ { * } \sin \theta , \qquad y ^ { \prime } = x ^ { * } \sin \theta + y ^ { * } \cos \theta .
$$

The hyperbolic orbit is defined by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x ^ { \prime } = a ( \epsilon - \cosh u ) , { y ^ { \prime } } = a ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sinh u , } } \\ { { t ^ { \prime } - t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = a ^ { 3 / 2 } ( \epsilon \sinh u - u ) ; } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
a = { \frac { 1 } { 2 h ^ { * } } } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) } } ,
$$

$$
\epsilon = ( 1 + 2 h ^ { * } M ^ { * 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } = [ 1 + 4 c ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

$$
t ^ { \prime } - t _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { 1 } { \mu } } \{ t - [ \tau _ { 0 } + \mu \tau _ { 1 } + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) ] \} .
$$

When the parameter $\pmb { \mu }$ is eliminated and $y ^ { * }$ is expressed as a function of $x ^ { * }$ ，we obtain

$$
y ^ { * } = \frac { ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } { 2 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \biggl \{ - x ^ { * } + a ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) \pm x ^ { * } \biggl [ 1 - \frac { 2 a } { x ^ { * } } + \frac { a ^ { 2 } ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { x ^ { * 2 } } \biggr ] ^ { 1 } 
$$

where the positive sign indicates the motion toward the moon.

It can be seen that (173) for $y ^ { * }$ matches the outer expansion of the order $\mu$ ，because this function reduces to $- c$ for $x ^ { * } = - \infty$ and this is the value of ${ \pmb y _ { 1 } }$ at $x = 1$ . The composite expansion for the orbit is

$$
y ( x , \mu ) = \mu y ^ { * } ( x ^ { * } ) + \mu y _ { 1 } ( x ) + c \mu + O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

When $h ^ { * }$ ， $M ^ { * }$ and the orientation of the asymptote for $u \to \infty$ are known,all the initial conditions necessary for defining the second branch are known. Since $\pmb { h }$ corresponding to $h ^ { * } > 1$ is negative and $M$ relative to the earth is no longer of the order $\pmb { \mu }$ but of the order unity, the trajectory is an ellipse.This value of $M$ is $[ 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin 2 \theta .$

Now the outer expansion of the first branch for $t$ should be matched with the inner solution. The equations (l69) give with $h _ { 1 } = 0$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \dot { t } = \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { - 1 } \rho - \frac { ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } { \rho ^ { 2 } } } } \\ & { } & { + \mu \biggl \{ \frac { x ^ { * } } { ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } - \frac { 2 } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { - 1 } \rho + \frac { ( 2 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) } { \rho ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } } \\ & { } & { + \frac { 1 } { 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } [ \log \mu - \log 4 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } )   + \log ( - x ^ { * } ) ] } \\ & { } & { +   O ( \mu ^ { 2 } ) \} . ( 1 7 \rho   } \end{array}
$$

The inner expansion for $x ^ { * } \to - \infty$ is obtained from (171)，(172） for$u  - \infty$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \sqrt 2 t = \sqrt 2 \tau _ { 0 } } } \\ { { { } ~ + \mu \biggl \{ \sqrt 2 \tau _ { 1 } ( \mu ) + \frac 1 { 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \log { ( - x ^ { * } ) } + \frac { x ^ { * } } { ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } } } \\  { { } ~ + \frac 1 { 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \log { \frac { 4 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) } { [ 1 + 4 c ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } ] ^ { 1 / 2 } } } \biggr \} + { } ~ } \end{array}
$$

Comparing these two expressions, we see that the crucial terms proportional to $x ^ { * }$ and log $( - x ^ { * } )$ match and that we must set

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \sqrt 2 \ \tau _ { 0 } = \displaystyle \frac 1 { \rho ^ { 3 } } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } \rho - \rho ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \right] , } \\ & { \sqrt 2 \tau _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac 1 { 2 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \log \frac { \mu [ 1 + 4 c ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } ] ^ { 1 / 2 } } { 4 ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } - \frac 2 { \rho ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { - 1 } \rho } \\ & { \qquad \quad + \frac 2 { \rho ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) } } \end{array}
$$

The composite expansion is the sum of the inner expansion for $t$ given by (l67) and the outer expansion given by (l69),minus the inner limit of the outer expansion given by (174).

Thus the trajectory of a probe starting near the earth and passing close to the moon consists, to the order unity, of two separate Keplerian orbits with respect to the earth.The determination of the orbit after the lunar passage requires the correction, to the order $\pmb { \mu }$ ,of the first branch of the trajectory. Even though the details of the lunar passage may be described to the lowest order by a Keplerian hyperbola in terms of the coordinates and time that are of the order $\mu$ ,this hyperbola cannot be defined merely by the first-order information from the approach orbit. A correction to the order $\pmb { \mu }$ for the first branch of the earth orbit is necessary for matching it with the hyperbolic moon orbit. The determination of the first branch to the order $\mu$ requires not only the initial condition to the order $\mu$ but also the equations of motion to the order $\mu$ The motion is not Keplerian to this order. The second branch of the trajectory relative to the earth is determined to the lowest order if and only if the hyperbolic motion is determined.The limiting value of the velocity along the second asymptote of the moon orbit gives the initial condition at the position of the moon for the second branch of the trajectory relative to the earth.

As a preliminary to this fresh idea,Kevorkian (l962） discussed an asymptotic representation of the motion of a satelite in the vicinity of a planet,within the framework of the restricted three-body problem.He showed that, depending on the proximity of the satellite to the planet, there are two distinct sets of approximations to the restricted three-body equations.For satellite orbits where the gravitational attraction of the planet is of the same order as the centrifugal and Coriolis forces due to the planet's motion around the sun,we are led to Hil's equations for the motion of the moon.For orbits close enough to the planet for its gravitational attraction to be the dominant force,a similar set of equations is obtained for which the intermediary orbit is Keplerian in a nonrotating frame centered at the planet. Kevorkian showed that, by choosing the coordinates with respect to which the intermediary orbit remains stationary in the mean,we can derive an asymptotic solution to the approximate equations.

For treating the motion of a probe under the action of the moon and the earth Arenstorf and Davidson (l963) and Payne (l964) discussed the two-fixed-center problem (Section 2.24) in a uniformly rotating frame of reference by selecting scalar parameters in such a way as to reduce the effects of the nonintegrable terms in the perturbation equations.The terms corresponding to the centrifugal acceleration in the rotating frame are considered as perturbation.Payne presented six schemes for the choice and tested numerically for various portions of a typical lunar trajectory obtained by numerical integration. Sémirot (l949）and then Marchal (1966),after Andrade (1890),discussed the two-fixed-center problem and used the solution as a first approximation to the motion of artificial satellites, in accordance with the work of Vinti (Section 2.24).

Recently Déprit (197O) discussed the same problem with the polar coordinates $\xi \not \equiv 1$ ， $- 1 \leq \eta \leqq + 1$ such that

$$
x = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha \xi \eta , \qquad y = \zeta \alpha [ ( \xi ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

where $\zeta \equiv \zeta ( w ) = + 1$ for $0 \leq w < \pi$ (mod. $2 \pi ,$ and $\zeta = - 1$ for $\pi \leq w < 2 \pi$ $( \bmod { 2 \pi } )$ ，and $\pmb { \alpha }$ is the distance between the two fixed points.

As has just been said, the equations of motion for a close satellite of the smaller primary are Hill's equations (Sections l1.5 and l2.2); Kevorkian (1962) showed that Hill's equations are asymptotically exact as $\mu \to 0$ in the neighborhood of the order $\mu ^ { 1 / 3 }$ of the planet, where $\mu$ is the ratio of the smaller mass to the whole mass.If the motion occurs in a smaller region of the order $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 }$ , then the terms in Hil's equations representing the net solar perturbation become of the order $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and we can construct an asymptotic solution whose leading terms are Keplerian. This asymptotic solution correct to the order $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 }$ was given by Kevorkian for the case of small inclination and eccentricity of the satellite.Lidov (1961) found that large-amplitude oscillations of the eccentricity over very long periods occur only when the inclination is sufficiently large.Lidov treated the case of arbitrary inclination and eccentricity by a method closely related to the method of averaging that we discuss in Sections 13.4-l3.8. According to Kevorkian the result of Lidov was confirmed and Eckstein extended the planar problem to arbitrary eccentricities.

In the case of a very close satelite and of a very small mass ratio $\mu$

Eckstein, Shi, and Kevorkian (l966) studied an asymptotic representation of the solution valid for a very long time for satellite motion with arbitrary eccentricity and inclination; this may be of use for an artificial satellite of Venus. The equations of motion are written in dimensionless planetocentered rotating coordinates. The reciprocal radius $\pmb { u } _ { : }$ ，the inclination $I ,$ the longitude of the node $\Omega$ ，the normal component $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ of the angular momentum,and time $\pmb { t }$ are regarded as functions of the independent variable $\phi$ ,which measures the instantaneous angle between the node and radius vector. The same method was used by these three authors (l966a) for the satellite motion around an oblate planet.But now they use three independent variables $\phi$ ， $\mu ^ { 1 / 4 } \phi$ ，and $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 } \phi$ ，and replace the usual ordinary differential equations by partial differential equations depending on $\mu ^ { 1 / 4 } \phi$ and $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 } \phi$

For the limiting case in which the satellite is very close to the smaller primary the differential equations reduce, with $\epsilon = \mu ^ { 1 / 2 }$ to

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \ddot { x } = - \frac { x } { r ^ { 3 } } + 2 \epsilon \dot { y } + 3 \epsilon ^ { 2 } x + O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \ddot { y } = - \frac { y } { r ^ { 3 } } - 2 \epsilon \dot { x } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \ddot { z } = - \frac { z } { r ^ { 3 } } - \epsilon ^ { 2 } z + O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where the distances are divided by $\epsilon ^ { 2 }$ times the distance between the primaries, while the unit of time is normalized by $\epsilon / 2 \pi$ times the period of the two primaries (Struble,1960,196l; Section i3.11),or with polar coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d \phi } { d t } = - \epsilon \frac { d } { d t } \left( r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \right) - 3 \epsilon ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \sin \psi \cos \psi + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( r ^ { 2 } \frac { d \theta } { d t } \right) - r ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d \psi } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \sin \theta \cos \theta } } \\ { { = 2 \epsilon r \Big ( \frac { d \psi } { d t } \Big ) \cos \theta \sin \theta + \epsilon ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } ( 1 + 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \psi ) \sin \theta \cos \theta + } } \end{array}
$$

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { { \frac { { { \mathbf { \ell } } ^ { 2 } } r } { t ^ { 2 } } } - r \left( { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } - r \left( { \frac { d \psi } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \\ & { \qquad = - { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } + 2 \epsilon r { \frac { d \psi } { d t } } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \epsilon ^ { 2 } r ( 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \psi - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta ) + O ( { \mathrm { \Omega } } ^ { 5 } ) } \end{array} }
$$

The equations for variation of $u , I , \Omega , p ,$ $t$ as functions of $\phi$ are derived. These variables are expressed in asymptotic series of functions of $\phi$ and $\epsilon$ correct to $O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ as $\epsilon  0$

A novel feature of this procedure is the appearance of two slow time scales differing by one order of magnitude in $\epsilon$ For high inclinations there exist variations that are measured over periods one order of magnitude higher than the planetary year.We choose as the fast variable the angle $\phi$ and introduce two slow variables $\phi _ { 1 } = \epsilon \phi$ ， $\phi _ { 2 } = \epsilon ^ { 2 } \phi$ .The dependent variables are assumed to possess asymptotic expansions of the form

$$
I = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } I _ { n } ( \phi , \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) , \qquad \Omega = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } \Omega _ { n } ( \phi , \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) ,
$$

$$
\dot { p } = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } \dot { p } _ { n } ( \phi , \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) , \qquad \boldsymbol { u } = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } u _ { n } ( \phi , \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) .
$$

Substitutingtheseexpansions inthediferentialequationsfor $I , \Omega , \ L _ { \phi } , u ,$ we obtain to $O ( \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } )$

$$
\frac { \partial I _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi } = \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi } = \frac { \partial / _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi } = 0 , \qquad \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } u _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi ^ { 2 } } + u _ { 0 } = \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 } } { \beta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

The solution is

$$
I _ { 0 } = I _ { 0 } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } I _ { 0 n } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\Omega _ { 0 } = \Omega _ { 0 } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } \Omega _ { 0 n } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\dot { p } = \dot { p } _ { 0 } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } \dot { p } _ { 0 n } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\mu _ { 0 } = \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 } } { \dot { p } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } [ 1 + e \cos { ( \phi - \omega ) } ] ,
$$

$$
e = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } e _ { n } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\omega = \sum _ { n = 0 } \epsilon ^ { n } \omega _ { n } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ) .
$$

This is a Keplerian motion with eccentricity $\pmb { e }$ and pericenter longitude $\omega _ { s }$ measured from the node.

To the order $O ( \epsilon )$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial L _ { 3 } } { \partial \phi } - \frac { \partial L _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } - \frac { \sin 2 L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \frac { - \sin 2 L _ { 0 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \frac { \cos 2 \phi } { \sin \phi } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \sin 2 L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \sin 2 \phi , } \\ & { \frac { \partial G \Omega } { \partial \phi } = - \frac { \partial G _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } - \frac { \cos L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \frac { \partial M _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi } \sin 2 \phi - 2 \frac { \cos L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi , } \\ & { \frac { \partial L _ { 1 } } { \partial \phi } - \frac { \partial L _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial L _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi } ( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi ) + \frac { 1 } { \mu \delta } \sin ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } \sin 2 \phi , } \\ & { + u _ { 1 } = - 2 \frac { \partial ^ { 3 } L _ { 0 } } { 3 \phi \partial \phi _ { 1 } } + 2 \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } } { \mu \delta } - u _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } - 2 \frac { \cos ^ { 4 } L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \frac { \sin 2 \phi } { \partial \phi } } \\ & { \quad \quad - \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \frac { \partial L _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi } \frac { \partial L _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi ^ { 2 } } - 4 \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } L _ { 0 } } { \beta \mu \delta } \frac { \sin 2 \theta } { \sin \phi } \cos 2 \phi } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

Replacing $I _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } , \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { 0 } , e , \omega$ by their expressions, we require that $I _ { 1 } , \Omega _ { 1 } , / _ { 1 }$ $\pmb { u } _ { 1 }$ shall be bounded functions of $\phi$ ,that is,

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { \frac { \partial I _ { 0 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } = 0 , } } & { { ~ \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 0 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } = - \frac { \dot { p } _ { 0 0 } ^ { 3 } } { \cos ^ { 3 } I _ { 0 0 } \cdot ( 1 ~ - \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } , ~ \frac { \partial \dot { p } _ { 0 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } = 0 , } } \\ { { } } & { { ~ \frac { \partial e _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } = 0 , ~ \frac { \partial \omega _ { 0 } } { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

The solution is given by

$$
I _ { 1 } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { \sin 2 I _ { 0 } } { \phi _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi ,
$$

$$
\Omega _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \cos I _ { 0 } } { \beta _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \sin 2 \phi - \frac { \phi _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } { \cos ^ { 3 } I _ { 0 } } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { k } b _ { 2 k } \sin k ( \phi - \omega _ { 0 } ) ,
$$

$$
{  \rlap / p _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi ) , \qquad u _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 } } { \not p _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial u _ { 0 } } { \partial \not \phi } \sin 2 \phi , }
$$

$$
I _ { 0 0 } = I _ { 0 0 } ( \phi _ { 2 } ) , \qquad \Omega _ { 0 0 } = - \frac { \phi _ { 0 0 } ^ { 3 } } { \cos ^ { 3 } I _ { 0 0 } } ( 1 - e _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 3 / 2 } \phi _ { 1 } + W _ { 0 } ( \phi _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\dot { p } _ { 0 0 } = \dot { p } _ { 0 0 } ( \phi _ { 2 } ) , \qquad e _ { 0 } = e _ { 0 } ( \phi _ { 2 } ) , \qquad \omega _ { 0 } = \omega _ { 0 } ( \phi _ { 2 } ) .
$$

Breakwell and Perko (1966) matched heliocentric ellipses - corrected for the influence of planetary perturbation in the neighborhood of a planet - with local hyperbolas. They found that the attraction of the destination planet alone causes a displacement of the arrival asymptote and a correction for the time of arrival. This last correction consists of two parts: a gross time bias and a local time bias, the latter depending only on the eccentricity of the arrival hyperbola. Similar corrections, including velocity corrections, are needed by the effect of the planet of departure. The method was applied for the computation of flyby interplanetary trajectories such as a trajectory from the earth to Mars via Venus,and the computation was seen to be greatly simplified if the planetary attractions could be neglected and the trajectory idealized as two heliocentric Keplerian orbits joined together.

Gormally (1966)referred to van der Corput's theory (1956) of asymptotic series (see Section 13.1).

Breuer and Riddell (l964) analyzed the minimum inclination of the lunicentered orbital plane of an earth-launched vehicle to the moon's equatorial plane. The general characteristics of the launch window, e.g., the allowable azimuth angle for orbital launch to the moon,were presented by Reich (l964).He gave the earth-orbit launch criteria for lunar trajectories, the departure trajectory sensitivities to orbital parameters,the mission and vehicle sensitivities to time and energy requirements,and the analytical technique for determining optimum launch conditions. The launch windows were further studied by Shute (1964).

The characteristics of trajectories that initiate on the moon's surface and terminate at a specific landing site on the earth's surface were studied by Dallas (l964).He employed the matched conic method of Egorov and Penzo.Penzo (l964） himself analyzed the free return of circumlunar trajectories with a simplified model of motion in the earth-moon system. Penzo's model was based on the concept of the sphere of activity,inside which the trajectory is represented by a mooninfluenced conic and outside which it is represented by an earthinfluenced conic.The conics are so chosen that the position and velocity match at the boundary of the sphere.Penzo classified six possible circumlunar trajectories. Johnson (l964) treated a similar problem for free-return circumlunar trajectories from launch windows with fixed launch azimuths.

Shute (l966)， for studying moon-to-earth trajectories,employed a reduced form，obtained by considering the selenocentric velocity asymptotes,of the matched-conic method for determining the initial elements of a particle launched or ejected from the moon's surface with any arbitrary starting conditions.He derived explicit analytic functions for geocentric energy, the Jacobi constant,angular momentum, standard orbital elements,and the conditions for moon-to-earth trajectories.


<!-- chunk 0007: pages 241-280 -->
Portions of randomly ejected material that initially strike the earth are found to be in retrograde motion or to go into heliocentric orbits.

Stellwacher-Amilhat (1967)，after discussing the sphere of activity (Section l0.34) around a planet by means of the equations of motion in planetocentered coordinates, studied the motion of the Venus probe Mariner II.

The method of matching two different asymptotic expressions has been employed for the discussion of the motion in an orbit at critical inclination (Section 9.12） by Eckstein, Shi and Kevorkian (1966a, 1966b).They considered the model equation

$$
\ddot { y } + y + 2 \epsilon y [ 1 - 5 \cos ^ { 2 } { ( y ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } ] = \epsilon ^ { 2 } ( y ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos { t } ,
$$

with $\epsilon \ll 1$ . In the absence of the forcing function this equation can be integrated exactly. When the radius $r = ( y ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ in the phase space takes the critical values $r _ { c } = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( 5 ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$ , the motion reduces to simple harmonic oscillations with the critical amplitude $\pmb { r } _ { c }$ and unit frequency. For each annular region bounded by two consecutive values of $\boldsymbol { r } _ { c } ,$ the integral curves are ovals with their axes aligned alternatively parallel either to $\pmb { y }$ or to $\dot { y }$ . Thus the addition ofa forcing term with unit frequency causes local resonance in the neighborhood of the critical amplitude $r _ { c }$

We use two variables $t$ and $\tilde { t } = \epsilon t$ ，and consider the asymptotic expansion

$$
y ( t ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } y _ { i } ( t , \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) \epsilon ^ { i }
$$

in accordance with Cole and Kevorkian (1963). Then ${ \pmb y _ { 0 } }$ is determined by

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } y _ { 0 } } { \partial t ^ { 2 } } + y _ { 0 } = 0 ,
$$

obtaining the solution

$$
y _ { 0 } ( t , \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = \alpha ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) \cos { [ t - \beta ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) ] } .
$$

Cole and Kevorkian call the functions $\alpha ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon )$ and $\beta ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon )$ the integration constants, which will be determined at the next step:

$$
\alpha ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } \alpha _ { i } ( \tilde { t } ) \epsilon ^ { i } , \qquad \beta ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } \beta _ { i } ( \tilde { t } ) \epsilon ^ { i } .
$$

Then $y _ { 1 }$ is determined by

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } } { \partial t ^ { 2 } } + y _ { 1 } = 2 \frac { d \alpha _ { 0 } } { d \tilde { t } } \sin \left( t - \beta \right) - 2 \alpha _ { 0 } \bigg [ \frac { d \beta _ { 0 } } { d \tilde { t } } + \left( 1 - 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 0 } \right) \bigg ] \cos \left( t - \beta \right) ,
$$

The boundedness of $y _ { 1 }$ requires that

$$
\frac { d \alpha _ { 0 } } { d \tilde { t } } = 0 , \frac { d \beta _ { 0 } } { d \tilde { t } } = ( 1 - 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 0 } ) \equiv s _ { 0 } ,
$$

which gives

$$
\alpha _ { 0 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad \beta _ { 0 } = s _ { 0 } \tilde { t } + b _ { 0 } ,
$$

where $b _ { 0 }$ is a constant depending on the initial conditions.The solution for ${ \pmb y } _ { 1 }$ is $y _ { 1 } ( t , \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = 0$

We proceed similarly to higher-degree terms in $\epsilon$ .Whenever

$$
\alpha = \alpha _ { c } = \cos ^ { - 1 } \big ( 5 ^ { - 1 / 2 } \big ) ,
$$
