the nonlinear system degenerates to a simple harmonic motion with a frequency equal to that of the forcing term.Hence the amplitude increases appreciably in some neighborhood of $\alpha _ { c }$ before the nonlinear term comes into play and destroys the resonance of the forcing term. Owing to this effect of local resonance, the forcing term (which would otherwise be of the order $\epsilon ^ { 2 }$ ）now takes on a more important role.Thus we introduce the forcing term immediately in the equation of the order $\epsilon$ from the right-hand side of the given model differential equations

$$
\frac { d \alpha } { d \tilde { t } } = \frac { \epsilon \alpha } { 2 } \sin \beta , \frac { d \beta } { d \tilde { t } } = - ( 1 - 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha ) + \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } \cos \beta .
$$

These equations admit an integral

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 2 H = 3 \alpha + \frac { 5 } { 2 } \sin 2 \alpha + \epsilon \alpha \cos \beta = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } . } \end{array}
$$

The singular points are at $\beta = \beta _ { s } = \pi \pi ( n = 0 , \pm 1 , + 2 , . . . )$ and cos $\begin{array} { r } { \alpha = \cos \alpha _ { s } = \pm ( \frac { 1 } { 5 } \mp \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \epsilon ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \end{array}$ . These points form an alternative pattern of central points and saddle points with the solution curves (Figure 5). The integral curves passing through two adjacent saddle points for a given value of $\pmb { \alpha } _ { s }$ form the boundaries of oval regions with a width of $O ( \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } )$ inside which both $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ undergo bounded oscillations. The separatrix forming the boundary corresponds to a motion where $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ approach the values at the saddle point asymptotically as $\tilde { t } \to \infty$ There are three types of possible motion if we consider the integral curves in vertical strips with a width of the order $\epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 }$ centered about any of the critical amplitudes. The characteristic frequency is $O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } )$ and the amplitudes of oscillation are $O ( \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } )$ . The slow time scale appropriate for the motion near the vertical amplitudes is $\bar { t } = \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } t$ and an expression for $_ y$ in powers of $\epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 }$ should be looked for.Away from the critical amplitude $\pmb { \alpha }$ oscillates with amplitude and frequency of the order $\epsilon$

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/54d67965595d9d09d4409eef383ade50d9edc49ee9aee8f45e5b3278680a7672.jpg)  
FIGURE 5

The motion away from the critical amplitude is represented by an expansion in powers of $\epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 }$ , called the outer expansion:

$$
y ( t ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } y _ { i / 2 } ( t , \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) \epsilon ^ { i / 2 } ,
$$

whose leading term is

$$
y _ { 0 } ( t , \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = \alpha ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) \cos { [ t - \beta ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) ] } ,
$$

$$
\alpha ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } \alpha _ { i / 2 } ( \tilde { t } ) \epsilon ^ { i / 2 } , \qquad \beta ( \tilde { t } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } \beta _ { i / 2 } ( \tilde { t } ) \epsilon ^ { i / 2 } .
$$

The boundedness requirements are

$$
\frac { d \alpha _ { 0 } } { d \tilde { t } } = 0 , \qquad \frac { d \beta _ { 0 } } { d \tilde { t } } = - \left( 1 - 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 0 } \right) \equiv s _ { 0 } , \qquad s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { d s _ { 0 } } { d \alpha _ { 0 } } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } } { d \tilde { t } } = 0 , \frac { d \beta _ { 1 / 2 } } { d \tilde { t } } = s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } = - 5 ( \sin 2 \alpha _ { 0 } ) \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

which give $y _ { 1 / 2 } = y _ { 1 } = y _ { 3 / 2 } = 0$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathsf { \Omega } _ { 0 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } = a _ { 0 } , \qquad \mathsf { \Omega } _ { \beta _ { 0 } } = s _ { 0 } \tilde { t } + b _ { 0 } , \qquad \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } = a _ { 1 / 2 } , } \\ & { \mathsf { \Omega } _ { \beta _ { 1 / 2 } } = s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 / 2 } \tilde { t } + b _ { 1 / 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\iota = - \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } } { 2 s _ { 0 } } \left[ 1 - \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 / 2 } } { s _ { 0 } } + \epsilon \left( \frac { s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 / 2 } } { s _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] ( \cos \beta - \cos b ) + a _ { 1 } ,
$$

Thus,with the initial conditions $\beta = b$ and $\alpha = a$ at $t = 0$ ，we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \beta = b = b _ { 0 } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } b _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon b _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ { \alpha = a = a _ { 0 } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } a _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon a _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

The expression for $\alpha _ { 1 }$ shows the nonuniformity of the expansion near $s _ { 0 } = 0$

The outer expansion fails to be valid as $s _ { 0 } \to 0$ .A solution that is valid and does not become unbounded at the critical amplitude is called the inner expansion. Let $s _ { 0 } = \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \bar { s } _ { 0 } .$ ,and assume that

$$
y ( t ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } y _ { i / 2 } ^ { * } ( t , \bar { t } ; \epsilon ) \epsilon ^ { i / 2 } , \qquad \bar { t } = \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } t = \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \tilde { t } ;
$$

then the equation for $y _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ is

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } y _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { \partial t ^ { 2 } } + y _ { 0 } ^ { * } = 0 ,
$$

with the solution

$$
y _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( t , \bar { t } ; \epsilon ) = \alpha ^ { * } ( \bar { t } ; \epsilon ) \cos { [ t - \beta ^ { * } ( \bar { t } ; \epsilon ) ] } .
$$

We expand

$$
\alpha ^ { * } ( \bar { t } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } \alpha _ { i / 2 } ^ { * } ( \bar { t } ) \epsilon ^ { i / 2 } , \qquad \beta ^ { * } ( \bar { t } ; \epsilon ) = \sum _ { i = 0 } \beta _ { i / 2 } ^ { * } ( \bar { t } ) \epsilon ^ { i / 2 } ;
$$

then we obtain by the boundedness of $y _ { i / 2 } ^ { * }$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \frac { d \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { d \bar { t } } = 0 , } & & { \frac { d \beta _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { d \bar { t } } = s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } , \qquad s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } = \frac { d ^ { 2 } s _ { 0 } } { d \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { \frac { d \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } } { d \bar { t } } = \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { 2 } \sin \beta ^ { * } , } & & { \frac { d \beta _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } } { d \bar { t } } = s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { * } + \frac { s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { 2 } ( \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos \beta ^ { * } , } \end{array}
$$

from which follow $y _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } = y _ { 1 } ^ { * } = y _ { 3 / 2 } ^ { * } = y _ { 2 } ^ { * } = 0$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } = a _ { 0 } ^ { * } , \qquad \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } = \bar { K } _ { 0 } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } [ \bar { K } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \bar { K } _ { 2 } ( \cos \beta ^ { * } - \cos b ^ { * } } \\ & { } & \\ { \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { * } = - \displaystyle \frac { \bar { K } _ { 0 } } { \bar { K } _ { 2 } } [ \bar { K } _ { 1 } ^ { * } + \bar { K } _ { 2 } ( \cos \beta ^ { * } - \cos b ^ { * } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } - \displaystyle \frac { \cos \beta ^ { * } - \cos b ^ { * } } { 2 s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } + a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
\overline { { { K } } } _ { 0 } = - \frac { \bar { s } _ { 0 } } { s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \overline { { { K } } } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = [ \bar { s } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } ] ^ { 2 } , \qquad \overline { { { K } } } _ { 2 } = - a _ { 0 } ^ { * } s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and the initial conditions $\alpha _ { i / 2 } ^ { * } = a _ { i / 2 } ^ { * } , \beta ^ { * } = b ^ { * }$ ,at $t = 0$

Now we match the two expansions so that (i) the direct matching of the two limit-process expansions will prove the existence of a common overlap domain and rule out the possibility of an even third limit-process expansion for some value of $\pmb { \mu }$ for which $s _ { 0 } = O ( \epsilon ^ { \mu } ) , 1 > \mu > 1 / 2 ,$ and (ii) the matching will provide the necessary information for obtaining a representation of the motion for all values of $s _ { 0 }$ in the above order interval once the behaviors at the end-points of this order interval have been calculated.According to the general principle of matching of Kaplun and Lagerstrom (l957） and Lagerstrom and Kevorkian (1964) it is sufficient to show that the inner solution for large values of $\bar { s } _ { 0 }$ agrees with the inner limit of the outer expansion.

Writing $\alpha ^ { * }$ in terms of the outer variables and expanding for $\bar { s } _ { 0 } \to \infty$ ， we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \mathrm {  ~ \Sigma ~ } ^ { * } = \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } + \epsilon \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { * } + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } ) } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = a _ { 0 } ^ { * } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } a _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon a _ { 1 } - \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } \frac { \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { s _ { 0 } } \left( \cos \beta ^ { * } - \cos b ^ { * } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } \left( \frac { s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } { s _ { 0 } } \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } - 1 \right) a _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } ( \cos \beta ^ { * } - \cos b ^ { * } ) + { \cal O } ( \epsilon a ^ { * } ) } } \end{array}
$$

From the outer expansion we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } : = \alpha _ { 0 } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon \alpha _ { 1 } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } ) } } \\ { { \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } = a _ { 0 } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } a _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon a _ { 1 } - \frac { \epsilon \alpha _ { 0 } } { 2 s _ { 0 } } \biggl [ 1 - \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { \delta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 / 2 } } { s _ { 0 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \biggr ] ( \cos \beta - \cos b ) } } \end{array}
$$

Comparing these,we see that the inner expansion contains the outer expansion explicitly to the order $\epsilon ^ { 2 }$ ，noting that in the overlap domain $a _ { i / 2 } = a _ { i / 2 } ^ { * }$ . Thus the composite expansion valid to $O ( \epsilon )$ everywhere is

$$
\alpha _ { c } = \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } + \epsilon \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { * } .
$$

The matching of $\beta$ and $\beta ^ { * }$ is carried out with respect to $d \beta / d { \bar { t } }$ and $d \beta ^ { * } / d \bar { t }$ . The composite expansion valid to the order $\epsilon ^ { 2 }$ for all $s _ { 0 }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d \beta _ { c } } { d \tilde { t } } = \bar { s } _ { 0 } + s _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \biggl [ s _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { * } + \frac 1 2 \cos \beta ^ { * } + \frac { s _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } 2 ( \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } ) ^ { 2 } \biggr ] } } \\ { { - \frac 5 4 \epsilon ( \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } ) ^ { 2 } \bar { s } _ { 0 } \sin 2 \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } + ( \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } - \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } ) } } \end{array}
$$

Finally the solution of the given model differential equation fr $y$ which is valid to $O ( \epsilon )$ for all $s _ { 0 }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { y ( t ; \epsilon ) = ( \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { * } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { * } + \epsilon \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { * } ) \cos { [ t - \beta _ { c } ( t ; \epsilon ) ] } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Returning now to the problem of the motion of a satellite around an oblate planet we see that it reduces in principle to the solution of a problem in nonlinear oscillations analogous to the model just described. Instead of two slowly varying $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ , we now have six slowly varying orbital elements. The problem hinges on solving the coupled equations for the inclination and apse which are the analogues of $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$

Take $\phi$ ,the angle between the ascending node and the radius vector, as the independent variable and put $u = 1 / r , \enspace \phi = r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot d \psi / d t ,$ cos $\theta = \sin I \sin \phi$ ；then the equations with $\epsilon = J _ { 2 }$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \frac { \phi } { t } = \frac { { \beta } { { u } ^ { 2 } } } { \cos { I } } + \frac { \cos ^ { 3 } { I } \cos { \theta } } { \beta \sin ^ { 2 } { I } \sin { \theta } } \left[ \frac { \partial { U } } { \partial \theta } + \tan { I } \frac { \cos { \phi } } { \sin { \theta } } \frac { \partial { U } } { \partial \psi } \right] , } \\ { \mathrm { \theta } ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { \epsilon } { 3 { { r } ^ { 3 } } } \left( 1 - 3 \cos ^ { 2 } { \theta } \right) } \\ { \mathrm { \theta } + \frac { 4 \epsilon ^ { 2 } } { 3 5 r ^ { 5 } } \left( 3 5 \cos ^ { 4 } { \theta } - 3 0 \cos ^ { 2 } { \theta } + 3 \right) + O ( \cos ^ { 3 } { \theta } ) . } \end{array}
$$

reduce (Struble, 1960,196la) to

$$
\frac { d I } { d \phi } = - \epsilon \frac { u } { \phi ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { 3 } I \sin I \sin 2 \phi + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d \phi ^ { 2 } } + u
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { = \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } I } { \dot { p } ^ { 2 } } + \epsilon \bigg [ \frac { 4 u ^ { 2 } } { \dot { p } ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { 4 } I \sin ^ { 2 } \phi + \frac { u } { \dot { p } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { d u } { d \phi } \right) \cos ^ { 2 } I \cdot ( 1 - 3 \cos ^ { 2 } I ) \sin 2 \phi } \\ { - \frac { 2 } { \dot { p } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { d u } { d \phi } \right) ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 4 } I \sin ^ { 2 } \phi + \frac { u ^ { 2 } } { \dot { p } ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { 2 } I \cdot ( 1 - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } I \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \\ { - 4 \frac { u } { \dot { p } ^ { 4 } } \cos ^ { 6 } I \sin ^ { 2 } \phi \bigg ] + O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

We introduce $\tilde { \phi } = \epsilon \phi$ ， and proceed as in to the foregoing model equations. We match the inner and outer expansions; then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { u _ { c } = \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } I _ { 0 c } } { \dot { p } ^ { 2 } } [ 1 + e _ { c } \cos { ( \phi - \omega _ { c } ) ] } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } u _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon u _ { 1 } , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { I _ { c } = I _ { 0 c } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } I _ { 1 / 2 } + \epsilon I _ { 1 } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\omega _ { c }$ is obtained by integrating an equation of the form

$$
\frac { d \omega _ { c } } { d \bar { \phi } } = \bar { S } _ { 0 } + S _ { 1 } I _ { 0 ( 1 / 2 ) } ^ { * } + \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \bigl [ { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } S _ { 2 } \bigl ( I _ { 0 ( 1 / 2 ) } ^ { * } \bigr ) + S _ { 1 } I _ { 0 1 } ^ { * } + A _ { 0 } + A _ { 2 } \cos { 2 \omega }
$$

$$
\bar { \phi } = \epsilon ^ { 3 / 2 } \phi = \epsilon ^ { 1 / 2 } \tilde { \phi } ,
$$

with constants $\bar { S } _ { 0 } , S _ { 1 } , S _ { 2 } , A _ { 0 } , A _ { 2 }$ . The apsidal motion is given by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \bar { \phi } - \bar { \phi } _ { 0 } = \pm \int _ { 0 } ^ { \nu } \frac { d \xi } { [ ( \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 8 \kappa _ { 1 } ) ( \xi - \lambda ) \xi ( \xi - 1 ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } } , } } \\ { { \nu = \sin ^ { 2 } \omega ^ { * } , \qquad \lambda = \frac { \kappa _ { 1 } - \bar { \kappa } _ { 0 } } { 2 \kappa _ { 1 } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\kappa _ { 1 }$ and $\bar { \kappa } _ { 0 }$ are constants. $\kappa _ { 1 }$ is'positive near the critical inclination. There are three cases to distinguish: (i) $- \kappa _ { 1 } < \bar { \kappa } _ { 0 } < \kappa _ { 1 }$ or $0 < \lambda < 1$ ， (ii) $\bar { \kappa } _ { 0 } = \kappa _ { 1 }$ or $\lambda = 0$ (iii) $\bar { \kappa } _ { 0 } > \kappa _ { 1 }$ or $\lambda < 0$ .In (i) the perigee performs a pendulum motion around $\pi / 2$ or $3 \pi / 2$ with a maximum amplitude $\omega _ { \mathrm { m a x } } ^ { * } = \pm \cos ^ { - 1 } \lambda ,$ while $\lambda$ dependsontheinitialconditions.In(u), $\omega ^ { * }$ approaches O or $\pi$ asymptotically as $\bar { \phi }$ goes to infinity. In (ii),the motion of the perigee is secular with additional oscillations.The motion of the node leads to the use of elliptic functions (see Hunter, 1967).

Shi and Eckstein (l967) recently extended their work on the problem of earth-to-moon trajectories to a more realistic model, incorporating higher-order effects to allow for direct practical application in problems of circumlunar flight. The particle of negligible mass is assumed to travel along a nonplanar orbit constrained only to the extent that it starts from a distance of the order $\mu$ from the earth and reaches a distance of the order $\mu$ from the moon,where $\mu$ is the mass ratio of the moon to the earth.Since the motion of the moon is taken from the lunar theory with the eccentricity of the order $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 }$ , and the action of the sun is taken into account, the matching of higher-order terms necesstates the use of a more general matching principle than the one by Lagerstrom and Kevorkian just mentioned. They solved the problem to the order $\mu ^ { 3 / 2 }$ by using the matched asymptotic method. The coordinates are expanded in powers of $\mu ^ { 1 / 2 }$ . The matching of the outer and inner solutions is carried out in the intermediate region for which $x _ { \alpha } = \mu \bar { x } / \mu ^ { \alpha }$ ， $y _ { \alpha } = \mu { \bar { y } } / \mu ^ { \alpha } ;$ $z _ { \alpha } = \mu \bar { z } / \mu ^ { \alpha }$ ， $t _ { \alpha } = \bar { t } / \mu _ { \alpha }$ ,and $0 < \alpha < { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ ，where $\bar { x } , \bar { y } , \bar { z } , \bar { t }$ denote the inner variables, $\dot { \alpha } = 0$ corresponds to the outer solution,and $\propto = 1$ corresponds to the inner solution.

Shi and Eckstein (l968,l969）applied the singular perturbation methods developed by Kaplun, Lagerstrom,and Kevorkian (described above） to resonance problems of the motion of an artificial satellite of small orbital eccentricity and inclination with a period commensurable with the rotation period of its primary (Section 9.26) by taking into account, besides $J _ { 2 0 }$ ， one of the most important tesseral harmonics, $J _ { 2 2 }$ ， associated with the ellipticity of the equator. The small divisors accom-panying the near resonance are removed by introducing asymptotic expansions appropriate for each of the domains of the initial conditions related to the resonance. The asymptotic expansions are constructed by the two-variable-expansion procedure. Separate asymptotic expansions are obtained for the resonant and nonresonant regions. The two expansions are matched to obtain a solution uniformly valid for all periods of the satellite including all possible resonances.It is shown that the assumption of constant eccentricity is inconsistent for satellites with 12- or 36-hour periods but that there is no instability for l2- or 36-hour resonances of an earth satellite. This is contrary to the conclusion of Blitzer,who used only a linear approximation (Section 9.26).

Kevorkian and Lancaster (l968） extended the asymptotic-solution method to the restricted three-body problem to find a one-parameter family of periodic orbits which pass close to both primaries with the commensurability $2 / 1$ in the earth-moon synodic system.

Everhart (l969） computed the trajectory of close planet-comet encounters by using a new conic-matching procedure of Kevorkian (1963)and studied the distributions for various orbital elements of the captured comets which, however, do not agree with those for the known short-period comets.Hence the capture is rare and he estimated that only once in 7OoO years such a comet of absolute magnitude l1.9 or brighter will be captured by Jupiter to a new period of shorter than 21 years with a new perihelion distance smaller than 2 A.U.

On the other hand, Everhart (l967, l968) calculated the change in total energy of comets passing through a planetary system with one planet by computing 180,Oo0 hypothetical long-period comets with orbits of random orientation. The change is seen to be the greatest for retrograde comets when their perihelion distance is nearly zero.The computed distribution is non-Gaussian in shape,symmetric about zero.

Lancaster (l97O） proved with analytical technique certain invariant properties of families of moon-to-earth trajectories. The analytical expressions for demonstrating these properties are derived from an analytical solution of the restricted three-body problem developed by the method of matched asymptotic expansions.

It is noted that Jefferys (l968) proposed a new perturbation theory for strongly perturbed dynamical systems by working from the surface-of-section method of Henon and Heiles (Section l7.6).

# 10.38DISINTEGRATION OF A COMET

Consider a comet as a finitely extended aggregate of materials.At a point on the surface of the comet the resultant attracting force due to the cometary materials is weaker than the force due to the sun; thus the comet might disintegrate due to the disturbing action of the sun during its passage through the solar neighborhood.

Suppose that the comet is composed of a meteoric swarm of particles with uniform distribution. Let $_ { m }$ be the center of mass of the swarm, and $N$ be a particle in the swarm.Take the coordinate system with the sun $S$ as origin and the $O \xi$ -axis in the direction SM.Denote by $U$ the resultant attraction at $N$ of the swarm particles.It is directed along NM.Let $S M = r ,$ $M N = \rho$ ， $S M = R$ .Denote the coordinates of $N$ by $( X , Y , Z )$ ， and those of $M$ by $( x , y , 0 )$ . Then the equations of motion of the particles at $M$ and $N$ are respectively

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { k ^ { 2 } x } { r ^ { 3 } } } = 0 , { \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { k ^ { 2 } y } { r ^ { 3 } } } = 0 , { \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { k ^ { 2 } z } { r ^ { 3 } } } = 0 ,
$$

where $r ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 }$ ,and

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } X } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } X } { R ^ { 3 } } = - \frac { X - x } { \rho } U , \frac { d ^ { 2 } Y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } Y } { R ^ { 3 } } = - \frac { Y - y } { \rho } U ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } Z } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } Z } { R ^ { 3 } } = - \frac { Z } { \rho } U , \qquad \rho ^ { 2 } = \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } .
$$

Let $( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ in these equations be the coordinates with $M$ as origin and with SM as the $\xi$ -axis; then,

$$
X = x + \frac { x \xi + y \eta } { r } , \qquad Y = y + \frac { y \xi - x \eta } { r } , \qquad Z = \zeta .
$$

Put $R = r + \xi$ and neglect the higher-degree terms $\xi ^ { 2 } , \eta ^ { 2 } , \zeta ^ { 2 }$ ; then,

$$
x \bigg ( \frac { x \xi + y \eta } { r } \bigg ) ^ { \prime \prime } + y \bigg ( \frac { y \xi - x \eta } { r } \bigg ) ^ { \prime \prime } - 2 \frac { k ^ { 2 } \xi } { r ^ { 2 } } + U r \frac { \xi } { \rho } = 0 ,
$$

$$
y \Big ( \frac { x \xi + y \eta } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime } - x \Big ( \frac { y \xi - x \eta } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime \prime } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } \eta } { r ^ { 2 } } + U r \frac { \eta } { \rho } = 0 .
$$

We know that

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle x \Big ( \frac { x } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime } + y \Big ( \frac { y } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime } = 0 , } } & { { \quad x \Big ( \frac { x } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime \prime } + y \Big ( \frac { y } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime \prime } = - \frac { k ^ { 2 } \rlap / p } { r ^ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle x \Big ( \frac { y } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime } - y \Big ( \frac { x } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime } = \frac { k \sqrt { \rlap / p } } { r } , } } & { { \quad x \Big ( \frac { y } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime \prime } - y \Big ( \frac { x } { r } \Big ) ^ { \prime \prime } = - 2 \frac { k \sqrt { \rlap / p } r ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { k \sqrt { \beta } } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { d \eta } { d t } - \frac { k ^ { 2 } \xi } { r ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac { \beta } { r } + 2 \right) - 2 \frac { k \sqrt { \beta } } { r ^ { 3 } } \eta \frac { d r } { d t } + U \frac { \xi } { \rho } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { k \sqrt { \beta } } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { d \xi } { d t } - \frac { k ^ { 2 } y } { r ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac { \beta } { r } - 1 \right) + 2 \frac { k \sqrt { \beta } } { r ^ { 3 } } \xi \frac { d r } { d t } + U \frac { \eta } { \rho } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Assume that the orbit of $M$ is circular around the sun. Then,

$$
r = \phi , \frac { d r } { d t } = 0 , R = n \phi ^ { 3 / 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 n \frac { d \eta } { d t } - 3 n ^ { 2 } \xi + U \frac { \xi } { \rho } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 n \frac { d \xi } { d t } + U \frac { \eta } { \rho } = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d t ^ { 2 } } + n ^ { 2 } \zeta + U \frac { \zeta } { \rho } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Suppose that the particle $N$ is inside the swarm. Then the potential is

$$
U = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \frac { \pi \rho ^ { 3 } k ^ { 2 } D } { \rho ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \rho k ^ { 2 } D ,
$$

where $D$ denotes the mean density. Let $_ m$ be the total mass of the swarm, and $\pmb { \rho _ { 0 } }$ the radius; then,

$$
m = \frac 4 3 \pi \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } D , \qquad k ^ { 2 } M = n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } , \qquad \frac 4 3 \pi k ^ { 2 } D = n ^ { 2 } \biggl ( \frac { a } { \rho _ { 0 } } \biggr ) ^ { 3 } \frac m M .
$$

Put

$$
\frac { m } { M } \left( \frac { a } { \rho _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 3 } = \mu ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 n \frac { d \eta } { d t } + ( \mu - 3 ) n ^ { 2 } \xi = 0 , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 n \frac { d \xi } { d t } + \mu n ^ { 2 } \eta = 0 , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d t ^ { 2 } } + ( \mu + 1 ) n ^ { 2 } \zeta = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

If we try a solution

$$
\xi = { \cal A } \cos { ( s n t + \alpha ) } , \qquad \eta = { \cal B } \sin { ( s n t + \alpha ) } ,
$$

this gives

$$
( s ^ { 2 } - \mu ) ( s ^ { 2 } - \mu + 3 ) = 4 s ^ { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
2 s ^ { 2 } = 2 \mu + 1 \pm { \sqrt { 1 6 \mu + 1 } } .
$$

Since $\mu > 0$ ,the roots $s ^ { 2 }$ are real. For $\mu > 3$ there are two positive roots $s ^ { 2 }$ For $\mu < 3$ , one root is positive and the other negative.Hence the condition for stability of the swarm against its destruction is $\mu > 3$ or

$$
\frac { m } { M } > 3 \bigg ( \frac { \rho _ { 0 } } { a } \bigg ) ^ { 3 } .
$$

Next, suppose that $N$ is outside the swarm. Then,

$$
U = { \frac { k ^ { 2 } m } { \rho ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } } { \rho ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { m } { M } } ,
$$

and the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \xi } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 n \frac { d \eta } { d t } - 3 n ^ { 2 } \xi + \frac { m n ^ { 2 } } { M } \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \xi = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \eta } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 n \frac { d \xi } { d t } + \frac { m n ^ { 2 } } { M } \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \eta = 0 , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \zeta } { d t ^ { 2 } } + n ^ { 2 } \zeta + \frac { m n ^ { 2 } } { M } \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \rho ^ { 3 } } \zeta = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

The first two equations are those appearing in Hil's lunar theory (Section 11.1).We obtain the Jacobi integral

$$
\frac { V ^ { 2 } } { n ^ { 2 } } = 2 \frac { m } { M } \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \rho } + 3 \xi ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } + C ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { V ^ { 2 } } { n ^ { 2 } } = \frac { m } { M } a ^ { 3 } \biggl ( \frac { 2 } { \rho } - \frac { 1 } { b } \biggr ) + 3 \xi ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } .
$$

Here, $b$ is the semimajor axis of the instantaneous orbit of $N$ around $M$ if the solar attraction on $N$ were to cease to act. The surface $V ^ { 2 } = 0$ gives

$$
\frac { m } { M } a ^ { 3 } \bigg ( \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { b } \bigg ) + 3 \xi ^ { 2 } - \zeta ^ { 2 } = 0 .
$$

This is a closed surface and $V ^ { 2 } > 0$ inside this surface. If the particle is inside this closed surface initially, then it remains always inside the surface, that is, the motion of $N$ is stable.

This discussion is due to Picart (1892) and was followed by Bobrovnikoff (1929). Bok (1934) applied the same analysis for discussing the stability of a globular cluster during its galactic rotation. Klauder (1937), using the same method, discussed the stability of a dark cloud in our own Galaxy,and Hoppe (l937） the stability of an interstellar meteoritic cloud.For reference, see the report of the IAU Conference on the Instability of Systems of Galaxies (Santa Barbara, California, 1961), published in the Astronomical Journal 66 (1961),pp. 533-636.

The origin of comets is attributed by Oort (1950) to the disintegration of a planet. Some say that comets were formed by condensation during the passage of the solar system through a dense interstellar gas several million years ago. Van Woerkom (1948) says that a comet with very large orbital semimajor axis, say $1 0 ^ { 4 }$ A.U., has had its period changed by the action of Jupiter. According to Whipple (Whipple,1950，1963; Hamid and Whipple, l953) a comet is the sublimation product of icy conglomerates which were projected forward by the reaction of sublimation;as a result, the mean motion decreased while the eccentricity increased,as seen in Comet d'Arrest and Comet Wolf I. If the cometary nucleus was rotating in the reverse direction, then the effect would be the opposite, as shown by Encke's comet. Thus Encke's comet lost $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 5 0 0 } }$ of its mass during one perihelion passage,and the radius of the nucleus was reduced to $4 \mathrm { k m }$ by this sublimation. Since a comet with a larger nucleus may project meteoric matter with a higher velocity at the same heliocentric distance, the meteoric swarm of a larger comet is distributed uniformly over its orbit while that of a smaller comet has only local condensations (Whipple and Wright, 1954). The meteoric swarms, Perseids, ${ \pmb v }$ Orionids, and $\eta$ Aquarids,are said to have been projected from Halley's comet; $\gamma$ Andromedids and Leonids were projected from the comets Giacobini and Biela,and the mother comet of Leonids, respectively.Recently Galibina (197O) computed the secular perturbation of the Leonid meteor stream for over 4,000 years (Porter, 1952; Lyttleton, 1953).

Hamid （l95l） developed this theory for the comet $1 8 6 2 { \mathrm { ~ c ~ } }$ The projected velocity according to Whipple is

$$
\Delta V = \left( \frac { R _ { c } } { n s } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { 3 . 2 8 } { q ^ { 9 / 8 } } \mathrm { m / s e c } ,
$$

where $R _ { c }$ is the radius of the cometary nucleus in $\mathbf { k m }$ ， $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ the radius of the meteoric particles in cm, $1 / n$ the heat absorption coefficient, and $q$ the perihelion distance ofthe cometary orbit.Whipple(1950,195l) considered that the small particles lost by a comet have their velocities retarded by the solar radiation pressure and drop toward the sun in spiral orbits. This is called the Poynting-Robertson effect. Robertson (1937； Briggs 1962) tried to explain the decrease of the major axis and the eccentricity of Encke's comet by this effect.According to Whipple such particles are added to the material that causes the zodiacal light.Whipple (1954) discovered 21 new meteoric swarms by using Baker-Schmidt cameras. If we suppose that $K = \log { [ a ( 1 + e ) / ( 1 - e ) ] } - 1 > 0$ for a cometary orbit and $K < 0$ for an asteroidal orbit, then 96 percent of the orbits of meteoric swarms that Whipple has computed are cometary. This is true also for the meteors discovered only by radio observations at Jodrell Bank and Ottawa.Whipple identified Encke's comet with Taurids and found a meteoric swarm δ Aquarids approaching the sun to 0.06lA.U. at the perihelion and approaching Jupiter at the aphelion with eccentricity O.98 and period 4.2 years. Thus the effect of the solar radiation at the perihelion certainly causes the disintegration at the perihelion due to the solar radiation and at the aphelion due to Jupiter's gravitational action.

Whipple (1938,1940,1943,1947,1952) and his colleagues also made experiments by ultra-speed pellets for examining the mechanism of the loss of meteoric mass, together with the variation of the emitted light and spectra. He concluded that a meteorite disintegrates rapidly in droplets and increases its light in the form of a flare.The question of the deceleration of comets in their orbits (Section 8.5) should be discussed on such grounds. See the proceedings of the Symposium on the Astronomy and Physics of Meteors (Cambridge, Mass.， l961)，published as the Smithsonian Contribution to Astrophysics,7,1963.

The difference between comets and asteroids, according to Kresak (1965), is due to the steady supply of new short-period comets that have resulted from the capture by Jupiter, to the resulting concentration of cometary aphelia near Jupiter's orbit, and to the gradual disintegration of comets.A pronounced gap corresponding to the commensurability $2 / 1$ is shown to divide the short-period comets into two groups differing in physical behavior, stability,and discovery condition, the inner group undergoing rapid changes due to Jupiter's perturbation and disintegration.

Marsden (1968,l969) computed the nongravitational force acting on comets and concluded that the radial component is directed outward from the sun, the transverse component is much smaller,and no component is normal to the orbital plane.The result is consistent with Whipple's icy-conglomerate nucleus.Sekanina (l969） showed after studying the gas concentration in the atmospheres of 23 short-period comets that the vast amount of gas liberated from the cometary nuclei can account for the dynamical effect, and discussed the disintegration of the six comets computed by Marsden.

Marsden (l970) suggested that P/Neujmin l and P/Arend-Rigaux may represent a transition phase between comets and asteroids.With the exception of Hidalgo all known asteroids can avoid encounters with Jupiter.A new orbital analysis of Hidalgo suggested that nongravitational forces may be acting. It is questionable whether the Apollo objects, for example, can be ex-comets. Marsden's long-term analysis (Section l0.34） shows that short-period comets are sometimes affected by librations similar to those involving asteroids.P/Neujmin 1 librates about the $3 / 2$ resonance with Jupiter in spite of the strong gravitational influence of Saturn. He thinks it reasonable that a comet avoiding Jupiter and temporarily librating could be approaching the end of its cometary life.

Brady (l97O) calculated the original and future values of the eccentricities and reciprocal semimajor axes of 143 nearly parabolic comets and determined a critical value of the eccentricity which divides these comets into two distinctly different populations.

# BOHLIN'S THEORY

# 10.39BOHLIN’S QUINTIC EQUATION

Bohlin's theory (1907,1908,1908a,1915,1916)on the general threebody problem has rather a special character. He developed a particular

kind of expansion of the mutual distances of the three bodies on the basis of the five roots of a quintic algebraic equation (192l, l922, 1923, 1925,1931,1933,1933a,1933b,1934,1935).

Take, for example, an equation of the third degree

$$
y ^ { 3 } + 3 p y = 2 \tau ,
$$

which has a double root if

$$
\tau ^ { 2 } + \phi ^ { 3 } = 0 .
$$

Let the corresponding values of $y$ be $\tau _ { 1 }$ and $\tau _ { 2 }$ . At those points we have

$$
y = \Re ( \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } } ) ,
$$

or

$$
y = \Re ( \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 2 } } ) ,
$$

where $\mathfrak { P }$ denotes an integral power series of the argument. Only if, in addition, we have $\tau _ { 1 } = \tau _ { 2 } ,$ then

$$
y = c ( \tau - \tau _ { 1 } ) ^ { 1 / 3 } .
$$

A function that does not become zero at any finite point is called a restricted function. The function $u = \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } } \stackrel { . } { + } \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 2 } }$ is a restricted function, since it does not vanish at any finite point. Thus the solution of the cubic equation (176) at $\tau _ { 1 }$ is

$$
y - a = { \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } } } [ a _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 } { \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] ,
$$

and at $\tau _ { 2 }$

$$
y - a = \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 2 } } [ b _ { 0 } + b _ { 1 } \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] .
$$

At $\tau = \infty$

$$
y = P \tau ^ { 1 / 3 } + Q \tau ^ { - 1 / 3 } ,
$$

where $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ are uniform integral functions of $\pmb { \tau }$ Thus, except for $\tau _ { 1 } = \tau _ { 2 }$ and $\tau = \infty$ ， $y$ is expressed as a function of $\sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } }$ and $\sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 2 } }$ . For a cubic equation we can construct a restricted function by means of $[ \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } } + \sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 2 } } ] ^ { 2 / 3 }$ . For a quartic equation it is not so easy to construct the analytic expression. It is even harder for a quintic equation.

Consider a quintic equation of the normal form after Tschirnhaus's transformation (Serret, l885; Burnside and Panton, 1928):

$$
z ^ { 5 } + 5 s z = 4 \tau .
$$

The condition for a double root is $z ^ { 4 } + s = 0 .$ This quintic equation can have neither a triple nor a quadruple root.Thus the roots are either all 'simple,or two equal and three simple. The condition $z ^ { 4 } + s = 0$ is written $\tau ^ { 4 } + s ^ { 5 } = 0 \ /$ Let the roots be $\tau _ { 1 } , \ \tau _ { 2 } , \ \tau _ { 3 } , \ \tau _ { 4 } .$ .The root $z$ of the quintic equation can be expressed by

$$
\sqrt { ( \tau - \tau _ { 1 } ) ( \tau - \tau _ { 2 } ) ( \tau - \tau _ { 3 } ) ( \tau - \tau _ { 4 } ) } \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad \sqrt { \tau ^ { 4 } + s ^ { 5 } } .
$$

For $\tau = \infty$ , we obtain

$$
z = A \tau ^ { 1 / 5 } + \frac { B } { \tau ^ { 3 / 5 } } + \frac { C } { \tau ^ { 7 / 5 } } + \frac { D } { \tau ^ { 1 1 / 5 } } + E ,
$$

where $A , B , C , D , E$ are uniform functions that can be expanded in decreasing powers of $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ . A restricted function w corresponding to these properties should be a root of an algebraic equation

$$
f ( y , \tau ) = y ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } y ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n - 1 } y - \tau = 0 .
$$

Suppose that the restricted function w is given by

$$
w ^ { 4 } + 4 \tau w = 3 s ^ { 5 / 3 } .
$$

Denote the three values of

$$
t = [ \tau ^ { 2 } + \sqrt { \tau ^ { 4 } + s ^ { 5 } } ] ^ { 1 / 3 } + [ \tau ^ { 2 } - \sqrt { \tau ^ { 4 } + s ^ { 5 } } ] ^ { 1 / 3 }
$$

by $t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } ,$ and $t _ { 3 }$ ; then,

$$
w = \frac { \sqrt { t _ { 1 } } + \sqrt { t _ { 2 } } + \sqrt { t _ { 3 } } } { \sqrt { 2 } } .
$$

The degree of ${ \pmb w }$ at $\tau = \infty$ is given by $w = \tau ^ { 1 / 3 }$ or $w = 1 / \tau$ .Then the root $_ z$ is expressed by

$$
z = 3 ^ { - 3 / 5 } [ w _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 5 } + w _ { 2 } ^ { 3 / 5 } + w _ { 3 } ^ { 3 / 5 } + w _ { 4 } ^ { 3 / 5 } ] ,
$$

where $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , w _ { 3 } , w _ { 4 }$ are the functions obtained by cyclic changes of the roots of the quartic equation. The expansion at $\tau = \infty$ is

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { w _ { i } = \tau ^ { 1 / 3 } \Big [ 1 + \frac { s _ { 1 } ^ { ( i ) } } { \tau ^ { 4 / 3 } } + \frac { s _ { 2 } ^ { ( i ) } } { \tau ^ { 8 / 3 } } + \frac { s _ { 3 } ^ { ( i ) } } { \tau ^ { 1 2 / 3 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \Big ] \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) , } } \\ { { w _ { 4 } = \frac { 3 s ^ { 5 / 3 } } { 4 \tau } \left[ 1 + \frac { s _ { 1 } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { \tau ^ { 4 } } + \frac { s _ { 2 } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { \tau ^ { 8 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] , } } \end{array}
$$

where $s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots$ take different values for different w. Thus

$$
z = \tau ^ { 1 / 5 } \biggl [ a _ { 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } } { \tau ^ { 4 / 3 } } + \frac { a _ { 2 } } { \tau ^ { 8 / 3 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \biggr ] + \frac { 1 } { \tau ^ { 3 / 5 } } \biggl [ b _ { 0 } + \frac { b _ { 1 } } { \tau ^ { 4 } } + \frac { b _ { 2 } } { \tau ^ { 8 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \biggr ] .
$$

By analogy with a quartic equation we put,for the quintic equation,

$$
z = \frac { a } { w ^ { 1 / 5 } } + b w ^ { 3 / 5 } + c w ^ { 7 / 5 } + d w ^ { 1 1 / 5 } + e ,
$$

where $a , b , c , d , e$ are uniform functions of $_ w$ developable in decreasing powers of w. Substituting (l79) in (177),we see, with the notation $\zeta = z ( w / 3 ) ^ { 1 / 5 } / s ^ { 1 / 3 }$ ， $u = ( w / 3 ) ^ { 4 / 5 } / s ^ { 1 / 3 }$ ,that

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \zeta = \left[ 1 - { \frac { 6 } { 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 5 } - { \frac { 5 2 } { 5 \cdot 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 1 0 } + \cdots \right] } \\ & { \qquad - u \left[ 1 + { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 5 } + { \frac { 7 7 } { 5 \cdot 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 1 0 } + \cdots \right] } \\ & { \qquad - u ^ { 2 } \left[ 1 + { \frac { 2 } { 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 5 } - { \frac { 3 3 } { 5 \cdot 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 1 0 } + \cdots \right] } \\ & { \qquad - u ^ { 3 } \left[ 1 + { \frac { 1 1 } { 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 5 } + { \frac { 1 6 8 } { 5 \cdot 5 } } { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { 1 0 } + \cdots \right] } \\ & { \qquad + u ^ { 4 } \left[ ~ * ~ * ~ * ~ * ~ \right. } \end{array} }
$$

where the terms $u ^ { 4 + 5 n } ( n = 0 , 1 , . . . )$ are lacking. Substitute the definition of $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ and $\pmb { u }$ in (177); then

$$
\zeta ^ { 5 } + 5 u \zeta = 1 - 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 \cdot u ^ { 5 } ,
$$

which Bohlin called the free equation.It is remarked that the series is not uniformly convergent.The condition for a double root is

$$
( 1 - 3 ^ { 3 } u ^ { 5 } ) ^ { 4 } + 4 ^ { 4 } u ^ { 5 } = 0 .
$$

This equation is reducible, because, if we put $u ^ { 5 } = v _ { ; }$ ：the condition becomes

$$
\Big ( v + \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 4 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \Big [ v ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 4 } { 3 ^ { 4 } } v + \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 4 } } \Big ] = 0 .
$$

The restricted function is transformed to

$$
w ^ { 4 } + ( 1 - 2 7 u ^ { 5 } ) w = 3 u ^ { 5 / 3 } .
$$

Bohlin called this the resoluing equation. If we put $\eta = \zeta / u ,$ then (181) becomes

$$
\eta ^ { 5 } + \frac { 5 } { u ^ { 3 } } \eta = \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 5 } } - 2 7 .
$$

Also, put

$$
\displaystyle v _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \frac { w _ { i } } { u ^ { 5 / 3 } } ;
$$

then,

$$
( v _ { i } - 1 ) \Big ( v _ { i } ^ { 3 } + v _ { i } ^ { 2 } + v _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 7 u ^ { 5 } } \Big ) = 0 ,
$$

and the resolving equation is obtained by eliminating the frst factor, which gives a constant value to $\boldsymbol { v } _ { i \cdot }$ ，

$$
v _ { i } ^ { 3 } + v _ { i } ^ { 2 } + v _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 7 u ^ { 5 } } = 0 .
$$

# 10.40AUXILIARY FUNCTIONS

We consider the symmetric combination $\begin{array} { r } { v = \sum _ { i } v _ { i } ^ { 3 / 5 } } \end{array}$ in the case of a quintic equation, and call it the auxiliary function. The expansion for $\eta$ in terms of $\pmb { u }$ is everywhere regular except for the double points and $u = 0$ 、At a double point, where $u _ { 0 } ^ { 5 } = ( 7 ^ { - } - i \sqrt { 3 2 } ) / 8 1$ ,and $\overline { { i } } = \sqrt { - 1 }$ ， we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { 3 v _ { 1 } = ( - 1 + i \sqrt { 2 } ) + \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } \mathfrak { P } _ { 1 } ( \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } ) , } \\ & { 3 v _ { 2 } = ( - 1 + i \sqrt { 2 } ) + \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } \mathfrak { P } _ { 2 } ( \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } ) , } \\ & { 3 v _ { 3 } = ( - 1 - i \sqrt { 2 } ) + \mathfrak { P } _ { 3 } ( u - u _ { 0 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Consider two auxiliary functions $v$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ , such that $v = v _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 5 } + v _ { 2 } ^ { 3 / 5 } + v _ { 3 } ^ { 3 / 5 }$ ， ${ \pmb \ p } = ( v _ { 2 } v _ { 3 } ) ^ { 3 / 5 } + ( v _ { 3 } v _ { 1 } ) ^ { 3 / 5 } + ( v _ { 1 } v _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 5 }$ .Then $\pmb { u }$ and $\pmb { \eta }$ can be expressed as rational functions of $v$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ .It is simpler if we use three auxiliary functions: $\not p _ { 1 } = \boldsymbol { v } _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 5 } , \not p _ { 2 } = \boldsymbol { v } _ { 2 } ^ { 3 / 5 } , \not p _ { 3 } = \boldsymbol { v } _ { 3 } ^ { 3 / 5 }$ . Then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { / p } _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } + \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } \mathfrak { P } _ { 1 } ( \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } ) , } \\ & { { / p } _ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } + \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } \mathfrak { P } _ { 2 } ( \sqrt { u - u _ { 0 } } ) , } \\ & { { / p } _ { 3 } = c _ { 3 } + \mathfrak { P } _ { 3 } ( u - u _ { 0 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

It can be proved that $\eta$ is a regular function $\mathrm { \bf f } f _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , \boldsymbol { p } _ { 3 }$ at $\begin{array} { r } { \pmb { u } = \pmb { u } _ { 0 } , } \end{array}$ since $\pmb { u }$ is a regular function of ${ p } _ { 1 } , { p } _ { 2 } , { p } _ { 3 } ,$ , and that $\eta$ is a regular function of $\pmb { u }$ at any other point. We have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mid = A _ { 0 } + A _ { 1 } ( \phi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 3 } ) + A _ { 2 } ( \phi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \phi _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { + B _ { 2 } ( \phi _ { 1 } \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } \phi _ { 3 } + \phi _ { 3 } \phi _ { 1 } ) + A _ { 3 } ( \phi _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } + \phi _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } + \phi _ { 3 } ^ { 3 } ) + \cdot \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

Write, in general,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { p ( r , s ) = p _ { 1 } ^ { r } \beta _ { 2 } ^ { s } + p _ { 2 } ^ { r } \beta _ { 3 } ^ { s } + p _ { 3 } ^ { r } \beta _ { 1 } ^ { s } + p _ { 2 } ^ { r } \beta _ { 1 } ^ { s } + p _ { 3 } ^ { r } \beta _ { 2 } ^ { s } + p _ { 1 } ^ { r } \beta _ { 3 } ^ { s } , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
{ \bf \Phi } ^ { \prime } = \phi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 3 } , \qquad { \bf \phi } ^ { \prime \prime } = \phi _ { 1 } \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } \phi _ { 3 } + \phi _ { 3 } \phi _ { 1 } , \qquad { \bf \phi } ^ { \prime \prime } = \phi _ { 1 } \phi _ { 2 } \phi _ { 3 } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { l c r c l } { { \displaystyle \upsilon , 0 ) = 6 , } } & { { \displaystyle \rlap / \ p ( 0 , 1 ) = 2 \rlap / p ^ { \prime } , } } & { { \hslash ( 0 , 2 ) = 2 \rlap / p ^ { \prime 2 } - 4 \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle , 0 ) = 2 p ^ { \prime } , } } & { { \hslash ( 1 , 1 ) = 2 \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \hslash ( 1 , 2 ) = \rlap / p ^ { \prime } \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime } - 3 \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle , 0 ) = 2 \rlap / p ^ { \prime 2 } - 4 p ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \hslash ( 2 , 1 ) = \rlap / p ^ { \prime } \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime } - 3 \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \hslash ( 2 , 2 ) = 2 \rlap / p ^ { \prime \prime 2 } - 4 \rlap / p ^ { \prime } \rlap / p ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \ldots . , } } & { { \hslash \ldots . } } & { { \hslash \ldots } } & { { \hslash } } \end{array}
$$

There are recurrence relations

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { p ( r + 1 , s ) = p ^ { \prime } { \cdot } p ( r , s ) - p ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } p ( r - 1 , s ) + p ^ { \prime \prime \prime } { \cdot } p ( r - 2 , s ) , } \\ & { p ( r , s + 1 ) = p ^ { \prime } { \cdot } p ( r , s ) - p ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } p ( r , s - 1 ) + p ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } p ( r , s - 2 ) . } \end{array}
$$

We have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \gamma = - \frac { - \gamma } { 8 } - 1 } & { \Big \lfloor 1 - \frac { 3 } { 5 ^ { 3 } } 1 2 8 ^ { 6 } - \frac { 5 } { 5 ^ { 7 } } ( 2 6 5 3 8 { u } ^ { 1 9 } ) + \frac { 5 } { 5 ^ { 1 0 } } \left( 2 7 0 0 9 u ^ { 1 5 } \right) } \\ & { } & { + \frac { 3 } { 5 ^ { 1 0 } } \left( 7 8 8 5 2 0 6 \tilde { u } ^ { 2 0 } \right) \Big \rfloor , } \\ & { \gamma = \frac { 2 } { 3 ^ { 1 / 8 } u ^ { 2 } } \left[ 1 - \frac { 3 ^ { 2 } } { 5 ^ { 3 } } \left( 4 6 u ^ { 8 } \right) - \frac { 8 ^ { 8 } } { 5 ^ { 7 } } \left( 1 3 3 2 u ^ { 1 0 } \right) + \frac { 3 ^ { 2 } } { 5 ^ { 1 0 } } \left( 4 5 5 5 8 u ^ { 1 5 } \right) \right. } \\ & { } & { + \left. \frac { 3 ^ { 1 0 } } { 5 ^ { 1 4 } } \left( 2 0 7 0 1 5 5 5 1 u ^ { 2 0 } \right) \right] , } \\ & { \gamma = \frac { 2 } { 3 ^ { 1 / 8 } u ^ { 2 } } \left[ 1 - \frac { 3 ^ { 2 } } { 5 ^ { 3 } } \left( 1 3 u ^ { 8 } \right) + \frac { 3 ^ { 8 } } { 5 ^ { 7 } } \left( 2 3 7 u ^ { 1 0 } \right) + \frac { 3 ^ { 7 } } { 5 ^ { 1 0 } } \left( 6 7 7 1 6 u ^ { 1 5 } \right) \right. } \\ & { } & { + \left. \frac { 3 ^ { 1 2 } } { 5 ^ { 1 4 } } \left( 1 7 8 8 0 5 6 u ^ { 2 0 } \right) \right] , } \end{array}

# I0.4IANALYTICAL EXPANSION

Denote the three mutual distances of three bodies of mass $M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ by $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , r _ { 3 }$ .Consider three constants $a , b , c$ corresponding to the semimajor axes for the undisturbed motion.Put

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { m _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 1 } } { a b ^ { 2 } } , ~ } } & { { m _ { 1 3 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 1 } } { a c ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { m _ { 2 2 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 3 } + M _ { 1 } } { b ^ { 3 } } , ~ } } & { { m _ { 2 3 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 2 } } { b c ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { m _ { 3 2 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 3 } } { c b ^ { 2 } } , ~ } } & { { m _ { 3 3 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } } { c ^ { 3 } } ; } } \end{array}
$$

$m _ { 1 1 } , m _ { 2 2 } , m _ { 3 3 }$ correspond to the three mean motions for the undisturbed motion. Then the $\pmb { x } .$ -components of the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } / a ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - m _ { 1 1 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 1 } / a } { ( r _ { 1 } / a ) ^ { 3 } } + m _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 2 } / b } { ( r _ { 2 } / b ) ^ { 3 } } + m _ { 1 3 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 3 } / c } { ( r _ { 3 } / c ) ^ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( x _ { 2 } / b ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } = m _ { 2 1 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 1 } / a } { ( r _ { 1 } / a ) ^ { 3 } } - m _ { 2 2 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 2 } / b } { ( r _ { 2 } / b ) ^ { 3 } } + m _ { 2 3 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 3 } / c } { ( r _ { 3 } / c ) ^ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } ( x _ { 3 } / c ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } = m _ { 3 1 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 1 } / a } { ( r _ { 1 } / a ) ^ { 3 } } + m _ { 3 2 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 2 } / b } { ( r _ { 2 } / b ) ^ { 3 } } - m _ { 3 3 } ^ { 2 } \frac { x _ { 3 } / c } { ( r _ { 3 } / c ) ^ { 3 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
n _ { 1 } d t = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { a } d u , \qquad n _ { 2 } d t = \frac { r _ { 2 } } { b } d v , \qquad n _ { 3 } d t = \frac { r _ { 3 } } { c } d w .
$$

The expressions $n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , n _ { 3 }$ reduce to the mean motion for the undisturbed motion; the expressions $u , v , w$ reduce to the eccentric anomaly for the undisturbed motion.A variable such as $\boldsymbol { u }$ was employed by Sundman (1907,1908,1913) and Levi-Civita (1903,1906,1919) for the regularization of the three-body problem (as discussed in Chapter 22).Bohlin expanded the unknown functions in the form

$$
\sum _ { h = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { l = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { h k l } \exp { \{ h u + k v + l w \} } .
$$

In the neighborhood ofa collsion of two of the bodies we have,since the effect of the third body is negligible,

$$
{ \frac { r } { a } } = 1 - e \cos u , \qquad s = n t + c - u = - e \sin u .
$$

At the instant of a collision, cos $u = 1 / e$ Let

$$
\bar { \mathfrak { j } } ( 1 , 0 ) = \cos u , \qquad \bar { \mathfrak { j } } ( 0 , 0 ) = 1 , \qquad \bar { \mathfrak { p } } ( 1 , 0 ) = \frac 1 \epsilon , \qquad \bar { \mathfrak { p } } ( 0 , 0 ) = 1 ,
$$

$$
\bar { \bar { q } } ( 1 , 0 ) = \sin u , \qquad \bar { \bar { q } } ( 0 , 0 ) = 0 , \qquad q ( 1 , 0 ) = \frac { \bar { \bar { q } } ( 1 , 0 ) } { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 1 , 0 ) } ,
$$

$$
q ( 0 , 0 ) = \frac { \bar { \bar { q } } ( 0 , 0 ) } { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 0 , 0 ) } = 0 ,
$$

and

$$
\hat { p } ( m , n ) = \frac { \bar { \bar { p } } ( m , n ) } { \bar { \bar { p } } ( m , n ) } \cdot
$$

Then $\boldsymbol { r } / a = + \boldsymbol { \phi } ( 0 , 0 ) - \boldsymbol { \phi } ( 1 , 0 )$ and $s / \alpha = q ( 0 , 0 ) - q ( 1 , 0 )$ ,where $\alpha = 1$ in the two-body problem.

For Euler's rectilinear motion of three bodies (Section 3.2) we have the differential equations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { M _ { 1 } + M _ { 3 } } { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { M _ { 2 } } { x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { M _ { 2 } } { ( x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } , } \\ { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { M _ { 2 } + M _ { 3 } } { x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { M _ { 1 } } { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { M _ { 1 } } { ( x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Writing $x _ { 2 } = \lambda x _ { 1 }$ ， we obtain a quintic equation in $\lambda$ ：

$$
- ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 3 } ) + \frac { M _ { 2 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } - \frac { M _ { 2 } } { ( 1 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } } { \bf \biggr ] } = - \frac { M _ { 2 } + M _ { 3 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } + M _ { 1 } - \frac { M _ { 1 } } { ( 1 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } }
$$

Bohlin's expansions are related to the roots of a quintic equation, based on the general solution studied by Halphen (l891).The solution is a rational function of the coefficients of the equation, the square root of the discriminant,and the roots of a particular equation. The last roots are $\wp ( 2 \omega / 5 )$ and $\wp ( 4 \omega / 5 )$ ,where $\wp$ is an elliptic function such that $\wp ^ { \prime 2 } = 4 \wp ^ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } \wp - g _ { 3 } ,$ and $\pmb { \omega }$ is one of the periods.The other solutions were given by Hermite and Kronecker.

If an algebraic equation (178) has a simple root at $\tau = \tau _ { 0 } ,$ then the root $\pmb { y }$ can be expanded in powers of $\tau \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \tau _ { 0 }$ 、The expansion can be obtained by substituting

$$
y = a _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 } ( \tau - \tau _ { 0 } ) + a _ { 2 } ( \tau - \tau _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

in the equation (l78). If it has a double root at $\tau = \tau _ { 0 } ,$ then the root can be expanded in powers of $\sqrt { \tau - \tau _ { 0 } } .$ If it has a triple root at $\tau = \tau _ { 0 } ,$ then the root can be expanded in powers of $\sqrt [ 3 ] { \tau - \tau _ { 0 } } .$ Suppose that $y = a _ { 0 }$ for $\boldsymbol { \tau } = \boldsymbol { \tau } _ { 0 } . \mathbf { I f } \boldsymbol { \tau } = \boldsymbol { \tau } _ { 0 }$ is a singular point of the root, then $\partial f / \partial y = 0$ for $\begin{array} { r } { y = a _ { 0 } } \end{array}$ . If two roots coincide at $\tau = \tau _ { 0 } ,$ ，then $\partial ^ { 2 } f / \partial y ^ { 2 } \neq 0$ ，but if three roots coincide at $\tau = \tau _ { 0 } ,$ then $\partial ^ { 2 } f / \partial y ^ { 2 } = 0$

In the expansion (183) we put

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { P _ { 1 } = A _ { h } x ^ { h } , \quad } & { { } } & { A _ { h } = A _ { h k } y ^ { k } , } \\ { P _ { 2 } = A _ { h k } x ^ { h } y ^ { k } , \quad } & { { } } & { A _ { h k } = A _ { h k l } z ^ { l } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x = \lambda e ^ { i u } , \qquad y = \mu e ^ { i v } , \qquad z = \nu e ^ { i w } ; } \end{array}
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rlap / p ( m , n ) = \Re \left| \lambda ^ { m } \mu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m u + n v ) } + \mu ^ { m } \nu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m v + n w ) } + \nu ^ { m } \lambda ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m w + n u ) } \right. \ } \\ { + \left. \lambda ^ { m } \nu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m u + n w ) } + \mu ^ { m } \lambda ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m v + n u ) } + \nu ^ { m } \mu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m v + n w ) } \right| , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \iota ( m , n ) = \Re ( \lambda \mu \nu ) ^ { l } e ^ { i l ( u + v + w ) } } } \\ & { } & { \times \ \times | \begin{array} { l } { \lambda ^ { m } \mu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m u + n v ) } + \mu ^ { m } \nu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m v + n w ) } + \nu ^ { m } \lambda ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m w + n u ) } } \\ { + \lambda ^ { m } \nu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m u + n w ) } + \mu ^ { m } \lambda ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m v + n u ) } + \nu ^ { m } \mu ^ { n } e ^ { i ( m v + n u ) } } \end{array}  } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { e }$ is the base of the natural logarithms.

From the expansion

$$
P = \sum \rlap / p ( m , n ) + ( x y z ) \sum \rlap / p _ { 1 } ( m , n ) + ( x y z ) ^ { 2 } \sum \rlap / p _ { 2 } ( m , n ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$

We obtain the three radius vectors if we assume that

\begin{array} { r l } { r _ { 1 } = 0 \colon } & { { } \quad u _ { 1 } = a _ { 1 } i , \qquad v _ { 1 } = b _ { 1 } i , \qquad w _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } i ; } \\ { r _ { 2 } = 0 \colon } & { { } \quad u _ { 2 } = a _ { 2 } i , \qquad v _ { 2 } = b _ { 2 } i , \qquad w _ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } i ; } \\ { r _ { 3 } = 0 \colon } & { { } \quad u _ { 3 } = a _ { 3 } i , \qquad v _ { 3 } = b _ { 3 } i , \qquad w _ { 3 } = c _ { 3 } i ; } \end{array}

\frac { r } { a } = \frac { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 0 , 0 ) } { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 0 , 0 ) } - \frac { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 1 , 0 ) } { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 1 , 0 ) } , \qquad n t + \epsilon = u - \frac { \bar { \bar { q } } ( 1 , 0 ) } { \bar { \bar { p } } ( 1 , 0 ) } ,

Von Haerdtl (l89l） computed the orbits for a special case of the three-body problem.Two masses, $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 3 } .$ each describe a circular orbit around the mass $M _ { 2 }$ Take $r _ { 1 } = \overline { { M _ { 2 } M _ { 3 } } } = 1$ ， $r _ { 3 } = \overline { { M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } } } = 2 ;$ ， $M _ { 1 } = M _ { 2 } = 1$ ， $M _ { 3 } = 0$ (Figure 7). Suppose $M _ { 2 }$ fixed; $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 3 }$ are initially on the same straight line with $M _ { 2 }$ .At the initial instant $M _ { 3 }$ has the velocity $v _ { 3 }$ upward and $M _ { 1 }$ has the velocity $v _ { 1 }$ downward,both perpendicular to the line $\overline { { { M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } M _ { 2 } } } }$ .Then,

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { { \frac { \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 } } { a } } = \operatorname { c i } \left( { \boldsymbol { u } } _ { 1 } , { \boldsymbol { v } } _ { 1 } , { \boldsymbol { w } } _ { 1 } \right) , } \\ & { } \\ & { { \frac { \boldsymbol { r } _ { 2 } } { b } } = \operatorname { c a } \left( { \boldsymbol { u } } _ { 2 } , { \boldsymbol { v } } _ { 2 } , { \boldsymbol { w } } _ { 2 } \right) , } \\ & { } \\ & { { \frac { \boldsymbol { r } _ { 3 } } { c } } = \operatorname { c e } \left( { \boldsymbol { u } } _ { 3 } , { \boldsymbol { v } } _ { 3 } , { \boldsymbol { w } } _ { 3 } \right) ; } \end{array} }
$$

FIGURE 7

that is,

$$
{ \begin{array} { l } { { \frac { r _ { 1 } } { a } } = 1 - { \frac { \cos u + \mu _ { 1 } \cos \left( v + M _ { 1 } \right) + \nu _ { 1 } \cos \left( w + N _ { 1 } \right) } { \cosh a _ { 1 } + \mu _ { 1 } \cosh b _ { 1 } + \nu _ { 1 } \cosh c _ { 1 } } } } \\ { { \frac { r _ { 2 } } { b } } = 1 - { \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } \cos \left( u + L _ { 2 } \right) + \cos v + \nu _ { 2 } \cos \left( w + N _ { 2 } \right) } { \lambda _ { 2 } \cosh a _ { 2 } + \cosh b _ { 2 } + \nu _ { 2 } \cosh c _ { 2 } } } , } \\ { { \frac { r _ { 3 } } { c } } = 1 - { \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } \cos \left( u + L _ { 3 } \right) + \mu _ { 3 } \cos \left( v + M _ { 3 } \right) + \cos w } { \lambda _ { 3 } \cosh a _ { 3 } + \mu _ { 3 } \cosh b _ { 3 } + \cosh c _ { 3 } } } } \end{array} }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { n _ { 1 } t + \epsilon _ { 1 } - u = - \frac { \sin u + \mu _ { 1 } \sin \left( v + M _ { 1 } \right) + \nu _ { 1 } \sin \left( w + N _ { 1 } \right) } { \alpha _ { 1 } \cosh a _ { 1 } + \mu _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } \cosh b _ { 1 } + \nu _ { 1 } \gamma _ { 1 } \cosh c _ { 1 } } , } \\ & { } \\ & { n _ { 2 } t + \epsilon _ { 2 } - v = - \frac { \lambda _ { 2 } \sin \left( u + L _ { 2 } \right) + \sin v + \nu _ { 2 } \sin \left( w + N _ { 2 } \right) } { \lambda _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } \cosh a _ { 2 } + \beta _ { 2 } \cosh b _ { 2 } + \nu _ { 2 } \gamma _ { 2 } \cosh c _ { 2 } } , } \\ & { } \\ & { n _ { 3 } t + \epsilon _ { 3 } - w = - \frac { \lambda _ { 3 } \sin \left( u + L _ { 3 } \right) + \mu _ { 3 } \sin \left( v + M _ { 3 } \right) + \sin w } { \lambda _ { 3 } \alpha _ { 3 } \cosh a _ { 3 } + \mu _ { 3 } \beta _ { 3 } \cosh b _ { 3 } + \gamma _ { 3 } \cosh c _ { 3 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where the constants $a _ { r } , ~ b _ { r } , ~ c _ { r }$ and $\psi , ~ \mu _ { \tau } , ~ \nu _ { \tau }$ are so chosen that ${ \pmb P } _ { 1 } ( m )$ should always vanish, and $L , M , N$ are constant phases.

Wintner (l930),by varying the mass ratio of the two bodies and the Jacobi constant (Section 17.4)，proved that the orbit of von Haerdtl is a continuation of Darwin's periodic orbits for a satellite.

# SOLUTION BY LAMBERT'S SERIES

# 10.42CHARLIER's THEORY

Charlier (1892), following Lambert's work of 1767, tried to solve the three-body problem in ascending integral power series of time,and compared his results with von Haerdtl's computations. Charlier's procedure is to expand the coordinates in power series of time and determine the various coefficients by substituting these series in the equations of motion. The calculation is complicated by the presence of the reciprocals of the mutual distances of the three bodies.

Then Charlier (l893) tried a completely different type of series for the same purpose.Let

$$
n = \frac { \sqrt { m ^ { \prime } + m ^ { \prime \prime } } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } , ~ n ^ { \prime } = \frac { \sqrt { m + m ^ { \prime \prime } } } { a ^ { \prime 3 / 2 } } , ~ \nu = n - n ^ { \prime } ,
$$

$$
\Delta = \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 a a ^ { \prime } \cos { ( \nu t + \epsilon ) } } ,
$$

where $\pmb { n }$ and $\scriptstyle { \pmb { n } } ^ { \prime }$ are the undisturbed mean motions of the two bodies with smaller mass relative to the third heavy body. We expand the relative polar coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { r = a ( 1 + \rho ) , } & & { r ^ { \prime } = a ^ { \prime } ( 1 + \rho ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ & { v = n t + c + s , } & & { v ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + c ^ { \prime } + s ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { r ^ { \prime \prime 2 } = \Delta ^ { 2 } + f , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { = 2 a ^ { 2 } \rho + 2 a ^ { \prime 2 } \rho ^ { \prime } - 2 a a ^ { \prime } \cos \left( \nu t + \epsilon \right) \cdot \left( \rho + \rho ^ { \prime } \right) \hfill } } \\ { { = 2 a a ^ { \prime } \sin \left( \nu t + \epsilon \right) \cdot \left( s - s ^ { \prime } \right) + \cdot \left( \frac { \ d } { \ d t } \right) \cdot \left( \epsilon - \frac { \ d } { \ d t } \right) \cdot \left( \epsilon - \frac { \ d } { \ d t } \right) \cdot \left( \epsilon - \frac { \ d } { \ d t } \right) } } \end{array}
$$

in the form

$$
\rho = \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { k } \Delta ^ { k } , \qquad \rho ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { k } ^ { \prime } \Delta ^ { k } ,
$$

$$
s = 2 a a ^ { \prime } \sin { ( \nu t + \epsilon ) } \cdot \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } B _ { k } \Delta ^ { k } ,
$$

$$
s ^ { \prime } = 2 a a ^ { \prime } \sin \left( \nu t + \epsilon \right) \cdot \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } B _ { k } ^ { \prime } \Delta ^ { k } ,
$$

$$
f = \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } E _ { k } \Delta ^ { k } , \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } = \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } F _ { k } \Delta ^ { k } ,
$$

where $E _ { k }$ and $F _ { k }$ are functions of $A _ { k } , A _ { k } ^ { \prime } , B _ { k } ,$ and $B _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ . Put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { s - s ^ { \prime } = 2 a a ^ { \prime } \sin { \left( \nu t + \epsilon \right) } \cdot u , } \end{array}
$$

and expand

$$
f = \sum _ { k , l , m } ^ { \infty } M _ { k l m } \rho ^ { k } \rho ^ { \prime l } u ^ { m } ,
$$

$$
\frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } = \sum _ { k , l , m = 0 } ^ { \infty } N _ { k l m } \rho ^ { k } \rho ^ { \prime l } u ^ { m } ,
$$

$$
r ^ { 2 } r ^ { \prime } \cos { H } = \sum _ { k , l , m = 0 } ^ { \infty } H _ { k l m } \rho ^ { k } \rho ^ { \prime l } u ^ { m } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { M _ { 1 0 0 } = a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } + \Delta ^ { 2 } , \qquad M _ { 0 1 0 } = - a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } + \Delta ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { M _ { 0 0 1 } = - ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + 2 ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) \Delta ^ { 2 } - \Delta ^ { 4 } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \displaystyle N _ { 0 0 0 } = \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } , ~ N _ { 1 0 0 } = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } , } } } \\ { { \ } } \\  { { \displaystyle N _ { 0 1 0 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle N _ { 0 0 1 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } - 3 \frac { a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Delta } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle . . . , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { H _ { 0 0 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } a ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - \Delta ^ { 2 } ) , \qquad H _ { 1 0 0 } = a ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - \Delta ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { H _ { 0 1 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } a ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - \Delta ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { H _ { 0 0 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \bigl [ - ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + 2 ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } ) \Delta ^ { 2 } - \Delta ^ { 4 } \bigr ] , } \end{array}
$$

The equations of motion are

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l r } & { r ^ { 2 } { \cfrac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - r ^ { 3 } \left( { \cfrac { d v } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + m ^ { \prime } + m ^ { \prime \prime } = m P , } & { \qquad r ^ { 2 } { \cfrac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + 2 r { \cfrac { d r } { d t } } { \cfrac { d v } { d t } } = m } \\ & { \cdot 2 { \cfrac { d ^ { 2 } r ^ { \prime } } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - r ^ { \prime 3 } \left( { \cfrac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + m + m ^ { \prime \prime } = m ^ { \prime } P ^ { \prime } , } & { \qquad r ^ { \prime 2 } { \cfrac { d ^ { 2 } v ^ { \prime } } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + 2 r ^ { \prime } { \cfrac { d r ^ { \prime } } { d t } } { \cfrac { d v ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = m } \end{array} }
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \mathrm { } } & { { } = \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \Big ) r ^ { 2 } r ^ { \prime } \cos { H } - \frac { r ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } , } & { \quad Q = - \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \Big ) r r ^ { \prime } \sin { H } , } \\ { \mathrm { } } & { { } = \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \Big ) r ^ { \prime 2 } r \cos { H } - \frac { r ^ { \prime 3 } } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } , } & { \quad Q ^ { \prime } = - \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime \prime 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \Big ) r r ^ { \prime } \sin { H } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
P = \sum _ { k , l , m = 0 } ^ { \infty } P _ { k l m } \rho ^ { k } \rho ^ { \prime l } u ^ { m } ,
$$

$$
r r ^ { \prime } \sin H = 2 a a ^ { \prime } \sin { ( \nu t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) } \cdot \sum _ { k , l , m = 0 } ^ { \infty } I _ { k l m } \rho ^ { k } \rho ^ { \prime l } u ^ { m } ,
$$

$$
Q = 2 a a ^ { \prime } \sin { ( \nu t + \epsilon ) } \cdot \sum _ { k , l , m = 0 } ^ { \infty } Q _ { k l m } \rho ^ { k } \rho ^ { \prime l } u ^ { m } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P _ { 0 0 0 } = \displaystyle - \frac { a ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) } { 2 \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \frac { a } { 2 \Delta } - \frac { a } { 2 a ^ { \prime } } \left( 1 + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \right) + \frac { a \Delta ^ { 2 } } { 2 a ^ { \prime 3 } } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { P _ { 1 0 0 } = \displaystyle \frac { 3 a ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 4 \Delta ^ { 5 } } - \frac { a ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } ) } { 2 \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \frac { a } { 4 \Delta } - \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } \left( 1 + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \right) + \frac { a \Delta ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime 3 } } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { P _ { 0 1 0 } = \displaystyle - \frac { 3 a ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 4 \Delta ^ { 5 } } + \frac { a ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } ) } { 2 \Delta ^ { 3 } } + \frac { a } { 4 \Delta } + \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime } } \left( 1 + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \right) - \frac { a } { a ^ { \prime 3 } } } } \\ { { \ . . . , } } \end{array}

The equations of motion are transformed to

\begin{array} { r } { a ^ { 3 } ( 1 + \rho ) ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho } { d t ^ { 2 } } - a ^ { 3 } ( 1 + \rho ) ^ { 3 } \Big ( n + \displaystyle \frac { d s } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + m ^ { \prime } + m ^ { \prime \prime } = m P , } \\ { a ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \rho ) ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 a ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \rho ) \displaystyle \frac { d \rho } { d t } \Big ( n + \displaystyle \frac { d s } { d t } \Big ) = m Q . } \end{array}
$$

Expand $\pmb { \rho }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ in the form

$$
\rho = \mu \rho _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \quad \quad s = \mu s _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } s _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $m = b \mu$ ， $m ^ { \prime } = b ^ { \prime } \mu$ ; then,

$$

where $\Omega _ { k - 1 }$ and $\Psi _ { k - 1 }$ are rational functions of $\rho _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , \rho _ { k - 1 } , s _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , s _ { k - 1 }$ and can be computed in powers of $\Delta$ . If we take the relation

$$
n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = m ^ { \prime } + m ^ { \prime \prime }

into account, the equations reduce to

\frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 3 n ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } - 2 n \frac { d s _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { b ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { 3 } } P _ { 0 } , \frac { d ^ { 2 } s _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 n \frac { d \rho _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { b } { a ^ { 2 } } Q _ { 0 } .

Eliminating $s _ { 1 }$ and substituting the explicit expressions for $\scriptstyle P _ { 0 }$ and $Q _ { 0 }$ ， we obtain

\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + n ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } = I , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \left[ - \frac { a ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) } { 2 \Delta ^ { 3 } } + \frac { a } { \Delta } \left( \frac { 2 n } { \nu } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) - \frac { a } { 2 a ^ { ' } } \left( 1 ~ { + } ~ \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime 2 } } \right) \right. } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } \left. \qquad + ~ 2 a n C + \frac { a \Delta ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime 3 } } \left( \frac { 2 n } { \nu } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
\rho _ { 1 } = \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { k } \Delta ^ { k } ;

then,

$$

a _ { k } A _ { k } - b _ { k } A _ { k + 2 } + c _ { k } A _ { k + 4 } = 0 ,

where

For large values of $k$

= \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { \nu } { 2 } \left[ - k B _ { k - 2 } + 2 ( a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ( k + 1 ) B _ { k } \right. \qquad \\  \qquad \quad - \left. ( a ^ { 2 } - a ^ { \prime 2 } ) ( k + 2 ) B _ { k + 2 } \right] \Delta
