If $\pmb { c }$ and $- \pmb { c }$ are the pair of the nontrivial characteristic exponents, that is, if $2 \pi c$ is the period of the motion of the perigee, then the two nontrivial solutions for these variational equations are

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { v ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) = \gamma \cdot \displaystyle \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { k } \cos { [ ( 2 k + 1 + c ) \xi + \delta ] } , } \\ & { w ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) = \gamma \cdot \displaystyle \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } B _ { k } \sin { [ ( 2 k + 1 + c ) \xi + \delta ] } \quad \quad ( \gamma \neq 0 ) , } \end{array} }
$$

δ being an arbitrary constant, and $\pmb { c }$ $A _ { k } , B _ { k } ,$ analytic functions of m.

As $\mathbf { m } \to 0 _ { \cdot }$ ，the coefficients $A _ { k } ( \mathbf { m } )$ and $B _ { \kappa } ( \mathbf { m } )$ behave similarly to $a _ { k } ( \mathbf { m } )$ in the expression for $X$ for a large value of $| k |$ . The limiting values $\left( A _ { k } \right) _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 }$ and $( B _ { k } ) _ { \bf m = 0 }$ vanish unless $| 2 k + 1 | = 1$ .If $| 2 k + 1 | = 1$ ，so that $k = 1$ or $k = - 1$ , then

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { ( A _ { 0 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } \ne 0 , } } & { { ( B _ { 0 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } = ( A _ { 0 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } , } } \\ { { ( A _ { - 1 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } = - 3 ( A _ { 0 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } , } } & { { ( B _ { - 1 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } = 3 ( B _ { 0 } ) _ { \bf m = 0 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus the expressions for $v ( t ; \mathbf { m } )$ and $w ( t ; \mathbf { m } )$ become,by omitting the nonzero constant factor ${ \pmb \gamma }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { [ v ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) ] _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 } = - \cos \xi \cos [ ( c ) _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 } \xi + \delta ] - 2 \sin \xi \sin [ ( c ) _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 } \xi + } \\ & { } & { ( } \\ & { w ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) ] _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 } = - \sin \xi \cos [ ( c ) _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 } \xi + \delta ] + 2 \cos \xi \sin [ ( c ) _ { \mathbf { m } = 0 } \xi + } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { [ { \boldsymbol v } ^ { 2 } ( t ; { \mathbf m } ) + { \boldsymbol w } ^ { 2 } ( t ; { \mathbf m } ) ] _ { { \mathbf m } = { \boldsymbol 0 } } } } \\ & { } & { = \cos ^ { 2 } \big [ \big ( c \big ) _ { { \mathbf m } = { \boldsymbol 0 } } \xi + \mathfrak { s } \big ] + 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \big [ ( c ) _ { { \mathbf m } = { \boldsymbol 0 } } \xi + \mathfrak { s } \big ] . } \end{array}
$$

Since this continuous and nonvanishing functin of $\xi$ is periodic, there exists a sufficiently small constant $\alpha _ { 0 }$ which depends on the integration constant δ and $\gamma \neq 0$ but is such that

$$
[ v ^ { 2 } ( t ; { \bf m } ) + w ^ { 2 } ( t ; { \bf m } ) ] _ { { \bf m } = 0 } > \alpha _ { 0 } > 0 ( - \infty < \xi < + \infty ) .
$$

Hence, for a sufficiently small value of $\mathbf { m } ^ { * } > 0$ such that

$$
- \mathbf { m } ^ { * } < \mathbf { m } < \mathbf { \tau } + \mathbf { m } ^ { * } ,
$$

we can choose a positive constant $\alpha = \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } }$ for which the almost periodic functions $v ( t ; \mathbf { m } )$ and $w ( t ; \mathbf { m } )$ of $\xi$ .or of $t = \mathbf { m } \xi$ satisfy

$$
[ v ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) ] ^ { 2 } + [ w ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) ] ^ { 2 } > \alpha _ { \mathbf { m } } > 0 \qquad ( - \infty < t < + \infty ) ,
$$

where $\alpha _ { \mathbf { m } }$ is supposed to be a function of the integration constants $\gamma$ and δ for a fixed value of $\mathbf { m }$ ,but $\mathbf { m } ^ { * }$ is independent of $\gamma$ and 8.

Now we introduce $\varpi = \varpi ( t )$ as a function of $t$ or $\xi$ by

$$
v ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) = \rho \cos \varpi , \qquad w ( t ; \mathbf { m } ) = \rho \sin \varpi ;
$$

then $\varpi = \varpi ( t )$ is determined for every $t$ by continuity, if an initial normalizing condition $0 \leq \varpi ( 0 ) < 2 \pi$ is assigned. Put

$$
z ( t ) = v + { \sqrt { - 1 } } w = ( v ^ { 2 } + w ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } } \varpi \} .
$$

Then $z ( t )$ is an almost periodic function (Section 2.12). Furthermore, we have seen that $| z ( t ) | > \alpha _ { \mathbf { m } } ^ { 1 / 2 } > 0$ for every $t .$ Hence Bohr's theorem (1930; Section 27.7) can be applied to $\begin{array} { r } { z ( t ) = | z | \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \varpi \} } . } \end{array}$ where ${ \pmb { \varpi } } ( t )$ should be of the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \pmb { \sigma } ( t ) = \mu t + \mathrm { a n ~ a l m o s t ~ p e r i o d i c ~ f u n c t i o n ~ o f ~ } t , } } \\ { { \pmb { \mu } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . } } } \end{array}
$$

From the definition we see that

$$
{ \pmb { \varpi } } ^ { \prime } \equiv { \frac { d { \pmb { \varpi } } } { d t } } = { \frac { v \dot { v } - w \dot { v } } { v ^ { 2 } + w ^ { 2 } } }
$$

is an almost periodic function. Since the integral of an almost periodic function is almost periodic if it is bounded (Section 2.l2),this expression (63) for ${ \boldsymbol { \varpi } } ( t )$ can be infered from the latter expression (64) for ${ \pmb { \varpi } } ^ { \prime }$ by integration, if and only if $\varpi ( t ) \ : - \ : \mu t$ is bounded, where $\pmb { \mu }$ denotes the constant term in the Fourier series of the almost periodic function $\scriptstyle { \overline { { \pmb { \varpi } } } } ^ { \prime }$ ， such that

$$
\mu = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { T \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \varpi ^ { \prime } ( t ) \ d t .
$$

On the other hand, the integral of an almost periodic function that has no constant term in its Fourier expansion is, according to Favard (1932; Section l4.25), almost periodic if there is no arbitrary smallfrequency, that is, if there is no small divisor. Otherwise nothing can be said about the almost periodicity,as is known by classical perturbation theory.Thus the expressions (6l)， (64) and the inequality (62) show that the frequencies of the almost periodic function $\varpi ^ { \prime } ( \mathbf { m } \xi )$ or $\varpi ^ { \prime } ( \mathbf { m } \xi ) - \mu$ are contained in the double sequence

$$
\nu _ { 1 } c + \nu _ { 2 } \qquad ( \nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , . . . ) .
$$

Since $c = c ( \mathbf { m } )$ is a continuous and nonconstant function of $\mathbf { m }$ ,and can be supposed to be irrational, this double sequence is identical with small divisors in classical perturbation theory.All frequencies of the almost periodic function $\varpi ^ { \prime } ( \mathbf { m } \xi )$ are contained in this double sequence.Hence the Fourier series of the almost periodic function of the right-hand member of (63) is of the form

$$
\varpi ( t ) \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \mu t \approx \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { k = - \infty } ^ { \infty } C _ { j k } \cos \left[ \frac { c ( \mathbf { m } ) j + k } { \mathbf { m } } t + \delta _ { j k } \right] .
$$

We have

$$
\mu = \frac { c r + s } { \bf m } , \qquad c = c ( \bf m ) ,
$$

where $r$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ are integers. This agrees with Bohr's theorem (1932) on an almost periodic motion, as will be described in Chapter 27 (see also Section 2.12).

Hill considered only the secular part $\mu$ of the motion of the perigee and put $\varpi ( t ) = \mu t$ ， $\varpi ^ { \prime } ( t ) = \mu$ . This assumption of Hill is now justified by the relation (65),although Hill has not written the periodic part in the form of (65).The mathematical postulates of the lunar theory of Hill and Brown,in which higher powers of the eccentricities and inclination are taken into account, should be studied by an entirely different approach.

# THE PLANETARY ACTIONS

# 11.9ACTION DUE TO PLANETS

The effect of any disturbing cause on the motion of the moon can be studied by inserting the appropriate additional periodic and constant terms in the disturbing function.The planetary action was studied by

Hansen (l862,l864). The period and the coeffcients of such terms in the disturbing function can be determined with sufficient accuracy for practical purposes; thus,Hill's method (1883,1885,1886a)ofintegration for the lunar inequalities due to Jupiter, founded on Delaunay's formulas (1867)，is recommended.The coefficients of the inserted terms are generally small, and it is scarcely necessary to consider the changes produced in the inserted terms due to those changes of the elements which occur when any one of the original or the inserted periodic terms is eliminated by Delaunay's procedures. The operations are similar to those described in Section 9.1, but the operations can be abridged owing to the circumstance that the numerical values of the moon's orbit can be used. The numerical procedure was given by Radau (1892,1895) by modifying Hill's method.

We suppose that the periodic terms arising only from the action of the sun, supposed to be moving in an elliptic orbit, have been eliminated, and that the disturbing function contains only the remaining constant portion together with the inserted periodic term in question.

Let $x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } , z ^ { \prime } , r ^ { \prime }$ be the coordinates of the sun, $x , y , z , r$ those of the moon,referred to the axes fixed in direction and passing through the earth, and let $S$ be the cosine of the angle between the vectors $r$ and $\scriptstyle { \pmb { r } } ^ { \prime }$ The disturbing function due to the sun for the motion of the moon is

$$
R = { \frac { m ^ { \prime } r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } } \left( { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } S ^ { 2 } - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \right) + { \frac { m ^ { \prime } r ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { \prime 4 } } } \left( { \frac { 5 } { 2 } } S ^ { 3 } - { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } S \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

If $x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } , z ^ { \prime } , r ^ { \prime }$ are considered to be the sun's position referred to the center of mass of the earth and moon, then the second term should be multiplied by the mass ratio $( m _ { E } - m _ { M } ) / ( m _ { E } + m _ { M } )$ . Let $\xi , \eta , \zeta , D$ be the coordinates of a planet $P$ referred to the same axes,and $m ^ { \prime \prime }$ be the planet's mass.The corresponding disturbing function for the moon's motion is

$$
R ^ { \prime } = \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } r ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac 3 2 S ^ { \prime 2 } - \frac 1 2 \right) + \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } r ^ { 3 } } { D ^ { 4 } } \left( \frac 5 2 S ^ { \prime 3 } - \frac 3 2 S ^ { \prime } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$S ^ { \prime }$ being the cosine of the angle between the vectors $\pmb { r }$ and $D , \operatorname { I f } \xi , \eta , \zeta , D$ are referred to the center of mass of the earth and moon,then the second term should be multiplied by $( m _ { E } - m _ { M } ) / ( m _ { E } + m _ { M } )$

Since the ratios $r / r ^ { \prime } , r / D , m ^ { \prime \prime } / m ^ { \prime }$ are small, it is practically sufficient to consider only the first term of $R ^ { \prime }$ . The inequalities produced by $R ^ { \prime }$ are said to be due to the direct action of the planet.In considering the effect due to several planets, the terms produced by the combination of two terms, each belonging to the disturbing function due to the corresponding two planets, can be neglected, so that the effects of different planets can be added after computing the effect of each planet separately. The solar inequalities due to the purely elliptic motion of the sun are supposed to have been determined. The actions of planets on the motion of the earth may produce small deviations from this purely elliptic motion. These actions,when substituted in $R$ ，are small corrections $\delta \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \prime }$ ，8y'， $\delta \boldsymbol { z ^ { \prime } }$ to the solar coordinates $x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } ,$ $z ^ { \prime }$ .Hence it is sufficient to limit $R$ to its first term for the inequalities produced in the moon's motion. The lunar inequalities produced due to such causes are said to be due to the indirect action of the planets. Since $m ^ { \prime \prime }$ is very small compared with $m ^ { \prime }$ ，these corrections $\delta \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \prime }$ ，8y'， $\delta { z } ^ { \prime }$ are not necessary in $R ^ { \prime }$

Thus we confine ourselves to consider in $\pmb { R }$ and $\pmb { R ^ { \prime } }$ only their first terms:

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m ^ { \prime } } R = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } + z z ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m ^ { \prime \prime } } R ^ { \prime } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( x \xi + y \eta + z \zeta ) ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 5 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 3 } } ; } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m ^ { \prime } } R = \frac { r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } - \frac { 3 z ^ { \prime 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } \right) \ + \ 3 \frac { x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \frac { x ^ { \prime 2 } - y ^ { \prime 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } } & { } & \\ { \displaystyle + \ 3 x y \left( \frac { x ^ { \prime } y ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } \right) \ + \ 3 x z \left( \frac { x ^ { \prime } z ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } \right) \ + \ 3 y z \left( \frac { y ^ { \prime } z ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 5 } } \right) , } & { } & \\ { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m ^ { \prime } } R ^ { \prime } = \frac { r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { D ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 \zeta ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 5 } } \right) \ + \ 3 \frac { x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 5 } } } & { } & \\ { \displaystyle + \ 3 x y \Big ( \frac { \xi \eta } { D ^ { 5 } } \Big ) \ + \ 3 x z \Big ( \frac { \xi \zeta } { D ^ { 5 } } \Big ) \ + \ 3 y z \Big ( \frac { \eta \zeta } { D ^ { 5 } } \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

in which the moon's coordinates are separated from the sun's or the planet's coordinates.

Suppose the ecliptic to be fixed perpendicular to the $z \mathrm { . }$ -axis. Let $\scriptstyle L$ denote the distance of the moon from its node, $\pmb { h }$ the longitude of the node, and $\gamma$ the sine of half the inclination $\pmb { I }$ of the moon's orbit; then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x = r \cos L \cos h - r \cos I \sin L \sin h , } \\ { y = r \cos L \sin h + r \cos I \sin L \cos h , } \\ { z = r \sin L \sin I ; } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x = ( 1 - \gamma ^ { 2 } ) r \cos { ( L + h ) } + \gamma ^ { 2 } r \cos { ( L - h ) } , } \\ & { y = ( 1 - \gamma ^ { 2 } ) r \sin { ( L + h ) } - \gamma ^ { 2 } r \sin { ( L - h ) } , } \\ & { z = z \gamma \sqrt { 1 - \gamma ^ { 2 } } \cdot r \sin { L } . } \end{array}
$$

Denote by $V ^ { \prime }$ the longitude of the earth as seen from the sun; then we have

$$
x ^ { \prime } = - r ^ { \prime } \cos V ^ { \prime } , { } ~ y ^ { \prime } = - r ^ { \prime } \sin V ^ { \prime } , { } ~ z ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

The coordinates of $P$ relative to the earth are $\xi , \eta , \zeta ,$ which are the sums of the coordinates of $P$ relative to the sun and those of the sun relative to the earth.Let $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ be the sine of half the inclination of the planet's orbit to the ecliptic, $h ^ { \prime \prime }$ the longitude of the planet's node on the ecliptic, $V ^ { \prime \prime }$ the longitude of $\pmb { P }$ as seen from the sun,counted along the ecliptic to its node and then along the orbit,and $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime \prime }$ the planet's distance from the sun; then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \xi = - r ^ { \prime } \cos V ^ { \prime } + ( 1 - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } ) r ^ { \prime \prime } \cos V ^ { \prime \prime } + \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } r ^ { \prime \prime } \cos \left( V ^ { \prime \prime } - 2 h ^ { \prime \prime } \right) ; } \\ & { \eta = - r ^ { \prime } \sin V ^ { \prime } + ( 1 - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } ) r ^ { \prime \prime } \sin V ^ { \prime } - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } r ^ { \prime \prime } \sin \left( V ^ { \prime \prime } - 2 h ^ { \prime \prime } \right) , } \\ & { \zeta = 2 \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \sqrt { 1 - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } . r ^ { \prime \prime } \sin \left( V ^ { \prime \prime } - h ^ { \prime \prime } \right) ; } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { D ^ { 2 } = \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } = D _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + 4 \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } r ^ { \prime } \sin \left( V ^ { \prime } - h ^ { \prime \prime } \right) \sin \left( V ^ { \prime \prime } - h ^ { \prime \prime } \right) } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { D _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = r ^ { \prime 2 } + r ^ { \prime \prime } - 2 r ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime \prime } \cos \left( V ^ { \prime } - V ^ { \prime \prime } \right) } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { D ^ { q } } = \frac { 1 } { D _ { 0 } ^ { q } } + \frac { q \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } r ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime \prime } } { D _ { 0 } ^ { q } + 2 } \left[ \cos \left( V ^ { \prime } + V ^ { \prime } - 2 h ^ { \prime } \right) - \cos \left( V ^ { \prime } - V ^ { \prime \prime } \right) \right] + \cdot } . } } \end{array}
$$

By means of these formulas the disturbing functions $\pmb { R }$ and $\scriptstyle { R ^ { \prime } }$ can be expressed in terms of $r , r ^ { \prime } , r ^ { \prime \prime } , L , V ^ { \prime } , V ^ { \prime \prime } , h , h ^ { \prime } , h ^ { \prime \prime } , \gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ .Then the elliptic values for the coordinates of the three bodies are substituted in various parts of the disturbing function.The terms arising in $\pmb R$ from nonelliptic terms in the coordinates of the three bodies are taken into account. Finally the disturbing function can be expressed as a sum of periodic terms.

Let $g$ be the distance of the moon's perigee from its node and $f$ be the true anomaly; then we have, up to the order $\gamma ^ { 4 }$ ，

$$
\frac { 1 } { 4 } \frac { r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 1 - 6 \gamma ^ { 2 } + 6 \gamma ^ { 4 } \right) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \gamma ^ { 2 } ) \cos 2 L ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { 3 } { 4 } } { \frac { x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } ( 1 - \gamma ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } \cos { ( 2 L + 2 h ) } + { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \gamma ^ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 L - 2 h ) }
$$

$$
L = g + f ,
$$

and similar expressions for ${ \scriptstyle \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ( x y / r ^ { 2 } ) , { \scriptstyle \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ( x z / r ^ { 2 } ) , { \scriptstyle \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ( y z / r ^ { 2 } )$ ,and

$$
{ \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } } = 1 + { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } e ^ { 2 } - \left( 2 e - { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } e ^ { 3 } \right) \sin l + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

By giving suitable values to $\propto$ we can express ${ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } ( r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } )$ ， ${ \frac { 3 } { 4 } } ( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } )$ ， and similar expressions in $R$ and $R ^ { \prime }$ in series of cosines or sines with $l , g , h$ as arguments and $\boldsymbol { e } , \gamma$ as coefficients. Thus we obtain (Brown, 1896)

$$
r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } = \sum A _ { 0 } e ^ { k } \cos k l + \gamma ^ { 2 } \cdot \sum B _ { 0 } e ^ { k } \cos { ( 2 M _ { 0 } - 2 h \pm k l ) } ,
$$

$$
M _ { 0 } = l + g + h ,
$$

where $A _ { 0 } , B _ { 0 }$ are the coeffcients of the zero-th order containing powers and products of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ . Thus the portions of the disturbing function that depend only on the coordinates of the moon are expressed as sums of periodic terms.

Denote by $M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ and $M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ the mean longitudes of the earth and the planet, respectively; then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \mathrm { } ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } = \sum A _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } { } ^ { 2 { i } } e ^ { \prime \kappa ^ { \prime } } e ^ { \prime \pi \kappa ^ { \prime } } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \times \cos { [ 2 M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - 2 i M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + j ( M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } ) + 2 i l ^ { \prime \prime } \pm k ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } \pm k ^ { \prime \prime } ] } } } \end{array}
$$

where $A _ { 0 }$ is the coefficient of the zero-th order, $k ^ { \prime } , k ^ { \prime \prime }$ are positive integers or zero,and $i , j$ are positive integers or zero such that $i + j < 3$ Thus the portions of the disturbing function that involve the coordinates of the sun and the planet in the second degree are expressed as sums of periodic terms.

The divisors $D _ { 0 } ^ { q }$ are functions which cause the great difficulty in finding the planetary inequalities in the moon's motion.This is caused by the near equality of $r ^ { \prime } , r ^ { \prime \prime }$ or of $n ^ { \prime } , n ^ { \prime \prime }$ for those planets which are not far from the earth.The expression $D _ { 0 } ^ { - q }$ is expanded in the form

$$
D _ { 0 } ^ { - q } = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } B _ { q } ^ { ( 0 ) } + B _ { q } ^ { ( 1 ) } \cos \left( V ^ { \prime } - V ^ { \prime \prime } \right) + B _ { q } ^ { ( 2 ) } \cos 2 ( V ^ { \prime } - V ^ { \prime \prime } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $B _ { 0 } ^ { ( j ) }$ are homogeneous functions of $\scriptstyle { \pmb { r } } ^ { \prime }$ and $r ^ { \prime \prime }$ . When $\scriptstyle { \pmb { r } } ^ { \prime }$ and $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime \prime }$ are of comparable magnitude, the formal convergence of the expansion is very slow and higher terms must be taken into account.For a superior planet the expansion should be made in powers of $r ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime \prime }$ ；and for an inferior planet, in powers of $r ^ { \prime \prime } / r ^ { \prime }$ . Substitute the elliptic expansions f $\mathrm { { } } \operatorname { x } r ^ { \prime } / a ^ { \prime } , r ^ { \prime \prime } / a ^ { \prime \prime } ;$ ， $V ^ { \prime } , V ^ { \prime \prime }$ in terms of $l ^ { \prime } , l ^ { \prime }$ ; then, we see that

$$
D _ { 0 } ^ { - q } = \sum A _ { 0 } e ^ { \prime k ^ { \prime } } e ^ { \prime \prime k ^ { \prime \prime } } \cos { [ i ( M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - M _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } ) \pm k ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } \pm k ^ { \prime \prime } l ^ { \prime } ] } ,
$$

where $A _ { 0 }$ is a homogeneous function of $\scriptstyle { a ^ { \prime } }$ and $a ^ { \prime \prime }$ and of the zero-th order with respect to $e ^ { \prime }$ and $e ^ { \prime \prime }$ ; and $j , k ^ { \prime }$ ,and $k ^ { \prime \prime }$ are positive integers or zero. Thus the parts of $R ^ { \prime }$ arising from the divisor $D _ { 0 } ^ { q }$ are expressed as sums of periodic terms.

For the terms arising in $R$ from nonelliptic terms present in the coordinates of the three bodies, we may either suppose that $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { r } } ^ { \prime }$ ， $v ^ { \prime }$ receive corresponding small increments $\delta r ^ { \prime } , \delta v ^ { \prime }$ ，or suppose that the additional terms are given as small corrections to the elements of the sun's orbit. In the process of eliminating the periodic terms of $R$ due to the solar action by Delaunay's theory, the moon's elements present in $R ^ { \prime }$ should be changed at each operation.

The indirect action of a planet is taken into account by an abridged form of the disturbing function,

$$
\delta R = - \frac { 3 R } { r ^ { \prime } } \delta r ^ { \prime } - \frac { \partial R } { \partial v } \delta V ^ { \prime } ,
$$

by neglecting the perturbation of the plane of the ecliptic and the ratio of the parallaxes. Most of the larger inequalities of long periods arise from the term $m ^ { \prime } r ^ { 2 } / ( 4 r ^ { \prime 3 } )$ of $R$ .We neglect the eccentricity of the sun's orbit,and obtain

$$
\delta R = - { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } { \frac { n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { \prime } } } \delta r ^ { \prime } \approx - { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 } A \cos \theta ,
$$

where $\delta r ^ { \prime } = a ^ { \prime } A$ cos $\theta$ with constants $\pmb { A }$ and $\theta$ independent of the moon's elements, and

$$
\delta M _ { 0 } = \delta l \approx \frac { 2 } { 9 } \rlap / p A \sin \theta , ~ \phi = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { \bf w } ,
$$

where M is the motion of the argument $\theta _ { , }$ ， such that $\theta = \mathbf { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { u } } } t +$ constant, in Delaunay's theory. Furthermore, we have approximately

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { { \displaystyle \mathfrak { z } r ^ { \prime } = \ \delta a ^ { \prime } , \quad } } & { { } { 2 a ^ { \prime } \ \delta n ^ { \prime } = \ - 3 n ^ { \prime } \ \delta a ^ { \prime } = \ - 3 n ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } A \cos \theta , } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { \displaystyle \delta V ^ { \prime } = \int \delta n ^ { \prime } \ \delta t = \ - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \rlap / p A \sin \theta . } } \end{array}
$$

Combining these, we obtain approximately

$$
\delta v = \delta M _ { 0 } = - { \textstyle \frac { 4 } { 2 7 } } \delta V ^ { \prime } .
$$


<!-- chunk 0011: pages 401-440 -->
A complete investigation of the inequalities produced by the direct and indirect action of the planets has been made by Newcomb (1895), criticizing Neison's (1877) work,and by Radau (1892, l895) and by Brown (1904a,1908a,1908b).

A theory of general planetary perturbations in the motions of natural planetary satellites has been developed by Musen (1969) on the basis of a modified Hansen's theory (Musen, 1963；Section 10.10).Musen considered the outer Jovian satellites.The direct solar effects are dominant. Two types of planetary effects are included: (i) the direct planetary effects caused by the direct perturbation of Saturn on the satellite and (ii) the indirect effects caused by the deviation of the solar motion from its Keplerian approximation. Naturally, terms affected by small divisors appear in the integration process.The indirect effects must be included in the solar part of the disturbing function.For including the direct planetary effects necessary changes are made in the formulation of the problem.The basic small parameter in front of the solar part of the disturbing function is larger than that for the planetary part, which means that the influence of the mutual perturbations of Jupiter and Saturn is greater in the indirect part than in the direct planetary perturbations of the satellite.Under this influence the mean argument of the perigee and the mean longitude of the ascending node of the satellite contain terms quadratic in time.Musen applied to these his numerical theory,a modification of Hansen's (Musen,196la; Section 10.10).

# II.IO INEQUALITY DUE TO THE EARTH'S FIGURE

Inequalities arising from the figure of the earth have been studied by Hill (1884a) equally by means of Delaunay's theory.

Let $A , B , C$ be the moments of inertia of the earth about three rectangular axes at the center of mass of the earth, and $\pmb { I }$ be the moment of inertia about the line drawn through the center of mass of the earth and that of the moon. The difference between the earth and a spherically symmetrical body of equal mass in the attraction on the moon produces a potential (Plummer, 1918)

$$
( A + B + C - 3 I ) / ( 3 r ^ { 3 } ) .
$$

We suppose that one principal axis of the earth is the polar axis, and that the moments of inertia about the other two axes are approximately equal.in the accuracy needed at present, i.e., $A = B$ ，(although recent observations of artificial earth satellites reveal that this is not strictly true;see Section 8.6). If $d$ is the declination of the moon, then we have

$$
I = A \cos ^ { 2 } d + C \sin ^ { 2 } d .
$$

The new part to be added to the disturbing function is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \delta R = \frac 1 { 2 r ^ { 3 } } ( 2 A + C - 3 A \cos ^ { 2 } d - 3 C \sin ^ { 2 } d ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { = \frac { C - A } { 2 r ^ { 3 } } ( 1 - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } d ) = \frac { \mu k ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac 1 3 - \sin ^ { 2 } d \right) , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\mu k ^ { \prime } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( C - A ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { \mu }$ is the sum of the masses of the earth and moon.If the earth's surface is supposed to be an equipotential surface, then it is known (Tisserand,1894) that

$$
\mu k ^ { \prime } = m _ { E } R ^ { 2 } ( \alpha - \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ) ,
$$

where $m _ { E }$ is the earth's mass, $\pmb { R }$ its equatorial radius, $\pmb { \alpha }$ its ellipticity, and $\beta$ the ratio of the centrifugal force to the gravity at the equator.

Let $_ { x }$ in Figure 3 be the ascending node of the ecliptic on the equator and ${ \pmb { \omega } } _ { 1 }$ be the inclination of the two planes. Let $z ^ { \prime }$ be the pole of the equatorial plane; then

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { z z ^ { \prime } = \omega _ { 1 } , ~ } } & { { ~ z ^ { \prime } M = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - d , } } \\ { { z M = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - U , ~ } } & { { ~ z ^ { \prime } z M = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - v . } } \end{array}
$$

The triangle $z ^ { \prime } z M$ gives

$$
. d = \sin U \cos \omega _ { 1 } + \cos U \sin \omega _ { 1 } \sin v ,
$$

$$
U = \sin I \sin L
$$

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0011_pages_0401-0440/ocr/images/6e86e73916ab7eb2f915e1c0b619d5c425bff98ccde041ce5672ff57e8c1ddee.jpg)

![](../chunks/Celestial Mechanics - Volume 2 Part 2 Peturbation Theory (Yusuke Hagihara) (z-library.sk,_part_0011_pages_0401-0440/ocr/images/4cd8434dfd8f1c617050cfea301cb6b8caaa5ace1de6e0b3ad3b8d03d023dc00.jpg)  
FIGURE 4

and also (Figure 4)

Hence,

$$
\cos U \sin v = \cos ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin ( L + h ) - \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin ( L - h ) .
$$

Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { d = \cos { \omega _ { 1 } } \sin { I } \sin { L } + \cos ^ { 2 } { \displaystyle \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin { \omega _ { 1 } } \sin { ( L + h ) } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - \sin ^ { 2 } { \displaystyle \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin { \omega _ { 1 } } \sin { ( L - h ) } } } \end{array}
$$

Put sin $( I / 2 ) = \gamma$ and neglect higher powers of $\gamma$ ; then,

$$
\sin d = \sin \omega _ { 1 } \sin ( L + h ) + 2 \gamma \cos \omega _ { 1 } \sin L .
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle = \frac { \mu k ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } - \sin ^ { 2 } d \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { \mu k ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { 3 } } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { 3 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 1 } \cos 2 ( L + h ) \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left. - \gamma \sin 2 \omega _ { 1 } [ \cos h - \cos ( 2 L + h ) ] \right\} } } \end{array}
$$

where the elliptic values are substituted for $r$ and $L$

The terms in $\delta R$ that produce the largest coefficients are those of long period.There is only one such term with argument $h$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \delta R = - \mu k ^ { \prime } \Bigl ( \frac { \gamma } { a ^ { 3 } } \Bigr ) \sin 2 \omega _ { 1 } \cos h . } \end{array}
$$

We have,as in the inequalities due to the planetary action,

$$
\dot { p } = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { h _ { 0 } } = - 1 8 . 6 0 ,
$$

$$
A ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { } - \phi \frac { \mu } { a ^ { 3 } } \frac { k ^ { \prime } \gamma } { n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { 2 } } \sin 2 \omega _ { 1 } = 1 8 . 6 0 \frac { n ^ { 2 } } { n ^ { \prime 2 } } \frac { k ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { 2 } } \gamma \sin 2 \omega _ { 1 } ;
$$

hence,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \delta a = \delta e = 0 , \qquad \quad \delta \gamma = - 0 . 4 1 4 A ^ { \prime } \cos { h } , } } \\ { { \delta M _ { 0 } = + 0 . 6 9 0 A ^ { \prime } \sin { h } , \qquad \quad \delta l = + 1 . 1 1 8 A ^ { \prime } \sin { h } , } } \\ { { \delta h = 9 . 2 8 6 A ^ { \prime } \sin { h } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hill (1884),in his discussion of pendulum observations over the earth's surface of those days,obtained

$$
{ \frac { k ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { 2 } } } \sin 2 \omega _ { 1 } = 0 \mathrm { : 0 7 2 } \ 8 5 4 .
$$

The corresponding inequalities in the coordinates are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \delta v = \delta M _ { 0 } = 7 \mathrm { { ' } } 5 8 \sin h \qquad \delta U = - 8 \mathrm { { ' } } 7 9 \sin ( h + g + l ) } \\ { [ 7 \mathrm { { ' } } 3 1 7 \mathrm { { ~ B r o w n } } ] , \qquad [ 8 \mathrm { { ' } } 3 5 5 \mathrm { { ~ B r o w n } } ] \qquad } \\ { - 0 \mathrm { { ' } } 3 1 \sin ( g + l - h ) . } \end{array}
$$

The arguments are the longitude of the node and the mean longitude. The period is l8.6 years and one mean sidereal month. These numerical values should be revised on the basis of the observations of artificial earth satellites.

Owing to the planetary action, the plane of the earth's orbit is not fixed.It is the cause of the planetary precession. The motion of the ecliptic affects the motion of the moon when the latter motion is referred to the instantaneous position of the moving ecliptic.The effect was first noticed by Airy in 1854 (Tisserand,1894).Adams (188l),Hill (1884), Radau (1892）and,of course,Brown (1904b,1908,1908b) studied the effect (see Brown,1896).

New data on the ellipticity of the earth obtained from the artificial satellite observations make it necessary to revise this whole theory to some minor extent.

# II.IIADAMS'S THEOREM

Adams (l877a) considered the constant term in the reciprocal of the moon's radius vector,and showed that the constant part of the lunar parallax with the factors $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ is identically zero and that the coefficients of $e ^ { 4 }$ $e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 4 }$ are connected by two ratios to the four coefficients of $e ^ { 2 }$ $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ in the expressons for the mean motions of the perigee and node. Besides the terms factored by powers of ${ \mathbf { } } a / a ^ { \prime }$ , the nonperiodic part of the expression of $a / r$ is written in the form

$$
A + B e ^ { 2 } + C \gamma ^ { 2 } + E e ^ { 4 } + 2 F e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where the coefficients $A , B , C , E , F , G , \dots$ are functions of $e ^ { \prime 2 }$ ；for example,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { A = A _ { 0 } + A _ { 1 } e ^ { \prime 2 } + A _ { 2 } e ^ { \prime 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \quad B = B _ { 0 } + B _ { 1 } e ^ { \prime 2 } + B _ { 2 } e ^ { \prime 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { C = C _ { 0 } + C _ { 1 } e ^ { \prime 2 } + C _ { 2 } e ^ { \prime 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}
$$

$A _ { i } , B _ { i } , C _ { i } , \ldots$ ，being expanded in powers of $\mathbf { m }$ . Adams's first theorem states that $B _ { 0 } = C _ { 0 } = 0 _ { \mathrm { { : } } }$ ， $B _ { i } = C _ { i } = 0$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \dots )$ .Hence there is no term factored by $e ^ { 2 } e ^ { \prime 2 p }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 } e ^ { \prime 2 a }$ in the expansions, where $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } , \pmb { q }$ are integers.

Let $x , y , z , \tau$ and $\pmb { x } ^ { \prime } , \pmb { y } ^ { \prime } , 0 , \pmb { r } ^ { \prime }$ be the coordinates of the moon and sun, respectively. Neglecting the terms producing the parallactic inequalities, we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \ddot { x } + \displaystyle \frac { \mu x } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { \prime } x } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } = \frac { 3 \mu ^ { \prime } x ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } ( x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ & { \ddot { y } + \displaystyle \frac { \mu y } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { \prime } y } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } = \frac { 3 \mu ^ { \prime } y ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } ( x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ & { \ddot { z } + \displaystyle \frac { \mu z } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \mu ^ { \prime } z } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Let $x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } , r _ { 1 } = \sqrt { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }$ be the coordinates of a fictitious moon with different initial conditions. The equations of motion for this fictitious moon are the same as those of the real moon. Combining these two sets of equations,we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mu ( x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } ) \displaystyle \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \right) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( x _ { 1 } \dot { x } - x \dot { x } _ { 1 } + y _ { 1 } \dot { y } - y \dot { y } _ { 1 } + z _ { 1 } \dot { z } - z \dot { z } _ { 1 } \right) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \mu z z _ { 1 } \displaystyle \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \right) = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( z _ { 1 } \dot { z } - z \dot { z } _ { 1 } \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus $( x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } ) [ ( 1 / r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } ) - ( 1 / r ^ { 3 } ) ]$ and $z z _ { 1 } [ ( 1 / r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } ) - ( 1 / r ^ { 3 } ) ]$ are the derivatives of a function that can be expanded in series proceeding

with sines or cosines of multiples of the argument $\alpha t + \beta$ without a constant term. Since

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \colon _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ 2 r r _ { 1 } + ( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( x - x _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { } & { \qquad - ( y - y _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( z - z _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } ] , } \\ & { \quad \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } = \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) \Big [ \frac { 3 } { r r _ { 1 } } + \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \Big ] , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \big ( x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } \big ) \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \Big ) } } \\ { \displaystyle { \qquad = 3 \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) + r x _ { 1 } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) } } \\ { \displaystyle { \qquad \times \ [ ( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( x - x _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( y - y _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( z - z _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } ] } } \\ { \displaystyle { \qquad \times \ \Big [ \frac { 3 } { r r _ { 1 } } + \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \Big ] } \ . } \end{array}
$$

Hence $x - x _ { 1 } , y - y _ { 1 } , z - z _ { 1 } , ( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) - ( 1 / r )$ can be considered as of the first order, and

$$
( x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } ) ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } ) - 3 ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } )
$$

as of the third order at least. The same can be applied to each coefficient of the expansions in series proceeding with sines or cosines of multiples of $\alpha t + \beta$ ,and, in particular, can be applied to the nonperiodic part of the expansion. Since the nonperiodic part of

$$
( x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } ) \left[ { \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } } \right]
$$

is zero, the nonperiodic part of $( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r )$ is at least of the third order.

Now the coordinates of the real moon and the fictitious moon, from Hill's result (Section 11.2),can be expressed as

Real: $\begin{array} { r l } & { x = u \cos { ( n t + \epsilon ) } - v \sin { ( n t + \epsilon ) } , } \\ & { y = u \sin { ( n t + \epsilon ) } + v \cos { ( n t + \epsilon ) } , } \\ & { x _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } \cos { ( n t + \epsilon ) } - v _ { 1 } \sin { ( n t + \epsilon ) } , } \\ & { y _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } \sin { ( n t + \epsilon ) } + v _ { 1 } \cos { ( n t + \epsilon ) } , } \end{array}$ Fictitious:

with the same values of $a , \epsilon ,$ and $\pmb { n }$ .They differ only in the values of $\pmb { \ell }$ and $\gamma$ Since

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = u ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } , \qquad x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } = u u _ { 1 } + v v _ { 1 } , } } \end{array}
$$

the expression $( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( x - x _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( y - y _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( z - z _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 }$ can be replaced by $( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( u - u _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( v - v _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( z - z _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 }$ . The trigonometric expansions of $x , y$ depend on five arguments,while those of $u , v$ depend on only four. It is known that $a / r$ and $\pmb { u }$ can be expanded in cosine series of the argument $2 i \pm { j \phi } \pm { j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } } \pm 2 k \eta .$ and $v$ in sine series of the same argument, the coefficients being of the form $e ^ { j } e ^ { \prime j ^ { \prime } } \gamma ^ { 2 k }$ arranged in powers of $\mathbf { m } ^ { 2 }$ ， $e ^ { 2 } , e ^ { \prime 2 } , \cdot$ $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ .The quantity $z$ is expressed in sine series of the argument $2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \pm ( 2 k + 1 ) \eta ,$ the coefficients being of the form $e ^ { j } e ^ { \prime \ j ^ { \prime } } \gamma ^ { 2 k + 1 }$

Suppose that $x , y , z$ correspond to $e = 0$ ， $\gamma = 0$ ; and that $x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 }$ correspond to $\epsilon \neq 0$ ， $\gamma = 0$ . Then we have $z = z _ { 1 } = 0$ and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac 1 { r _ { 1 } } = \sum \left( \mathcal { A } _ { 0 } + \mathcal { A } _ { 1 } e ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { A } _ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right) \cos { \left( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \right) } } \\ { \displaystyle \pm \sum \mathcal { B } e ^ { j } \cos { \left( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \right) } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac 1 { r } = \sum \mathcal { A } _ { 0 } \cos { ( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } ) } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } = \displaystyle \sum \left( \mathcal { A } _ { 1 } e ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right) \cos { \left( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \right) } } \\ { + \displaystyle \sum \mathcal { B } e ^ { j } \cos { \left( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \right) } . } \end{array}
$$

Similar expansions are valid for $\boldsymbol { r } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { u } - \boldsymbol { u } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } _ { 1 }$ .Then, from (67), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \bigg \{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \right) [ ( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( u - u _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( v - v _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { } & { \times \left[ \frac { 1 } { r r _ { 1 } } + \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \bigg \} . } \end{array}
$$

From similar expressions for $\boldsymbol { u } - \boldsymbol { u } _ { 1 }$ and $\boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } _ { 1 }$ we see that the constant part of $( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r )$ should contain at least $e ^ { 3 }$ as a factor. The constant parts of $1 / r$ and $1 / r _ { 1 }$ are of the form

$$
A + B e ^ { 2 } + C \gamma ^ { 2 } + E e ^ { 4 } + 2 F e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Put $\gamma = 0$ . Then the constant part of the expansion of $( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r )$ is

$$
( A + B e ^ { 2 } + E e ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) - A = B e ^ { 2 } + E e ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

This expression should contain $e$ at least to the power $e ^ { 3 }$ . Hence,we should have $\mathcal { B } = 0$ .Since this does not depend on the values of m and $e ^ { \prime }$ ， we should have $B _ { 1 } = B _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0$

Next suppose that $x , y$ $_ z$ correspond to $e = \gamma = 0$ and that $x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 }$ correspond to $e = 0$ ， $\gamma \neq 0$ .Then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { \mathrm { i } } { r _ { 1 } } = \sum \big ( \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } + \mathcal { C } _ { 1 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { C } _ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \big ) \cos \big ( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \big ) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \sum \mathcal { D } \gamma ^ { 2 k } \cos \big ( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \pm  } \\ { \displaystyle  \frac { 1 } { r } = \sum \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } \cos \big ( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \big ) , \qquad } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } = \sum \left( \mathcal { C } _ { 1 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { C } _ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right) \cos { \left( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \right) } } & { } \\ { + \displaystyle \sum \mathcal { D } \gamma ^ { 2 k } \cos { ( 2 i \xi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \pm 2 k \eta ) } } & { \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : } \end{array}
$$

Hence $( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r )$ is factored by $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ . Similar expansions are obtained for $r - r _ { 1 } , u - u _ { 1 } , v - v _ { 1 } ;$ and $z _ { 1 }$ is factored by $\gamma$ From (67) we obtain

$3 ~ \times$ constat par of $\left( { \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { r } } \right)$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \mathrm { p a r t } \mathrm { o f } \{ \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } ) [ ( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( u - u _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 }  } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad - ( v - v _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times [ \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { r r _ { 1 } } + ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } ) ^ { 2 } ] \} \cdot \quad ( 6 9 ) } } \end{array}
$$

The first part of the right-hand member is factored by $\gamma ^ { 6 }$ ，and the second part by $\gamma ^ { 4 }$ . The constant part of $( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r )$ is, on the other hand,

$$
( A + C \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) - A = C \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Hence we should have $C = 0$ ； accordingly $C _ { 1 } = C _ { 2 } = \cdots = 0$ This completes the first part of Adams's theorem.

Adams computed directly

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \mathrm { ~ } } & { { } = \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 2 5 } { 1 2 8 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 3 } , \quad } & { F = \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 6 3 } { 1 6 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 3 } , \quad } & { G = - \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 9 } { 8 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 3 } , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { 1 + \cdot \cdot \cdot + e ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { 3 } { 8 } { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 6 7 5 } { 6 4 } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } \Big ) + \gamma ^ { 2 } \Big ( 6 { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 8 9 } { 8 } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } \Big ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { d \phi } { n d \iota } } & { } & \\ { 1 + \cdot \cdot \cdot + e ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { 3 } { 2 } { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 8 9 } { 3 2 } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } \Big ) } & { } & \\ { + \gamma ^ { 2 } \Big ( - \frac { 3 } { 2 } { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 7 } { 1 6 } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } \Big ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { = \frac { d \eta } { n d \iota } } & { } & \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { c = 1 + \cdot \cdot \cdot + H e ^ { 2 } + K \gamma ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { g = 1 + \cdot \cdot \cdot + M e ^ { 2 } + N \gamma ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { H = \mathrm { \frac { 3 } { 8 } } { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { \frac { 6 7 5 } { 6 4 } } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } , \quad } } & { { K = 6 { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { \frac { 1 8 9 } { 8 } } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } , } } \\ { { M = \mathrm { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { \frac { 1 8 9 } { 3 2 } } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } , \quad } } & { { N = \mathrm { \ - \frac { 3 } { 2 } } { \bf m } ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { \frac { 2 7 } { 1 6 } } { \bf m } ^ { 3 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Comparing with the expressions for $E , ~ F , ~ G$ we see that we have approximately,

$$
\frac { H } { E } = \frac { K } { F } , ~ \frac { M } { F } = \frac { N } { G } .
$$

Adams proved that these relations hold rigorously even when we take higher-order terms into account. This constitutes the second part of Adams's theorem.

Suppose that $x , \ y , \ z$ correspond to $\ell \neq 0 , \gamma = 0$ ; and $x _ { 1 } , ~ y _ { 1 } , ~ z _ { 1 }$ correspond to $e = e _ { 1 } \neq 0 .$ ， $\gamma \neq 0$ .We determine $\gamma \ s o$ that $\phi _ { 1 }$ ，and accordingly $\pmb { c _ { 1 } }$ ,is the same for the two moons.From the above formula for $\pmb { c }$ we obtain

$$
H e ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = H e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + K \gamma ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Hence $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ is factored by $e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 }$ . The expansion for $1 / r _ { 1 }$ is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac 1 { r _ { 1 } } = \sum [ \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } ^ { _ { ( 1 ) } } + \mathcal { C } _ { 1 } ^ { _ { ( 1 ) } } \gamma ^ { 2 }  + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] \cos ( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } ) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \sum \mathcal { F } ^ { _ { ( k ) } } \gamma ^ { 2 k } \cos ( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } + 2 k \eta ) \qquad k } \end{array}
$$

Replacing $\gamma$ by $0 , e _ { 1 }$ by $e , \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ by $\mathcal { C } _ { 0 }$ ， without touching $\xi , \phi , \phi ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime } , { \bf m } ,$ we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { r } = \sum \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } \cos { ( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } ) } .
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle - \frac { 1 } { r } = \sum [ \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } ^ { ( 1 ) } - \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } - \mathcal { C } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \gamma ^ { 2 }  + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] \cos ( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } ) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \sum \mathcal { F } ^ { ( k ) } \gamma ^ { 2 k } \cos ( 2 i \xi \pm j \phi \pm j ^ { \prime } \phi ^ { \prime } \pm 2 i \phi  } \end{array}
$$

where $\mathcal { C } _ { 0 } ^ { ( 1 ) } - \mathcal { C } _ { 0 } = \Theta ( e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) - \Theta ( e ^ { 2 } )$ is factored by $e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 }$ ,and the other terms by $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ ,and accordingly by $e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 }$ ,because of (70). Thus

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { r } } , \qquad r - r _ { 1 } , \qquad u - u _ { 1 } , \qquad v - v _ { 1 }
$$

are all factored by $e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 }$ . From (69) we see that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { r r _ { 1 } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ^ { 2 } , } & { \quad \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ( r - r _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } , \qquad \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ( u - u _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } , } \\ & { \quad \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ( v - v _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

are all factored by $( e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 } ) ^ { 3 }$ . The quantity $[ ( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r ) ] z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ is factored by $( e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 } ) \gamma ^ { 2 }$ or by $( e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 }$ because of (7O). Thus the constant part of $( 1 / r _ { 1 } ) \ : - \ : ( 1 / r )$ is factored by $( e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 }$ . However, since it is known that this constant part contains only even powers of $\pmb { e }$ and $\pmb { e _ { 1 } }$ ,it should be factored by $( e ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 }$ . On the other hand,we have

$$
\frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } = A + E e _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } + 2 F e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot +
$$

or, from (70),

$$
\frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } = A + E e _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } + \frac { 2 F H } { K } e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( e ^ { 2 } - e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) + G \frac { H ^ { 2 } } { K ^ { 2 } } ( e ^ { 2 } - e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Putting $e _ { 1 } = e _ { : }$ we find

$$
\frac { 1 } { r } = A + E e ^ { 4 } + \cdots .
$$

Hence,

$$
\frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } = E ( e _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } - e ^ { 4 } ) + \frac { 2 F H } { K } e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( e ^ { 2 } - e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { G H ^ { 2 } } { K ^ { 2 } } ( e ^ { 2 } - e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

+ periodic terms.

Since the constant part must be divisible by $( e ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 }$ ,the same should be true for $E ( e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + e ^ { 2 } ) \ : - \ : ( 2 F H / K ) e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ Hence we should have

$$
2 E e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 2 F H } { K } e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = 0 , \qquad \frac { H } { E } = \frac { K } { F } \cdot
$$

Suppose,finally,that $x , y , z$ correspond to $e = 0$ ， $\gamma \neq 0$ ，and that $x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 }$ correspond to $\epsilon \neq 0$ ， $\gamma = \gamma _ { 1 } \neq 0 .$ . By a similar process it can be shown that

$$
{ \frac { M } { F } } = { \frac { N } { G } } .
$$

Thus the second part of Adams's theorem is proved (Tisserand, 1894).

Brown (l896；cf. 1896a, 1896b) showed, in a general manner,as a generalization of Adams's theorem, that the constant part of the parallax is connected with the motion of the perigee and node.He gave a more definite form to the ratio of the four coefficients of $e ^ { 2 }$ and $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ in the expressions for the mean motions of the perigee and node in Adams's second theorem,and indeed in the form of four relations instead of two (Brown，l895)，the new factors introduced being functions of the coefficients of periodic terms.Further,he showed that the principal part of the acceleration of the mean motion that is independent of $\boldsymbol { e } , \gamma , \boldsymbol { a } / a ^ { \prime }$ can be obtained from the corresponding part of the constant term in the expression for the solar parallax. The basis is the consideration of the constant parts of various functions that appear in the solution obtained by varying arbitrary constants. There are arbitrary constants that must be determined from observation. There are also arbitrary constants that may be found accurately by observation but that depend on those constants previously found. The comparison of these two kinds of values forms an important test of the accuracy of the theoretical calculations.

Take the canonical variables (Sections 5.17)

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l } & { \phi _ { 1 } = L , } & & { \phi _ { 2 } = G - L , } & & { \phi _ { 3 } = H - G , } \\ & { q _ { 1 } = l + g + h , } & & { q _ { 2 } = g + h , } & & { q _ { 3 } = h , } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 2 } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 3 }$ are small quantities of the order $e ^ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { 2 }$ ，respectively,and $\pmb { q _ { 1 } }$ $\ 1 , \ q _ { 2 } , \ q _ { 3 }$ are the longitudes of the moon, the perigee, and the node, respectively, the nonperiodic parts being the mean values of these angles. Consider the equations of motion

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial R } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial R } { \partial q _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) ,
$$

where

$$
- R = \frac { ( m _ { E } + m _ { M } ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 L ^ { 2 } } + \Omega ,
$$

$: = m ^ { \prime } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - y ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( z - z ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } + z z ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } -$ Let $x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime }$ ， $z ^ { \prime }$ be the coordinates of the sun, and suppose that $z ^ { \prime } = 0$ ， and that $x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime }$ have their elliptic values. Then, by means of the principal function $s$ (Brown,1896a, 1896b),we obtain

$$
{ \bf \nabla } \cdot \mathrm {  ~ \nabla ~ } \ p _ { i } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad \frac { \partial S } { \partial t } = - R \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) .
$$

Thus we can express ${ p } _ { 1 } , { p } _ { 2 } , { p } _ { 3 }$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { } } & { { \displaystyle p _ { i } = c _ { i } + \sum s j _ { i } \cos N ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { \displaystyle N ^ { \prime } = j _ { \bar { \imath } } q _ { 1 } + j _ { 2 } q _ { 2 } + j _ { 3 } q _ { 3 } + j ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } t + \alpha , } } \end{array}
$$

where $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ are arbitrary constants; the coefficients $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ depend only on $c _ { 1 } , \ c _ { 2 } , \ c _ { 3 } , \ n ^ { \prime } , \ e ^ { \prime }$ ； the phases $\propto$ are absolute constants; and $j _ { 1 } , j _ { 2 } , j _ { 3 }$ are positive or negative integers or zero.Because of the relation

$$
\frac { \partial { \mathbf { \mathit { p } } _ { i } } } { \partial t } = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } S } { \partial { { q } _ { i } } \partial t } = \mathrm { - } \frac { \partial R } { \partial { { q } _ { i } } } ,
$$

all quantities are expressed in terms of $q _ { i } , c _ { i } , t .$ We have

$$
R = - B - \sum \acute { z } j ^ { \prime } m ^ { \prime } \cos N ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where $\mathcal { B }$ is a constant analogous to $\pmb { \mathscr { s } } .$ The solution for $s$ is

$$
{ \cal S } = c _ { 1 } q _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } q _ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } q _ { 3 } + B t + \sum s \sin N ^ { \prime } .
$$

By the Hamilton-Jacobi theory, the solution of the equations of motion is

$$
\dot { p _ { i } } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad l _ { i } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial c _ { i } } = q _ { i } + t \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { i } } + \sum \frac { \partial S } { \partial c _ { i } } \sin { N } ,
$$

where $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , l _ { 3 }$ are new arbitrary constants.

So far, we have referred to Delaunay's theory. We see that

$$
l _ { i } - t \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { i } }
$$

are the nonperiodic parts of $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , q _ { 3 }$ Hence $- \partial B / \partial c _ { 1 } , \ \partial B / \partial c _ { 2 } , \ \partial B / \partial c _ { 3 }$ are the mean motions of the moon, the perigee, and the node, respectively.As in Delaunay's theory, $\boldsymbol { B }$ represents the constant remainder of the disturbing function after all periodic terms have been eliminated. Thus $\displaystyle c _ { 1 } , c _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } , c _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } , B$ are the same as Delaunay’s $L , G , H , R ,$ respectively,after the completion of his operations (Section 9.1),so that they are the constant parts of Delaunay's initial values $L , G , H ,$ and $\scriptstyle R$ ， expressed in terms of $c _ { i } , \ q _ { i } ,$ and time.

Suppose that there is one unknown coefficient $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ in each periodic term. Using (7l） we substitute these values of $\pmb { \hat { p } _ { i } }$ in $\pmb { R }$ and equate the result to $R$ in (72). Then $\pmb { B }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ are given in terms of $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 } , n ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime 2 }$ . When these are substituted in (74)and the series obtained are inverted,then $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , q _ { 3 }$ are given in terms of the constants and time. Substituting these again in (71） the functions ${ p } _ { 1 } , { p } _ { 2 } , { p } _ { 3 }$ are given in terms of time. The next new canonical variables are $c _ { i }$ and $l _ { i } - t ( \partial B / \partial c _ { i } )$ . But it is not necessary to consider only one periodic term.We may take two or more terms at one operation,as in von Zeipel's theory (Section 9.19).

Denote by $b _ { 1 } , \ b _ { 2 } , \ b _ { 3 }$ the coefficients of $\pmb { t }$ in the nonperiodic parts of $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , q _ { 3 } ,$ ,respectively.We have

$$
b _ { i } = \mathrm { ~ - { \frac { \partial \cal B } { \partial c _ { i } } } ~ } ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) .
$$

Brown (1896) showed that

$$
b _ { 1 } c _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } c _ { 2 } + b _ { 3 } c _ { 3 } + B = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \bigg ( \frac { m _ { E } + m _ { M } } { r } \bigg ) _ { 0 } ,
$$

where $( Q ) _ { 0 }$ represents the nonperiodic part of any function $Q$ as expressed in terms of sines or cosines of time and the constants.

In fact, we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d S } { d t } = \sum _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { i } } + \frac { \partial S } { \partial t } } } \\ { { = \sum _ { i } \dot { p } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } - R . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\left( \frac { d S } { d t } \right) _ { 0 } = \left( \sum _ { i } \dot { p } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } \right) _ { 0 } - ( R ) _ { 0 } ,
$$

where the left-hand member is the coefficient of $\pmb { t }$ in the nonperiodic part of $s .$ If $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , q _ { 3 }$ are expressed in terms of time and substituted in (73),then the periodic terms $\sum s \sin { N ^ { \prime } }$ in (73） produce only periodic terms. The nonperiodic part of $q _ { i }$ is $l _ { i } + b _ { i } t .$ Hence the nonperiodic part of the coefficient of $\pmb { t }$ in $s$ is $b _ { 1 } c _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } c _ { 2 } + b _ { 3 } c _ { 3 } + B .$ .Thus we have

$$
\left( \sum _ { i } \beta _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } \right) _ { 0 } = b _ { 1 } c _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } c _ { 2 } + b _ { 3 } c _ { 3 } + B + ( R ) _ { 0 } .
$$

The disturbing function is represented by

$$
R = - \Omega - \frac { \kappa ^ { 2 } } { 2 / { p } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , \qquad \kappa = m _ { E } + m _ { M } ,
$$

and the equations of motion are

$$
\ddot { x } = \frac { \partial } { \partial x } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } + \Omega \right) , \qquad \ddot { y } = \frac { \partial } { \partial y } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } + \Omega \right) , \qquad \ddot { z } = \frac { \partial } { \partial z } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } + \Omega \right) .
$$

The solar parallax is known to occur in the constant parts only to the order $m ^ { 2 } a ^ { 2 } / a ^ { \prime 2 }$ ， which can be neglected. As a matter of fact, it actually amounts to less than O"OOl per century per century in the secular acceleration. Thus we have

$$
\Omega = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \left[ \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } + z z ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \right] .
$$

Hence,

$$
\pmb { x } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial x } + y \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial y } + z \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial z } = 2 \Omega .
$$

Multiply the equations of motion respectively by $x , y , z ,$ and add; then from this relation we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } \left( r ^ { 2 } \right) - 2 T = - \frac { \kappa } { r } + 2 \Omega , 2 T = \dot { x } ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } ^ { 2 } + \dot { z } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Since the first term cannot produce any constant term, we see that

$$
2 ( T ) _ { 0 } = \left( { \frac { \kappa } { r } } \right) _ { 0 } - 2 ( \Omega ) _ { 0 } .
$$

From the formulas for elliptic motion we have

$$
T = \frac { \kappa } { r } - \frac { \kappa ^ { 2 } } { 2 / \frac { 2 } { 3 } } ,
$$

or

$$
T - R = \frac { \kappa } { r } + \Omega .
$$

Hence

$$
( R ) _ { 0 } + 2 ( \Omega ) _ { 0 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } .
$$

The coefficients of the elliptic values of $x , y , z$ are expressed in terms of ${ p } _ { 1 } , { p } _ { 2 } , { p } _ { 3 }$ only,and, indeed,as homogeneous functions. By definition, the $\beta _ { i }$ are of dimension $\mathrm { [ m a s s ] ^ { 1 / 2 } [ l e n g t h ] ^ { 1 / 2 } }$ .Hence $x , y , z$ are of the same dimension as $[ \hbar ] ^ { 2 }$ ,the mass factor being excluded. Accordingly, $\Omega$ is of the order $[ \phi ] ^ { 4 }$ .Thus,

$$
p _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial { p } _ { 1 } } + p _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial { p } _ { 2 } } + p _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial { p } _ { 3 } } = 4 \Omega .
$$

From (77), we have

$$
\sum _ { i } \dot { p _ { i } } \dot { q } _ { i } = \sum _ { i } \dot { p _ { i } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \dot { p _ { i } } } = \frac { \kappa ^ { 2 } } { \dot { p } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - 4 \Omega = - 6 \Omega - 2 R .
$$

Hence,

$$
\displaystyle \left( \sum _ { i } \dot { p _ { i } } \dot { q _ { i } } \right) _ { 0 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } + \mathbf { \Sigma } ( R ) _ { 0 } .
$$

Thus,from (76) we obtain Brown's relation

$$
b _ { 1 } c _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } c _ { 2 } + b _ { 3 } c _ { 3 } + B = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } .
$$

Replace $b _ { 1 } , \ b _ { 2 } , \ b _ { 3 }$ by $n , \ \pi _ { 1 } , \ \theta _ { 1 ; }$ ，respectively. Then, Brown's relation (78) becomes

$$
n c _ { 1 } + \pi _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } + \theta _ { 1 } c _ { 3 } + B = { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \left( { \frac { \kappa } { r } } \right) _ { 0 } ,
$$

and (75) takes the form

$$
\pi = - { \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { 1 } } } , \qquad \pi _ { 1 } = - { \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { 2 } } } , \qquad \theta _ { 1 } = - { \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { 3 } } } .
$$

Suppose that these quantities are expressd as functions of $n , e ^ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { 2 } , n ^ { \prime } ;$ $e ^ { \prime 2 }$ ， $\kappa .$ .Differentiate (78a) with respect to $n , e ^ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { 2 }$ ; then, since

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { \partial B } { \partial n } = \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial c _ { 1 } } { \partial n } + \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial c _ { 2 } } { \partial n } + \frac { \partial B } { \partial c _ { 3 } } \frac { \partial c _ { 3 } } { \partial n } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = - n \frac { \partial c _ { 1 } } { \partial n } - \pi _ { 1 } \frac { \partial c _ { 2 } } { \partial n } - \theta _ { 1 } \frac { \partial c _ { 3 } } { \partial n } , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { c _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n } + c _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial n } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } , } \\ & { } & { c _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } + c _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } , } \\ & { } & { c _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } + c _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } . } \end{array}
$$

Let $\kappa = n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 }$ ,and write as in Adams

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \left( \frac { a } { r } \right) _ { 0 } = A + B e ^ { 2 } + C \gamma ^ { 2 } + E e ^ { 4 } + 2 F e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \pi _ { 1 } = n ( P + H e ^ { 2 } + K \gamma ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) , } } \\ { { \theta _ { 1 } = n ( T + M e ^ { 2 } + N \gamma ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $A , B , . . . , P , H , . . . , T , M , . . .$ are functions of $\mathbf { m }$ and $e ^ { \prime 2 }$ only. If these expressions are substituted in the second and third equations of (79) and various powers of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ are equated, then, since $c _ { 2 3 } \ c _ { 3 }$ contain respectively $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ as factors in all of their terms, we should have at frst $B = 0$ ， $C = 0$ .This is the first part of Adams's theorem.Then, from the coefficients of $e ^ { 4 } , e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { 4 }$ ，we see that

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { c _ { 2 } H = { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } n a ^ { 2 } E e ^ { 2 } , } } & { { \quad c _ { 3 } M = { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } n a ^ { 2 } F \gamma ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { c _ { 2 } K = { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } n a ^ { 2 } F e ^ { 2 } , } } & { { \quad c _ { 3 } N = { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } n a ^ { 2 } G \gamma ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,after the elimination of $c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 } ,$ we finally obtain

$$
\frac { E } { F } = \frac { H } { K } , \qquad \frac { F } { G } = \frac { M } { N } ,
$$

which is.the second part of Adams's theorem.

The first equation of (79) can be written, if all powers of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ are neglected,

$$
c _ { \mathbf { i } } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial n } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } .
$$

If only the first powers of $e ^ { 2 }$ $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ are considered, then the same equation, combined with Adams's frst theorem, gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \mathrm { c o e f f i c i e n t ~ o f } \ : e ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ i n ~ } c _ { 1 } = - c _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { c o e f f i c i e n t ~ o f } \ : \gamma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ i n ~ } c _ { 2 } = - c _ { 3 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n } . } } \end{array}
$$

Newcomb (1895) showed, further, that the secular acceleration may be obtained by means of the equations

$$
\delta c _ { 1 } = 0 , \qquad \delta c _ { 2 } = 0 , \qquad \delta c _ { 3 } = 0 ,
$$

where $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ are expressed in terms of $n , e ^ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { 2 } , n ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime 2 } , \kappa$ A variation $\delta e ^ { \prime 2 }$ due to the planetary action induces the variations $\delta n , \ \delta e ^ { 2 } , \ \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }$ Then the secular acceleration is given by

$$
\int \delta n d t , \qquad \int \delta \pi _ { 1 } d t , \qquad \int \delta \theta _ { 1 } d t .
$$

Applying the variation δ to the three equations (79), we have, since $\delta c _ { 1 } = \delta c _ { 2 } = \delta c _ { 3 } = 0 _ { : }$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { c _ { 2 } \delta \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n } + c _ { 3 } \delta \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \delta \frac { \partial } { \partial n } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { c _ { 2 } \delta \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } + c _ { 3 } \delta \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \delta \frac { \partial } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { c _ { 2 } \delta \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } + c _ { 3 } \delta \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \delta \frac { \partial } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The presence of the factor $e ^ { 2 }$ in $c _ { 2 }$ or $\delta e ^ { 2 }$ ,and of the factor $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ in $c _ { 3 }$ or $\delta \gamma ^ { 2 }$ ， enables us to compute the principal parts of $\delta n$ ， $\delta \pi _ { 1 } , \delta \theta _ { 1 }$ when $\pmb { \pi _ { 1 } }$ ， 0, $( \kappa / r ) _ { 0 }$ are known.

If all powers of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ are neglected, then the first of these equations gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \delta \frac { \partial } { \partial n } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\delta n = \bigg [ \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial n \partial e ^ { \prime 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } \bigg ] \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } \div \bigg [ \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial n ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } \bigg ] ,
$$

which gives the part of $\delta n$ independent of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ . When $\delta n$ is found, the corresponding parts of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ are given by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \delta \pi _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n } ~ \delta n + \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } } ~ \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \delta \theta _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n } ~ \delta n + \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } } ~ \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

If all powers of $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ above the first are neglected,we obtain, by the first part of Adams's theorem,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { c _ { 2 } \displaystyle [ \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n ^ { 2 } } ( \delta n ) _ { e ^ { 0 } } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n \partial e ^ { \prime 2 } } \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } ] = \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( \delta n ) _ { e ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial n ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } , } } \\ { { c _ { 3 } \displaystyle [ \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n ^ { 2 } } ( \delta n ) _ { e ^ { 0 } } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n \partial e ^ { \prime 2 } } \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } ] = \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( \delta n ) _ { \gamma ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial n ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \kappa } { r } \right) _ { 0 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $( \delta n ) _ { e ^ { \downarrow } } , ( \delta n ) _ { \gamma ^ { \downarrow } }$ denote respectively the parts of $\delta { \boldsymbol { n } }$ which are factored by $e ^ { i } , \gamma ^ { i }$ . These equations give the terms factored by $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ in $\delta n$ .However, this is similar to finding $\delta e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\delta \gamma ^ { 2 }$ from $\delta c _ { 2 } = 0 , \delta c _ { 3 } = 0 .$ ,and substituting in

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \delta \pi _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } \delta e ^ { 2 } + \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } + \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial n } \delta n + \frac { \partial \pi _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { \prime 2 } } \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \delta \theta _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { 2 } } \delta e ^ { 2 } + \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma ^ { 2 } } \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } + \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial n } \delta n + \frac { \partial \theta _ { 1 } } { \partial e ^ { \prime 2 } } \delta e ^ { \prime 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Brown (1895,1897) further extended Adams's theorem.Let

$$
x = X _ { q } , \qquad y = Y _ { q } , \qquad z = Z _ { q }
$$

be the general solution of the equations of motion when quantities in the coefficients are neglected beyond the order $e ^ { p } \gamma ^ { p - q }$ $\mathcal { , } ^ { p - q } \left( \phi = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . , q \right)$ · Furthermore, let

$$
X _ { q } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { q } x _ { i } , \qquad Y _ { q } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { q } y _ { i } , \qquad Z _ { q } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { q } z _ { i } ,
$$

$$
R _ { q } ^ { 2 } = X _ { q } ^ { 2 } + Y _ { q } ^ { 2 } + Z _ { q } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $x _ { q } , y _ { q }$ are of the form

$$
\alpha _ { q } e ^ { q } + \alpha _ { q - 2 } e ^ { q - 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + \alpha _ { q - 4 } e ^ { q - 4 } \gamma ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

and $z _ { q }$ is of the form

$$
\alpha _ { a - 1 } e ^ { q - 1 } \gamma + \alpha _ { q - 3 } e ^ { q - 3 } \gamma ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

The coeffcients $\alpha _ { q } , \alpha _ { q - 2 } , \ldots$ are functions of ${ \bf m } , e ^ { \prime }$ ， with the arguments

$$
2 \nu ( t - t _ { 0 } ) , \qquad n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } , \qquad c \nu ( t - t _ { 1 } ) , \qquad 2 g \nu ( t - t _ { 2 } ) .
$$

The coefficients $\alpha _ { q - 1 } , \alpha _ { q - 3 } , \ldots .$ are functions of ${ \bf m } , e ^ { \prime }$ ， with the arguments $2 \nu ( t - t _ { 0 } )$ ， $n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ ， $c \nu ( t - t _ { 1 } )$ , and odd multiples of $g \nu ( t - t _ { 2 } )$

Brown first proved that,if $x , y$ ， $z$ have been calculated to the order $2 q - 2$ ,then the constant part in $1 / { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } } }$ can be obtained to the order $2 q$ without further reference to the equations of motion. Denote by $C _ { 2 q } [ Q ]$ the constant part of the order $2 q$ in the expansion of $Q$ ； then the theorem states that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle C _ { 2 q } \biggl [ \frac { 3 } { R _ { 2 q } } \biggr ] = C _ { 2 q } \biggl [ \frac { 3 } { R _ { 2 q - 2 } } - \frac { x _ { 0 } X _ { 2 q - 2 } + y _ { 0 } Y _ { 2 q - 2 } } { R _ { 2 q - 3 } ^ { 3 } } } } \\ { { \qquad \qquad - ~ 3 \frac { z _ { 1 } Z _ { 2 q - 2 } } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 5 } } ~ ( x _ { 0 } x _ { 1 } + y _ { 0 } y _ { 1 } ) \biggr ] . } } \end{array}
$$

Put $q = 1$ ; then, since $z _ { 0 } = 1$ , this equation takes the form

$$
C \Big [ \frac { 3 } { R ^ { 2 } } \Big ] = C _ { 2 } \Big [ \frac { 3 } { R _ { 0 } } - \frac { x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } \Big ] = C _ { 2 } \Big [ \frac { 2 } { r _ { 0 } } \Big ] = 0 .
$$

Let $\gamma = 0$ ； then it shows that the term in $e ^ { 2 }$ is zero. Let $e = 0$ ; then it shows that the term in $\gamma ^ { 2 }$ is zero.It is remarked that $\boldsymbol { e } , \gamma$ occur in the constant part of $1 / R$ only in even powers, where $e ^ { \prime 2 }$ and its powers are included.This is the first theorem of Adams.

Let

$$
C _ { 4 } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { R } \Big ] = E e ^ { 4 } + 2 F e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } + G \gamma ^ { 4 } ,
$$

$$
C _ { 2 } [ ( c ) ] = H e ^ { 2 } + K \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad C _ { 2 } [ ( g ) ] = M e ^ { 2 } + N \gamma ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $E , F , G , H , K , M , N$ are functions of $\mathbf { m }$ ， $\scriptstyle { e ^ { \prime } }$ only.In order to extend Adams's second theorem, Brown (1903,1905a) considered from (65) and (56a)

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle e ^ { 2 } T _ { e } = 2 \frac { a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \kappa } \sum _ { j } \{ [ 2 j + 1 + \mathbf { m } + ( c ) ] ( \epsilon ) _ { j } ^ { 2 } } \ ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + ~ [ 2 j - 1 - \mathbf { m } + ( c ) ] ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) _ { - j } ^ { 2 } \} , } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \gamma ^ { 2 } T _ { \gamma } = 4 \frac { a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { \kappa } \sum _ { j } [ 2 j + ( g ) ] ( K ) _ { j } ^ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $T _ { e } , \ T _ { \gamma }$ are expressed in terms of the same eccentricity $\boldsymbol { e }$ and the latitude constant $\gamma$ as in $1 / R , ( c ) , ( g )$ .Brown showed at first the relations

$$
C _ { 4 } \Big [ { \frac { 6 } { R _ { 4 } } } \Big ] = C _ { 4 } \Big [ \frac { x _ { 1 } X _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } Y _ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } Z _ { 2 } } { ( R _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 z _ { 1 } z _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \Big ] ,
$$

$$
C _ { e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } } \bigg [ { \frac { x _ { 1 } X _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } Y _ { 2 } } { ( R _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 z _ { 1 } z _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } } \bigg ] = C _ { e ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } } \bigg [ { \frac { z _ { 1 } Z _ { 2 } } { R _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } } \bigg ] ,
$$

$$
C _ { e } { ^ \ 6 } \biggl [ { \frac { 9 } { R _ { 6 } } } \biggr ] = C _ { e ^ { 6 } } \biggl [ { \frac { 9 } { R _ { 4 } } } - 3 { \frac { x _ { 0 } X _ { 4 } + y _ { 0 } Y _ { 4 } } { R _ { 4 } ^ { 3 } } } \biggr ] ,
$$

and then

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { H T _ { e } = 6 E , } & { { } } & { M T _ { \gamma } = 6 F , } \\ { K T _ { e } = 6 F , } & { { } } & { N T _ { \gamma } = 6 G . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { T _ { e } } { T _ { y } } = \frac { M } { K } .
$$
