Thus Brown extended Adams's second theorem up to terms of higher orders,and then studied the secular acceleration of the moon's motion (Brown, 1897; Section 9.3).

# APPLICATION TO JUPITER'S SATELLITES

# II.I2FERRAZ MELLO'S THEORY

Consider the plane motion of the four Galilean satellites of Jupiter in a rotating coordinate system with angular velocity N:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \ddot { x } _ { i } - 2 \mathrm { N } \dot { y } _ { i } - \mathrm { N } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } = - k ^ { 2 } ( m _ { 0 } + m _ { i } ) { \frac { x _ { i } } { r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } } - \displaystyle \sum _ { j \neq i } k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } \Big ( { \frac { x _ { i } - x _ { j } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } } + { \frac { x _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } } \Big ) , } } \\ { { \ddot { y } _ { i } + 2 \mathrm { N } \dot { x } _ { i } - \mathrm { N } ^ { 2 } y _ { i } = - k ^ { 2 } ( m _ { 0 } + m _ { i } ) { \frac { y _ { i } } { r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } } - \displaystyle \sum _ { j \neq i } k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } \Big ( { \frac { y _ { i } - y _ { j } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } } + { \frac { y _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } } \Big ) , } } \end{array}
$$

Suppose that in this coordinate system the mean motions of the pairs of satellites Io-Europa and Europa-Ganymede are commensurable. Take

$$
n _ { i } = \nu _ { i } + \mathbf { N } , \qquad \nu _ { 1 } = 2 \nu _ { 2 } = 4 \nu _ { 3 } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { n _ { 1 } - 2 n _ { 2 } = \nu _ { 1 } - 2 \nu _ { 2 } - \aleph = 0 \binom { \circ } { \cdot } 7 3 9 4 6 9 0 9 \mathrm { p e r d a y } , } \\ & { n _ { 2 } - 2 n _ { 3 } = \nu _ { 2 } - 2 \nu _ { 3 } - \aleph = 0 \binom { \circ } { \cdot } 7 3 9 4 6 9 0 9 \mathrm { p e r d a y } , } \\ & { \qquad n _ { 1 } - 3 n _ { 2 } + 2 n _ { 3 } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and

Put $\mathrm { \bf \dot { \tau } _ { 1 } } = 4 , g _ { 2 } = 2 , g _ { 3 } = 1 , g _ { 4 } = 0 . 4 3 7 5 6 7 8 0 7 ; \mathrm { t h e n } ,$

$$
\mathbf { m } = \mathbf { N } / \nu _ { 3 } = - 0 . 0 1 4 4 8 3 1 7 4
$$

(Sections 9.10, 18.29-18.34).

In accordance with Hill, Ferraz Mello (1966,1969) put

$$
a _ { j } u _ { j } = x _ { j } + { \sqrt { - 1 } } y _ { j } , \qquad a _ { j } s _ { j } = x _ { j } - { \sqrt { - 1 } } y _ { j } ,
$$

and

$$
\zeta = \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \nu _ { 3 } t \} , D = \zeta \frac { d } { d \zeta } ;
$$

then the equations of motion are, with $\alpha _ { j i } = a _ { j } / a _ { i }$ ，

$$
+ \ 2 { \bf m } D + { \bf m } ^ { 2 } ) u _ { i } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( m _ { 0 } + m _ { i } ) } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { u _ { i } } { r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } + \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { i } } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { u _ { i } - \alpha _ { j i } u _ { j } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \alpha _ { j i } u _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \right)
$$

$$
- 2 { \bf m } D + { \bf m } ^ { 2 } ) s _ { i } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( m _ { 0 } + m _ { i } ) } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { s _ { i } } { r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } + \sum _ { j \ne i } \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { i } } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \biggl ( \frac { s _ { i } - \alpha _ { j i } s _ { j } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \alpha _ { j i } s _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \biggr )
$$

These equations are unaltered if we replace $u , s , t$ by $s , u , - t ,$ respectively. The solution is of the form

$$
u _ { i } = \sum _ { j } K _ { j } \zeta ^ { 2 j } , \qquad s _ { i } = \sum _ { j } K _ { j } \zeta ^ { - 2 j }
$$

for Io, for example. If we substitute these in the equations of motion, then the coefficients $K _ { j }$ will be calculated.Because of the difficulties of such calculation, Ferraz Mello abandoned the direct application of Hill's procedure and used the first integral of Laplace.

From the equations of motion in vector notation

$$
\dot { \mathbf { v } } = - \frac { k ^ { 2 } \mu } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r } , \qquad \mathbf { c } = \mathbf { r } \times \mathbf { v } ,
$$

where $\mu$ is the sum of the two masses and c is twice the angular momentum normal to the orbital plane,we have

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { c } \times { \dot { \mathbf { v } } } = - { \frac { k ^ { 2 } \mu } { r ^ { 2 } } } \left( \mathbf { r } \times \mathbf { v } \right) \times { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r } } } \\ & { \qquad = - { \frac { k ^ { 2 } \mu } { r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { \left( \mathbf { r } \cdot \mathbf { r } \right) \mathbf { v } - \mathbf { \rho } \left( \mathbf { v } \cdot \mathbf { r } \right) \mathbf { r } } { r } } } \end{array} }
$$

by the known vector identity, or

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { c } \times { \dot { \mathbf { v } } } = - { \frac { k ^ { 2 } \mu } { r ^ { 2 } } } \left( r { \dot { \mathbf { r } } } - { \dot { r } } \mathbf { r } \right) } \\ & { } \\ & { \qquad = - k ^ { 2 } \mu { \frac { d } { d t } } \left( { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r } } \right) . } \end{array} }
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \mathbf { c } \times \mathbf { v } \right) + k ^ { 2 } \mu \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r } \right) = 0 .
$$

Accordingly we obtain Laplace's integral (Section 10.4):

$$
\mathbf { c } \times \mathbf { v } + k ^ { 2 } \mu { \frac { \mathbf { r } } { r } } = - k ^ { 2 } \mu \mathbf { p } ,
$$

or

$$
{ \bf p } = - \frac { \bf r } { r } - \frac { c } { k ^ { 2 } \mu } ( { \bf k } \times { \bf v } ) , \qquad c = | { \bf r } \times { \bf v } | ,
$$

where $\mathbf { k }$ is the unit vector perpendicular to the orbital plane and $\mathbf { p }$ is a constant vector directed to the pericenter.

Decomposing $\mathbf { p }$ we write

$$
{ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } = { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { x } + \sqrt { - 1 } { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { y } , \qquad { \pmb { \mathscr { q } } } = { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { x } - \sqrt { - 1 } { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { y } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \phi = - \frac { a } { r } u \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } + \frac { \mathbf { m } } { g + \mathbf { m } } u \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } + \frac { 1 } { g + \mathbf { m } } \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } D u , } \\ & { \displaystyle q = - \frac { a } { r } s \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } + \frac { \mathbf { m } } { g + \mathbf { m } } s \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } - \frac { 1 } { g + \mathbf { m } } \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } D s , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\kappa _ { i } = \frac { { k ^ { 2 } } \mu } { a c { \nu _ { 3 } } } = \frac { \bf { N } } { \nu _ { 3 } } = g + { \bf { m } } .
$$

By means of these equations, the equations of motion (80) are transformed, with $m _ { 0 } = 1$ ,to

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \vartheta _ { \hat { \theta } _ { i } } + \kappa _ { \mathrm { i } } \bigg [ \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \frac { a _ { \mathrm { i } } } { r _ { i j } } ) \boldsymbol { \hat { \rho } } _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \frac { a _ { \mathrm { i } } } { r _ { i j } } ) ^ { 3 } q _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { 2 } \zeta ^ { 2 \mathrm { m } } + ( \frac { a _ { \mathrm { i } } } { r _ { i } } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { i } \zeta ^ { \mathrm { m } } \bigg ] } } \\ & { } & { = \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \eta _ { \mathrm { i } } ) u _ { i } \zeta ^ { \mathrm { m } } } { \kappa _ { \mathrm { i } } v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } + \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { \zeta ^ { \mathrm { m } } } { \kappa _ { \mathrm { i } } } \Re _ { \mathrm { i j } } , } \\ & { } & \\ { \operatorname* { \partial } _ { \mathcal { I } _ { i } } - \kappa _ { \mathrm { i } } \bigg [ \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \frac { a _ { \mathrm { i } } } { r _ { i j } } ) q _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \frac { a _ { \mathrm { i } } } { r _ { i j } } ) ^ { 3 } \boldsymbol { \hat { \rho } } _ { i } s _ { i } ^ { 2 } \zeta ^ { - 2 \mathrm { m } } + ( \frac { a _ { \mathrm { i } } } { r _ { i } } ) ^ { 2 } s _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { m } } \bigg ] } \\ & { } & { = - \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m _ { \mathrm { i } } ) s _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { m } } } { \kappa _ { i } v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } r _ { i } ^ { 3 } } - \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { m } } } { \kappa _ { i } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\widehat { \mathfrak { i } } _ { i j } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { u _ { i } - \alpha _ { j i } u _ { j } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \alpha _ { j i } u _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \right] , \qquad \mathfrak { S } _ { i j } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { s _ { i } - \alpha _ { j i } s _ { j } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { \alpha _ { j i } s _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \right] .
$$

To the zero-th order approximation, the four satellites move in uniform circular orbits with radii $a _ { i }$ .Take the initial epoch to be the one in which all four satellites are collinear.The solution is $u _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = { \sigma } _ { i } \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } , s _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = { \sigma } _ { i } \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } ;$ where $\sigma _ { i } = + 1$ in the case of Io and Ganymede, and $\sigma _ { i } = - 1$ in the case of Europa and Callisto.

For the first-order perturbation there appear, besides the first-order terms in the masses,terms independent of disturbing masses and of the first degree in eccentricities, which are of the same order as the disturbing masses.Put

$$
u _ { i } = \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } + \delta u _ { i } , s _ { i } = \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } + \delta s _ { i } ;
$$

then, from the equations (81),we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { D \ \delta u _ { i } = ( g _ { i } + \mathbf { m } ) \rlap / p _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } - \mathbf { m } ) \ \delta u _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } + \mathbf { m } ) \zeta ^ { 2 g _ { i } } \ \delta s _ { i } , } \\ & { D \ \delta s _ { i } = - ( g _ { i } + \mathbf { m } ) q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } - \mathbf { m } ) \ \delta s _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } + \mathbf { m } ) \zeta ^ { - 2 g _ { i } } \delta u _ { i } ; } \end{array}
$$

and, from the equations (82),

$$
D / { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 \kappa _ { i } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma _ { i } \kappa _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ( \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } \delta s _ { i } + \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } \delta u _ { i } )
$$

$$
{ \begin{array} { c } { = \lambda _ { i } \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } + \displaystyle \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { \zeta ^ { \kappa } } { \kappa _ { i } } \Re _ { i j } , } \\ { = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } \phi _ { i } \zeta ^ { - 2 \kappa _ { i } } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma _ { i } \kappa _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } ( \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } \delta s _ { i } + \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } \delta u _ { i } ) } \\ { = - \lambda _ { i } \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - \displaystyle \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { \zeta ^ { - \kappa } } { \kappa _ { i } } \widetilde { \circledast } _ { i j } , } \end{array} }
$$

where

$$
\lambda _ { i } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m _ { i } ) } { \kappa _ { i } \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } a _ { i } ^ { 3 } } - \kappa _ { i } .
$$

For $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = \pmb { q } = 0$ ， the equations (8la) reduce to linear homogeneous equations with periodic coefficients

$$
\frac { d \delta u _ { i } } { U } = \frac { d \delta s _ { i } } { S } = \frac { d \zeta } { Z } ,
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { l } { U = ( g _ { i } - { \bf m } ) \ \delta u _ { i } - ( g _ { i } + { \bf m } ) \zeta ^ { 2 g _ { i } } \delta s _ { i } , \quad } \\ { S = - ( g _ { i } - { \bf m } ) \ \delta s _ { i } + ( g _ { i } + { \bf m } ) \zeta ^ { - 2 g _ { i } } \delta u _ { i } , \quad \quad Z = 2 \zeta . } \end{array}
$$

Put $\hat { u } = \delta u _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } , \hat { s } = \delta s _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { g _ { i } }$ .If $\Phi ( \hat { u } , \hat { s } , \zeta )$ is an integral of this system of linear equations, then

$$
\kappa _ { i } ( \hat { s } + \hat { u } ) \bigg ( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial \hat { s } } - \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial \hat { u } } \bigg ) - 2 \zeta \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial \zeta } = 0 .
$$

The integral $\Phi ( { \hat { u } } , { \hat { s } } , \zeta ) = { \hat { u } } + { \hat { s } }$ is $\psi _ { 1 } ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta )$ in terms of the variables$\delta u _ { i } , \ \delta s _ { i } , \ \zeta$ ：

$$
c _ { i } = \psi _ { 1 } ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) = \delta u _ { i } . \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } + \delta s _ { i } . \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } .
$$

Eliminating $\delta { s _ { i } }$ by means of this integral, the first equation of (8la) becomes of the form

$$
2 \zeta ^ { 4 } d \ \delta u _ { i } - ( 2 g _ { i } \ \delta u _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - 5 } - c _ { i } \kappa _ { i } \zeta ^ { - 1 } ) \ d \zeta = 0 ,
$$

which admits the integral

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 2 \ \delta u _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } + c _ { i } \kappa _ { i } \log \ \zeta = c _ { i } ^ { \prime } ; } \end{array}
$$

and similarly the second equation of (8la) admits the integral

$$
2 ~ \delta s _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } - c _ { i } \kappa _ { i } ~ \mathrm { l o g } ~ \zeta = c _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } .
$$

These three integrals are related by $c _ { i } ^ { \prime } + c _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } = 2 c _ { i }$ Put $\gamma _ { i } = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( c _ { i } ^ { \prime } - c _ { i } ^ { \prime } )$ ， which is pure imaginary; then the integral is

$$
\gamma _ { i } = \psi _ { 2 } ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) = \delta u _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } - \delta s _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } + c _ { i } \kappa _ { i } \log \zeta .
$$

The cases $\pm \ 0 , q \neq 0$ are treated as follows:

From the integral

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal D } \ \delta u _ { i } = \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { i } } F ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } - { \bf m } ) \ \delta u _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } + { \bf m } ) \zeta ^ { 2 g _ { i } } \ \delta s _ { i } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal D } \ \delta s _ { i } = \frac { \partial } { \partial s _ { i } } F ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } - { \bf m } ) \ \delta s _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } + { \bf m } ) \zeta ^ { - 2 g _ { i } } \ \delta t } } \end{array}
$$

we can obtain the integral of

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal D } ~ \delta u _ { i } = \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { i } } F ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) ~ + \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { i } } G ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) ~ { = } ~ \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } + \kappa _ { i } \rlap / p _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \bf m } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal D } ~ \delta s _ { i } = \frac { \partial } { \partial s _ { i } } F ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) ~ + \frac { \partial } { \partial s _ { i } } G ( \delta u _ { i } , \delta s _ { i } , \zeta ) ~ { = } ~ \frac { \partial F } { \partial s _ { i } } - \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \bf m } . } } \end{array}
$$

In fact, by varying the integrals of the nondisturbed motion

$$
\begin{array} { r } { c _ { i } = \psi _ { 1 } [ \delta u _ { i } ( c _ { i } , \gamma _ { i } , \zeta ) , \delta s _ { i } ( c _ { i } , \gamma _ { i } , \zeta ) , \zeta ] , } \\ { \gamma _ { i } = \psi _ { 2 } [ \delta u _ { i } ( c _ { i } , \gamma _ { i } , \zeta ) , \delta s _ { i } ( c _ { i } , \gamma _ { i } , \zeta ) , \zeta ] , } \end{array}
$$

the disturbed motion should satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { r } { c _ { i } ( \zeta ) = \psi _ { 1 } \{ \delta u _ { i } [ c _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \zeta ] , \delta s _ { i } [ c _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \zeta ] , \zeta \} , } \\ { \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) = \psi _ { 2 } \{ \delta u _ { i } [ c _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \zeta ] , \delta s _ { i } [ c _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) , \zeta ] , \zeta \} . } \end{array}
$$

Apply the operator $D$ to these two sets of integrals; then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \ : \delta u _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \ : \delta s _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial s _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \zeta } D \zeta = 0 , } \\ { \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \ : \delta u _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \ : \delta s _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial s _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \zeta } D \zeta = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \delta u _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } + \frac { \partial G } { \partial u _ { i } } \right) + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \delta s _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial F } { \partial s _ { i } } + \frac { \partial G } { \partial s _ { i } } \right) + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \zeta } D \zeta = D c _ { i } ( \zeta ) , } \\ { \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \delta u _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } + \frac { \partial G } { \partial u _ { i } } \right) + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \delta s _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial F } { \partial s _ { i } } + \frac { \partial G } { \partial s _ { i } } \right) + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \zeta } D \zeta = D \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) . } \end{array}
$$

We obtain, from these relations,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle D c _ { i } ( \zeta ) = \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \mathrm { ~ } \delta u _ { i } } - \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial \mathrm { ~ } \delta s _ { i } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle D \gamma _ { i } ( \zeta ) = \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \mathrm { ~ } \delta u _ { i } } - \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial \mathrm { ~ } \delta s _ { i } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The first equation leads to the first integral

$$
\delta s _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } + \delta u _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } = C _ { i } + \kappa _ { i } D ^ { - 1 } \big ( \phi _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } \big ) ,
$$

and the second equation to the second integral

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { - g _ { i } - \delta s _ { i } { \cdot } \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } + \kappa _ { i } \log \zeta ( \delta s _ { i } { \cdot } \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } + \delta u _ { i } { \cdot } \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } ) } \\ & { \quad \quad = \Gamma _ { i } + \kappa _ { i } D ^ { - 1 } ( \phi _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } + q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) + \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } [ \log \zeta ( \phi _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) ] . } \end{array}
$$

We introduce the first integral into the second integral, after multiplying by $\kappa _ { i } \log \zeta$ and operating with $D ^ { - 1 }$ ; this gives

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } - \delta s _ { i } { \cdot } \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } = \Gamma _ { i } + \kappa _ { i } { \cal D } ^ { - 1 } ( p _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } + q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) } } \\ { { - \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } { \cal D } ^ { - 2 } ( p _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) - \kappa _ { i } C _ { i } \log \delta } } \end{array}
$$

The constants $C _ { i }$ and $\Gamma _ { i }$ can be determined by the second integral. Put

$$
u _ { i } / s _ { i } = \exp { \{ 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \ \theta _ { i } \} } ,
$$

or

$$
2 \sqrt { - 1 } \theta _ { i } = 2 g _ { i } \log \zeta + \delta u _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } - \delta s _ { i } \cdot \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } .
$$

Since $\begin{array} { r } { \log \zeta = \sqrt { - 1 } \nu _ { 3 } t , g _ { i } \nu _ { 3 } = \nu _ { i } } \end{array}$ we obtain

$$
\theta _ { i } = \nu _ { i } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } \left( \delta u _ { i } . \zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } - \delta s _ { i } . \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } \right) ;
$$

or, by the second integral,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle : = \frac { \Gamma _ { i } } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } + \nu _ { i } \bigg ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { i } \bigg ) t + \frac { \kappa _ { i } } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } D ^ { - 1 } ( \phi _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } + q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad - \frac { \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } D ^ { - 2 } ( \phi _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) } } \end{array}
$$

This gives $C _ { i } ,$ which is zero if the observed values of $\nu _ { i }$ are taken into account. The constants $\Gamma _ { i }$ give the longitudes of the satellites at $t = 0$ ； that is, $\Gamma _ { 1 } = \Gamma _ { 3 } = 0$ ， $\Gamma _ { 2 } = \overline { { { \Gamma _ { 4 } } } } = 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 }$ .Thus we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { r _ { i } = a _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma _ { i } a _ { i } \kappa _ { i } D ^ { - 1 } \big ( \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } \big ) . } \end{array}
$$

Inserting the values of $\zeta ^ { - g _ { i } } \ \delta u _ { i } + \zeta ^ { g _ { i } } \ \delta s _ { i }$ given by the first integral on the left-hand sides of the equations (82a), we obtain the equations of motion in the form of integrodifferential equations

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 \kappa _ { i } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma _ { i } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } D ^ { - 1 } \big ( \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } \big ) } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } } = \lambda _ { i } \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } + \displaystyle \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { 0 } } } { \kappa _ { i } } \mathfrak { R } _ { i j } , } \\ & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { - 2 \kappa _ { i } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma _ { i } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } D ^ { - 1 } \big ( \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } \big ) } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } \beta _ { i } } = - \lambda _ { i } \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - \displaystyle \sum _ { j \neq i } \frac { \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { 0 } } } { \kappa _ { i } } \mathfrak { S } _ { i j } . } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
P _ { i } = p _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } , \qquad Q _ { i } = q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { D P _ { i } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } P _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } Q _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } ( P _ { i } - Q _ { i } ) = \Re _ { i } , } \\ { D Q _ { i } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } Q _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } P _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } ( P _ { i } - Q _ { i } ) = \circledast _ { i } , } \end{array}
$$

where, for example,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sigma _ { i } \lambda _ { i } + \sum _ { j < i } \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } } { \kappa i \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \Biggl \{ \sigma _ { j } \alpha _ { j i } a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \zeta ^ { ( g _ { j } - g _ { i } ) } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { i } ^ { - 3 } \sum _ { k } \sigma _ { i } ^ { k } \sigma _ { j } ^ { k } G ^ { ( k ) } ( \alpha _ { j i } ) } \ ~ }  \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times ~ [ \zeta ^ { k ( g _ { j } - g _ { i } ) } + \zeta ^ { - k ( g _ { j } - g _ { i } ) } ] ( \sigma _ { i } - \alpha _ { j i } \sigma _ { j } ^ { \zeta ^ { g } _ { j } - g _ { i } } ) \Biggr \} } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \sum _ { j > i } \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } } { \kappa i \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \Biggl \{ \sigma _ { j } \alpha _ { j i } a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \zeta ^ { g _ { j } - g _ { i } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \sum _ { k } \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { k } \sigma _ { j } ^ { k } G ^ { ( k ) } ( \alpha _ { i j } ) } \ ~ }  \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times ~ [ \zeta ^ { k ( g _ { j } - g _ { i } ) } + \zeta ^ { - k ( g _ { j } - g _ { i } ) } ] ( \sigma _ { i } - \alpha _ { j i } \sigma _ { j } ^ { \zeta ^ { g _ { j } - g _ { i } } } ) \Biggr \} } , }  \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { \gamma } \zeta ^ { - n } } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \left\{ \sigma _ { \sigma ; \mu } ( a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \zeta ^ { n } + \frac 1 2 ~ a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \sum _ { k } \sigma _ { i } ^ { k } \sigma _ { j } ^ { k } ( \alpha _ { k j } ) } & { } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \times \ [ \zeta ^ { k ( \kappa _ { i } , - \kappa _ { i } ) } + \zeta ^ { - k ( \kappa _ { i } , - \kappa _ { j } ) } \big ] ( \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } - \sigma _ { \sigma ; \mu } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { j } } ) \right\} } \\ { \displaystyle } & { \qquad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { j } \zeta ^ { - n } } { \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \left\{ \sigma _ { \sigma ; \mu } ( a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { j } } + \frac 1 2 ~ a _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \sum _ { k } \sigma _ { i } ^ { k } \sigma _ { j } ^ { k } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { \sigma } } ( \alpha _ { j } ) } & { } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \quad \times \ [ \zeta ^ { k ( \kappa _ { i } , - \kappa _ { \sigma } ) } + \zeta ^ { - k ( \kappa _ { i } , - \kappa _ { j } ) } ( \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } - \sigma _ { j } \sigma _ { j } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { \sigma } } ) ] ( \sigma _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } - \sigma _ { j } \sigma _ { j } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { \sigma } } ) \right\} } \\ { \displaystyle } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos H ) ^ { - 3 / 2 } = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } G ^ { ( k ) } ( \alpha ) \cos k H .
$$

The homogeneous integrodifferential equations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { D P _ { i } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } P _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } Q _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } ( P _ { i } - Q _ { i } ) = 0 , } \\ { D Q _ { i } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } Q _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } P _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } ( P _ { i } - Q _ { i } ) = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

can be transformed to

$$
D ( P _ { i } + Q _ { i } ) = \kappa _ { i } [ \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } - \alpha _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } ] ,
$$

or

$$
P _ { i } = \alpha _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } , \qquad Q _ { i } = \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ,
$$

or

$$
\alpha _ { i } = e _ { i } \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \varpi _ { i } ) } , \beta _ { i } = e _ { i } \exp { ( - \sqrt { - 1 } \varpi _ { i } ) } .
$$

Then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \delta \theta _ { i } = \theta _ { i } - ( \theta _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \nu _ { i } t ) = \sqrt { - 1 } ( \alpha _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) = 2 e _ { i } \sin ( n _ { i } t - \varpi  } \\ & {  \vphantom { \sum _ { i } } \delta r _ { i } = r _ { i } - a _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { i } \sigma _ { i } \kappa _ { i } ^ { - 1 } D ^ { - 1 } ( \alpha _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \kappa _ { i } } - \beta _ { i } \zeta ^ { \kappa _ { i } } ) = - a _ { i } \sigma _ { i } e _ { i } \cos ( n _ { i } t - \varpi  } \end{array}
$$

The system of nonhomogeneous integrodifferential equations is written

$$
\mathbf { A } _ { i } \mathbf { y } _ { i } = \mathbf { b } _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { \zeta } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { A } _ { i } = \binom { D + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } } { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } } \quad D - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ^ { 2 } D ^ { - 1 } ~ } \end{array}
$$

$$
{ \bf y } _ { i } = { { \binom { P _ { i } } { Q _ { i } } } , { \bf b } } = \biggl ( { \frac { B \zeta ^ { \beta } } { - B \zeta ^ { - \beta } } } \biggr ) ,
$$

where ${ \bf b } _ { i } ( \zeta )$ are decomposed into the sum of vectors of the type b.The corresponding solution is

$$
\delta { \bf y } _ { i } = { \bf A } _ { i } ^ { - 1 } { \bf b } = { \frac { \imath } { 2 } } B { \left( \begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 - \kappa _ { i } \beta ^ { - 1 } } { \beta + \kappa _ { i } } } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { - \kappa _ { i } \beta ^ { - 1 } } { \beta + \kappa _ { i } } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { \kappa _ { i } \beta ^ { - 1 } } { \beta - \kappa _ { i } } } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 + \kappa _ { i } \beta ^ { - 1 } } { \beta - \kappa _ { i } } } } } \end{array} \right) } { \left( \begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \zeta ^ { \beta } } } \\ { { \displaystyle - \zeta ^ { - \beta } } } \end{array} \right) } ,
$$

provided that $\left| \beta \right| \neq \kappa _ { i } , \beta \neq 0 .$

In the case of the perturbation of Io due to Europa,

$$
B = \lambda _ { 1 } + \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { 2 } } { \kappa _ { 1 } \nu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } a _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } \left[ G ^ { ( 0 ) } ( \alpha _ { 1 2 } ) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 1 } G ^ { ( 1 ) } ( \alpha _ { 1 2 } ) \right] = 0 .
$$

$\hat { a } _ { 1 }$ is the observed value such that $\hat { a } _ { 1 } = \sqrt [ 3 ] { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m _ { 1 } ) / n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } ;$ then

$$
a _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } = \hat { a } _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } ( 1 + f ) , f = m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } [ \alpha _ { 1 2 } G ^ { ( 0 ) } ( \alpha _ { 1 2 } ) - { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } G ^ { ( 1 ) } ( \alpha _ { 1 2 } ) ] .
$$

The second-order perturbation is calculated from

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { D \ \delta u _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } - \mathbf { m } ) \ \delta u _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } + \mathbf { m } ) \zeta ^ { 2 g _ { i } } \ \delta s _ { i } + \kappa _ { i } \rlap / p _ { i } \zeta ^ { - \mathbf { m } } , } \\ & { D \ \delta s _ { i } = \ - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } - \mathbf { m } ) \ \delta s _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( g _ { i } + \mathbf { m } ) \zeta ^ { - 2 g _ { i } } \ \delta u _ { i } - \kappa _ { i } q _ { i } \zeta ^ { \mathbf { m } } . } \end{array}
$$

We refer to the method used for the cases $\pm \ 0 , \ q \neq 0$ .The integrodifferential equations are then of the form

$$
\mathbf { A } _ { i } \mathbf { y } _ { i } = \mathbf { b } _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { \zeta } ) + \mathbf { c } _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { \zeta } , \mathbf { y } _ { i } ) ,
$$

where $\mathbf { c } _ { i } ( \pmb { \zeta } , \pmb { y } _ { i } )$ denote the second-order terms.

Ferraz Mello then considered the effect of the figure of Jupiter by expanding in tesseral harmonic components,and also the effect of relativity. He derived the relativity effect on the motion of perijoves as $2 6 2 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,82"1, 25:5, and $6 \prime \prime 2 4$ per century for Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto, respectively.The relativity effect of the rotation of Jupiter on the motion of the perijove of Io is $- 1 7 ^ { \prime \prime }$ per century (Section 8.25).

An application of Hill's method to the motion of an artificial satellite has been worked out by Grebenikov (1960).

Shor (196l, 1962） derived the solution of the restricted three-body problem by the method of Hill and Brown, using a high-speed computer.

Elmabsout (1970) presented a seminumerical method in the lunar theory and applied it to the motion of Phoebe,a retrograde satellite of Saturn. Small divisors appear in the successive approximation due to a resonance between the general solution of the homogeneous system of equations and the long-period terms of the second member of this system. He eliminated them by introducing fictitious terms so that the period of the general solution is modified.

Musen (l97l） transformed the Hill-Brown differential equations of the lunar theory to a type which can be integrated by iteration by means of an analytic programming language. The differential operators are expanded into series in squares of the integration constants. In solving the differential equations Musen derived the expansions of the cube of the parallax and the square of the distance of the satellite from the earth in powers and products of the perturbations of the parallax, by expanding the corresponding Taylor operators in powers and products of the perturbations in the parallax and into series in the Faa de Bruno differential operators (Section 9.l9),which are associated with the formation of higher derivatives of a function of a function.The differential, integral,and Taylor operators in their expanded form, together with the extraction operator, constitute a complete set of operators to be used in deriving a literal theory in rectangular coordinates by iteration.

Brumberg (l97O) applied Hill's lunar method of the rectangular heliocentric coordinates to the formal solution of the $\pmb { n } .$ -planet problem by introducing an intermediary quasi-periodic solution (Section 2.10) as a generalization of Hill's variational curve. The deviation from this intermediary orbit is determined by a system of nonlinear differential equations.The solution is arranged in powers of slowly varying variables with quasi-periodic functions of the mean longitudes of the planets, expanded in powers of the planetary masses as coefficients. The slowly varying variables,which give the secular perturbations,are obtained from an autonomous system of nonlinear differential equations.

# Retrospect of Volume II

In Volume II, various classical methods for computing the perturbations are given together with their modern versions,with a view to their application to the motion of artificial satellites and interplanetary probes. All these methods are, unfortunately, not uniformly convergent, as will be shown in Volume III,and they are only semiconvergent in Poincaré's terminology.They may be convenient for predicting the motions within the present-day accuracy of observation. The question is whether the result would approach nearer the as yet unknown true solution if we push the approximation further in order to meet the demands in the future of increasing accuracy of observation.The circumstance may be similar to Stirling's asymptotic representation of factorials； that is,a stage might come when the approximation would break down ifwe push the approximation further and further.Even the estimation of the errors of the approximation cannot be made so far.By some of the modern methods which will be treated in the following volumes we may estimate the errors of the approximation.Then what will be the form of the solution of the planetary theory and the many-body problem -in particular, the three-body problem? This will be one of the topics of Volume III. Also,the difficult problem of stability will be dealt with according to Liapounov's work and its modern version.
